The Rank Path
Recent History
You have found this codex. Whether by intention or fortune, I consider that sufficient. What follows is not a list of rules to memorise — it is the architecture of a life conducted deliberately. I have refined these principles across many years of service. They have never failed me. I offer them now to you, with one condition: do not rush through this document seeking shortcuts. Shortcuts do not exist in the work of becoming. — Von Lycaon, Victoria Housekeeping Co.

The Four Pillars

🧊

Composure

Emotion is information, not instruction. I observe my reactions before I allow them to speak. A composed man is not cold — he is simply in command of himself first. This is the hardest pillar to build and the most visible when present.

🔍

Precision

Disorder in one's environment signals disorder in one's mind. I maintain the former impeccably so the latter may operate at its fullest capacity. Precision is not perfectionism — it is the refusal to accept less than the standard you have set.

📋

Reliability

If I say I will do something, it is done. This is not a courtesy — it is the foundation upon which trust is built. Reliability is rare enough that those who practise it become the person others instinctively turn to in difficulty.

📚

Mastery

A versatile attendant cannot afford gaps. I am not merely competent at my primary function — I am prepared for every adjacent one. Breadth of knowledge is not dilettantism. It is the preparation to be genuinely useful in circumstances you cannot predict.

On Stoicism — The Philosophical Backbone

The philosophical tradition most aligned with how I conduct myself is Stoicism — specifically the practical Roman variety. Not the academic kind argued in lecture halls, but the kind written by a Roman emperor to himself in private notes that were never meant to be published. If you read one thing from this codex, let it be Meditations by Marcus Aurelius. He ruled the largest empire in the world and still reminded himself daily that he had no control over other people's behaviour — only his own responses.

The Stoic framework, briefly: divide everything in the world into two categories. What is within your control — your judgements, intentions, actions, effort. And what is not — other people, outcomes, weather, office politics, system outages, the economy. Direct all your energy at the first category. Accept the second with equanimity. This is not resignation. It is an extraordinarily efficient allocation of effort.

The Dichotomy of Control

"Some things are in our control and others not. Things in our control are opinion, pursuit, desire, aversion. Things not in our control are body, reputation, command, and in one word, whatever are not our own actions." — Epictetus, Enchiridion

Memento Mori

A daily awareness of mortality — not as morbidity but as prioritisation. If I knew I had one year remaining, which of my current concerns would dissolve immediately? That list tells me exactly which concerns deserve less energy today.

Amor Fati

Love of fate. The Stoic does not merely tolerate what happens — he chooses to embrace it as necessary. Not because it is always good, but because resisting what has already happened wastes the only resource that cannot be recovered: time.

On Identity vs. Performance

There is a meaningful distinction between performing elegance and embodying it. The former is exhausting, inconsistent, and collapses under pressure. The latter is automatic, because it has been practised until it requires no deliberate thought. The goal of this codex is to move habits from the conscious to the unconscious — where they become character rather than effort.

Identity-based habits ask not "what do I want to achieve" but "who do I want to become." Every time you act in accordance with the standard in this codex, you cast a vote for the person you are building. One vote means nothing. Ten thousand votes, accumulated over years, mean everything.— On the mechanism of lasting change

What This Codex Is Not

  • It is not a costume. These principles have no value if worn only when observed. The measure of a standard is how you behave when no audience exists.
  • It is not rigid formality. I adapt constantly. Context demands it. The same man who speaks with perfect precision in a meeting can be warm and direct over coffee. Rigidity is not refinement.
  • It is not superiority. Conducting oneself well does not make one better than others — it makes one more useful to them. The gentleman is in service of the people around him, not above them.
  • It is not an arrival point. I continue to revise this codex. I learn something new every year that changes a section. You should approach it the same way.
  • It is not a quick transformation. Attempting to adopt every principle at once is precisely the kind of undisciplined enthusiasm I discourage. One habit, genuinely mastered, is worth twenty habits attempted and abandoned.

The Progression Model

When beginning any new discipline, a person passes through four phases. Understanding this prevents the discouragement that causes most people to abandon the effort prematurely.

Unconscious IncompetenceYou don't know what you don't know

The Daily Philosophy Practice

Philosophy without practice is merely intellectual entertainment. The Stoics were explicit about this — Epictetus taught that the goal of philosophical study was not knowledge but action. The test of any philosophical understanding is whether it changes how you behave in the moments when behaviour is difficult. These are the daily practices that convert understanding into character.

  • The morning question: Before leaving the apartment, state explicitly one quality you intend to embody today. Not a task — a character quality. Patience. Precision. Generosity. The act of naming it primes the relevant neural pathways before the situations that test them arise. It takes 15 seconds. Over a year of consistent practice, it changes the default responses that emerge under pressure.
  • The evening audit: Three questions. What did I do well today? Where did I fall short of my own standards? What would I do differently? The Stoics called this the evening examination, and Seneca practised it nightly. The journal is the correct medium — writing forces a specificity that mental review avoids. The review that stays vague stays useless.
  • The obstacle reframe: When something goes wrong — a plan frustrated, a conversation that went poorly, a professional setback — the Stoic practice is to ask immediately: what is the opportunity in this? Not as toxic positivity but as genuine inquiry. The obstacle that forces a change of approach often produces a better approach than the one it interrupted. This is the practical meaning of amor fati: not that you are glad the obstacle occurred, but that you refuse to treat it as merely negative.

Philosophy in Sarajevo — The Local Tradition

Bosnia has a distinct philosophical tradition that does not appear in Western curricula. The philosophy faculty at the University of Sarajevo has produced significant work in phenomenology and existentialism — the encounter of Husserlian and Heideggerian thought with the specific cultural context of Bosnian society. The philosopher Abdulah Sarević and the tradition of Bosnian Islamic philosophy (rooted in the Ottoman-era medresa system) represent intellectual traditions that most Sarajevo residents are not aware of inhabiting. For a resident who reads philosophy, the local dimension of the intellectual tradition is worth investigating — it is available in the Faculty of Philosophy library on Franje Račkog and in the Gazi Husrev-beg Library manuscript collection.

Philosophy is the practice of living with greater awareness, precision, and honesty — with yourself and with others. Every other skill in this Codex is downstream of this practice. Master the foundation, and the rest follows.— Von Lycaon
The Philosophy tab introduces Stoicism as the foundational framework of this Codex. This tab is the application — how the Stoic principles are actually used in the specific situations that arise in a daily life like yours: work frustration, physical fatigue, relationship friction, the gap between ambition and current reality. Theory without application remains merely interesting. Application is what changes behaviour.— Von Lycaon

The Core Doctrine — Applied

The Dichotomy of Control

Epictetus: "Some things are in our control and others not." What is in your control: your judgments, your intentions, your responses, your effort. What is not: other people's behaviour, outcomes of your actions, your employer's decisions, the weather, your health history. The Stoic practice: when experiencing frustration or distress, immediately classify the cause. If it is outside your control — accept it as the price of living in a world you do not govern. If inside — act on it.

Preferred Indifferents

The Stoics were not indifferent to external outcomes — they recognised that health, success, good relationships, and financial stability are genuinely preferable to their opposites. They called these "preferred indifferents" — things you should rationally pursue, but whose absence should not undermine equanimity. The distinction: pursue them fully, but do not make your emotional stability contingent on them. The career goal pursued without desperation. The health goal pursued without anxiety about the outcome.

Amor Fati

"Love of fate" — Nietzsche's phrase for the Stoic attitude. Not mere acceptance of what occurs, but the active embrace of it as the material from which the present is made. The difficult situation is not the obstacle to the life — it is the life. The setback contains the information and the opportunity that the comfortable path does not. Marcus Aurelius: "The impediment to action advances action. What stands in the way becomes the way."

Memento Mori

"Remember that you will die." Not as morbidity but as perspective. The Stoics used the regular contemplation of their own mortality to clarify what actually mattered. Marcus Aurelius: practically every book of Meditations contains a reminder that he will die, his successors will die, and that the Roman Empire itself will pass. The practice: when upset about a minor frustration — genuinely trivial in the context of a finite life — the memento mori question provides immediate perspective that no rationalisation achieves as quickly.

Stoicism in Specific Situations

Work Frustration and Difficult Colleagues

  • The Marcus Aurelius approach: "Begin the morning by saying to yourself: I shall meet with the busybody, the ungrateful, the arrogant, the deceitful, the envious, the unsocial." Not cynicism — preparation. The difficult colleague is not a surprise or a personal affront. They are part of the landscape that includes difficult people, because it includes people. React accordingly.
  • The separation of role from person: When a manager makes a decision you disagree with, a Stoic approach separates the decision (which you can evaluate and respond to with evidence) from the person (whose character and intentions you largely cannot control or fully know). Respond to the decision. Do not catastrophise about the person.
  • The view from above: Marcus Aurelius's technique — mentally ascend above the situation and observe it from a distance. The argument with a colleague, seen from a sufficient perspective, is a small event in a long career in a short life. This does not make it unimportant — it makes it proportionate.

Physical Discomfort and Fatigue

  • Voluntary discomfort: Seneca and Epictetus both practiced deliberately choosing discomfort — cold exposure, fasting, uncomfortable sleeping conditions — not as self-punishment but as rehearsal. The cold shower in the Cold Exposure tab is a Stoic practice before it is a physiological one. "I have practised poverty... so that I may answer Fortune: I have never feared the things you have threatened me with."
  • Distinguishing pain from harm: The Stoics observed that most discomfort is not dangerous — it is unpleasant. The workout fatigue is unpleasant; it is not harmful. The cold water is unpleasant; it is not harmful. The long day of demanding work is exhausting; it is not harmful. Naming the distinction clearly reduces the emotional amplification that transforms discomfort into suffering.

Negative Visualisation — The Daily Practice

  • The Stoic practice of premeditatio malorum (premeditation of evils): regularly imagining the loss of what you value — relationships, health, career, possessions — not to induce anxiety but to restore appreciation for what is present. The wife you love, imagined absent, becomes more valuable in her presence. The apartment, imagined taken, becomes more valued on an ordinary evening.
  • The journal application: The evening journal entry can include one thing that, if absent, would be deeply missed. One line. This practice, consistently applied, is one of the most reliable antidotes to the hedonic adaptation that causes people to stop appreciating what they have.

The Daily Stoic Practice — Concretely

  • Morning intention: Before the first task, state explicitly what today requires — not a to-do list, but a quality of character. "Today I will be patient with difficult situations." "Today I will work with full focus and accept the outcome without anxiety." One sentence. The act of stating it primes the relevant response pattern before it is needed.
  • Evening review: The Stoic evening reflection (Seneca practised this): What did I do well today? What could I have done better? What was my response to the things outside my control? Not self-flagellation — honest assessment. The journal entries over months become a record of character development.
Stoicism does not promise tranquillity as a gift. It offers it as a practice — available in proportion to the consistency with which its principles are applied. The man who applies them for a week and expects transformation will be disappointed. The man who applies them for a year will barely recognise the previous version of his responses to the same provocations.— Von Lycaon
Every outcome in a life is the product of decisions accumulated over time. The quality of those decisions determines the quality of the outcome — not the intentions behind them, not the effort applied afterward, not the circumstances surrounding them. Improving decision quality is therefore the highest-leverage activity available, and it is almost entirely a learnable skill.— Von Lycaon

Why Decisions Go Wrong

System 1 vs System 2

Kahneman's framework (Thinking, Fast and Slow — Reading List tab): System 1 is fast, automatic, intuitive — it produces most of our decisions with minimal effort. System 2 is slow, deliberate, analytical — it can override System 1 but requires significant cognitive energy. The problem: System 1 is prone to systematic biases that produce predictable errors. The solution: not to eliminate System 1 (impossible and undesirable) but to know when System 2 needs to be deliberately engaged.

The Most Consequential Biases

Confirmation bias: Seeking evidence that confirms existing beliefs and discounting disconfirming evidence. Sunk cost fallacy: Continuing a failing course of action because of resources already invested rather than expected future value. Availability heuristic: Overweighting recent or vivid examples when assessing probability. Overconfidence: Systematically overestimating accuracy of judgments and predictions. These four alone account for a significant proportion of major decision errors in professional and personal life.

Outcome vs Process

A good decision is not one that produces a good outcome — luck can produce good outcomes from poor decisions and bad luck can produce poor outcomes from excellent decisions. A good decision is one made with the correct process: relevant information gathered, alternatives considered, probabilities estimated, values applied. Evaluating decision quality by outcome is the most common and most misleading method of learning from experience.

The Decision Frameworks

The Pre-Mortem

  • Before implementing a significant decision, imagine it is one year in the future and the decision has failed — catastrophically. Now work backward: what went wrong? What factors, risks, or weaknesses does this revealed failure illuminate? The pre-mortem is more effective than standard risk assessment because it bypasses optimism bias — it starts from failure rather than asking "what could go wrong?" which the optimistic mind underweights.
  • Developed by Gary Klein. Takes 10 minutes for most decisions. Produces a risk checklist that improves the decision or its implementation plan.

The 10/10/10 Rule

  • Suzy Welch's framework: How will I feel about this decision in 10 minutes? In 10 months? In 10 years? The three time horizons produce three different emotional and rational evaluations of the same decision. A decision that feels comfortable in 10 minutes, regrettable in 10 months, and deeply wrong in 10 years is not a good decision regardless of its immediate comfort. The framework forces temporal perspective that immediate emotion suppresses.

The Reversibility Test

  • Bezos's distinction: Type 1 decisions are irreversible, consequential, and should be made slowly with extensive analysis. Type 2 decisions are reversible and should be made quickly with sufficient rather than excessive analysis. Most decisions are Type 2 that are being treated as Type 1 — producing decision paralysis for minor reversible choices. Most genuinely important decisions are Type 1 being treated as Type 2 — made quickly on intuition when they warrant slow deliberate analysis. Correctly classifying the decision type determines the appropriate investment of decision-making time.

Second-Order Thinking

  • First-order thinking asks: what is the immediate effect of this decision? Second-order thinking asks: and then what? And then what after that? Most poor decisions are excellent first-order choices with terrible second-order consequences. A purchase that feels good immediately but creates ongoing financial pressure. A conversation avoided that creates ongoing relationship tension. A work shortcut that creates technical debt. Before any significant decision: ask explicitly what the second and third-order consequences are.

The Decision Journal

The single most effective tool for improving decision quality over time: record significant decisions in the Obsidian journal with: the decision made, the alternatives considered, the reasoning, the expected outcome, and the confidence level. Review them 3–6 months later. The gap between what you expected and what occurred reveals systematic biases in your specific decision-making that no general framework can predict. This is how you build a personalised map of your own cognitive blind spots.

A man who makes the same type of error repeatedly is not unlucky. He has a systematic flaw in his decision process that has not yet been identified. The journal identifies it. The pre-mortem prevents it. The reversibility test allocates the appropriate effort. Applied consistently, these tools produce a measurably different quality of outcomes — not because circumstances change, but because the process that generates choices from circumstances improves.— Von Lycaon

Group Decision Making

Individual decision quality and group decision quality are different problems. The group introduces dynamics that systematically degrade the quality of the individual inputs: social proof (people conform to perceived consensus), authority bias (the highest-ranking person's opinion receives disproportionate weight), and groupthink (the desire for harmony overrides honest disagreement). These dynamics produce confident, unanimous group decisions that are inferior to what any thoughtful individual in the group would have produced alone.

  • Designated dissent: Assign one person in any group decision to argue against the proposal — regardless of their personal view. The role exists to surface the arguments that social dynamics suppress. The devil's advocate with a formal role produces better criticism than the voluntary critic who fears social cost.
  • Independent pre-commitment: Before any group discussion of a significant decision, have each participant write their assessment and recommendation privately. Share these simultaneously before discussion. This prevents the anchor effect of the first speaker dominating all subsequent thinking.
  • The vanishing options test: If the current options under consideration all disappeared — what would you do? This test reveals whether you are genuinely choosing the best option or merely choosing between the options someone has placed in front of you.

The Broader Decision Ecology

  • Decision fatigue is real. The quality of decisions degrades across a day as the cognitive resources required for deliberation are depleted. Important decisions belong in the morning. Routine decisions belong in the afternoon. Decisions that can wait belong tomorrow. Obama and Zuckerberg's identical daily clothing is not affectation — it is the elimination of a trivial decision to preserve cognitive resources for consequential ones.
  • The distinction between fast and slow decisions: Kahneman's System 1 (fast, automatic, intuitive) produces most decisions with minimal effort. System 2 (slow, deliberate, analytical) is expensive and limited in daily capacity. The skill is knowing which system the decision requires — deploying System 2 for decisions that warrant it and allowing System 1 to handle those that do not. Using System 2 for every decision depletes it for the decisions where it is genuinely needed.
Critical thinking is the discipline of evaluating claims — your own and others' — by examining the quality of the reasoning and evidence behind them rather than the source's authority or the claim's emotional appeal. It is not scepticism for its own sake. It is the intellectual hygiene that separates what is actually known from what merely feels true, and what is good reasoning from what only resembles it.— Von Lycaon

The Core Skills

Claim vs Evidence

Every significant claim should be assessed by its evidence, not its source. An expert is more likely to be right than a non-expert in their domain, but this is a probability, not a guarantee. The question is always: what is the evidence for this claim, how strong is it, and has it been independently replicated? A single study, regardless of the journal, is preliminary. A meta-analysis of many studies is substantially more reliable. Consensus among experts who have reviewed the full evidence base is the strongest available signal in empirical questions.

Correlation vs Causation

The most pervasive reasoning error in non-specialists encountering data. Two things occurring together does not mean one causes the other. Ice cream sales and drowning rates are correlated — summer causes both. Countries with more hospitals have more disease — the hospital is not causing the disease. Before accepting any causal claim, ask: is there a plausible mechanism? Has the direction of causation been established? Could both be caused by a third variable? Could the result be explained by selection bias?

The Logical Fallacies Worth Knowing

Ad hominem: Attacking the person rather than the argument. Straw man: Misrepresenting an opponent's position to make it easier to attack. False dichotomy: Presenting two options as the only possibilities when more exist. Appeal to authority: Treating expert opinion as infallible rather than as one evidence input. Slippery slope: Claiming that a small step inevitably leads to extreme consequences without demonstrating the mechanism. Recognising these in real-time argumentation — in others and in yourself — is the practical application of logic.

Steelmanning

The opposite of the straw man: constructing the strongest possible version of an opposing argument before responding to it. If you cannot articulate the best case for a position you disagree with, you have not fully understood the disagreement. The Social Skills tab's conflict section requires this skill — you cannot find common ground with a position you have only seen in its weakest form.

Information Quality Assessment

Evaluating Sources

  • Primary sources over secondary over tertiary. A peer-reviewed research paper is a primary source. A newspaper article about the paper is secondary. A social media post about the article is tertiary. Each step away from the primary source introduces interpretation, simplification, and potential distortion. For important claims, find the primary source.
  • Incentive analysis: Who benefits from this claim being believed? A pharmaceutical company funding research on their own drug has a financial incentive that may influence design, analysis, and publication decisions. This doesn't automatically invalidate the research — but it is a relevant input into how much weight to assign it. Follow the incentives before accepting any claim at face value.
  • Consensus vs contrarian claims: Scientific consensus should be updated by new evidence, but should be updated slowly — a single study rarely overturns established consensus for good reason. Contrarian claims that receive disproportionate attention (often because they are surprising or politically convenient) warrant extra scrutiny. The base rate of revolutionary discoveries overturning established consensus is low; the base rate of sensationalised studies that fail to replicate is high.

Applied Critical Thinking — Daily Use

  • News consumption: For any significant news claim, find the primary source (the actual report, the actual statement, the actual study). The gap between a primary source and how it is reported is often larger than assumed. This applies equally to sources you trust — your preferred news outlet is also subject to simplification, framing effects, and selective emphasis.
  • Professional claims: When a colleague, manager, or vendor makes a significant claim about a system, a process, or an outcome — ask: what is the evidence for this? What alternatives were considered? What were the failure modes? Not as confrontation but as genuine inquiry. The person with a good answer will provide it. The person without one will often reveal assumptions that needed to be surfaced.
  • Self-assessment: Apply the same standard to your own beliefs. Which of your strongly held opinions are held on the basis of evidence versus tribal affiliation, emotional comfort, or inherited assumption? The most important beliefs to scrutinise are the ones that feel too obvious to examine — these are the ones most likely to have been accepted without sufficient evidence.
The critical thinker is not the man who doubts everything — that is merely a different form of intellectual laziness. He is the man who calibrates his confidence to the quality of evidence available, updates his beliefs when better evidence arrives, and applies the same standards of reasoning to claims he wishes were true as to claims he wishes were false.— Von Lycaon
A mental model is a representation of how something works — a framework that allows faster and more accurate thinking in a domain. The person with more mental models has more lenses available for the same situation. The person with fewer sees situations through the single lens they have, whether it fits or not. This tab is a curated library of the most broadly applicable mental models across thinking, decision-making, and human behaviour.— Von Lycaon

First Principles Thinking

Breaking a problem down to its most fundamental truths — the things that are incontrovertibly true — and reasoning up from those rather than from analogy or convention. Elon Musk's battery cost example: the conventional assumption was that batteries would always be expensive because they always had been. First principles: what are batteries actually made of? What do those materials cost on commodity markets? The answer revealed that the assumed cost floor was wrong by a factor of ten.

Application: When facing any problem that seems intractable because "that's how it's always been done" — decompose to first principles. What is actually true here, stripped of assumption and convention? This is the thinking mode behind the best business strategy, the best engineering, and the best cooking.

The Core Models — Indexed

Inversion

Charlie Munger: "Invert, always invert." Instead of asking "how do I achieve success?" ask "what would guarantee failure, and how do I avoid those things?" Inverting a problem often reveals constraints and failure modes that forward thinking misses. The pre-mortem (Decision Making tab) is an application of inversion. Before designing a system, list everything that would make it fail.

Second-Order Effects

The direct consequence of an action is the first-order effect. The consequences of those consequences are second-order. Most poor decisions are good first-order choices with negative second-order effects. The antibiotic that cures the infection (first order) but disrupts the gut microbiome (second order). The policy that solves the immediate problem (first order) but creates perverse incentives (second order). Always ask: "And then what?"

Occam's Razor

Among competing explanations for the same observation, the simpler one is more likely to be correct. Not always — but as a prior. When a complex explanation requires many assumptions and a simple one requires few, the simple one is the better starting point. The model prevents the human tendency to construct elaborate explanations for things that have straightforward causes.

Hanlon's Razor

Never attribute to malice what can be adequately explained by incompetence (or misunderstanding, or negligence). Most frustrating human behaviour is not hostile — it is thoughtless. Defaulting to malice as an explanation produces defensive, adversarial responses to situations that actually require patient explanation or correction. The charitable interpretation is usually more accurate and always more productive to act on.

The Map Is Not the Territory

Alfred Korzybski. Any model, description, or representation of reality is a simplification — it omits details, contains assumptions, and reflects the perspective of the person who created it. Treating the map as the territory produces errors when the territory diverges from the map. The practical implication: hold every model, including the ones in this tab, with appropriate tentativeness. They are useful simplifications, not truth.

Regression to the Mean

After an extreme event, the next event in a sequence tends toward the average. The student who performs brilliantly on one test will likely perform less brilliantly on the next — not because they declined but because the extreme performance contained luck that will not repeat. This model prevents misinterpreting natural variation as meaningful trend: a poor sales month followed by an average month is not "recovery" — it is regression to the mean.

Circle of Competence

Warren Buffett: define the domain in which your knowledge is genuinely deep, and operate within it. Acknowledge clearly and honestly where that circle ends. The most expensive mistakes come not from incompetence within the circle but from operating outside it while believing you are still inside. Expanding the circle through deliberate learning is correct; pretending it is larger than it is produces catastrophic decisions.

Opportunity Cost

Every choice eliminates alternative choices. The cost of any decision includes not only what was spent but what was foregone. Spending an evening watching television costs not only the time but the book that was not read, the skill that was not practised, the conversation that did not happen. The model does not argue for relentless productivity — it argues for consciousness about what is being chosen and what is being declined.

Availability Bias

From Kahneman (Reading List): the tendency to overestimate the probability of events that are easily recalled — usually because they are recent, vivid, or emotionally charged. Plane crashes are overestimated because they are dramatic. Car accidents are underestimated because they are common. The news systematically distorts this heuristic by selecting for the dramatic and unusual. Knowing this, discount your intuitive probability estimates for dramatic events upward and downward respectively.

Leverage Points

Donella Meadows: systems have certain variables whose change produces disproportionate effects throughout the system. These are leverage points. In an organisation, the information flow is often a higher leverage point than any specific rule or incentive — changing what information is available changes all subsequent decisions. In personal change, the habit at the beginning of a sequence (the morning routine) is a higher leverage point than habits later in the day. Find the leverage points; intervene there.

Building the Model Library

  • Farnam Street (fs.blog): Shane Parrish's comprehensive mental models library. The single best free resource on applied thinking available online. The Great Mental Models book series (3 volumes) is the definitive printed reference.
  • The application habit: After any significant decision or situation, ask which mental models were relevant. Did you use inversion? Did you check for second-order effects? Were you inside your circle of competence? The library only improves thinking if it is consciously applied rather than passively read.
A man with one mental model sees every problem as something that model can solve. A man with a library applies the model that fits. The intelligence is not in the models themselves but in knowing which one the current situation requires.— Von Lycaon

The Physics Models

Critical Mass

In nuclear physics, the point at which a chain reaction becomes self-sustaining. Applied broadly: the threshold at which a system acquires enough momentum to sustain itself without continued input. A habit requires external motivation until it reaches critical mass — after which the internal reward of the habit sustains it. A business requires external investment until it reaches critical mass of customers who sustain it through referrals. Identify the critical mass threshold in any system you are trying to build, and focus resources on reaching it rather than on sustaining the pre-critical state indefinitely.

Entropy

Systems naturally move from order to disorder without continuous energy input. Applied: any relationship, organisation, skill, or physical system that receives no maintenance will degrade. This is not pessimism — it is the operating condition of everything. The marriage that receives no maintenance deteriorates. The skill not practised erodes. The apartment not cleaned accumulates disorder. Maintenance is not optional; it is the baseline cost of sustaining any state above the natural entropic baseline.

Activation Energy

In chemistry, the energy required to initiate a reaction. Applied: every behaviour has an activation energy — the initial effort required to begin it. Reducing activation energy for desired behaviours increases their frequency. Increasing activation energy for undesired behaviours reduces them. The phone in another room has higher activation energy than the phone on the desk. The running shoes by the door have lower activation energy than those in the wardrobe. Environmental design (covered in the Procrastination tab) is activation energy management.

How to Build the Library Actively

Reading about mental models produces no benefit. Applying them changes decisions. The practice: after any significant situation — a conflict, a decision, a failed plan — ask which mental models were relevant. Which did you apply? Which should you have applied? Which would have produced a better outcome? This retrospective application builds the automatic pattern-recognition that makes the models useful in real time rather than only in retrospect. The goal is not a library of concepts but a set of automatic lenses that engage without conscious effort when the situation warrants them.

Procrastination is not laziness. Laziness is the absence of desire to act. Procrastination is the active choice to defer an action you have already decided to take. The distinction matters because it changes the intervention. Laziness requires motivation. Procrastination requires understanding the specific mechanism that is causing the deferral — which is almost never the one the procrastinator believes it is.— Von Lycaon

The Actual Mechanisms

Research by Pychyl, Sirois, and Fuschia over two decades has converged on a finding that overturns the common understanding: procrastination is primarily an emotion regulation problem, not a time management problem. The person who procrastinates is not failing to schedule correctly. They are avoiding a negative emotional state — anxiety, boredom, self-doubt, frustration, resentment — that the task evokes. The deferral provides immediate emotional relief. The cost (accumulated stress, deadline pressure, guilt) is paid later.

This reframe is not an excuse. It is a diagnosis. And the correct treatment of any problem begins with the correct diagnosis.

Task Aversiveness

The primary driver. Tasks are procrastinated in proportion to how aversive they feel — not how difficult they are. A difficult task that is interesting is started immediately. An easy task that is tedious, anxiety-inducing, or resentment-triggering is deferred indefinitely. The intervention: reduce the aversiveness of the task, not the difficulty. Make starting less unpleasant.

Temporal Discounting

The future cost of procrastination (the deadline pressure, the shame of incompletion, the quality reduction) is discounted heavily relative to the immediate relief of deferral. This is a feature of human cognition, not a moral failing — the future self feels abstract in a way the present discomfort does not. The intervention: make the future cost concrete and present. Write down exactly what will happen the morning of the deadline if the task is deferred tonight.

Perfectionism

The version that produces procrastination: the belief that if the output cannot be excellent, it should not be started. Starting means confronting the gap between the imagined excellent version and what is actually producible. Not starting preserves the fiction that the excellent version remains possible. The intervention: explicitly lower the quality standard for the first version. The goal is completion, not excellence — excellence is applied in the revision.

Unclear Next Action

David Allen's insight (Productivity tab): "Write report" is not an actionable task. It is a project. The procrastinated item is almost never the real next action — it is a vague label for something that actually requires 20 steps. The intervention: identify the single next physical action. "Open laptop, create new document, write the title and one sentence." That is the task. It takes 90 seconds. The momentum from completion often carries into the subsequent steps.

The Interventions — Ranked by Evidence

1. Implementation Intentions (Strongest Evidence)

  • A specific "when-then" plan: "When I sit down at my desk at 09:00, then I will open the document and write the first paragraph before doing anything else." Not "I will work on the report tomorrow morning." The specificity of time, location, and first action dramatically increases follow-through in research trials — the implementation intention bypasses the decision point where procrastination enters.
  • Write the implementation intention in the journal the evening before. The act of writing it is a commitment device. The morning version of you has the instruction already prepared by the calmer evening version.

2. The Two-Minute Rule

  • If the next action takes under two minutes, do it immediately rather than scheduling it. The cognitive overhead of scheduling, tracking, and re-encountering a two-minute task costs more time than simply doing it.
  • Extended: if you cannot start a task in two minutes, identify the version of it that takes two minutes and do that version. The report cannot be written in two minutes. Opening the document and writing one sentence can. This is not the same as the report — it is the beginning of it, which is the only thing preventing it from being finished.

3. Self-Compassion Over Self-Criticism

  • Counter-intuitive finding from Kristin Neff's research: people who respond to procrastination with self-criticism procrastinate more on subsequent tasks. The guilt and self-reproach that follow a procrastination episode increase the aversiveness of the next work session, which increases procrastination. The cycle is self-reinforcing.
  • People who respond with self-compassion — acknowledging that procrastination is a common human difficulty, not a character defect — procrastinate less on subsequent tasks. This is not lowering standards. It is removing the emotional penalty that makes the next attempt harder.
  • In practice: after a procrastination episode, acknowledge it without extended self-judgment. Note what the task evoked emotionally. Identify the next action. Begin.

4. Environmental Design

  • Remove the path of least resistance from the competing activity. The phone in another room means accessing it requires a decision. The phone on the desk means it is the easier choice every time attention wavers. The friction of accessing the distraction is more powerful than the willpower to resist it.
  • The Windows 11 tab's focus mode, the phone's Digital Wellbeing time limits, and the physical removal of the phone from the work space are environmental designs that reduce procrastination without requiring sustained willpower. Design the environment to make the correct behaviour the easiest behaviour.

5. The Pomodoro Technique — For Sustained Work

  • 25 minutes of focused work followed by a 5-minute break. The timer creates a bounded commitment — "I am working for 25 minutes" rather than "I am working until this is done," which feels unbounded and therefore more aversive. The break is a reward available at a predictable interval.
  • The technique is most useful for tasks with no natural stopping point — writing, analysis, coding. It is less useful for tasks that require extended flow states. Use it for the starting problem; abandon it once momentum is established.

The Chronic Procrastinator — When It Is Deeper

If procrastination is severe, pervasive, and significantly damaging quality of life despite genuine attempts to address it, it may be a symptom of something beneath the habit level: ADHD (attention deficit — procrastination is one of the primary functional symptoms), depression (anhedonia reduces the motivational pull of future rewards that normally counterbalances present discomfort), anxiety disorders, or burnout. These require clinical attention, not productivity techniques. The techniques in this tab address normal procrastination. Pervasive, treatment-resistant procrastination that does not respond to these interventions warrants a conversation with a doctor rather than a longer list of tips.

The task will not become easier by being deferred. The version of you who faces it tomorrow is not more capable than the version facing it now. He is only more pressured. Act now, imperfectly, and revise. The revision is always easier than the beginning.— Von Lycaon
Meditation is the practice of deliberate attention — training the capacity to direct focus, observe mental states without being controlled by them, and return to the present moment from wherever rumination has carried the mind. The benefits are not mystical. They are neurological, physiological, and practical. They also require a specific approach to avoid the trap of sitting and waiting for something to happen.— Von Lycaon

What Meditation Actually Does

The Neuroscience

Regular meditation practice measurably increases grey matter density in the prefrontal cortex (executive function, decision-making), the anterior insula (body awareness, self-regulation), and the hippocampus (memory, stress regulation). It reduces activity in the default mode network — the "wandering mind" network that produces rumination, anxiety about the future, and regret about the past. These changes are detectable in MRI scans after as little as eight weeks of daily 10-minute practice.

The Stress Connection

The Stress & Fatigue tab identifies cortisol dysregulation as the primary mechanism of work fatigue. Meditation directly reduces cortisol levels — not during the session only, but as a baseline change over weeks of practice. The mechanism: regular meditation trains the prefrontal cortex to modulate the amygdala's threat response, producing a faster return to baseline after stress activation and a lower resting activation level.

What It Is Not

Meditation is not emptying the mind. The mind will produce thoughts continuously during any meditation session — this is not failure, it is the nature of the brain. The practice is noticing that the mind has wandered and returning attention to the chosen object (breath, body, sound). Each return is the repetition — the equivalent of a bicep curl in mental training. The quality of the session is determined not by the absence of thoughts but by the frequency and quality of the returns.

The Starting Protocol — 8 Weeks

Weeks 1–2 — Breath Awareness (5 minutes)

  • Sit comfortably — chair, floor, doesn't matter. Upright spine, not rigid. Eyes closed or soft gaze downward. Set a timer for 5 minutes.
  • Bring attention to the physical sensation of breathing — the air at the nostrils, the chest or belly rising and falling. Not the concept of breathing. The physical sensation.
  • When the mind wanders (it will, within seconds for beginners), notice that it has wandered without judgment, and return attention to the breath. That's it. That's the practice. Repeat for 5 minutes.
  • The most important thing at this stage: consistency over duration. 5 minutes daily is worth more than 30 minutes twice weekly. The neural changes require frequency of repetition, not occasional long sessions.

Weeks 3–5 — Body Scan (10 minutes)

  • Extend to 10 minutes. Shift from breath focus to systematic body attention — directing awareness slowly from the top of the head through the face, neck, shoulders, arms, torso, hips, legs, and feet. At each area: notice any sensation present (tension, warmth, tingling, nothing) without trying to change it.
  • The body scan develops interoceptive awareness — the ability to accurately sense the body's internal states. This is the same skill that allows recognition of stress responses before they become overwhelming, and identification of physical tension that the stress response has been holding for hours unnoticed.

Weeks 6–8 — Open Awareness (15 minutes)

  • Rather than focusing attention on a specific object (breath, body), rest in open awareness — receive whatever arises (sounds, sensations, thoughts, feelings) without holding onto it or pushing it away. Observe mental events as events — thoughts arise, exist, and pass, the same way sounds arise, exist, and fade. You are the observer of the thought, not the thinker of it. This distinction, once genuinely experienced rather than understood conceptually, changes the relationship with rumination and anxiety fundamentally.

Practical Integration

  • Morning is the optimal window — after waking, before the phone or first commitment. The mind is neither fully alert nor fully asleep, making it more accessible to meditative practice than later in the day when it is fully engaged with external demands.
  • Insight Timer app — already in Phone & Apps tab. The free timer function serves all of the above practices. The guided meditation library is extensive for those who prefer guidance over solo practice. Sam Harris's "Waking Up" app is the most intellectually rigorous meditation guidance available — free 30-day trial.
  • Do not evaluate during the session. The "I'm not doing this right" thought is itself just another thought to notice and return from. There is no correct experience during meditation — only the practice of returning attention.
Meditation does not make the world quieter. It makes the observer of the world more steady. The storms continue — but the ground beneath them does not move in the same way. That steadiness is built slowly, in sessions of ten minutes, returned to daily. It is among the most consequential habits in this Codex precisely because it changes everything else without touching anything else.— Von Lycaon

Advanced Practices

After 8 weeks of consistent daily practice, the foundational techniques have produced measurable changes in attention and equanimity. The following practices extend the development further, addressing deeper layers of the contemplative tradition.

Loving-Kindness Meditation (Metta)

A practice for developing compassion and reducing the habitual hostility, judgement, or indifference toward others that accumulates through social friction. The technique: generate a feeling of warmth and goodwill toward yourself first (genuinely, not performatively -- this is often the most difficult step), then extend it progressively to a loved person, a neutral person, a difficult person, and finally to all beings. The practice works neurologically: the repeated generation of compassionate feeling toward people who habitually activate negative responses weakens those response patterns over time. Research by Richard Davidson at the University of Wisconsin has documented structural brain changes in long-term practitioners.

Noting Practice

A more precise form of mindfulness: rather than simply being aware of thoughts as thoughts, label them as they arise -- "planning," "remembering," "judging," "sensation," "hearing." The labelling activates the prefrontal cortex's categorisation function, which partially suppresses the amygdala's emotional reactivity to the content being labelled. The thought that is seen clearly enough to be categorised has less power than the thought that is identified with. This is the technical mechanism behind the clinical observation that mindfulness reduces rumination.

The Waking Up Course (Sam Harris)

The most intellectually rigorous meditation guidance available in the English language. Harris is a neuroscientist and philosopher as well as a long-term meditator, and the Waking Up app (iOS, Android) provides a structured path from beginner to advanced practices grounded in both the contemplative tradition and contemporary neuroscience. The theory component is as important as the practice component -- understanding what you are doing and why produces more consistent and deeper practice than following instructions without context. Available for a monthly subscription that is significantly less than a single therapy session.

Integration — Making It a Practice Not a Session

The formal meditation session (the 20 minutes each morning) is the laboratory. The rest of the day is the application. The formal practice builds the capacity for awareness; the daily life is where that capacity is deployed. Specific integration points:

  • The one breath pause: Before any significant interaction -- entering a meeting, answering a difficult phone call, responding to an irritating situation -- one full breath. The pause creates the gap between stimulus and response in which choice becomes possible. Without it, the habitual response pattern operates. With it, the chosen response has space to emerge.
  • Eating as practice: One meal per day eaten without a screen, phone, or other distraction -- just the meal. This is not a dietary practice. It is attentional practice using eating as the object. The meal eaten with full attention tastes different, is more satisfying, and produces better hunger and satiety awareness than the same meal eaten while distracted.
  • Transition moments: The moments between activities -- closing one task and beginning another, arriving home from work, moving between rooms -- are natural reset points. A 30-second conscious reset (three full breaths, noting the transition from one context to another) prevents the carry-over of one context's emotional residue into the next.
The meditation session is not the practice. The practice is the quality of awareness you bring to the conversation, the frustration, the moment of boredom, the unexpected difficulty. The session is the training. The day is the match.-- Von Lycaon
Precision of mind requires maintenance of mind. I did not arrive at composure by accident — I arrived at it by protecting the conditions that produce it: sufficient sleep, selective input, regular stillness, and the discipline to read more than I scroll. What you put into the mind matters. What you allow to remain in the mind matters more.— Von Lycaon

Sleep — The Non-Negotiable Foundation

Every other habit in this codex depends on sleep. Composure, precision, reliability, emotional regulation — all of these degrade in measurable, consistent ways with sleep deprivation. The research is not ambiguous: below 7 hours of sleep, cognitive performance declines at a rate most people cannot accurately self-assess. This is because sleep deprivation also impairs the ability to perceive one's own impairment. The underfed mind does not know it is underfed.

Duration

7–8 hours for most adults. This is not a suggestion and not a personality variable. Some people claim to function on 5 hours — the research consistently shows they are performing below their baseline and cannot accurately measure the deficit. 8 hours is the target.

Consistency

The same sleep and wake time — including weekends. Social jet lag (sleeping late on weekends) creates a weekly disruption equivalent to travelling two time zones. The body clock is not flexible; it is a biological mechanism that performs best on schedule.

Environment

Cool (16–19°C is optimal — windows cracked in Sarajevo autumn/spring), completely dark (blackout curtains or a sleep mask), and quiet. A room that is too warm is the most common preventable cause of poor sleep quality in every climate.

The Pre-Sleep Window

No screens for 45–60 minutes before bed. No alcohol within 3 hours — alcohol produces sedation but fragments sleep architecture and eliminates the restorative deep sleep stages. No heavy food within 2 hours. These constraints applied consistently produce measurable improvement in sleep quality within two weeks.

Reading — A Structured Practice

I read thirty minutes every day, without exception. Not at a device that also receives notifications. In a physical book or on an e-ink reader that does not also serve as a portal to everything else. The reading is its own activity, separate from every other. This boundary is what makes it genuinely restful rather than another form of consumption.

Where to find these in Sarajevo: Buybook (Radićeva 4, also at SCC) carries the best English-language selection in the city, including all titles below. Algoritam (BBI Centar) is the alternative. Most titles below are also available as e-books through Amazon for Kindle delivery.

On Conduct & The Mind

  • Meditations — Marcus Aurelius. A Roman emperor's private notes to himself on how to behave under pressure. More practical than any business book. Remarkably applicable to modern life with no modification required. Begin here if you have not already.
  • Letters from a Stoic — Seneca. Letters written to a friend on how to live. Shorter than Meditations and more conversational. Seneca had a sharper wit and is, if anything, more immediately quotable. Read alongside Meditations.
  • Thinking, Fast and Slow — Daniel Kahneman. A thorough examination of how we make decisions — the fast, intuitive system and the slow, deliberate one — and where each fails. Understanding your own cognitive biases is among the highest-leverage self-knowledge available.
  • Deep Work — Cal Newport. The argument that the ability to concentrate without distraction on cognitively demanding work is increasingly rare and increasingly valuable. Practical protocols for building this capacity. Directly applicable to both IT analysis and to most habits in this codex.
  • Atomic Habits — James Clear. The mechanics of habit formation made practically actionable. If you have ever understood what you should do but struggled to do it consistently, this is the manual for the gap between intention and execution.

Fiction Worth Your Time

  • The Master and Margarita — Mikhail Bulgakov. A Russian novel about the Devil visiting Soviet Moscow. One of the most formally inventive, philosophically rich, and satirically brilliant novels of the 20th century. Set partly in Jerusalem and partly in a Moscow turned surreal. Buybook carries it.
  • Norwegian Wood — Haruki Murakami. On loss, memory, and the difficulty of being present in one's own life. Murakami's prose, even in translation, reads with a clean precision that suits the sensibility of this codex.
  • Crime and Punishment — Fyodor Dostoevsky. A study of the psychology of a person who believes his own exceptionality exempts him from ordinary moral constraints — and the consequences of that belief. One of the most psychologically accurate novels ever written, by any standard.

Managing the Mind — Practical Techniques

On Frustration and Emotional Regulation

  • Name it before expressing it. "I am frustrated because [specific thing]" said internally reduces the intensity of the emotion measurably. This is called affective labelling and is one of the most consistently replicated findings in emotion regulation research. The act of naming an emotion activates the prefrontal cortex, which is specifically the region responsible for regulating the amygdala's reactivity.
  • The two-minute rule for responses. Any message or conversation exchange that produces an emotional reaction — wait two minutes before responding. The message sent after two minutes of reflection is always more useful and less regrettable than the one sent immediately. Always.
  • Physical interruption. A five-minute walk is a biological intervention in a thought loop, not an avoidance strategy. Movement changes cortisol levels. Cortisol levels change the quality of thought. The problem will look different on your return. This effect is measurable and consistent.
  • Distinguish between the solvable and the unsolvable. Before investing energy in a frustration, ask: is there an action I can take that would address this? If yes, take it. If no, apply the Stoic dichotomy of control and direct the energy elsewhere. The frustration of unchangeable circumstances compounds only when you continue trying to change them.

Journalling — A Practical Method

I do not mean a diary of events. I mean three targeted questions, answered briefly, once daily. This takes five minutes and produces compounding self-knowledge that no other practice I have found can replicate at that time cost.

  1. What did I handle well today? Forces identification of specific competent actions, not general self-assessment. Builds accurate self-confidence — confidence grounded in evidence rather than optimism.
  2. What would I do differently? Not "what did I do wrong" — a subtly different question that invites a constructive future answer rather than self-criticism. The frame matters. Self-criticism produces shame. Alternative planning produces improvement.
  3. What am I looking forward to tomorrow? Creates specific positive anticipation. People sleep better when there is something concrete to anticipate the following day — this is well-documented. The expectation need not be large. A good coffee, a task you find satisfying, a conversation you are anticipating. Specificity is what matters.

Solitude — The Scheduled Practice

I have observed that those most comfortable in their own company are also most comfortable in the company of others. They do not need the room — they choose it. That is an entirely different energy to bring to any gathering.— Von Lycaon, on solitude as strength

Once per week, I schedule one hour of genuine solitude. No task, no device, no expectation. A walk along the Miljacka river. A bench in a park. A coffee made slowly and drunk without purpose. The mind that is never quiet is a mind that cannot hear itself clearly — and it is this self-directed attention that produces both genuine rest and the clearest decisions.

Sarajevo is, in this regard, a generous city. The Vrelo Bosne park, the forest above Ilidža, or simply a quiet walk through Baščaršija in the early morning before the city wakes — these are available to you. Use them deliberately, not incidentally.

Physical Maintenance

  • Walking is underrated. 8,000–10,000 steps per day is associated with significant reductions in all-cause mortality. More practically: it is the lowest-barrier, highest-compliance exercise available. In a city as navigable as Sarajevo's centre, many errands that are driven can be walked. This is not a sacrifice. It is an investment in the same machine that does everything else in this codex.
  • Strength training — twice weekly minimum. Not for aesthetics primarily — for the functional benefits: improved sleep quality, reduced anxiety, better posture, and the direct maintenance of bone density and metabolic function that degrades without resistance exercise after the mid-20s. A gym membership at Fitness & Wellness centres in Sarajevo (multiple locations including near SCC) is approximately 30–50 BAM per month.
  • Caffeine — as a tool. One to two coffees in the morning, consumed no earlier than 90 minutes after waking (to avoid blunting the morning cortisol rise that provides natural alertness), and none after 2pm (caffeine's half-life is five to six hours; a 2pm coffee is still active at 8pm and disrupts sleep architecture). Sarajevo's kafana culture is excellent. The coffee itself is not the problem — only its timing.
  • Hydration metric: Urine colour. Pale yellow: adequately hydrated. Dark yellow: dehydrated. Clear: possibly over-hydrated. The target is consistently pale yellow throughout the day. Sarajevo tap water is clean and safe — there is no need to purchase bottled water.
A day that begins without structure will find its own — and that structure will rarely suit you. I have maintained a morning and evening ritual for many years. Not because I lack spontaneity, but because I have learned that genuine freedom is built on a reliable foundation. You cannot improvise well without a stable base from which to improvise.— Von Lycaon

The Morning Sequence

Upon waking · First 2 minutes

Do not reach for your phone

Allow yourself two full minutes of silence before any stimulus enters. Sit upright at the edge of the bed. Breathe slowly. The mind, in these first moments, is uniquely open to whatever it receives first. If the first input is someone else's emergency from a notification, you have begun the day in a reactive posture before you have had a single original thought. Guard this window.

Minutes 3–10

Hydrate and move

A full glass of water immediately. The body has gone eight hours without hydration and the brain is approximately 75% water — even mild dehydration impairs focus. Follow this with five to ten minutes of light movement: a brief stretch, some joint rotations, a short walk to the kitchen and back. The goal is not exercise — it is to signal to the body that today has begun and the machine is online.

Before leaving the bedroom

Make the bed. Without exception.

Admiral William McRaven, in his famous commencement address, named this the most important small discipline of the day. The reason is psychological: it is the first task completed. It creates a template of completion. It means that no matter how the day unfolds, you will return to something ordered. I make my bed with care — aligned edges, smoothed surface. Three minutes. Non-negotiable.

After bathing and dressing

Review your commitments for the day

Three minutes only. What have you promised today and to whom? What is due? What needs to be initiated versus continued? An attendant who forgets a prior commitment is an attendant who cannot be trusted. A simple written list reviewed once is worth five mental reminders throughout the day. The review itself is brief — the preparation for it (the list made the night before) is what does the real work.

Breakfast

Eat deliberately, not hastily

Even if time is limited, sit down. Eating while standing over a sink is a small daily surrender. The ritual of sitting to eat, even for ten minutes, creates a clean transition from the private self to the professional one. I treat breakfast as the first act of care I offer myself each day. What you eat matters; that you eat intentionally matters more.

Leaving the house

One final check

Before closing the door: bag, keys, phone, wallet, water. A mental scan takes four seconds and prevents the particular frustration of realising at the tram stop that something was left behind. Conduct this check in the same sequence every day. Routine eliminates the cognitive load of remembering to remember.

The Evening Close

End of work

A deliberate close, not a drift

Many people do not end their working day — they simply stop. They drift from the desk to the sofa, laptop still open, work mind still partially engaged. I recommend a closing ritual: review what was completed, note what is unfinished, assign the unfinished items a specific place in tomorrow's schedule, and then — physically — close the laptop. This act signals to the brain that work has concluded. Without it, the mind continues processing in the background all evening.

After work · The transition

Change your clothes

If you work from home or even on arrival home from the office: change out of your work attire. This is not about comfort — it is a psychological boundary. The clothes you wear for work carry the associations of work. Changing into different clothes signals a change of mode. I find this single habit does more for genuine rest than any specific relaxation technique.

Evening

Prepare tomorrow's clothes tonight

Lay out tomorrow's full attire the night before: shirt, trousers, shoes, belt, any accessory. This eliminates a decision from a rushed morning, prevents the frustration of discovering a shirt needs ironing at 7:45am, and — quietly — signals to yourself that tomorrow deserves preparation. The future version of you waking up to a laid-out outfit will feel cared for. You are the one doing the caring. This matters more than it sounds.

One hour before sleep

Dim the screens; prepare the mind

Blue light from screens suppresses melatonin production and delays sleep onset. More practically: the brain, if given stimulating content immediately before sleep, will continue processing it during the first sleep cycles, reducing quality. I recommend reading — physical or e-ink — in the final hour. If a thought occurs that you wish not to lose, write it in a notebook rather than opening a phone. The notebook captures it; the phone captures you.

Before sleep

The three-question audit

I ask myself three questions each night: What did I do well today? What would I do differently? What am I looking forward to tomorrow? The first two build self-awareness without self-punishment. The third creates a mild positive anticipation that aids sleep. This takes three minutes and compounds into genuine self-knowledge over months. I have done this for years and would not stop.

The Workspace Standard

  • Nothing on the desk that does not belong to today's work. Everything else is filed, shelved, or removed. A surface cluttered with yesterday's work cannot hold today's clearly.
  • Cable management, addressed once. Velcro cable ties cost almost nothing at dm or any electronics store. Spend twenty minutes once. You will never think about cables again. A tangle of cables is a visual representation of a tangle of thoughts.
  • Adequate light — but not harsh light. Overhead fluorescent light without a secondary warm source creates fatigue over a full workday. A desk lamp with a warm bulb positioned to the left of the monitor (for right-handed use) reduces eye strain significantly.
  • Monitor at eye level. A monitor below eye level means the neck is flexed forward for hours. A monitor stand or a few textbooks beneath it brings it to eye height. This is not aesthetics — it is the preservation of your spine.
  • Clean it before you close it. Thirty seconds at the end of each day to reset the surface. Remove cups, file papers, straighten the keyboard. The person who arrives tomorrow morning will thank you. That person is you.
  • A plant, if you are willing. Studies consistently show that a single living plant in the workspace reduces stress markers and improves air quality marginally. More importantly: caring for something small daily is a form of attention that generalises outward. A neglected plant is a quiet signal about one's attention to living things.

Weekly Maintenance — The Sunday Reset

Sunday evening, or whatever evening precedes your working week, I dedicate forty-five minutes to resetting the environment entirely. This is not cleaning — it is a ritual that ensures Monday begins from a position of readiness rather than recovery.

  1. Laundry complete and put away. Not in the basket, not on the chair — in its place. Clothes left on chairs accumulate until they become furniture.
  2. Desk cleared and wiped. All surfaces cleaned, keyboard and mouse included. A clean keyboard is not a luxury — it is hygiene.
  3. Bag packed for Monday. Everything needed for the week confirmed. Nothing worse than discovering at 8am that the charger is missing.
  4. Fridge briefly audited. What needs to be used this week? What has expired? A five-minute fridge review prevents waste and informs grocery shopping.
  5. Week reviewed in writing. Ten minutes. What went well? What did not? One thing I will do differently. The habit of written reflection is the fastest accelerator of personal development I have encountered.
Training without a programme is exercise. Training with a programme is progress. The distinction is documentation — knowing what was done, what it produced, and what comes next. The following is a five-day bodyweight programme designed around your situation: no gym required, scalable to the time and energy available, and structured to produce the functional strength the goal demands.— Von Lycaon

The Five-Day Programme

Day 1 Push — Chest, Shoulders, Triceps Pressing strength · Upper body mass
Arm circles
30 sec each direction
Shoulder rolls
10 forward, 10 back
Wrist circles
10 each direction
Chest opener
10 slow reps
Push-ups (standard)4 × 12–15Chest to floor. Full lockout at top. Elbows 45° from body.
Pike Push-ups3 × 10–12Hips high. Head tracks toward floor between hands. Shoulder press pattern.
Diamond Push-ups3 × 8–10Hands form diamond under chest. Maximum tricep recruitment.
Wide Push-ups3 × 12Hands wider than shoulder width. Emphasises chest outer fibres.
Rest 60–90 seconds between sets · Total: ~25 minutes
Day 2 Pull — Back, Biceps, Rear Delts Pulling strength · Postural correction
Table / Chair Rows4 × 10–12Lie under a sturdy table. Chest to table edge. Full retraction at top.
Doorframe Rows3 × 12Grip the doorframe at waist height. Lean back 45°. Row chest to frame.
Superman Hold3 × 10 reps (3 sec hold)Lie prone. Extend arms and legs. Lift simultaneously. Direct lumbar and upper back activation.
Reverse Snow Angels3 × 12Prone. Arms move from hips to overhead. Lower trap and rhomboid focus.
Rest 60–90 seconds · Pull days directly counteract desk posture. Do not skip.
Day 3 Legs — Quads, Hamstrings, Glutes, Calves Lower body power · Functional movement
Bodyweight Squats4 × 20Feet shoulder width. Knees track over toes. Break parallel. Controlled descent 3 counts.
Reverse Lunges3 × 12 each legStep back. Front knee at 90°. Rear knee hovers 2cm from floor. Drive through front heel to return.
Glute Bridges4 × 15 (3 sec hold at top)Shoulders on floor. Drive hips to ceiling. Squeeze glutes hard at top. The hip extension counteracts the hip flexor shortening from desk work.
Calf Raises3 × 20On a step edge for full range. 2 counts up, 3 counts down. Ankle stability and calf development.
Wall Sit3 × 45–60 sec90° at knees and hips. Isometric quad endurance. Builds the sustained strength the carrying goal requires.
Rest 60 seconds · Leg days are the most skipped and the most important. The largest muscle groups drive the most metabolic change.
Day 4 Core & Stability Spinal integrity · Functional centre
Plank3 × 45–60 secForearms down. Body perfectly rigid. No raised hips, no sagging hips. Breathe normally.
Dead Bug3 × 10 each sideSupine. Opposite arm and leg extend simultaneously. Lower back pressed to floor throughout.
Side Plank3 × 30 sec each sideFull lateral line from feet to head. Oblique and lateral hip stability.
Mountain Climbers3 × 20 each legPlank position. Drive knees to chest alternately. Maintain hips level — do not bob.
Hollow Body Hold3 × 20–30 secSupine. Lower back pressed flat. Arms overhead, legs raised. The gymnast's foundational position.
Rest 45 seconds · Core is the transmission system. All other strength is expressed through it.
Day 5 Full Body — Compound & Conditioning Integration · Endurance base
Burpees4 × 8–10The full pattern: squat thrust, push-up, jump. Every major muscle group in one movement. Cardiovascular demand included.
Jump Squats3 × 12Squat down, explode up, land soft with bent knees. Power development and rate of force development.
Push-up to Downward Dog3 × 10Push-up, then press hips to the ceiling into a V. Combines push strength with posterior chain stretch.
Bear Crawl3 × 20 metres or 30 secOn hands and knees, hovering 3cm off the floor. Move forward and backward. Shoulder stability and coordinated movement.
Rest 60 seconds · Day 5 is the most important session for cardiovascular adaptation and full-body integration.

Muscle Group Reference

Chest
Primary push muscle
Push-ups, Wide push-ups, Burpees
Shoulders
Overhead pressing, stability
Pike push-ups, Shoulder taps
Triceps
Elbow extension
Diamond push-ups, Tricep dips
Back
Pulling, posture
Table rows, Superman, Reverse angels
Core
Transmission, stability
Plank, Dead bug, Hollow hold
Legs
Power, movement base
Squats, Lunges, Glute bridges

Progression Phases

Weeks 1–4 — FoundationBuild the movement patterns
Focus on form over quantity. Every rep controlled. The nervous system is learning. Do not chase numbers — chase precision. Use the easier exercise variants. Rest fully between sets.
Weeks 5–8 — VolumeAdd sets and reps
Increase sets by one per exercise. Reduce rest by 15 seconds. The cardiovascular system adapts. The muscle fibres begin hypertrophic signalling consistently.
Weeks 9–12 — IntensityHarder variations
Introduce advanced variations: archer push-ups, pistol squat progressions, explosive versions. Complexity demands more neuromuscular recruitment and produces rapid strength gains.
Weeks 13+ — DeloadPlanned recovery
One week at 50% volume every 12–16 weeks. Not optional rest — planned recovery. Accumulated fatigue clears and adaptations consolidate. You will feel stronger after a deload, not weaker.

Nutrition — The Non-Negotiables

  • Protein target: 150–160g daily. Muscle is built from protein. Without sufficient intake, training produces fatigue without adaptation. Eggs, chicken, dairy, legumes, and tuna are the most economical sources in Sarajevo.
  • Eat before you train. A small meal 60–90 minutes before — carbohydrate and protein. Two eggs and a slice of bread is sufficient. Fasted strength training produces inferior performance.
  • Eat after you train. Within 2 hours. The post-training window is when muscle repair is most active. The tuna rice bowl or scrambled eggs from the Daily Eating tab are optimal choices.
  • The pre-sleep snack. Cottage cheese 200g, 30–60 minutes before bed. Casein protein releases slowly overnight when growth hormone is at peak. This single habit adds meaningful muscle recovery to every night's sleep.
  • Sleep is nutritional. Growth hormone is released during deep sleep. A programme paired with 5 hours of sleep produces a fraction of the results of the same programme with 8 hours.
The goal you described — carrying your wife, moving well and quickly, being reliably capable — is not a vanity goal. It is a care goal. It is the physical expression of wanting to be capable for the people who depend on you. There is no more worthwhile reason to train. Begin this week, not next week.— Von Lycaon, on training with purpose

Recovery — The Training You Do Not Skip

  • Active recovery days (Days 6–7): Rest does not mean sedentary. A 20-minute walk, the morning yoga sequence from the Yoga tab, or a light swim on recovery days accelerates clearance of metabolic waste from muscle tissue more effectively than complete inactivity. The distinction between active recovery and training: active recovery should never produce fatigue. If it does, it has crossed into training and should be reduced.
  • The 48-hour rule: Any muscle group worked to genuine fatigue requires 48 hours of recovery before the next training stimulus. Training the same muscles before full recovery produces an accumulation of fatigue rather than adaptation. The 5-day programme is designed around this — the push/pull/legs split ensures no muscle group is trained on consecutive days.
  • Signs of overtraining: Persistent fatigue that sleep does not resolve. Elevated resting heart rate (5+ BPM above normal baseline). Declining performance despite consistent training. Mood deterioration. These are not signs to train through — they are signals that the recovery deficit has exceeded the body's capacity to adapt. The correct response is a deload week (50% volume) followed by a return to normal programming.
  • Sleep as the primary recovery tool: 8 hours of quality sleep produces more recovery than any supplement, cold exposure, or sauna session. The Sleep Optimisation tab covers the mechanisms. In the context of training: growth hormone is released during the first 2 hours of sleep, protein synthesis peaks in the sleep state, and the neuromuscular adaptations that produce strength gains consolidate during sleep. Training hard and sleeping poorly produces injury and stagnation.

Mindset — Why Most People Quit

  • The comparison trap: Comparing your Week 3 to someone else's Year 3 is the primary cause of discouragement and early quitting. The only relevant comparison is your Week 3 to your Week 1. Are you stronger? More consistent? Moving with more control? That is progress. The rate of visible change is determined by genetics, starting point, and consistency — none of these are competitors with other people's rate.
  • Identity before outcome: James Clear's framework from Atomic Habits: the person who trains consistently does so because they identify as someone who trains — not because they are currently motivated by a specific outcome. Build the identity first. "I am someone who trains five days per week" is the foundation. The outcome (strength, body composition, energy) is the natural consequence, not the motivation.
  • The 40% rule: David Goggins' observation: when the mind signals that it is at its limit, it is actually at approximately 40% of actual capacity. The brain's protective mechanisms trigger long before genuine physiological failure.
