UVB (short-wave) burns. Both contribute to skin cancer risk. SPF 30+ daily application to the face prevents the cumulative UV damage that produces brown spots, lines, and reduced skin elasticity. Sarajevo's mountain-adjacent location and summer altitude means UV index is higher than comparable-latitude lowland cities. The SPF in the Grooming tab morning routine is not a cosmetic choice — it is sun protection.

Common Conditions — Recognition and Response

Acne — Beyond Teenage Years

  • Adult acne (post-25) is more common than generally recognised and is frequently triggered by: hormonal fluctuation (testosterone spikes from training, stress), dietary factors (high glycaemic index foods, dairy in susceptible individuals), and comedogenic skincare or hair products. Before attributing adult acne to hygiene — assess these variables.
  • Mild to moderate acne: Salicylic acid cleanser (BHA — dissolves the keratin plugs that form blackheads and whiteheads), benzoyl peroxide 2.5–5% (kills acne-causing bacteria), niacinamide (reduces inflammation and sebum production). Available at dm and apoteke. Start low concentration, increase if tolerated.
  • Persistent or severe acne: Dermatologist referral. Prescription tretinoin (retinoid) or oral antibiotics are the evidence-supported treatments for moderate-severe acne. Do not attempt to solve significant acne with over-the-counter products alone — the delay in seeking treatment allows scarring that is far harder to address than the acne itself.

Skin Cancer Warning Signs — The ABCDE Rule

  • A — Asymmetry: One half of the mole doesn't match the other. B — Border: Ragged, notched, or blurred edges. C — Colour: Multiple colours (black, brown, tan, red, white, blue) within a single lesion. D — Diameter: Larger than 6mm (about the size of a pencil eraser). E — Evolving: Any change in size, shape, colour, or any new symptom (bleeding, itching).
  • Any lesion meeting one or more of these criteria warrants dermatologist examination. Melanoma is highly treatable when detected early and potentially fatal when detected late. Skin self-examination monthly takes 3 minutes.
  • Annual dermatologist mole check if you have significant sun exposure history, fair skin, or a family history of skin cancer. This is standard preventive care in Western Europe; it is accessible at Sarajevo's private dermatology clinics.

Other Common Conditions

  • Seborrhoeic dermatitis: Flaky, itchy skin on the scalp, eyebrows, or sides of the nose. Often misidentified as dry skin. Caused by an overgrowth of Malassezia yeast. Treatment: ketoconazole shampoo (Nizoral, available at apoteke) left on the skin for 2–3 minutes before rinsing. Most cases respond within 2–4 weeks.
  • Contact dermatitis: Red, itchy, blistered skin appearing in a defined area. Almost always caused by a specific irritant — a new detergent, a metal in a watch clasp, a shaving product. Identify and eliminate the irritant. Hydrocortisone 1% cream provides symptom relief while the cause is addressed.
  • Tinea (fungal infections): Tinea pedis (athlete's foot) and tinea cruris (jock itch) are common in men who exercise. Treatment: antifungal cream (Lamisil or clotrimazole — dm or apoteka). Prevention: dry thoroughly after showering, particularly between toes. Moisture is the fungi's environment — remove it.
The skin is the largest organ and the most externally visible one. Its condition communicates health, discipline, and age simultaneously — to every person who sees it. The 3 minutes of daily care in the Grooming tab is the maintenance. Understanding what the skin is doing and why is the intelligence behind the maintenance.— Von Lycaon
Swimming technique is the discipline where effort and efficiency are most visibly opposed. The swimmer who thrashes hard and moves slowly is working against the water. The swimmer who glides and moves fast is working with it. The difference is almost entirely technical — not fitness, not strength, but the mechanics of how the body moves through water. This tab converts the casual weekend pool session into deliberate skill development.— Von Lycaon

The Fundamentals — What Most People Get Wrong

Body Position

The single most important variable. The body should be horizontal and as close to the surface as possible — any downward angle of the hips or legs creates drag that no amount of arm pulling can overcome. The problem: beginners let the legs sink because they are kicking inefficiently. The fix: press the chest slightly downward into the water — this naturally elevates the hips. Not an intuitive motion, but it is the foundation of efficient swimming.

Head Position

The head should be neutral — looking straight down at the pool floor, not forward. Every centimetre the chin rises causes the hips to drop proportionally. Breathing in freestyle is done by rotating the body (not lifting the head) until one goggle clears the water. The mouth is at water level, not above it. This feels wrong initially. It is correct.

Kick

The kick in freestyle is not the primary propulsion source — the arms provide 70-80% of propulsion. The kick's primary function is balance and body position maintenance. A narrow, fast, low-amplitude kick (from the hip, not the knee) is more efficient than a wide cycling motion. Kick from the hip. Keep feet relaxed. Toes pointed.

The Catch

The most technically demanding part of the freestyle stroke. As the arm enters the water and extends forward, the forearm should rotate downward (early vertical forearm — EVF) so that the entire forearm and hand become a paddle pulling backward. Most beginners let the hand and forearm angle backward immediately, reducing the effective paddle surface by 60%. Developing the early vertical forearm requires deliberate drill practice but is the highest-return technical improvement available.

The Four Strokes — When to Use Each

  • Freestyle (front crawl): The most efficient stroke for sustained swimming. The primary stroke to develop. For the weekend pool session described in the Codex — this is 70% of your swimming.
  • Backstroke: Already highlighted in the Mobility tab as the most valuable stroke for desk workers — opens the anterior shoulders and chest in the opposite direction to desk posture. Should be 20% of every pool session specifically for this postural benefit. The technique: arms pull in a windmill pattern, body rotates with each stroke identically to freestyle.
  • Breaststroke: The most natural stroke for beginners but the least efficient. The knee valgus stress noted in the Mobility tab applies here — if knee discomfort occurs during breaststroke, switch immediately. Useful for recovery between harder sets.
  • Butterfly: The most demanding stroke technically and physically. Not necessary for recreational fitness swimming. If mastered, it produces extraordinary upper body and core strength gains — but the learning curve is steep enough that the time investment is only justified if swimming is a primary sport.

The Session Structure

Standard Weekend Session — 45 Minutes
Olympic pool · All fitness levels · Progressive structure
  • Warm-up (10 min): 4 lengths easy freestyle focusing only on body position. 2 lengths backstroke. 2 lengths with pull buoy (float held between thighs, arms only) — this isolates the arm stroke and forces attention to the catch.
  • Drill set (10 min): 4 lengths catch-up drill (one arm stays extended forward until the recovering arm touches it before the pull begins — this exaggerates the EVF position). 4 lengths fingertip drag drill (fingers drag along the water surface on recovery — ensures high elbow recovery).
  • Main set (20 min): 8 x 1 length with 20 seconds rest between each. Focus on one technical element per 2 lengths — body position, then head position, then catch, then breathing rotation. Not speed — quality.
  • Cool-down (5 min): 4 lengths easy backstroke. The backstroke cool-down is both cardiovascular cool-down and the postural correction benefit described in the Mobility tab.

Sarajevo Olympic Pool

  • Olimpijski bazen Otoka — the 50-metre Olympic-standard pool near Otoka. Open year-round. Lane swimming available during specified hours (check the schedule — public sessions and club training alternate). Locker facilities, sauna adjacent. The most accessible serious swimming facility in the city.
  • Ettiquette: Lane selection by speed — slow, medium, fast lanes exist in most pools. Choose honestly. Overtaking on the right during circle swimming (counterclockwise). Tap the feet of the swimmer ahead of you to signal overtaking intent. These are universal lane swimming conventions.
The swimmer who understands the water moves through it. The swimmer who does not fights it. This distinction applies equally to every domain where the environment has its own logic that efficiency requires learning rather than overcoming.— Von Lycaon
Running is the most accessible endurance discipline available — zero equipment beyond shoes, available everywhere in Sarajevo year-round, and producing cardiovascular adaptations that no other activity matches for time efficiency. The barrier is not fitness but the first four weeks, which are genuinely uncomfortable before the body adapts. This tab covers both the technique and the structured approach to building from zero to a sustainable habit.— Von Lycaon

Why Run — The Specific Benefits

Cardiovascular Foundation

Zone 2 cardio — sustained effort at 60-70% of maximum heart rate — is the most evidence-supported training mode for metabolic health, longevity, and the aerobic base that makes all other physical activity more sustainable. Peter Attia's longevity framework (Reading List tab) centres on Zone 2 training. Running is the most efficient delivery mechanism for this training stimulus available without equipment.

Mental Health

Running produces the most consistent mood regulation effect of any activity — more reliable than cycling, swimming, or weight training. The mechanism involves endorphins, endocannabinoids (the runner's high is an endocannabinoid response, not endorphin as popularly believed), and BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor), which promotes neuroplasticity. Regular running literally grows the hippocampus, the brain structure associated with memory and stress regulation.

The Sarajevo Context

Running in Sarajevo's valley — flat along the Miljacka, immediately hilly in any other direction — provides natural interval training without programming it. The flat riverside route is the Zone 2 session. The hill routes above Bistrik, Kovači, or toward Trebević are automatic threshold training. The city's geography is a natural training programme for anyone willing to use it.

Form — The Basics That Prevent Injury

  • Upright posture, slight forward lean from the ankles. Not from the waist — a waist-forward lean collapses the hip flexors and produces lower back pain. Think of falling forward and catching yourself with each step.
  • Foot strike under the body, not in front of it. Overstriding — landing with the foot significantly ahead of the hips — acts as a braking force on every stride and dramatically increases knee and hip injury risk. Shorten the stride and increase the cadence (steps per minute) instead.
  • Cadence: 170-180 steps per minute. Most recreational runners run at 160 or lower. Increasing cadence reduces impact forces significantly. Count your steps for 30 seconds and multiply by 4. If below 170, consciously quicken the turnover. A metronome app at 87 BPM (= 174 steps/min) trains this.
  • Arms at 90 degrees, driving backward not forward. The forward arm swing is a pendulum — the backward drive is the power. Arms should not cross the midline of the body. Relax the hands (imagine holding a crisp without breaking it).
  • Breathe through both nose and mouth. At easy paces, nose breathing is possible and reduces air volume while maintaining oxygen saturation. At threshold intensity, mouth breathing is inevitable and correct.

The Eight-Week Start Programme

Week 1–2 — Run/Walk Intervals

  • 3 sessions per week. Each session: 20 minutes total of alternating run 1 minute, walk 2 minutes. The running portions should feel easy — conversational pace. If you cannot speak a full sentence while running, slow down. Most beginners start too fast and quit too early.
  • This feels insufficiently demanding. That is correct. The cardiovascular and muscular systems require adaptation time that willpower cannot accelerate. Injury in the first month is the primary cause of failed running habits — it is prevented by this conservatism.

Week 3–4 — Extended Runs

  • Increase to run 3 min / walk 1 min. Total session time 25 minutes. The running blocks will begin to feel sustainable rather than effortful. This is the adaptation the first two weeks were building.

Week 5–6 — Continuous Running

  • 20 minutes of continuous running at genuinely easy pace. The benchmark: you should be able to run the entire 20 minutes and feel like you could have continued. If you cannot, reduce the pace further. Speed is irrelevant at this stage.

Week 7–8 — Build to 30 Minutes

  • Add 5 minutes every session toward a 30-minute continuous run by the end of week 8. This is the foundation from which any further running goal — a 5K race, a longer distance, a trail run up Trebević — is built.

Sarajevo Running Routes

  • Miljacka riverside path: Flat, along both banks of the river from Baščaršija to Ilidža. The primary Zone 2 training route. Early morning before traffic for the best air quality and the cleanest experience of the city.
  • Trebević trail (via Bistrik): 45-minute uphill climb from the city centre to the Trebević plateau. A natural threshold workout. The view from the summit — the city of Sarajevo in its valley surrounded by hills — earns every step.
  • Vrelo Bosne perimeter: The natural park at the source of the Bosna river near Ilidža. Flat, green, and quiet. The tram to Ilidža makes this accessible without a car for a longer zone 2 session in natural surroundings.
The first kilometre is the hardest. Not because the body is not capable — but because the mind has not yet agreed to the project. The agreement comes in the third week, when the run becomes something to look forward to rather than something to get through. Get to the third week.— Von Lycaon

The Heart Rate Approach

  • Zone 2 definition: 60–70% of maximum heart rate (HRmax). A rough formula for HRmax: 220 minus your age. At 30 years old, HRmax is approximately 190; Zone 2 is 114–133 BPM. The more accurate method: during Zone 2 running, you should be able to hold a full conversation in complete sentences without significant breathlessness. The nasal breathing test: if you can breathe exclusively through the nose, you are in Zone 2. If you must open the mouth, you have exceeded it.
  • Why Zone 2 matters: Zone 2 training develops mitochondrial density in slow-twitch muscle fibres — the foundation of aerobic endurance that determines how long you can sustain any physical activity before fatigue. It also trains fat oxidation as a fuel source rather than relying exclusively on glycogen, which is finite. Peter Attia's longevity research (Reading List tab) identifies Zone 2 capacity at age 65 as one of the strongest predictors of healthspan. The investment made in Zone 2 training in your 30s compounds over decades in a way that most people underestimate.
  • One harder run per week: Once Zone 2 base is established (8+ weeks of consistent training), adding one weekly threshold or interval session significantly accelerates cardiovascular improvement. A simple protocol: after a 10-minute Zone 2 warm-up, run 4 x 4 minutes at an effort where speaking more than a few words at a time is impossible, with 3 minutes of easy jogging between each. This produces the VO2max stimulus that Zone 2 alone does not.
Martial arts training develops something the gym does not: the ability to perform under pressure, to control a resisting opponent, and to remain calm when the body's threat response is fully activated. The physical benefits — coordination, spatial awareness, explosive power, and genuine functional strength — are significant. The psychological benefits are more significant. This tab covers the rational selection and entry into martial arts training in Sarajevo.— Von Lycaon

Which Discipline — The Honest Assessment

Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu (BJJ)

The most recommended starting point for an adult beginner with no martial arts background. Reason: it is almost entirely ground-based grappling, which means size and strength matter less than technique — a 70kg person with good technique regularly submits a 90kg person without it. Ego is difficult to maintain on the ground. The "rolling" (live sparring) is done from day one, which means you are genuinely learning to apply technique against resistance rather than rehearsing patterns. Available at several gyms in Sarajevo.

Wrestling / Sambo

The most athletically demanding option. Explosive takedowns, clinch fighting, and ground control. Bosnia has a strong wrestling tradition — regional clubs exist and the level of coaching is high relative to the cost. More physically brutal than BJJ in the short term. The athletic base it builds transfers to every other physical activity more than any other martial art.

Boxing

The purest striking art. Footwork, head movement, jab, cross, hook, uppercut — mastered over years. The training (bag work, pad work, sparring) produces exceptional cardiovascular fitness, coordination, and the specific quality of calm that comes from learning to remain composed when someone is attempting to hit you. Available at multiple clubs in Sarajevo. The fitness benefit is achievable without full sparring for those who want the conditioning without the contact.

Muay Thai

Eight-limb striking: fists, elbows, knees, and kicks. More complex than boxing, equally demanding cardiovascularly. The clinch (close-range striking and control) is particularly practical. The training culture emphasises conditioning as highly as technique. Available at several gyms in Sarajevo, often combined with MMA training.

The First Month — What to Expect

  • You will be worse than everyone else. This is unavoidable and temporary. Every person in the gym has been where you are. The culture in serious martial arts gyms is almost universally welcoming to genuine beginners — the gym's interest is in producing good training partners, which requires patient instruction of newcomers.
  • You will be physically exhausted after every session for the first month. Martial arts training uses muscles in coordination patterns that no gym programme develops. The exhaustion is real and passes.
  • Ego is the primary barrier. The person who accepts being controlled and submitted by smaller, less athletic people, learns from the experience, and returns the next day progresses rapidly. The person who cannot accept this does not return. The mat reveals character precisely because it offers no flattery.
  • Start with two sessions per week. The physical adaptation requires recovery time. Three sessions per week after the first month. Competitive training levels (4-5 per week) are for those who have decided this is a primary sport.

The Transferable Benefits

  • Spatial awareness and coordination improve measurably within weeks and transfer to every physical activity — the clumsiness that characterises most sedentary adults begins to disappear.
  • Stress response calibration: Regular exposure to controlled physical threat (sparring) recalibrates the nervous system's threat response. Over months, situations that previously activated high anxiety — confrontation, public speaking, high-stakes professional situations — produce a more measured response. The physiological mechanism is the same; the practised response to it is different.
  • Confidence without aggression: The paradox of martial arts training is that it almost universally produces calmer, more de-escalatory behaviour outside the gym. The person who knows they can handle physical confrontation rarely needs to.
The mat is the most honest mirror available. It reveals what you are made of — not through test questions or performance reviews but through direct physical reality. A man who has been submitted a thousand times and returned a thousand and one times knows something about himself that no other activity can teach.— Von Lycaon

Training Methodology

The person who trains martial arts correctly improves continuously. The person who trains without methodology produces injuries and plateaus. The difference is understanding what you are actually training and how adaptation works in combat sports.

Technical vs Sparring Sessions

Technical sessions (drilling, pad work, positional work) develop the movement patterns and techniques that form the vocabulary of the art. Sparring sessions test and refine the application of those patterns under pressure. Both are essential and irreplaceable. The common error: too much sparring too early (before patterns are sufficiently drilled, the practitioner reverts to untrained responses under pressure and trains bad habits). The correct ratio for a beginner: 70% technical, 30% sparring. For an intermediate: 50/50. For competition preparation: 40% technical, 60% sparring.

Deliberate Practice in Martial Arts

Ericsson's deliberate practice applied: identify the specific technique or position where you are weakest. Design drilling specifically to address that weakness. Repeat the specific scenario from live sparring that you consistently fail to handle correctly. This targeted approach produces faster improvement than general training, where you naturally gravitate toward positions and techniques you are already comfortable with, reinforcing strength while leaving weaknesses unaddressed.

Ego Management

The biggest barrier to improvement in martial arts is the avoidance of feeling incompetent. Beginners tap out of positions they should stay in longer to learn the escape. Intermediate practitioners refuse to drill with beginners (losing the speed advantage) or to train with advanced practitioners (losing the competence advantage). The practitioner who trains specifically to be uncomfortable -- who seeks out the positions they fail at, who trains with people who dominate them -- improves at rates that comfortable training cannot produce.

Recovery from Martial Arts Training

  • The joint priority: Combat sports place unique stress on shoulders (BJJ grips and wrestling underhooks), neck (wrestling and takedown defence), fingers (all grappling arts), and knees (any art involving footwork or ground movement). The Mobility tab's daily protocol is more important for martial arts practitioners than for gym trainees because the stress patterns are more varied and the joint compression forces are higher.
  • Skin care for grapplers: Ringworm (Tinea corporis) is a fungal infection common in grappling environments -- not dangerous but highly contagious and unpleasant. Prevention: shower immediately after training (never more than 15 minutes after leaving the mat), wash the training uniform after every session, inspect the skin for circular red patches weekly. Treatment: over-the-counter antifungal cream (clotrimazole, available at any apoteka).
  • When to skip training: Any open cut, skin infection, or illness that could be transmitted to training partners. Fever without exception -- training with a fever produces delayed recovery, not accelerated improvement. A sprained ankle or similar injury: train upper body, skip sparring. The mat will be there when you return.

The Sarajevo Scene

Sarajevo has an active martial arts community with established clubs across disciplines. Wrestling has historical depth -- Bosnia's Olympic tradition includes wrestling medals. Boxing clubs (the most accessible entry point for striking arts) operate in most city districts. BJJ has grown significantly since 2015 with several dedicated academies now operating in the city, many affiliated with international organisations. MMA gyms offering combined striking and grappling training are accessible to the general public alongside competitive athletes.

The discipline required to return to the mat after losing, to be dominated by someone smaller, to expose the gaps in your game by training against your weaknesses rather than your strengths -- this discipline is the same discipline required in every other domain where excellence is possible. The mat teaches it faster than any other available environment because the feedback is immediate, physical, and completely honest.-- Von Lycaon
Cooking is, at its foundation, another form of precision and care. I do not cook elaborate meals daily — I cook correct ones. Simple ingredients, treated with attention, produce results categorically superior to complex ingredients treated carelessly. Learn these recipes and you will never produce a meal worth apologising for. The kitchen rewards those who read first, rush never, and taste constantly.— Von Lycaon
Sarajevo sourcing: All ingredients are available at Konzum, Bingo, or Interspar. Fresh herbs, quality olive oil, and parmesan are stocked at all three. For exceptional produce visit Markale market Tuesday or Thursday mornings before 10am.

The Kitchen Principles

  • Read the recipe completely before beginning. Know the full arc before the first flame.
  • Mise en place. Every ingredient measured, chopped, and within reach before heat is applied. Prevents burning while you are still cutting.
  • Clean as you proceed. The cook who ends with a catastrophic kitchen added that catastrophe incrementally.
  • Taste at every stage. Seasoning is adjusted continuously, not done once.
  • Salt correctly. Most home cooking is under-salted. Salt makes food taste fully like itself.
  • Rest cooked proteins. Five minutes for chicken thigh. Ten for steak. The moisture redistributes — the difference is not subtle.

Core Techniques

The Sear

High heat, dry protein, hot oil. Do not touch for 2–3 minutes. It releases when the crust has formed. Impatience produces tearing.

The Emulsion

Fat and water combined into a stable sauce via agitation — tossing, whisking. Pasta water with oil, butter sauces, vinaigrette: same principle.

Caramelisation

Patience at moderate heat. Onions for forty minutes are sweet and complex. Five minutes at high heat produces bitter char. Choose the time.

Acid as Seasoning

Lemon at the end is not about sourness — it is brightness. Before adding more salt, try acid first. More often than not, it is the answer.

Building a Pan Sauce

After searing: add aromatics, deglaze with wine or stock, scrape the fond, reduce by half, finish with cold butter. The fond is concentrated flavour.

Knife Skills

Curl fingertips under, knuckles guiding the blade. Consistent cuts mean even cooking. One quality knife — Victorinox Fibrox — kept sharp is sufficient.

Meat & Poultry

Pan-Seared Chicken Thighs with Garlic & Herbs
30 min · 2 servings
  • Ingredients: 4 bone-in chicken thighs, 4 garlic cloves (crushed), fresh thyme or rosemary, olive oil, salt, black pepper, 50ml white wine or stock, 20g cold butter
  • Season: Pat thighs completely dry. Season generously with salt and pepper on both sides — this is the most important step.
  • Sear: Heat oven-safe pan over medium-high. Add oil. Thighs skin-side down. Do not touch for 7–8 minutes until skin is deep golden and releases freely.
  • Finish: Flip, add garlic and herbs. Transfer to 200°C oven for 15–18 minutes until internal temperature reaches 74°C.
  • Pan sauce: Remove chicken, rest 5 minutes. Return pan to heat, add wine, deglaze, reduce by half. Remove from heat, swirl in cold butter until glossy.
Garlic Butter Steak
15 min · 1–2 servings · Cast iron preferred
  • Ingredients: 2 ribeye or sirloin steaks (room temperature), 3 garlic cloves (crushed), 3 sprigs thyme, 40g butter, salt, pepper, neutral oil
  • Season: Pat completely dry. Salt all sides. Room temperature is not optional — cold meat cools the pan and prevents searing.
  • Sear: Cast iron at highest heat until smoking. Add oil. Steaks down — do not touch for 2–3 minutes. Flip once only.
  • Baste: After flipping, add butter, garlic, and thyme. Tilt pan, continuously spoon foaming butter over steak for 2 minutes. The garlic and herb flavour is carried by the butter into the meat.
  • Rest and doneness: 5 minutes on wire rack. Medium-rare = 54°C. Medium = 60°C. Above 65°C, ribeye fat stops rendering pleasurably.
Classic Beef Bolognese
2 hours · 4 servings
  • Ingredients: 500g minced beef (not lean), 1 onion, 2 carrots, 2 celery stalks, 4 garlic cloves, 2 tbsp tomato paste, 200ml red wine, 400g tinned tomatoes, 200ml whole milk, thyme, bay leaf, olive oil, salt, pepper
  • Soffritto: Dice onion, carrot, celery finely. Cook in olive oil over low heat 15–20 minutes until soft and sweet, not browned. This is the foundation — do not rush it.
  • Brown the meat: Increase heat. Add beef in batches. Let it sit undisturbed until a crust forms before breaking it up. Properly browned meat produces depth that stirred-immediately meat never achieves.
  • Build: Add tomato paste, cook 2 minutes. Add wine, reduce completely. Add tomatoes, herbs, bay leaf. Simmer covered on lowest heat 1–2 hours.
  • Finish: Stir in milk five minutes before serving. It sounds unusual — it rounds the acidity and enriches the sauce. Serve with pappardelle or tagliatelle. Never spaghetti.
Beef Stir-Fry with Vegetables
20 min · 2 servings
  • Ingredients: 300g beef sirloin (sliced thin against the grain), 1 red pepper, 1 courgette, 2 spring onions, 3 garlic cloves, 1 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tsp sesame oil, 1 tsp cornstarch, oil
  • Marinate: Toss beef with soy sauce, sesame oil, cornstarch, black pepper. 10 minutes. Cornstarch creates a protective coating that keeps beef tender at high heat.
  • Cook: Wok at highest heat, oil until smoking. Beef first — single layer, 1 minute undisturbed, then toss 30 seconds. Remove beef. Vegetables in, toss 2–3 minutes. Return beef, splash soy, toss once. Serve immediately over rice.

Pasta & Rice

Spaghetti Aglio e Olio
20 min · 2 servings · Poverty of ingredients, richness of technique
  • Ingredients: 200g spaghetti, 6 garlic cloves (sliced very thin), 80ml good olive oil, chilli flakes, large handful flat-leaf parsley, salt, 100ml reserved pasta water
  • Garlic: Into cold oil in a cold pan — heat together slowly. Garlic must turn golden and fragrant, never brown. Brown means bitter. This takes 5–7 minutes of full attention.
  • Pasta water: Salt the water until it tastes of the sea. Reserve a full cup before draining.
  • Emulsify: Remove pan from heat. Add 60ml pasta water and drained pasta. Toss vigorously — starch in the water combines with oil to coat each strand. Add more water as needed. Glossy, not oily, not dry.
Mushroom Risotto
35 min · 2 servings · Requires full attention
  • Ingredients: 180g arborio rice, 250g mixed mushrooms, 1 shallot, 2 garlic cloves, 100ml white wine, 700ml warm stock, 40g cold butter (cubed), 50g parmesan (grated), thyme, olive oil, salt, pepper
  • Mushrooms: Sear separately over high heat until golden. Do not crowd — they must sear, not steam. Season, set aside.
  • Toast: Soften shallot and garlic in butter. Add dry rice, stir 2 minutes. Add wine, stir until absorbed.
  • Ladling: Add warm stock one ladle at a time, stirring constantly, waiting until absorbed before the next. 18–22 minutes. The stirring releases starch — that starch is the creaminess. No shortcut exists.
  • Mantecatura: When rice is al dente, remove from heat. Add cold butter and parmesan, stir vigorously 2 minutes. Rest 1 minute. Serve immediately.
Shakshuka
25 min · 2 servings · One pan, any meal
  • Ingredients: 4 eggs, 400g tinned crushed tomatoes, 1 red pepper, 1 onion, 3 garlic cloves, 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp smoked paprika, chilli flakes, olive oil, salt, fresh parsley, crusty bread
  • Base: Cook onion and pepper in oil 8 minutes. Add garlic and spices, cook 1 minute — blooming spices in oil releases fat-soluble flavour compounds. Add tomatoes, simmer 10 minutes until thickened.
  • Eggs: Make four wells. Crack an egg into each. Cover, cook over low heat until whites are just set, yolks still runny — approximately 5 minutes. Overcooking the yolk is the only failure mode.
  • Serve: Directly from the pan, herbs scattered over, bread alongside. A complete meal for under 5 BAM.

Soups & Sides

Roasted Tomato Soup
1 hour · 4 servings
  • Ingredients: 1kg ripe tomatoes (halved), 1 head garlic (top cut off), 1 red pepper, 1 onion (quartered), olive oil, salt, pepper, 500ml stock, fresh basil, optional: 100ml cream
  • Roast: All vegetables on a tray, cut-side up. Drizzle with oil, season. 200°C for 40–45 minutes until caramelised and slightly charred at edges. The char is depth, not error.
  • Blend: Squeeze garlic from skin, transfer everything including roasting juices to a pot. Add stock, blend smooth. Strain for restaurant finish or leave with texture.
  • Finish: Reheat, adjust seasoning, add cream if using. Freezes perfectly for 3 months.
Roasted Vegetables with Feta
40 min · 2–4 servings
  • Ingredients: 1 courgette, 1 red pepper, 1 aubergine, 1 red onion, 200g cherry tomatoes, 100g feta, thyme, olive oil, balsamic vinegar, salt, pepper
  • Key rule: Cut all vegetables to similar size. Do not crowd the pan — vegetables steam when crowded, roast when given space. Use two trays if necessary.
  • Roast: 200°C for 25–30 minutes, turning once. Add cherry tomatoes in the last 10 minutes only.
  • Finish: Crumble feta over while hot. Drizzle balsamic. Equally good warm or at room temperature.

Bosnian Classics

Burek sa Mesom
1.5 hours · 6 servings · The Sarajevo staple
  • Ingredients: 500g minced beef and lamb mixed, 500g phyllo/yufka pastry sheets, 1 large onion (finely diced), 2 garlic cloves, 1 tsp salt, ½ tsp black pepper, 150ml neutral oil or melted butter
  • Filling: Mix raw mince with raw onion, garlic, salt, pepper. Do not cook the filling first — it cooks inside the pastry. Add 2 tbsp water if stiff.
  • Rolling: Brush phyllo sheet with oil. Place a rope of meat filling along one edge. Roll into a cylinder. Coil into baking tray from centre outward.
  • Bake: Brush top with oil. 200°C for 35–40 minutes until deep golden. Rest 5 minutes before cutting.
  • Serve: With plain yogurt. This is not optional — the cool acidity completes the dish.
Bosanski Lonac
3 hours · 4–6 servings · A one-pot meal of exceptional depth
  • Ingredients: 500g beef or lamb (bone-in preferred), 3 potatoes, 2 carrots, 1 parsnip, 1 leek, cabbage wedge, 3 garlic cloves, 2 tomatoes, oil, salt, pepper, parsley, 200ml water or stock
  • Method: Layer raw meat and vegetables alternately in a deep oven pot. Season each layer. Add liquid to come halfway up. Seal tightly with lid or foil.
  • Cook: 160°C for 2.5–3 hours. Do not open — you release the steam doing the work. The broth produced is the sauce.
  • Serve: With bread to capture the broth. Food that requires nothing but time and patience — both of which you should have if you have absorbed this codex.

The Technique Library

Braising

Browning at high heat, then cooking covered in a small amount of liquid at low temperature for an extended period. The Maillard reaction products from the initial sear dissolve into the braising liquid, which then re-coats the meat as it cooks. The result: a crust flavour distributed throughout the entire dish. For tough cuts (shoulder, shin, short rib, Bosnian junetina) braising is the correct technique — the collagen converts to gelatin, producing the unctuousness that distinguishes a proper braise from a boiled piece of meat. Temperature: 140–160°C oven or lowest simmer on the hob, 2–4 hours depending on the cut.

Roasting

High dry heat in the oven with the food exposed to the air. The surface browns through Maillard reaction and caramelisation; the interior cooks through conduction from the surface and convection from the hot air. The critical variables: starting temperature of the meat (room temperature meat roasts more evenly than cold from the fridge), oven temperature (high for thin cuts, lower for large joints that need even cooking through), and resting time. A 2kg chicken at 200°C for 90 minutes, rested 15 minutes uncovered, produces better results than any more complex approach.

Reducing

Simmering a liquid until water evaporates and the remaining concentration of flavour compounds, sugars, and proteins intensifies. A litre of stock reduced by 75% produces a demi-glace that carries the same flavour as the original litre but in 250ml. Reduction is the mechanism behind all good pan sauces, behind the deep flavour of a proper bolognese (cooked 3 hours with periodic small additions of liquid), and behind the intensity that distinguishes a professional sauce from a home cook's. Time and patience are the only requirements.

Adapting Recipes to Sarajevo Ingredients

  • The kajmak substitution problem: Recipes from other traditions often call for ingredients that have a partial equivalent in Bosnia. Smetana (sour cream) is the closest general substitute for the tangy dairy component in Eastern European recipes. Kiselo mlijeko (fermented milk) substitutes for buttermilk. Pavlaka (cooking cream) substitutes for crème fraîche in cooked applications though not in raw ones — the lower fat content means it splits at high heat where crème fraîche would not.
  • Seasonal eating from the pijaca: The Markale pijaca's seasonal availability is a natural recipe guide. April: wild garlic, new potatoes, fresh spinach. June: zucchini, new garlic, green beans. September: peppers, aubergines, late tomatoes, the mushroom season begins. November–March: root vegetables, cabbages, stored onions. Cooking to the pijaca calendar produces better food at lower cost than cooking to a fixed recipe list that ignores seasonality.
The difference between a cook and someone who follows recipes is knife skill, heat control, and an understanding of what is actually happening in the pan. The Cooking tab provides the recipes. This tab provides the underlying mechanics that make every recipe in the Codex more reliable and every improvised meal more successful.— Von Lycaon

Knife Skills — The Foundation

A sharp knife is safer than a dull one — it requires less force and therefore offers more control. A dull knife that slips under pressure is the primary cause of kitchen injuries. Sharpen before you doubt, not after you notice.

The Correct Grip

  • The pinch grip: Thumb and index finger pinch the blade itself just forward of the bolster, remaining three fingers wrap the handle. This grip provides maximum control and reduces fatigue significantly over a standard handle grip. It feels unnatural for approximately the first three sessions. It then becomes automatic and all other grips feel imprecise.
  • The claw: The guiding hand curls fingertips under, knuckles forward. The flat of the blade rides against the knuckles — which cannot be cut because they are perpendicular to the blade. The knuckle acts as a guide determining cut width. Never cut toward outstretched fingers.

The Essential Cuts

  • Julienne (šibice): Thin matchstick strips. Slice the vegetable into thin planks first, then stack planks and slice into strips. Used for stir-fries, salads, garnishes. The uniformity of cut determines uniformity of cooking — irregular pieces produce unevenly cooked food.
  • Brunoise: Fine dice — julienne first, then rotate 90 degrees and slice across. The finest dice used in professional cooking for aromatics. 3mm is the target.
  • Chiffonade: Fine ribbons from leafy herbs or greens — stack leaves, roll tightly, slice across the roll. The fastest and most elegant way to cut basil, mint, or spinach.
  • Rocking chop vs straight pull: The rocking chop (tip of knife stays on board, heel rocks) is fastest for large volumes of soft ingredients. The straight pull (draw the knife toward you through the ingredient) is more precise for firm vegetables requiring exact cuts. Both are correct for different ingredients.

Knife Care

  • Never the dishwasher. The heat, vibration, and detergent of a dishwasher degrades the edge and the handle of any quality knife within months. Wash by hand immediately after use. Dry immediately — water sitting on a blade produces rust on carbon steel and streaks on stainless.
  • The honing steel before every use: A honing steel does not sharpen — it realigns the edge that bends during cutting. 5 passes per side at approximately 15–20 degrees before each session maintains the edge between sharpenings. Takes 30 seconds.
  • Sharpening: A whetstone (brus) produces the best edge. A pull-through sharpener (available at dm and Bauhaus) produces a functional edge with less skill required. Sharpen when the honing steel no longer restores cutting performance — for a well-maintained kitchen knife, every 3–6 months of regular use.

Food Science — Why Things Work

🔥 The Maillard Reaction

The browning of meat, bread, and vegetables at temperatures above 140°C — not steam or boiling water, which cannot exceed 100°C. The Maillard reaction produces hundreds of flavour compounds simultaneously. This is why a wet pan produces grey steamed meat and a hot dry pan produces a brown crust with complex flavour. Dry the surface of protein before searing. Heat the pan before adding fat. Fat before the surface is dry produces steam rather than crust.

🧂 Salt and Osmosis

Salt draws moisture from vegetables and protein through osmosis. Salting meat 30–45 minutes before cooking draws moisture out, which is then reabsorbed with the salt dissolved in it — seasoning the interior rather than just the surface. Salting cucumber or cabbage for a salad draws excess water out, producing better texture. Salting pasta water to the sea's salinity (1.5–2%) seasons the pasta from within. Timing of salt application matters as much as quantity.

🥩 Resting Meat

Meat removed from heat continues cooking (carryover cooking) and the internal temperature rises 3–5°C after removal. More importantly, the protein fibres that contracted during cooking begin to relax and reabsorb the juices that were forced to the centre by heat. A rested steak cut immediately loses far less juice than one rested for 5–10 minutes. Rest time approximately equals cooking time for smaller cuts.

🧅 Caramelisation vs Maillard

Often confused: caramelisation is the browning of sugars specifically (above 160°C for most sugars). Maillard is the reaction of sugars and amino acids (proteins). Onions caramelise. Meat Maillards. Both produce browning and complex flavour — through different mechanisms at different temperatures. This is why onions cannot be caramelised quickly at high heat — they burn before the sugars break down properly. Low heat, long time, patience.

Heat Control — The Variable Most People Underestimate

  • Preheat properly. A cold pan with oil in it warms unevenly. An empty pan preheated to temperature, then oil added, heats instantly and evenly. The water test: a drop of water in a pan should skitter and evaporate in 2 seconds — this is the Leidenfrost point, indicating the pan is at the correct temperature for searing.
  • Do not crowd the pan. Protein releases moisture when heated. In a crowded pan, the steam has nowhere to go and the temperature drops below the Maillard threshold — producing steamed rather than seared food. Two batches in a hot pan produce better results than one batch in a crowded pan.
  • The residual heat principle. Eggs, fish, and delicate proteins continue cooking after the heat source is removed. Remove them 10–15 seconds before they appear done. The plate they rest on continues the cooking. Over-cooking delicate protein is almost always the result of waiting for visual doneness before removing from heat.
The cook who understands why produces food that no recipe can fully explain. The recipe is the instruction. The understanding is the capability that allows departure from it when the ingredient, the pan, or the moment requires something other than what was written.— Von Lycaon
Bosnian cuisine is one of the most coherent and historically layered food traditions in the Balkans — a synthesis of Ottoman culinary technique, Central European influence from the Austro-Hungarian period, and indigenous Slavic and Illyrian food traditions. Understanding it as a tradition rather than a collection of dishes produces both better cooking and a deeper appreciation of the cultural context that this Codex inhabits. Cook these dishes. They are the food of this place.— Von Lycaon

The Foundations of Bosnian Cooking

The Ottoman Heritage

Five centuries of Ottoman rule produced a culinary vocabulary still present in every Bosnian kitchen: the use of lamb, the techniques of slow braising and wood-fire cooking, the pastry tradition (phyllo/yufka), the sweet-savoury combinations, and the coffee culture. The words themselves reveal the history: ćevap (kebab), burek (börek), baklava, kahva (kahve/coffee), sokak (street), čaršija (bazaar). The cuisine is Ottoman in structure and Bosnian in character.

The Ingredients

Local lamb and beef dominate. Kajmak (a clotted cream product made from the skin of slowly simmered milk) is the defining Bosnian dairy product — richer than crème fraîche, less acidic than sour cream. Urnebes (spiced soft cheese with peppers) is the condiment of the grill. Somun (the round flatbread baked in a wood-fired pekara oven) is the bread tradition. Bosanska kafa (Bosnian coffee made in the džezva) is the cultural institution. Each of these is irreplaceable within Bosnian cooking.

The Cooking Method

Bosnian traditional cooking is fundamentally slow-cooking over moderate heat — the sač (a covered pot buried in embers), the slow-simmered pot, the long-braised tava (baked casserole dish). These methods produce tender, deeply flavoured results that quick high-heat cooking cannot replicate. Time is the primary ingredient in Bosnian traditional cooking.

The Essential Dishes

Bosanski Lonac — The Definitive National Dish
3–4 hours · Traditional slow pot · The real Bosnia
  • Ingredients: 800g mixed meat (beef short rib, lamb shoulder, bone-in — the bones are essential for the stock), 400g potatoes, 3 carrots, 2 parsnips, 1 small cabbage (quartered), 3 peppers, 4 tomatoes, 1 whole head of garlic, 1 onion, salt, pepper, bay leaves, parsley
  • The principle: Everything goes into the pot in layers — no browning, no sautéing, no added liquid except the vegetables' own moisture. Layer: meat on the bottom, root vegetables, cabbage, peppers, tomatoes, garlic, seasoning. The pot must be tightly sealed — traditionally with dough around the lid, practically with foil crimped tight under the lid. The steam and moisture from the vegetables cook the meat from above while the meat juices fall down and baste the vegetables.
  • Cook: 160°C oven for 3.5–4 hours. Do not open the pot during cooking. The temptation to check is natural. Resist it. Opening releases the steam that is cooking the ingredients and produces an inferior result. Open only when the cooking time is complete.
  • Serve: In the pot, at the table. Bosanski lonac is not plated — it arrives as it cooked. The broth at the bottom of the pot is consumed first, as a soup. The meat and vegetables follow. Crusty bread to accompany.
Klepe (Bosnian Dumplings)
1.5 hours · Comfort food · Worth the effort
  • Dough: 300g flour, 1 egg, pinch salt, 6–8 tbsp water. Mix into a firm dough. Rest 30 minutes covered. Roll thin (2mm).
  • Filling: 300g beef mince, 1 onion (finely minced), salt, pepper, 1 tsp paprika. Mix raw — do not pre-cook.
  • Shape: Cut dough into 5cm squares. Place 1 tsp filling in the centre. Fold into a triangle, pressing edges firmly. Then press two corners together to form a tortellini-like shape. The seal must be airtight — open klepe disintegrate in the water.
  • Cook: In well-salted boiling water, 5–7 minutes until the dough is tender. Drain and dress immediately with: melted butter, garlic-infused sour cream (kiselo vrhnje), and dried mint. The combination of hot dumplings, garlic butter, and cold sour cream is the flavour of Bosnian winter.
Tarhana Čorba
30 min · Bosnian fermented grain soup · Restorative
  • Tarhana: A dried fermented grain product sold at pijace and specialty shops in Sarajevo. If unavailable, use a high-quality vegetable stock with a small amount of yogurt stirred in to approximate the sourness.
  • Method: Sauté 1 diced onion in butter. Add 2 tbsp tarhana, stir 2 minutes. Add 1 litre lamb or beef stock gradually, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. Simmer 20 minutes. Season with salt and dried mint. Serve with a spoon of sour cream and fresh mint.
  • Context: This soup is eaten for breakfast in traditional Bosnian homes, particularly in winter. The fermentation products in tarhana are probiotic and the warm, slightly sour broth is genuinely restorative on cold Sarajevo mornings.

Kajmak — Making It at Home

Homemade Kajmak
2 days · Requires raw whole milk · The correct ingredient
  • Note: Authentic kajmak requires raw whole milk — available at the Sarajevo pijaca from dairy vendors. Pasteurised supermarket milk produces a pale approximation. If raw milk is unavailable, quality crème fraîche with a small amount of butter stirred in produces a reasonable substitute for cooking purposes.
  • Method: Pour raw whole milk into a wide, shallow pan. Simmer on very low heat for 30 minutes without boiling — a skin forms on the surface. Allow to cool and refrigerate overnight. The next day, carefully skim the thick cream layer from the surface with a spatula. Salt lightly. This is kajmak — used as a condiment with ćevapi, spread on warm somun, or incorporated into sauces.
To cook the food of a place is to understand the place in a way that tourism and observation alone cannot produce. Bosanski lonac is not a recipe — it is a cultural artifact, a method developed over centuries of resourcefulness, a dish that requires patience in exactly the measure that Sarajevo's history required patience from its inhabitants. Cook it slowly. It teaches patience in return.— Von Lycaon
The Balkan roštilj is a cultural institution, not merely a cooking method. In Sarajevo specifically, the mangal (charcoal grill) is the instrument of ćevapi, pljeskavica, and summer gatherings that constitute the most important social cooking context in the region. This tab covers both the technique of grilling itself — heat management, timing, the science of char and caramelisation — and the Bosnian grill tradition in the depth it deserves.— Von Lycaon

The Grill Setup

Charcoal vs Gas

Charcoal produces higher temperatures (up to 700°C+) and the Maillard reaction products at those temperatures produce flavour compounds unavailable from gas cooking. The smoke from dripping fat onto charcoal also contributes aromatic compounds. For roštilj in the Bosnian tradition — charcoal is not optional, it is definitional. Gas grills produce heat. Charcoal produces roštilj.

The Two-Zone Fire

The most important technique in charcoal grilling: arrange coals to create a hot zone (direct heat, for searing) and a cool zone (indirect heat, for cooking through without burning). Everything goes over the hot zone briefly to develop crust, then moves to the cool zone to finish. This eliminates the most common grilling failure — burnt outside, raw inside — entirely.

Temperature Reading

The palm test: hold your hand 10cm above the grill surface. 2–3 seconds before discomfort: very high heat (searing, 280°C+). 4–5 seconds: high heat (most grilling, 230–280°C). 6–8 seconds: medium heat (finishing, thicker cuts). 10+ seconds: low heat (slow cooking, smoking). This calibration is imprecise but sufficient for all practical grilling decisions.

The Resting Rule

Identical to the pan-cooking resting principle in Kitchen Mastery: meat removed from the grill rests for a period equal to approximately its cooking time before cutting. The juices that contracted toward the centre during cooking redistribute through the meat during rest. A pljeskavica cut immediately releases half its moisture onto the board. The same pljeskavica rested 5 minutes releases almost none.

The Bosnian Grill Canon

Ćevapi — The Correct Method
Sarajevo style · Beef only · The benchmark
  • Meat: Sarajevo ćevapi are made from beef only — not pork, not mixed, not lamb. The cut: a blend of chuck (for flavour) and sirloin trim (for texture). The fat content should be approximately 20% — lean beef produces dry, crumbly ćevapi. Ask the mesar (butcher) to mince the meat twice — once through a coarse die, once through a medium die.
  • Seasoning: Per 500g meat: 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp baking soda (the classic Sarajevo recipe — bicarbonate increases pH, which affects the Maillard reaction and produces a specific exterior crust character), ½ tsp black pepper, 2 cloves garlic (very finely minced or through a press). No egg. No breadcrumbs. No onion in the mix.
  • Mix and rest: Mix seasonings into the meat thoroughly but do not overwork. Shape into cylinders approximately 8–10cm long, 2cm diameter. Refrigerate overnight uncovered — the surface dries slightly, which promotes a better crust formation on the grill.
  • Grill: Hot direct heat. Turn every 60–90 seconds — 4–5 times total. They should be uniformly charred on the outside but not dried through. Total cooking time: 6–8 minutes. Serve immediately in somun with raw onion and kajmak.
Pljeskavica
The Sarajevo burger · Various styles
  • Base mix: Per 500g beef mince (20% fat): 1 tsp salt, ½ tsp baking soda, ½ tsp pepper, 2 garlic cloves. Mix well, rest 2–4 hours refrigerated. Shape into a flat round approximately 1.5cm thick — thinner than you think is necessary. It will swell slightly on the grill.
  • Punjena pljeskavica (stuffed): Divide the mix in half. Flatten both halves. Place filling in the centre of one (options: kajmak and diced onion, cheese and peppers, or simply additional fat). Press the second half over the top, seal the edges firmly. The filling must be completely sealed or it will leak and burn.
  • Grill: Medium-high direct heat. 4–5 minutes per side. Do not press down with a spatula — this expels the juices that the resting time would otherwise return to the meat. The characteristic dome that forms is natural and flattens when rested.

Grilling Technique — General Principles

  • Dry surface before grilling. Moisture on the surface of meat produces steam that suppresses the Maillard reaction until the water evaporates. Pat meat dry with paper towels. For steaks: salt 30–45 minutes before grilling (draws moisture out, which is then reabsorbed — drier surface, seasoned interior).
  • Oil the food, not the grill. Oiling a hot grill grate burns and smokes. Brush oil onto the meat or vegetable surface instead. Use a high smoke-point oil (sunflower, rapeseed) — olive oil at grill temperatures smokes and produces off-flavours.
  • The single flip rule for steaks: Place on the hottest zone. Do not move until it releases naturally — meat stuck to the grill is not ready to flip. It will release when the crust has formed. Flip once. Move to cool zone to finish.
The mangal on a summer evening, the smell of charcoal, the company gathered around it — this is not merely cooking. It is the Sarajevo summer ritual, and a man who does it well commands the occasion rather than merely attending it. Learn the technique. The culture is already present.— Von Lycaon
A sauce is not a finishing touch. It is a second language for the same ingredient — the same chicken breast, the same pasta, the same piece of fish becomes a different dish entirely depending on what surrounds it. Mastering a dozen core sauces multiplies every recipe in the Cooking tab by the number of sauces it can carry. This is the highest-return culinary investment available after knife skills.— Von Lycaon

The Five Mother Sauces — The Foundation

Auguste Escoffier's codification of French cuisine identified five sauces from which virtually all other sauces derive. Understanding the mother sauces is understanding the logic of sauce-making — not memorising recipes but comprehending the underlying structure.

1. Béchamel — Milk + Roux

Butter and flour cooked together (roux), then milk whisked in and cooked to a creamy white sauce. The base for: cheese sauce (add gruyère or cheddar), Mornay sauce, cream-based pasta sauces, moussaka topping. The roux must cook for 2 minutes before milk is added — undercooked roux produces a floury taste that no amount of seasoning corrects.

2. Velouté — Stock + Roux

The same roux base as Béchamel but with stock (chicken, veal, or fish) instead of milk. Lighter, more savoury. The base for: suprême sauce (add cream), allemande sauce (add egg yolk and lemon). The stock quality determines the sauce quality — a velouté made from a rich homemade stock is a different substance from one made from a cube.

3. Espagnole — Brown Stock + Mirepoix

A rich brown sauce built on deeply caramelised vegetables and brown veal stock. Reduced to a demi-glace, it is the foundation of the most complex French meat sauces. Time-intensive but produces a sauce with a depth that no shortcut replicates. The reduction process concentrates flavour — a litre of good stock reduced by 75% produces something that coats the back of a spoon and makes meat taste more like itself.

4. Hollandaise — Butter + Egg Yolk

An emulsion sauce — fat (clarified butter) suspended in water (egg yolk) through careful temperature control. The most technically demanding of the five. The eggs must be warm but not scrambled (65-70°C). The butter must be added very slowly while whisking continuously. When it works: velvety, rich, and bright with lemon. When it breaks: separated and greasy. Worth learning for eggs Benedict and asparagus. Not an everyday sauce — a special occasion one.

5. Tomato — Tomatoes + Aromatics

The most familiar and the most forgiving. Onion, garlic, tomatoes, seasoning. The variables that produce different results: fresh vs tinned tomatoes (tinned plum tomatoes are more consistent than fresh outside of summer), cooking time (30 minutes produces a bright fresh sauce; 2 hours produces a darker, more complex one), and fat type (olive oil for Mediterranean character, butter for richness). The foundation of Italian cooking in this Codex and of Bosnian preparations that use tomato.

Essential Everyday Sauces

Pan Sauce — The Weeknight Standard
5 min · Made in the pan after searing any meat
  • After removing seared chicken, steak, or pork from the pan — deglaze with wine, stock, or even water. The brown bits (fond) stuck to the pan are concentrated flavour.
  • Add shallots or garlic if using, cook 1 minute. Add liquid (150ml wine or stock). Scrape and dissolve all the fond. Reduce by half. Add a cold knob of butter and swirl off the heat until emulsified. Season. Pour over the rested meat.
  • This technique transforms a basic protein into a restaurant-quality dish in 5 minutes using the pan that was already in use. Master this before any other sauce.
Garlic Butter
3 min · Universal · The most versatile compound butter
  • 100g softened butter, 4 garlic cloves (minced or pressed), 2 tbsp fresh parsley (chopped), lemon zest, salt, pepper.
  • Mix all together. Roll in cling film into a log shape. Refrigerate or freeze. Slice off rounds as needed.
  • Uses: melt over steak, fish, or vegetables. Spread on bread before grilling. Toss through pasta as a simple sauce. Add to pan sauce for richness. The log keeps frozen for 3 months.
Aioli (Garlic Mayonnaise)
10 min · Essential condiment · Balkans-friendly
  • 2 garlic cloves (crushed to paste with salt), 2 egg yolks, 200ml light olive oil, 1 tbsp lemon juice, pinch of salt.
  • Whisk garlic into egg yolks. Add oil drop by drop while whisking continuously until the emulsion begins to form (first 50ml is critical — too fast and it breaks). Once emulsified, add remaining oil in a thin stream. Add lemon juice. Season.
  • The immersion blender method (all ingredients in a tall container, blender at the bottom, blend while slowly raising) makes this essentially foolproof and produces identical results in 60 seconds.

Bosnian Condiments Worth Mastering

  • Ajvar: Roasted red pepper and eggplant relish — the defining condiment of Bosnian and Serbian cuisine. September is the correct time to make it in Sarajevo when the peppers are at peak. The process: roast whole peppers and eggplants over flame or in the oven until charred and collapsed, peel while warm, blend or chop with garlic, vinegar, salt, and olive oil. Cook the mixture until thickened. Jar and seal. A jar of homemade ajvar made in September lasts through winter and is a categorically different product from the commercial version.
  • Urnebes: Fresh soft cheese (brinza or sir) blended with roasted peppers, chilli, garlic, and paprika. The standard condiment of the Bosnian grill. Pairs with ćevapi, pljeskavica, and grilled meats. Extremely simple — 5 minutes — and irreplaceable alongside the roštilj content in the Grilling tab.
A sauce is the difference between food that sustains and food that is remembered. The cook who cannot make a sauce must always rely on the quality of the raw ingredient. The cook who can makes every ingredient more than itself.— Von Lycaon
Spices are the most transformative ingredient in cooking by cost-per-impact — a 50g tin of smoked paprika costs 2 BAM and changes every dish it touches for six months. Yet most home cooks own 12 jars that are 3 years old and have lost most of their volatile oils. This tab is the guide to building, using, and maintaining a spice collection that actually improves your cooking.— Von Lycaon

The Essentials — Build This Collection First

The Heat Family

Black pepper: Buy whole peppercorns and grind fresh — pre-ground loses most of its aromatic volatile oils within weeks. The single most important spice in the kitchen. Chilli flakes: Heat and a gentle fruitiness. Bosnian Aleppo-style mild chilli (if available at the pijaca) is preferable to generic supermarket chilli flakes for cooking. Cayenne: Pure heat without much flavour — use to adjust heat level in dishes where you do not want additional paprika character.

The Warm-Sweet Family

Cumin: The most commonly used spice in the world. Earthy, warm, slightly bitter when raw but transformative when toasted or bloomed in fat. Essential in Bosnian, Middle Eastern, and Indian cooking. Coriander (ground): Citrusy, warm, and often paired with cumin. Cinnamon: Both sweet and savoury applications — in Bosnian cooking, it appears in meat dishes. Cardamom: The spice that makes Bosnian coffee what it is when used correctly.

The Paprika Family

Paprika is the defining spice of Balkan cooking. Three essential types: Sweet paprika (slatka): Mild, fruity, brick-red colour. The baseline. Hot paprika (ljuta): Heat with paprika character. Smoked paprika (dimljena): The most flavour-transformative spice in this list — a small amount changes eggs, meat, potatoes, and soups completely. Available in Sarajevo as Vegeta-adjacent products or imported Spanish pimentón at larger supermarkets.

The Aromatic Family

Bay leaves: Add to any slow braise, stock, or stew. Subtle but cumulative — a dish without bay leaves tastes slightly flatter than the same dish with. Dried thyme: The most versatile dried herb in Mediterranean cooking. Oregano: More assertive than thyme. The defining herb of Bosnian lamb and tomato dishes. Dried mint: Specific to Bosnian cooking — over klepe (dumplings), mixed with yogurt sauces, in lamb dishes. Fresh mint cannot substitute for dried in these applications.

The Three Techniques That Unlock Spice Flavour

1. Blooming in Fat

  • Adding ground spices to hot oil or butter for 30-60 seconds before adding other ingredients activates fat-soluble aromatic compounds that water-based cooking cannot extract. The spice sizzles briefly, darkens slightly, and the kitchen fills with fragrance that the finished dish will carry. This is why Indian curries smell and taste as they do — the spice flavour is fat-soluble and requires fat activation.
  • Application: when making any Bosnian čorba, any curry, any spiced rice — add the spices to the oil with the onion, not to the liquid. 30 seconds of blooming before the liquid enters the pot produces a measurably more flavourful result.

2. Toasting Whole Spices

  • Whole spices (cumin seeds, coriander seeds, peppercorns, fennel) toasted dry in a hot pan for 60-90 seconds until fragrant, then ground, produce a completely different flavour profile from the same spice bought pre-ground. The heat activates volatile aromatic compounds that were dormant. This is the single most effective technique for making a simple spice taste complex.
  • A small cast-iron or stainless pan, medium heat, constantly stirring or shaking until the spice begins to smoke slightly and the aroma becomes insistent. Remove immediately — the difference between toasted and burnt is 20 seconds.

3. Layering at Different Points

  • Adding the same spice at multiple points in a dish produces more complexity than adding it all at once. Paprika added at the start of cooking a goulash produces a deep background note. Paprika added in the last 5 minutes produces a brighter, more assertive note. Both in the same dish produces a layered flavour that neither alone achieves.
  • This technique is the explanation for why restaurant dishes taste more complex than the same ingredients cooked at home — not more expensive ingredients, but more deliberate construction of flavour across the cooking process.

Shelf Life and Storage

  • Ground spices: 6–12 months maximum. Not for safety reasons — for flavour. The volatile oils that carry most of the flavour begin degrading immediately after grinding. The test: open the jar and smell. If the aroma is faint, the spice has lost most of its purpose. Replace it. A fresh jar of cumin smells assertive and complex. A two-year-old jar smells like dust with cumin's ghost.
  • Whole spices: 1–2 years. The unbroken seed or pod protects the aromatic compounds until the moment of grinding. This is why buying whole spices and grinding as needed produces better results — but even whole spices eventually degrade.
  • Storage: dark, cool, dry, and sealed. The cabinet away from the stove, not the rack above it. Heat, light, and moisture are the three enemies of spice quality. The decorative spice rack on the counter above the stove is the worst possible storage location.
  • Sarajevo sourcing: The Markale pijaca and the specialty shops in Baščaršija carry freshly sourced spices — often whole, often regional, always cheaper than supermarket jars. Buying from the pijaca and storing in small sealed containers produces better results than any branded supermarket product.
A kitchen with fresh, well-stored spices and the knowledge of how to use them correctly produces food of a quality that no expensive ingredient can substitute for. The simplest dish — eggs, rice, lentils — becomes something worth eating when the spice is correct.— Von Lycaon
Meal planning is not a diet programme. It is an efficiency system — the application of the same batch-processing logic to food that productivity systems apply to work tasks. The person who plans meals once weekly spends less time deciding what to eat, less money on food, wastes less, eats better, and hits the 160g protein target in the Nutrition Science tab consistently rather than accidentally. The investment is 30 minutes Sunday. The return is every other day of the week.— Von Lycaon

The System

Sunday Planning Session — 30 Minutes

Review the coming week: how many dinners at home (remembering that your wife cooks dinner — plan around this), how many lunches need to be prepared, what the protein targets require. Identify the 3-4 meals that will be made. Write a shopping list from those meals. The planning session prevents the most expensive and unhealthy food decision available: opening the fridge at 7pm with no plan and ordering delivery.

Batch Cooking — What to Prepare Ahead

Some foods cook once and serve multiple times with minimal effort. These are the anchor of any efficient meal plan: a large batch of grains (rice or quinoa — 20 minutes, feeds 4 meals), roasted vegetables (40 minutes in the oven, serve warm or cold across multiple dishes), a protein base (a whole chicken roasted Sunday provides meat for 2-3 subsequent meals). The goal is not identical meals every day but flexible components that combine differently.

The Component Approach

Rather than planning complete dishes, plan components: protein (cooked chicken, boiled eggs, canned tuna), carbohydrate (cooked rice, bread, pasta), vegetables (roasted or raw). Any combination of these three produces a functional meal. This approach reduces the mental complexity of meal planning from recipe management to inventory management.

The Protein Priority

The 160g daily protein target from the Nutrition Science and Workout tabs cannot be met accidentally. It requires explicit planning. Map out when the protein is coming from across each day before planning anything else. Three 50g protein meals plus a 10g snack. Once the protein is distributed, the rest of the meal plan fills around it rather than the reverse.

The Weekly Template

Sunday — Batch Day (60–90 minutes total active time)

  • Cook a large protein: roast chicken (90 min passive), or a batch of marinated chicken thighs (30 min), or a bolognese sauce (45 min). This provides the base for 3-4 subsequent meals.
  • Cook a large grain batch: 400g dry rice or quinoa. Portion into containers. 5 days of carbohydrate base.
  • Roast a tray of vegetables: whatever is in season and available. Olive oil, salt, 200°C, 35-40 minutes. Serve alongside anything.
  • Hard-boil 8-10 eggs. Refrigerate. Ready for breakfast and snacks throughout the week with zero preparation time.
  • Prepare overnight oats jars for Monday and Tuesday mornings (from the Daily Eating tab).

Quick Meals Under 20 Minutes

The planned batch cooking provides components. These are the assembly methods for weeknight speed:

  • Rice bowl: Reheated rice + protein (from the batch) + a raw vegetable + a sauce (aioli, tahini, soy + sesame). 3 minutes assembly.
  • Egg and vegetable scramble: 3 eggs scrambled with whatever vegetables are in the fridge. 8 minutes. 21g protein, infinitely variable.
  • Tuna pasta: Cooked pasta + 2 tins tuna + olive oil + garlic + capers. 15 minutes from start. 45g protein per serving.
  • Soup from batch components: Stock + the batch protein (shredded) + whatever vegetable + spices. 15 minutes. The batch vegetable roasting from Sunday makes this a genuine 15-minute meal rather than a 45-minute one.

Sarajevo Shopping Rhythm

  • One major shop weekly (Konzum, Bingo, or Interspar) for dry goods, dairy, and pantry staples. Plan-driven — the shopping list from Sunday's meal plan session. Impulse purchases are expensive and unplanned.
  • Pijaca once or twice weekly for fresh vegetables, fruit, and seasonal produce. The pijaca provides better quality at lower cost than any supermarket for produce — and the seasonal variety enforces the cooking variety that prevents meal plan fatigue.
  • Butcher (mesar) for quality meat. The mesar at any Sarajevo pijaca provides better quality and more specific cuts than supermarket pre-packaged meat. For a Sunday roast chicken or for the minced beef for ćevapi, this is the correct source.
The man who plans his meals is not constrained by them. He is freed from the daily decision that, accumulated over a week, consumes significant cognitive energy and almost always produces worse food than the planned alternative. The plan is the infrastructure; the enjoyment is what the infrastructure makes possible.— Von Lycaon
Sous vide (French for "under vacuum") is the technique of cooking food sealed in a bag, submerged in water held at a precise temperature for a precisely determined time. It produces results impossible with conventional cooking — a chicken breast that is uniformly cooked to the exact temperature throughout rather than overdone at the edges and perfect at the centre, a steak that is edge-to-edge medium-rare with no grey band. The technique is scientific, the equipment is inexpensive, and the results are genuinely different from what a pan or oven can achieve.— Von Lycaon

The Science — Why It Works

Precise Temperature Control

Conventional cooking operates in an environment far hotter than the target internal temperature of the food. A 230°C oven cooking a chicken breast to 70°C internal means that the outer layers are exposed to 160°C more heat than they need. The result: overcooked exterior, correctly cooked interior, or correctly cooked exterior, undercooked interior. Sous vide eliminates this by setting the water temperature to exactly the target internal temperature — the food can never exceed the water temperature regardless of how long it cooks.

Time as the Variable

At 63°C, a chicken breast is safely pasteurised after 1 hour but remains tender and juicy. At 72°C (conventional safe temperature), it is overcooked and dry. The extended time at lower temperature does what higher temperature does instantly but more brutally. Additionally, longer cooking at low temperature breaks down collagen in tougher cuts — a 48-hour short rib at 72°C produces tender braised results without conventional braising.

The Sear

Sous vide food exits the bag without a crust. The Maillard reaction (Kitchen Mastery tab) requires temperatures above 140°C — the sous vide bath never reaches this. The correct method: pat the food completely dry after the bath, then sear in the hottest possible pan with a small amount of high-smoke-point oil for 45-60 seconds per side. This produces the crust without overcooking the interior that was just precisely cooked.

The Equipment

  • Immersion circulator: The device that heats and circulates the water to precise temperature. Anova and Inkbird are the two most reliable brands at accessible price points — both available on eMag.rs for 80-150 BAM. The circulator attaches to any large pot. No dedicated vessel required.
  • Vacuum sealer (optional): Produces the best results for long cooks. For shorter cooks (under 4 hours), the displacement method works equally well: place food in a zip-lock bag, seal almost completely, lower into water allowing water pressure to push air out, then seal fully. Zero cost, nearly identical result for most applications.
  • A large pot: Any existing stockpot. Minimum 6 litres to maintain temperature stability. The circulator clips to the side.

Core Temperature & Time Reference

🥩 Beef Steak

Rare: 52°C / 1-4 hours. Medium-rare: 57°C / 1-4 hours. Medium: 63°C / 1-4 hours. After the bath: pat completely dry, sear in cast iron at maximum heat 45 seconds per side including edges. The result is edge-to-edge the exact temperature specified, impossible with pan cooking alone.

🍗 Chicken Breast

Juicy and tender: 63°C / 1.5-4 hours. The difference between this and the same breast cooked conventionally to the same internal temperature is significant — conventional cooking produces dry, stringy results at 63°C because of the uneven temperature gradient. Sous vide produces a texture closer to a perfectly poached breast.

🐟 Salmon

Silky/translucent: 50°C / 30-45 minutes. Firmer: 55°C / 30-45 minutes. The most dramatic application of sous vide for anyone who normally overcooks fish. At 50°C, salmon is barely set — the texture is completely different from any conventionally cooked version. Finish with a 30-second skin-side sear only.

🥚 Eggs

Soft poached style: 63°C / 1 hour. A 63°C egg has a just-set white and a warm, creamy, barely-set yolk — the texture that brunch restaurants charge a premium to achieve. Serve directly from the shell over anything. This application requires only water, the circulator, and the eggs themselves.

🥓 Short Ribs / Tough Cuts

Tender as braise: 72°C / 48 hours or 85°C / 24 hours. The long cook breaks down collagen to gelatin without the drying that oven braising produces. The result is fork-tender with retained moisture. Finish with a sear or glazed under a broiler.

Integration With the Codex Recipes

  • The pan-seared chicken thighs in the Cooking tab can be sous vide at 74°C for 2 hours, then seared for the crispy skin — producing simultaneously the most tender and the crispiest skin result available.
  • The pre-sleep protein need: a batch of 8 chicken breasts sous vide on Sunday, refrigerated, provides lean protein for the entire week with zero daily preparation.
  • The garlic butter from the Sauces tab melts over a just-seared sous vide steak with a result that is genuinely better than most restaurant steaks available in Sarajevo.
Sous vide is not a gadget. It is a precision tool that removes the primary variable in cooking — uncontrolled heat — and replaces it with a controllable one. For the cook who has mastered the fundamentals and wants to extend what is possible, this is the most significant technique available at home.— Von Lycaon
Baking is the discipline of the kitchen that punishes improvisation most severely. Cooking tolerates adjustment; baking does not. A pinch more salt in a sauce is harmless. A pinch more baking soda in a cake is structural failure. The recipes here are tested ratios — follow them precisely the first time, understand why they work, then adapt from knowledge rather than guesswork.— Von Lycaon
Equipment worth owning: Kitchen scale (essential — volume measures are imprecise), hand mixer or stand mixer (for anything whipped), a 20cm round cake tin, a loaf tin, and a baking sheet. A digital thermometer for oven accuracy. Most ovens run 10–15°C off the dial setting — worth knowing before you begin.

Baking Principles

Weigh, Don't Measure

A cup of flour can vary by 30% depending on how it was scooped. A cup of sugar is similarly imprecise. Use a scale. This is not pedantry — it is the difference between a recipe working and not working.

Room Temperature Ingredients

Butter, eggs, and dairy should be at room temperature before combining unless explicitly stated otherwise. Cold butter does not cream. Cold eggs cause chocolate to seize. Plan 30–60 minutes ahead.

Do Not Overmix

Once flour meets wet ingredients, gluten begins developing. Overmixing produces tough, chewy baked goods. Mix until just combined and no more. A few streaks of flour visible when you stop is acceptable.

Oven Calibration

Know your oven. The first time you use a new recipe, check progress 5 minutes early. If things consistently burn or undercook, your oven temperature is off. Adjust accordingly and note it for future reference.

Pastries

Butter Croissants (Two-Day Method)
2 days · 12 croissants · Advanced — worth the effort
  • Dough ingredients: 500g strong white flour, 10g salt, 80g sugar, 7g instant yeast, 300ml whole milk (warm), 30g butter (softened)
  • Butter block: 250g cold unsalted butter (high fat content — at least 82% — Lurpak or similar)
  • Day 1 — Dough: Combine flour, salt, sugar, yeast. Add warm milk gradually. Knead 8–10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Add softened butter, knead another 5 minutes. The dough should be smooth and slightly tacky. Wrap and refrigerate overnight.
  • Day 1 — Butter block: Pound cold butter between two sheets of parchment into a 18x18cm square. Refrigerate alongside the dough.
  • Day 2 — Lamination: Roll dough into a 38x20cm rectangle. Place butter block in the centre. Fold dough over butter like an envelope, sealing the edges. Roll out to 60x20cm.
