  • Seasonally: Repot when roots emerge from the drainage holes or the plant becomes visibly pot-bound. One pot size larger, fresh potting mix. Spring is the ideal time for repotting — the plant is entering its growing season and recovers fastest.
A plant cared for daily develops the habit of attention to something that cannot speak its needs directly. The slight wilt before thirst, the yellowing leaf that indicates overwatering, the new growth that signals the care was correct — these are small communications that reward observation. The habit transfers.— Von Lycaon

Plant Care Science — What Plants Actually Need

Light — The Non-Negotiable

Every plant care problem is ultimately a light problem or a water problem. Most houseplant failures are light failures — plants placed in positions that feel decoratively right but are physiologically insufficient. The assessment: direct sun (within 30cm of a south-facing window) — only succulents and cacti thrive here. Bright indirect (within 1-2 metres of a south or east window) — the ideal for most popular houseplants. Medium light (2-4 metres from a window) -- tolerant species only. Low light (no direct or near-indirect sun) -- almost nothing thrives here, despite what labels claim. Measure it honestly with a light meter app before buying any plant.

Watering — The Actual Mechanism

Plants absorb water from the soil through osmosis -- water moves from areas of lower concentration to higher concentration. The practical consequence: thoroughly watering (until water drains from the bottom) and then allowing the soil to partially dry before the next watering is physiologically correct for most tropical houseplants. This cycle encourages roots to extend downward in search of moisture, producing a stronger root system. Frequent shallow watering encourages shallow roots and soil moisture at the surface that promotes fungus gnats and root rot.

Soil and Drainage

The vast majority of houseplant deaths by overwatering are actually soil failures, not watering failures. Standard potting mix retains moisture far longer than most tropical plants require. Adding 20-30% perlite (the white volcanic particles available at any garden centre) to any potting mix dramatically improves drainage and aeration. The roots of most tropical houseplants require both water and oxygen -- compacted, waterlogged soil suffocates roots as surely as drought kills them.

Humidity and the Sarajevo Winter Problem

Most popular houseplants originate from tropical environments with ambient humidity of 60-80%. Sarajevo apartments in winter, heated by radiators with no moisture addition, often reach 30-35% humidity. This explains the brown leaf tips on Monsteras, Calatheas, and most ferns during winter months -- the plant is losing moisture from its leaves faster than the roots can replace it. Solutions: group plants together (they create a micro-humid environment), use a pebble tray with water below the pot, or run a small ultrasonic humidifier near the plants during winter months.

Species Guide — Matched to Sarajevo Conditions

Pothos (Epipremnum aureum)

The most forgiving houseplant available. Tolerates low light, irregular watering, and winter dry air better than any other. Grows vigorously in bright indirect light. Trails beautifully from a shelf. Propagates by cutting a node and placing in water -- roots appear within 2 weeks. If you cannot keep a Pothos alive, the conditions of the space are not suitable for plants generally.

Snake Plant (Sansevieria)

Survives neglect that kills other plants. Tolerates low light (though grows slowly). Watered once every 2-4 weeks in winter. The most drought-tolerant non-succulent houseplant. The architectural form (upright sword-like leaves) suits the Lycaon aesthetic particularly well. Available widely in Sarajevo flower shops.

Monstera deliciosa

The fenestration (holes in mature leaves) only develops in bright indirect light. In lower light, the plant survives but leaves remain entire. Needs a moss pole or support for vertical growth and more dramatic leaf development. Water when the top 3-4cm of soil is dry. The most visually impressive houseplant available at accessible prices.

ZZ Plant (Zamioculcas)

Stores water in its thick rhizomes, making it extremely drought tolerant. Tolerates low light. Glossy leaves catch and reflect light beautifully. Growth is slow but reliable. The correct plant for a room with less natural light where most other species would decline.

Rubber Plant (Ficus elastica)

Grows into a significant indoor tree with proper light. The dark burgundy-leaved varieties (Abidjan, Burgundy) have an aesthetic that pairs with dark interior palettes. Needs bright indirect light and consistent watering. Wipe the large leaves monthly with a damp cloth -- the dust accumulation reduces photosynthesis significantly and dulls the inherently glossy surface.

The Seasonal Care Calendar — Sarajevo Specific

  • March-September (growing season): Water when the top 2-3cm of soil is dry. Fertilise monthly with a balanced liquid fertiliser (available at garden centres and dm). Repot if the plant is root-bound (roots emerging from drainage holes, or the plant drying out within 2-3 days of watering despite adequate volume). Propagate by cutting -- spring and summer cuttings root fastest.
  • October-February (dormant season): Reduce watering by 30-50% -- lower light and cooler temperatures mean lower metabolic activity and slower water uptake. Do not fertilise -- the plant is not actively growing and fertiliser will accumulate in the soil without being absorbed, eventually causing root burn. Increase humidity (see above). Move plants closer to windows as daylight decreases.
A living plant is a daily lesson in attentiveness. It responds to changes in light, temperature, and water on a timescale that rewards observation. The gardener who looks at their plants daily -- not to intervene, but to notice -- develops an observational skill that transfers to every other form of care.-- Von Lycaon
Scent is the sense most directly connected to memory and emotional state — processed by the olfactory bulb, which has direct connections to the hippocampus and amygdala before reaching the rational cortex. An apartment with a considered ambient scent produces a felt experience that visual design alone cannot replicate. This tab is about controlling that variable deliberately.— Von Lycaon

Scent as Environment Design

The Grooming tab covers personal fragrance. This covers ambient scent — the background olfactory environment of the apartment. The two should be complementary rather than competing. If the personal fragrance is aquatic (Acqua di Giò), the ambient scent should work with it — wood, green, light floral — not against it with heavy oriental or spice-dominant notes that create olfactory conflict.

Scent Families for Home

Wood and resin: Cedar, sandalwood, oud. Warm, grounding, masculine. Appropriate for living rooms, studies, winter evenings. Green and herbal: Eucalyptus, sage, vetiver. Clean, refreshing. Appropriate for bathrooms, morning contexts. Floral (restrained): White florals (jasmine, magnolia) — not sweet florals, which read as feminine. Appropriate for bedrooms at low intensity. Citrus: Bergamot, lemon, grapefruit. Light, energising, kitchen and morning contexts.

The One-Scent-Per-Room Principle

Each room should have one ambient scent identity — not several competing. The living room has one signature. The bedroom has one (different, quieter). The bathroom has one (clean, functional). Changing scents by season is legitimate — the same room can smell of cedar in winter and eucalyptus in summer. Changing mid-season creates olfactory confusion without the pleasure of either.

Intensity Control

Ambient scent should be noticeable on first entering a room and unnoticeable after five minutes — this is the correct intensity. If it remains noticeable after acclimatisation, it is too strong. If a guest comments on a pleasant smell, that is correct. If a guest comments that it is strong, reduce the source. The apartment should smell deliberate, not perfumed.

The Methods

🕯️ Candles

The most atmospheric delivery method — the light quality of a flame adds to the ambience beyond the scent alone. Quality matters: cheap paraffin candles produce soot and a synthetic scent character. Soy or beeswax candles with essential oil fragrance burn cleaner and smell more genuine. Candle care: Trim the wick to 6mm before each burn (prevents soot, extends burn time). Allow the wax to melt to the edges on the first burn (prevents tunnelling). Never burn in a draft. Extinguish with a snuffer, not by blowing — blowing spreads hot wax particles and produces smoke that contaminates the scent.

🌿 Reed Diffusers

Continuous low-intensity ambient scent without flame. Appropriate for areas where a burning candle is impractical (near bookshelves, in bathrooms, near soft furnishings). Flip the reeds weekly to refresh the scent output. A quality diffuser lasts 2–3 months. The disadvantage versus candles: no visual component and less scent intensity control.

🌸 Fresh Flowers

Natural scent that changes as the flowers develop. Peonies, lilies (particularly Stargazer), sweet peas, and freesias are the most fragrant cut flowers available at Sarajevo's pijaca and flower shops. Not a permanent solution — but as a weekend or occasion element, fresh flowers provide a quality of scent that no diffusion technology replicates.

🍋 Natural Scent Sources

Simmering a small pot of water with cinnamon sticks, orange peel, and cloves produces a natural winter ambient scent superior to most artificial alternatives. Fresh eucalyptus hung in the bathroom releases oils in the steam from the shower. A bowl of fresh citrus on the kitchen surface. These are zero-cost interventions with high impact.

Sourcing in Sarajevo

  • dm stocks a reasonable selection of soy candles and reed diffusers at accessible prices. The Yankee Candle range (at dm and larger Konzum locations) provides quality consistent burning. Local craft markets (particularly the Baščaršija weekend market) occasionally carry handmade beeswax candles from domestic producers — both superior quality and more culturally coherent with the apartment's aesthetic direction.
The apartment that smells considered is experienced as considered before a single designed element is seen. Scent is the first impression of a home — it enters the room before anything else is processed. Treat it with the same attention as the visual environment it precedes.— Von Lycaon

The Olfactory Architecture of the Home

The sense of smell is the only sense directly wired to the limbic system -- the brain's emotional and memory centre -- without passing through the thalamus. This is why scent produces the most immediate and involuntary emotional responses of any sensory input, and why the scent of a home produces a felt response before any visual or auditory impression registers. The man who manages the scent of his home manages a powerful and largely unconscious dimension of how that home is experienced by everyone who enters it.

The Living Room Scent

The anchor scent for the primary social space. Should be present, warm, and welcoming without being assertive. The correct character: woody and slightly sweet -- sandalwood, cedar, vetiver, light amber. Diptyque Baies (blackcurrant and rose -- more unisex than it sounds), Jo Malone Wood Sage and Sea Salt (austere, clean, modern), or the dm-accessible alternatives such as the Yankee Candle or WoodWick wood-based lines. Burn for 2-3 hours in the evening. Not during the day -- daytime scent should be natural light and ventilation.

The Bedroom Scent

Lighter and more intimate than the living room. The priority is sleep facilitation rather than social impression. Lavender (directly anxiolytic -- clinical evidence for reduction in cortisol levels), chamomile, or very light wood. A small diffuser with lavender essential oil set to run for 30 minutes before sleep is the most evidence-based scent intervention for sleep quality available. Avoid strong or complex fragrances in the bedroom -- they stimulate rather than calm.

The Kitchen Scent

The kitchen is the most challenging room because cooking produces its own powerful scents that can become unpleasant when they linger. Priority: elimination before addition. A functional extraction fan during cooking, vinegar-water surface cleaning after cooking, and immediate food waste disposal eliminates 90% of kitchen scent problems. The positive scent: simmering spices on the hob (cinnamon stick, cloves, and orange peel in a pot of water for 20 minutes) produces a kitchen scent that no candle perfectly replicates -- natural, warm, and genuinely welcoming.

Candle Quality — The Informed Purchase

  • Wax type: Paraffin wax (the cheapest, petroleum-derived, produces more soot and the fragrance throw is less clean). Soy wax (cleaner burn, longer-lasting, better fragrance throw, the correct choice for regular household use). Beeswax (the finest, burns cleanest, naturally honey-scented, most expensive -- appropriate for special occasions rather than daily use).
  • Fragrance load: A candle with 10-12% fragrance load (the weight of fragrance oil relative to wax weight) produces a strong throw. Below 6%: weak throw. The fragrance load is rarely stated on packaging -- it is inversely correlated with price. Budget candles have 4-6%; quality candles have 8-12%.
  • The first burn rule: The first time a candle is lit, it must be allowed to burn until the entire surface is liquefied (melted edge to edge). This prevents tunnelling -- the formation of a narrow shaft down the centre of the candle that wastes most of the wax. The first burn for a large candle may take 2-4 hours. Do not blow it out prematurely.
  • Wick maintenance: Trim the wick to 5-6mm before each burn. A long wick produces a large flame that burns too hot, consumes the candle too quickly, and produces excess soot. A trimmed wick produces a steady, moderate flame and a clean burn. Wick trimmers are available at home goods shops -- alternatively, clean scissors work identically.
The home that smells of nothing unpleasant is clean. The home that smells of something specific and beautiful is considered. The difference between these two states is 10 minutes of attention per week. Few investments in the quality of daily life are more immediately felt by everyone who enters.-- Von Lycaon
Quality furniture is one of the highest-return investments in the home — used daily, visible constantly, and capable of lasting decades when maintained correctly. The corollary: furniture maintained poorly deteriorates faster than its material warrants, and replacement costs several times what maintenance would have cost. This tab covers both the care of existing furniture and the intelligence to acquire quality pieces.— Von Lycaon

The Quality Assessment

Wood — Real vs Engineered

Solid wood (puno drvo): the grain runs through the entire piece, it can be sanded and refinished, it ages well and improves with patina. Engineered wood (MDF, particle board, HDF): a core of compressed wood particles with a veneer or laminate surface. Cannot be sanded or refinished. Swells irreversibly when wet. The price difference is real because the quality difference is real. Buy one good solid wood piece over three chipboard pieces of equivalent cost.

Upholstery

The frame quality determines longevity. Solid wood or metal frames outlast particle board frames by a decade. Check: lift one corner of a sofa — it should be heavy (solid frame). Press on the seat — quality springs or webbing should resist and return. The upholstery fabric is replaceable; the frame is not. A quality frame with worn upholstery can be reupholstered. A poor frame with excellent upholstery is a poor sofa.

The Heirloom Test

Before any significant furniture purchase: would this piece be worth keeping for twenty years? Would it survive being moved to a new apartment? Is it a design that will not feel dated in a decade? If the answer to any is no, buy cheaper and replace sooner — or save until the answer to all is yes. The worst furniture outcome is spending significant money on something that is discarded in five years.

Wood Care & Maintenance

Solid Wood — Annual Care Protocol
Preserves finish, prevents drying and cracking
  • Dusting: Microfibre cloth, with the grain. Never against — it creates micro-scratches in the finish. Avoid feather dusters which redistribute rather than remove particles.
  • Cleaning: A slightly damp cloth for most marks. For sticky residue: a small amount of white spirit on a cloth, test on an inconspicuous area first. Never excess water on wood — it raises the grain and can cause irreversible swelling.
  • Oiling or waxing annually: Oiled finishes (teak oil, linseed oil, Danish oil): reapply annually with a lint-free cloth, working with the grain, removing excess after 20 minutes. Waxed finishes: apply a thin coat of quality furniture wax (Briwax or equivalent), allow to haze, buff with a soft cloth. This maintains the finish, prevents drying, and deepens the patina over years.
  • Preventing damage: Coasters under drinks (always). Felt pads under objects that sit permanently on surfaces. Tablecloths or mats for heat — a hot cup directly on solid wood creates a white ring that requires significant effort to remove. Prevention is seconds; removal is hours.
Repairing Surface Damage
Minor scratches and marks
  • Minor scratches: A walnut (orah) rubbed along the scratch fills and disguises it through the natural oils in the nut — an old craft technique that works reliably on medium-dark wood. Or a touch of shoe polish in a matching colour, worked in with a cloth and buffed.
  • White rings from heat or moisture: Gently rub with a cloth and a small amount of mayonnaise or petroleum jelly (vaseline), leave overnight, remove and buff. The oil penetrates the finish and displaces the moisture that caused the ring. Works on most lacquer finishes.
  • Deep scratches: A wax fill stick (available at Bauhaus) in a matching colour fills the scratch and can be levelled flush with a credit card. Colour-matching is approximate but significantly better than an unfilled scratch.

Upholstery & Sofa Care

  • Vacuum weekly with the upholstery attachment — beneath cushions specifically, where crumbs and debris accumulate. Dirt that sits in fabric fibres acts as an abrasive and degrades the fabric.
  • Rotate cushions monthly if they are removable. Even wear across all cushion faces extends the visible life of the sofa significantly.
  • Fabric protection spray (Scotchgard or equivalent) applied to new fabric upholstery before first use significantly reduces staining and soiling. Reapply annually. 5 minutes of prevention versus a stained sofa.
  • Leather upholstery: Clean with a leather cleaner twice yearly. Condition with a leather conditioner (Leather Balsam, Collonil) to prevent cracking and drying. Avoid direct sunlight and heat sources — leather cracks from drying faster than from use.
The apartment that contains one well-maintained piece of quality furniture says more about its owner than ten mediocre pieces impeccably arranged. Quality ages. Mediocrity deteriorates. The investment is visible every day.— Von Lycaon

The Wood Care Protocol in Depth

Solid wood is a living material that continues to respond to moisture and temperature changes long after it has been made into furniture. Understanding this allows correct maintenance that preserves the furniture's condition and beauty for decades.

Oils vs Waxes

Oil finishes (linseed oil, tung oil, danish oil) penetrate the wood grain and harden within the fibres. They produce a natural, matte finish that enhances the wood's grain and feel. They require reapplication (typically annually for regular-use furniture) and the wood can be spot-repaired without visible patches. Wax finishes sit on the surface, provide a gentle sheen, and require more frequent reapplication (twice yearly) but are the easiest to maintain -- a new coat of wax covers most surface scratches. Lacquer and varnish finishes are harder and more water-resistant but cannot be repaired without stripping the entire piece.

The Annual Wood Care Session

Once per year, ideally in autumn when central heating begins drying the interior air: clean all oiled or waxed wood furniture with a barely damp cloth followed by a dry cloth. Apply a thin coat of appropriate oil or wax following the grain direction. Buff with a lint-free cloth after 15-20 minutes. For oiled furniture: a second coat after 24 hours if the wood absorbed the first coat completely. This annual session takes 2-3 hours and adds years to the furniture's condition.

The Humidity Problem in Sarajevo Winters

Central heating without humidification produces interior relative humidity as low as 25-30% in Sarajevo winters. At this level, solid wood loses moisture rapidly, leading to shrinkage, cracking along the grain, and joint loosening. The solution: maintain interior humidity above 45% using a room humidifier. This protects not only the furniture but also wooden floors, doors, and musical instruments (particularly relevant for the guitar in the Growth tab). A hygrometer (10-20 BAM from dm or online) monitors the humidity level.

Upholstery Care

  • Fabric upholstery: Vacuum with the upholstery attachment weekly -- this prevents the surface accumulation of dust and debris that gradually degrades the fabric. Treat stains immediately -- blot, never rub. A stain that has dried into the fibres requires far more effort (or professional cleaning) than the same stain addressed while wet. For general freshening: bicarbonate of soda left on the surface for 30 minutes then vacuumed removes odours that accumulate without a visible source.
  • Leather upholstery: Leather requires a different maintenance approach from fabric. Clean with a leather-specific cleaner (not all-purpose surface spray, which strips the natural oils). Apply leather conditioner twice yearly to prevent drying and cracking. The same low humidity that damages wooden furniture damages leather -- both require humidification in winter.
  • The rotation practice: Sofa cushions should be rotated and flipped quarterly to ensure even wear. Unrotated cushions develop permanent compression in the areas of heaviest use (where you habitually sit) while the rest of the cushion remains unused. Regular rotation distributes the wear evenly, doubling the cushion's usable life.
Furniture maintained correctly lasts for generations. Furniture neglected lasts for years. The difference in time investment between these two outcomes is measured in hours per year -- not weeks or months. The heirloom test is straightforward: would a craftsman who made this piece 50 years from now be proud or ashamed of its condition?-- Von Lycaon
Owning an apartment means the problems that arise are yours to solve — not a landlord's to ignore for three weeks. A man who understands his own home does not panic when something fails. He assesses, acts, and restores order. The following is everything you need to maintain, repair, and improve an owned apartment in Sarajevo — organised by frequency, urgency, and category.— Von Lycaon

The Apartment Owner's Mindset

Most apartment problems are small problems that were ignored until they became large ones. A dripping tap ignored for a month produces water damage. A small crack in grout ignored for a season produces mould. The maintenance calendar exists to eliminate the class of problem that grows in the dark while attention is elsewhere. Inspect deliberately, act promptly, and the apartment remains in the condition it was purchased in — or better.

The Tool Kit — Minimum Viable

Every owned apartment requires: hammer, flathead and Phillips screwdrivers (or a multi-bit set), adjustable wrench, pliers, measuring tape, spirit level, drill with basic bit set, utility knife, and a headlamp. Available at Bauhaus Sarajevo (Ilidža) or Lesnina. Total investment under 150 BAM. The cost of calling a majstor for tasks these tools handle is multiples of that per visit.

The Consumables Stock

Keep on hand: white wall filler (glet masa), sandpaper (various grits), WD-40, superglue, duct tape, electrical tape, PTFE plumber's tape, rawl plugs (tipli) in assorted sizes, wood screws and wall screws mixed, and a tube of silicone sealant (clear and white). These solve 80% of minor apartment issues without a trip to the shop.

When To Call A Majstor

Anything involving the main electrical panel, load-bearing walls, gas lines, or the building's shared plumbing stack. These are not pride issues — they are legal and safety issues. Everything else is learnable. The line is: if getting it wrong could injure someone or damage a neighbour's apartment, call a professional.

Sarajevo Suppliers

Bauhaus (Ilidža) — comprehensive, good quality. Lesnina — furniture and fittings. Interex — building materials. Local hardware shops (željezarije) throughout the city for small consumables — faster than driving to Ilidža for a box of screws. For appliance parts: specialist shops on Obala Kulina Bana and surrounding streets.

Monthly Maintenance

  • Test smoke and CO detectors. Press the test button. If it does not beep, replace the battery. A smoke detector with a dead battery is decoration. Sarajevo apartments with gas heating require a CO detector — carbon monoxide is colourless and odourless. If you do not have one, acquire one before the next heating season.
  • Check under sinks for moisture. Open the cabinet under the kitchen and bathroom sinks. Look for dampness, discolouration, or smell. A slow drip under a sink goes unnoticed for months in a closed cabinet and produces significant water damage and mould before it becomes visible elsewhere.
  • Clean extractor fan filters. Kitchen extractor fan filters accumulate grease rapidly. Remove and wash in hot soapy water monthly. A blocked filter is a fire risk and a noise problem. Most filters unclip without tools.
  • Run all drains with hot water. Pour boiling water down kitchen and bathroom drains monthly. Prevents grease and soap accumulation before it becomes a blockage. The monthly kettle pour costs nothing and eliminates most drain call-outs.
  • Check window seals. Run a finger along the rubber seal of each window. Cracked or compressed seals allow cold air infiltration and condensation, which in Sarajevo's winters produces significant heat loss and potential mould at the frame. Replace seals as needed — available at any željezarija, self-adhesive, ten minutes per window.

Seasonal Maintenance

Before Winter (September — October)

  • Bleed radiators. Turn the heating on. When radiators are warm, use a radiator key (available at any željezarija for 2 BAM) to open the bleed valve on each radiator until water flows steadily with no air bubbles. Air in the system produces cold spots in radiators and reduced efficiency. This takes five minutes per radiator.
  • Check boiler pressure. The pressure gauge on your boiler should read between 1.0 and 1.5 bar when cold. If below 1.0, top up via the filling loop (usually a small valve beneath the boiler — consult your boiler manual). If above 2.5 bar, call a heating engineer.
  • Inspect balcony drainage. Clear leaves and debris from balcony drain points before the first heavy rain. A blocked balcony drain in winter produces ice and water infiltration at the door threshold.
  • Seal any gaps around window and door frames with fresh silicone or expanding foam. Sarajevo winters are cold enough that unsealed gaps are measurable on the heating bill.

Before Summer (April — May)

  • Service air conditioning units. Clean or replace filters before first summer use. A dirty AC filter reduces efficiency by 15–25% and circulates dust. Most split-unit filters slide out and wash under a tap — allow to dry completely before reinserting.
  • Check for winter damage. Inspect external walls visible from the balcony for new cracks or spalling. Sarajevo's freeze-thaw cycle over winter opens small cracks. Document anything new and address before moisture enters.
  • Test all window insect screens. Repair or replace before mosquito season. Sarajevo summers produce significant mosquito activity, particularly near the Miljacka and Dobrinja channels.

Common Repairs — Step by Step

Filling a Wall Hole (Rawl Plug Size)
15 min · Tools: filling knife, sandpaper, paint
  • Clean the hole — remove any loose material. If a rawl plug remains, either push it fully in or remove it entirely.
  • Apply glet masa (wall filler) with a filling knife. Overfill slightly — it shrinks as it dries.
  • Allow to dry fully — minimum 2 hours, ideally overnight.
  • Sand flush with the surrounding wall using 120-grit sandpaper.
  • Apply a second thin coat if the first has sunk below surface level. Sand again when dry.
  • Paint to match. For Sarajevo apartments with magnolia or off-white walls — Chromos or Jupol paint from Bauhaus matched to the existing shade. A small roller produces a more even finish than a brush on a patched area.
Unblocking a Drain
10 min · Tools: plunger or drain snake
  • First attempt: Boiling water directly down the drain. Resolves most grease-based blockages without tools.
  • Second attempt: Baking soda (half a cup) followed immediately by white vinegar (half a cup). Cover the drain, wait 15 minutes, flush with boiling water. The chemical reaction breaks down organic matter.
  • Third attempt: Plunger. Cover the overflow opening with a wet cloth first (creates proper suction). Ten firm plunges. The blockage will either clear or move to a point accessible by the next step.
  • Fourth attempt: Remove the drain cover, use a drain snake or stiff wire to physically break up and extract the blockage. Hair is the most common cause in bathroom drains — this step is reliable.
  • If none of these work: The blockage is in the stack pipe behind the wall. Call a plumber (vodoinstalater). Do not use chemical drain cleaners repeatedly — they corrode pipes over time and are unnecessary if the mechanical methods above are applied correctly.
Resealing a Bath or Shower
45 min + 24hr cure · Tools: utility knife, silicone gun, masking tape
  • Remove all old silicone using a utility knife and silicone remover spray (available at Bauhaus). All old material must be removed — new silicone applied over old silicone fails within months.
  • Clean the joint thoroughly and allow to dry completely. Any moisture prevents adhesion.
  • Apply masking tape to both surfaces 3–4mm from the joint. This produces a clean line and is the difference between a professional-looking result and an amateur one.
  • Apply silicone in one continuous bead along the joint without stopping. A consistent bead requires a consistent gun pressure — practise the motion before touching the bath.
  • Smooth with a wet finger or silicone tool in one continuous pass. Do not go back over it — silicone that has been touched twice loses its smooth surface.
  • Remove masking tape immediately before the silicone skins over — pull at 45 degrees away from the joint.
  • Do not use the bath or shower for 24 hours minimum.

Appliance Basics

  • Washing machine — clean the filter monthly. The pump filter (usually behind a small panel at the bottom front of the machine) catches coins, lint, and debris. A blocked filter causes the machine to drain poorly and eventually fail. Unscrew, clear debris, replace. Have a towel ready — water will spill.
  • Refrigerator — clean condenser coils annually. The coils at the back or underneath the fridge accumulate dust and reduce efficiency. Vacuum or brush clean. A refrigerator working harder than necessary due to dusty coils increases electricity consumption and shortens the compressor lifespan.
  • Descale the kettle quarterly. Fill with equal parts water and white vinegar. Boil. Leave for 30 minutes. Rinse thoroughly. Sarajevo water is moderately hard — limescale accumulation is real and reduces heating efficiency. The vinegar method requires no specialist product.
  • Dishwasher — clean the filter weekly. The filter basket at the bottom of the dishwasher requires weekly removal and rinsing. A blocked dishwasher filter produces poorly cleaned dishes and eventual pump failure. Takes forty seconds.

Electrical Basics — Safe Knowledge

Rule: Never work on anything connected to the mains. The following covers low-voltage and safe interventions only.
  • Fuse box (razvodna tabla) — know where it is and what each breaker controls. Label each breaker if they are not already labelled. When a circuit trips, identify what caused the overload (too many appliances on one circuit is the most common cause), remove the excess load, then reset the breaker by switching it fully off and then on. A breaker that trips repeatedly has a fault on that circuit — call an electrician.
  • Replacing a light switch or socket faceplate. Turn off the circuit at the fuse box. Confirm it is dead with a non-contact voltage tester (under 15 BAM at any željezarija — own one). Unscrew the faceplate, photograph the wiring before disconnecting anything, replace the faceplate, reconnect identically to the photograph. Turn the circuit back on. This is the limit of safe DIY electrical work.
  • LED bulb replacement. Check the fitting type (E27 screw, E14 small screw, GU10 twist) before purchasing. LED equivalents for every fitting type are available at dm, Konzum, and Bauhaus. Always buy warm white (2700–3000K) for living spaces — cool white (5000K+) is for workshops and offices, not homes.
The apartment is not merely shelter. It is the physical environment in which everything in this Codex is executed — the sleep, the cooking, the recovery, the evenings with your wife. Maintaining it is not a domestic obligation. It is the maintenance of the conditions that make everything else possible.— Von Lycaon
A smart home is not a gadget collection — it is a set of systems that either genuinely reduce daily friction or introduce new complexity in exchange for minor convenience. The correct approach is selective: identify the specific friction points in daily home life and address exactly those, rather than purchasing every connected device available. Three well-chosen smart home additions are worth more than fifteen poorly integrated ones.— Von Lycaon

The Sarajevo Reality

Smart home devices are available in BiH primarily through online import (Amazon.de, eMag.rs) and dm's growing smart home section. The Bosnian internet infrastructure is reliable in Sarajevo's urban core — fibre broadband through BH Telecom or Telemach provides the bandwidth required for smart home devices without limitation. The primary consideration is which devices solve real problems rather than which devices exist.

The Three High-Return Categories

Lighting: Smart bulbs or switches that allow voice or app control, dimming, and colour temperature adjustment. The evening wind-down lighting protocol from the pre-sleep routine benefits significantly from automated dimming on a schedule. Climate: Smart thermostats or heating control for Sarajevo's cold winters. Automated schedules that reduce heating when away and restore it before return saves both energy and the discomfort of a cold apartment arrival. Security: A video doorbell and a smart lock if the building allows it. Both visible deterrents and practical convenience.

The Platform Decision

Choose one ecosystem and stay in it: Google Home (works with Android seamlessly, widely supported), Amazon Alexa (largest device compatibility), or Apple HomeKit (iOS only, best privacy). For an Android user already in the Google ecosystem — Google Home is the correct platform. The decision matters because switching ecosystems later is expensive and inconvenient.

Smart Lighting — The Best Starting Point

Philips Hue (premium, most reliable) or IKEA Tradfri (excellent value, widely compatible). Smart bulbs in the living room and bedroom, configured to dim automatically at 21:30 as part of the evening routine already described in the pre-sleep protocol. The transition from bright overhead to warm dim at a set time is one of the most effective sleep hygiene automations available and requires zero daily effort once configured.

Privacy Considerations

Every smart home device is a network-connected microphone or camera in your home. Choose devices from manufacturers with documented privacy policies and no history of data misuse. Avoid generic Chinese smart home devices with no clear privacy documentation — the price point reflects, in part, a business model that may involve data collection. Philips (Signify), IKEA, and Google Nest are the recommended manufacturers for privacy-conscious purchases.

Practical Setup — The Minimum Useful Configuration

Phase 1 — Lighting (Start Here)

  • Install 4–6 smart bulbs in the living room and bedroom (Philips Hue White Ambiance or IKEA Tradfri dimmable). Connect to Google Home.
  • Create two automations: "Evening" (21:30 — living room dims to 30%, warm white 2700K) and "Sleep" (22:45 — all lights off). These replace the manual actions already in the pre-sleep routine.
  • Create a "Morning" scene (07:00 — gradual brightening over 20 minutes, simulating sunrise). The gradual light increase assists natural cortisol awakening response — more pleasant than an alarm in the dark.

Phase 2 — Convenience

  • Smart plug (Google Home compatible): A single smart plug on the coffee machine allows voice or app activation. "Hey Google, turn on the coffee machine" while still in bed. Available at dm for 25–40 BAM. Small convenience; daily return.
  • Robot vacuum: The Xiaomi/Roborock entry-level models (available on eMag.rs, 200–400 BAM) schedule daily cleaning runs when away from the apartment. The time return — 2–3 hours of floor cleaning weekly — is one of the clearest ROI cases in smart home technology.
The home that works for you rather than requiring you to work for it returns time and reduces friction every day. Not through novelty — through the cumulative elimination of the small repetitive tasks that, added together, consume more of a life than they should.— Von Lycaon

Automation Philosophy

The value of a smart home automation is proportional to the frequency of the action it replaces. An automation that runs once per year saves no time. An automation that runs every evening for five years saves approximately 30 hours over that period -- equivalent to a full working week -- while also removing a small daily decision from your cognitive load. Prioritise automations by frequency of use multiplied by the friction they eliminate.

The Full Automation Stack

Time-Based Automations

Morning (07:00): Bedroom lights gradually brightens over 20 minutes (0-100%, 2700K). The gradual light increase produces a physiologically gentler wake than sudden brightness or an alarm tone, assisting the natural cortisol rise that the sleep science recommends. Evening (21:00): Living room lights dim to 30%, shift to warm white (2700K). Kitchen lights remain at 60% for meal preparation. Night (22:30): All lights shift to maximum warm, minimum brightness for the last wind-down period. Sleep (23:00 or whenever you trigger it): All lights off except a dim hallway guide light.

Presence-Based Automations

The most powerful category once properly configured. Leaving home: All lights off, smart plugs off (TV, coffee machine, any devices left on standby). Air purifier switches to low mode. Arriving home: Hallway light on at 50%, living room at 40% warm white -- a welcoming but not harsh arrival. Coffee machine power on if arriving in the morning window. These require the phone's location services and a geofence in the Google Home app -- a circular boundary around the home that triggers the automation when crossed.

Scene-Based Automations

Scenes: named combinations of light settings triggered by voice or tap. "Reading": Beside lamp at 100%, warm white; all other lights off or very dim. "Film": All lights off except a very dim backlight (behind the television -- reduces eye strain in a dark room). "Dinner": Dining area at 70%, warm white; kitchen at 40%. "Focus": Desk lamp at 100%, neutral white; all other lights off. Scenes eliminate the multi-step adjustment process and ensure the same configuration every time.

Robot Vacuum Integration

The Xiaomi/Roborock entry models support Google Home integration and zone cleaning (cleaning specific rooms on schedule rather than the whole apartment). The optimal configuration for a Sarajevo working-from-home schedule:

  • Bedroom: Cleans while you are at the gym or outside (09:30 on gym days). Never scheduled during sleep hours or morning routine hours.
  • Living room and hallway: Cleans at 11:00 Monday, Wednesday, Friday -- while you are working and the spaces are unoccupied.
  • Kitchen: After dinner, triggered manually rather than on schedule -- kitchen floor cleaning is most valuable after cooking rather than at a fixed time.
  • The total floor cleaning achieved with zero active time per week is the clearest return-on-investment case for any smart home device. The robot is not a luxury -- it is a labour replacement that pays for itself within the first year in time recovered.
The smart home does not make life more complex. It makes the home a system that serves you rather than requiring you to serve it. The correctly configured automation stack takes 2 hours to set up and then operates without further attention for years. Two hours invested once, returning dividends every day thereafter.-- Von Lycaon
Most formal education ends and most people stop learning deliberately. They accumulate experience but not knowledge — which is a different thing. Experience without structured reflection produces habit. Knowledge without experience produces theory. The man who pursues both deliberately, outside the structure of any institution, builds something that compounds in a way neither alone produces. This tab is the system for doing that.— Von Lycaon

The Learning Mindset

Self-directed learning fails for one reason almost universally: the absence of a system. Most people read a book, find it interesting, and retain perhaps 10% of it six months later. They watch a tutorial, understand it in the moment, and cannot reproduce it a week later. The information entered and exited without leaving a structure behind. Learning without retention is not learning — it is entertainment with intellectual packaging.

The Feynman Technique

The most reliable learning method known: after encountering any new concept, close the book and explain it out loud as if teaching it to someone who has never heard of it. Use simple language. Where you stumble, where the explanation becomes vague — that is where understanding actually ends and familiarity begins. Return to the source material only for those specific gaps. Repeat until the explanation is clean. You now own the concept rather than recognising it.

Spaced Repetition

The brain's memory consolidation follows a predictable curve — information is most efficiently retained when reviewed at increasing intervals: 1 day, 3 days, 7 days, 14 days, 30 days. Anki (free, available on Android) implements this algorithmically. Any fact, concept, vocabulary word, or formula entered as a flashcard will be surfaced at the optimal review interval automatically. Used consistently, it makes forgetting structurally difficult.

The Obsidian Knowledge System

Already in the Codex — but its learning application is specific. Every book read, every course completed, every concept learned gets a note. Not a summary — a note in your own words, connected to other notes with links. Over time, the graph view reveals the structure of your actual knowledge: where it is dense, where it is sparse, and which ideas connect across domains in ways that produce original insight.

Learning in Layers

Any subject worth learning has three layers: the vocabulary (the terms and definitions), the structure (how the parts relate), and the application (what you can actually do with it). Most people learn the vocabulary and mistake it for the structure. Begin any new subject by building a map of its structure before memorising its vocabulary. The map tells you where the vocabulary belongs.

The Learning Schedule

Consistency of twenty minutes daily produces more than two hours weekly in three sessions. The daily habit builds the context that makes each session immediately productive. The weekly session requires rebuilding context each time before learning begins.

Daily Learning Block — 20–30 Minutes

  • Morning (preferred): The prefrontal cortex — the part responsible for analytical learning and pattern recognition — is at peak function in the first 2–4 hours after waking. If a 20-minute learning block can be inserted before the first work demand, it uses the brain's highest-quality window. This is not always possible. When it is, use it.
  • Commute alternative: The tram commute described in the Music tab is 20–30 minutes of potential learning time. Audiobooks and podcasts at 1.3x speed. An Anki deck reviewed on the phone. The Kindle app. Not every commute needs to be a learning session — but the option exists and requires no additional time allocation.
  • Pre-sleep is the wrong time for new learning. The brain consolidates the day's learning during sleep. Introducing new complex information immediately before sleep competes with consolidation of what was already learned that day. The pre-sleep window belongs to light reading, the journal, and the wind-down routine.

The Weekly Learning Review — Sunday, 30 Minutes

  • Review the week's Obsidian notes. What was learned? What needs a second pass via the Feynman technique?
  • Process any Anki cards that accumulated during the week if not reviewed daily.
  • Identify next week's learning focus. One subject, one resource. Not three subjects and six resources — one and one. The person who studies one thing properly for a month knows that thing. The person who samples six things for a month knows none of them.

Subject Prioritisation Framework

Not all learning returns equal value. The following framework prioritises subjects by their compounding effect on the rest of life — subjects whose mastery makes other areas easier rather than adding isolated knowledge.

📊 Tier 1 — High Compounding

These subjects improve everything else: clear writing (improves communication across every context), basic statistics and probability (improves every decision involving uncertainty), personal finance mechanics (improves every financial decision), basic psychology and persuasion (improves every human interaction), and a second language (opens entire new domains of cultural and professional access). Any time invested here returns across the entire life, not just in the domain of study.

🔧 Tier 2 — Direct Application

Skills with immediate and specific return: the contents of this Codex — cooking, home maintenance, first aid, fitness — plus anything directly relevant to your IT analyst role (new tools, emerging technologies, project management methodology). Learn these when the application is near-term and concrete.

🎨 Tier 3 — Enrichment

History, art, literature, music theory, philosophy. These do not produce immediate practical return but build the cultural and intellectual context that makes a person genuinely interesting, capable of depth in conversation, and better at abstract thinking over time. One Tier 3 subject running alongside a Tier 1 or 2 subject at all times is the correct balance.

Resources — Starting Points

📱 Anki

Free on Android. The single most evidence-supported learning tool for retention. Start with 10 new cards per day maximum — the review burden accumulates quickly if new cards are added faster than they can be reviewed. Available subjects: pre-made decks for languages, medicine, history, geography. Custom decks for anything you are currently studying.

🎓 Coursera / edX

University-level courses from institutions including Yale, MIT, and London Business School. Accessible from Bosnia without restriction. Many courses free to audit — pay only if a certificate is required. Recommended starting points: "Learning How to Learn" (Barbara Oakley — the most-enrolled online course in history, 20 hours, directly applicable to everything in this tab), "Financial Markets" (Yale), any Python or data analytics course relevant to IT analysis.

🎙️ Podcasts — Structured Learning

Huberman Lab (neuroscience and performance — directly complements the Body & Mind tabs), Lex Fridman (long-form conversations with scientists and thinkers), Revisionist History (Malcolm Gladwell — historical case studies in non-obvious thinking), The Knowledge Project (Shane Parrish — mental models and decision-making). All free, all available in Sarajevo without restriction.

📺 YouTube Channels

3Blue1Brown (mathematics made visual — genuinely changes how mathematical thinking feels), Kurzgesagt (science and systems thinking), CrashCourse (structured introductions to any academic subject), Veritasium (physics and scientific thinking). These are the channels where an hour spent is genuinely educational rather than merely interesting. Cross-reference with the YouTube tab in the Codex.

The Deep Work Session

Shallow learning — reading while distracted, watching videos while the phone is nearby — produces the feeling of learning without much of the substance. Deep learning requires the same conditions as deep work: the phone removed from the room, a single focused session of 45–90 minutes with one resource and no context switching.

  • Schedule one deep learning session per week of 60–90 minutes on a topic you are actively studying. This session uses the Feynman technique at the end — close all resources, explain what was learned in the Obsidian note, identify the gaps.
  • The 90-minute limit is physiological. Ultradian rhythms produce a natural alertness cycle of approximately 90 minutes. After this window, a 10–20 minute break (not phone scrolling — a walk, water, movement) restores the cycle. Two 90-minute sessions with a proper break between them produce more than three hours of continuous attempted focus.
  • Active recall over passive review. Re-reading notes is the least effective review method available. Closing notes and attempting to recall the information — even imperfectly — produces retention at three to four times the rate of passive re-reading. This is uncomfortable precisely because it reveals what is not yet known. That discomfort is the mechanism of learning.
The educated man and the self-educated man differ in one thing: the self-educated man chose what to learn and why. The motivation is intrinsic and the relevance is personal. These two qualities produce a depth of engagement with the material that institutional education rarely matches. The library is open to anyone who enters it deliberately.— Von Lycaon

The Forgetting Curve and Why Most Learning Fails

Hermann Ebbinghaus's 19th-century research established the forgetting curve: without review, approximately 50% of newly learned information is forgotten within 24 hours, and 90% within a week. This is not a failing of memory — it is the brain's efficiency mechanism, purging information that has not been flagged as worth retaining. The flagging mechanism is retrieval — information that is retrieved repeatedly is retained; information that is not retrieved is forgotten. This is why passive review (re-reading notes, watching lectures again) produces minimal retention. Active retrieval is the mechanism.

  • The testing effect in practice: After reading any chapter, close the book and write down everything you remember. The effort of retrieval — including the frustration of not being able to remember — produces dramatically better long-term retention than re-reading the chapter. This feels less productive than re-reading because re-reading produces a feeling of fluency that retrieval does not. That feeling of fluency is an illusion; the retention from retrieval practice is real.
  • Interleaving: Rather than studying one topic until mastery before moving to the next (blocked practice), interleaving different topics within a single study session produces better long-term retention and transfer, though it feels harder during the session. The difficulty is the mechanism — the brain must work harder to retrieve the correct knowledge for each problem when topics are mixed, and this effort strengthens the memory traces.
  • Elaborative interrogation: After encountering any fact, ask "why is this true?" and "how does this connect to what I already know?" The effort of answering these questions connects the new information to existing knowledge networks, creating multiple retrieval pathways. A fact with one retrieval pathway is fragile. A fact connected to five existing concepts through five "why" answers is robust.
Memory is not a fixed capacity. It is a skill — and like every skill in this Codex, it responds to correct training. The techniques here are not tricks for memorising shopping lists. They are serious cognitive tools used by memory champions, medical students, lawyers, and anyone whose work requires reliable recall of large volumes of structured information. They work because they align with how the brain actually encodes and retrieves information, rather than working against it.— Von Lycaon

How Memory Actually Works

Encoding vs Retrieval

Memory has two distinct problems: encoding (getting information in) and retrieval (getting it out). Most people focus entirely on encoding — reading, re-reading, highlighting. Research consistently shows that retrieval practice (actively recalling information without looking at it) is 3-4x more effective for long-term retention than an equivalent time spent re-reading. The struggle of retrieval is the mechanism of learning, not a sign that learning has failed.

Associations, Not Facts

The brain does not store isolated facts. It stores networks of associations — a new piece of information is retained in proportion to how many connections it has to existing knowledge. This is why elaborative encoding works: connecting new information to existing knowledge, images, emotions, or locations creates more retrieval pathways. A fact connected to nothing is forgotten. A fact connected to five things is remembered.

Spaced Repetition

Already in the Learning tab. Worth a deeper note here: the spacing effect is the most robustly replicated finding in memory research. Reviewing information at increasing intervals (1 day, 3 days, 7 days, 14 days, 30 days) exploits the brain's consolidation process. Anki implements this automatically. The key insight: reviewing information just before you would have forgotten it produces a stronger memory trace than reviewing when you remember it well.

The Memory Palace (Method of Loci)

The most powerful memory technique known, used since ancient Greece. The principle: human spatial memory — the ability to remember the layout of familiar locations — is extraordinarily robust. Information attached to spatial locations in a familiar environment is retrieved through mental navigation rather than through linear recall.

Building Your First Memory Palace
15 min setup · Use any familiar location
  • Choose a location you know extremely well: Your apartment is ideal. You know every room, every surface, every object. The more familiar, the stronger the spatial anchors.
  • Define a route: A consistent path through the location — front door, hallway, living room, kitchen, bathroom, bedroom. The route must always be followed in the same direction. You will mentally walk this route each time you use the palace.
  • Create loci (stations): Along the route, identify 10-20 specific locations: the front door handle, the coat rack, the bookshelf, the sofa, the coffee table, the kitchen counter, the stove, the sink, the bathroom mirror, the bed. Each locus is a slot for one memory item.
  • Place items at loci with vivid imagery: The information to be remembered is converted into a vivid, unusual, or emotionally engaging mental image and placed at the locus. If memorising the word "revolution" — imagine a giant spinning wheel crushing your coat rack. The more bizarre and specific the image, the more reliably it is retrieved. Bland images are forgotten; unusual ones are not.
  • Retrieve by walking the route: To recall the list, mentally walk your apartment in the defined order and observe what is at each locus. The spatial memory retrieves the image; the image contains the information.

The Major System — Numbers as Images

A system for converting numbers into consonant sounds, then into words and images. Essential for memorising phone numbers, dates, statistics, and any numerical information.

  • The code: 0=S/Z, 1=T/D, 2=N, 3=M, 4=R, 5=L, 6=J/SH, 7=K/G, 8=F/V, 9=B/P
  • Example: The number 42 = R (4) + N (2) = "RaN" or "RaiN" or "RuN." The number 1492 = T/D (1) + R (4) + B/P (9) + N (2) = "TRaBoN" or "TuRBiNe." The year 1492 (Columbus) becomes a mental image of a turbine.
  • Application: Once fluent, any number becomes an image, which can be placed in a memory palace or linked to an associated fact. Phone numbers, historical dates, and numerical sequences all become memorable through this system.
  • Learning curve: The phonetic code requires 1-2 weeks of daily practice to become automatic. After that, numerical memorisation becomes qualitatively easier. Worth the investment for anyone who works with data, finance, or history.

Practical Applications

  • Names at social events: The most immediately useful application. On hearing a name, immediately create a vivid image connected to a distinctive feature of the person's face. "Mark" — a dark mark on the forehead. "Petra" — a stone (petra = stone) balanced on the nose. Retrieve by looking at the face and finding the image. This technique eliminates the social embarrassment of forgetting names within minutes of introduction.
  • Presentations and speeches: The memory palace replaces the need for notes. Structure the presentation into 8-10 key points, place each in the palace, and navigate the palace while speaking. The result: a presentation delivered from memory that appears entirely natural because the retrieval is spatial rather than linear memorisation.
  • Language learning: Vocabulary retention is dramatically improved by the combination of spaced repetition (Anki) and vivid encoding. For each new word, create a memorable image that connects the sound of the word to its meaning before entering it into Anki.
Memory is not intelligence. But a man who cannot remember what he has learned cannot compound it. The compound interest of knowledge depends on retention. These techniques are not shortcuts to understanding — they are the infrastructure on which understanding accumulates.— Von Lycaon
Speed reading claims range from the reasonable (most people read slower than their comprehension requires) to the fraudulent (reading 10,000 words per minute with full comprehension). This tab takes the evidence seriously. The honest answer: most people can double their reading speed with modest practice while maintaining or improving comprehension. The techniques that claim to multiply it ten times are destroying comprehension in the process. Here is what actually works.— Von Lycaon

The Honest Picture

Average adult reading speed: 200-250 words per minute with reasonable comprehension. Trained readers: 350-500 words per minute. Skilled speed readers: 600-800 words per minute. Claims above 1,000 words per minute with full comprehension are not supported by evidence — what people are doing at those speeds is skimming, which has a different and legitimate use case but is not "reading."

The primary bottleneck for most readers is not the eye's ability to process text — it is subvocalisation (the internal voice that "sounds out" every word) and excessive fixation (the eye pausing too long on each word). Both are addressable.

The Techniques That Work

1. Reduce Subvocalisation

  • Subvocalisation — mentally "saying" each word as you read — is the primary speed limiter. Speech operates at 130-180 words per minute; reading is artificially constrained to this speed by subvocalisation in most readers.
  • Technique: Read while counting (1, 2, 3...) or humming to occupy the verbal processing channel. This forces the visual cortex to process text without the verbal system. Initially comprehension drops; with practice it recovers as visual word recognition strengthens.
  • The limit: Some subvocalisation is beneficial for complex, dense, or poetic text — it is appropriate to slow down and vocalise mentally for material that rewards that attention. Speed reading techniques are not universal — they are appropriate for some material and wrong for others.

2. Expand the Visual Span

  • The eye reads in fixations — brief pauses where text is processed. Most slow readers fixate on individual words. Trained readers fixate on word groups (3-4 words per fixation), covering the same text in fewer eye movements.
  • Technique: Use a pointer (finger or pen) moving at a target speed beneath the line. The eye follows the pointer, which forces wider fixations than the default single-word pattern. Start at a speed that feels slightly uncomfortable — just beyond the current comfortable speed — and practise for 20 minutes daily for 2-3 weeks.

3. Preview Before Reading

  • Reading comprehension improves when the reader has a structural map before encountering the details. For non-fiction: read the table of contents, chapter headings, and summary or conclusion first. This creates the framework into which the detail is placed — comprehension and retention both improve.
  • This is not the same as skimming the whole book and calling it read. It is preparation that makes the actual reading more efficient.

4. Read Actively, Not Passively

  • Passive reading — eyes moving over text without engagement — produces very low retention regardless of speed. Active reading — asking questions of the text, predicting what comes next, disagreeing with arguments, connecting to existing knowledge — produces dramatically higher retention and allows faster processing because the mind is engaged rather than merely scanning.
  • The pen-in-hand method from the Reading List tab is active reading. Marking, annotating, and questioning while reading is not slower reading — for most text it is actually faster because the engaged mind processes more efficiently.

What to Speed Read and What Not To

  • Appropriate for speed reading: Business books (most contain one good idea expanded to fill a commercial book length), reports and documents, articles seeking specific information, email and professional correspondence.
  • Not appropriate for speed reading: Complex non-fiction requiring understanding of building arguments (read at the speed the argument requires). Fiction (reading fast through narrative prose is missing the point — prose style is part of what is being read). Poetry (never). Philosophy (never — the slow movement through a philosophical argument is the engagement with it).
  • The 30% rule: Aim to read non-essential non-fiction 30% faster than current comfortable speed with maintained comprehension. This is achievable. The 300% improvement claims are not.

Tools

  • Spreeder (spreeder.com): Free RSVP (Rapid Serial Visual Presentation) tool — displays words one at a time at a configurable speed. Paste any text, set the speed, and practise. Useful for building comfort with faster word processing rates.
  • Calibre (e-book management software): Allows stripping non-fiction e-books to their core content and reading with the pointer technique on any device. Free, open-source.
  • The physical pointer: A finger or pen moving at target speed beneath the line remains the most effective and free technique available. Use it for 15 minutes daily for 3 weeks before evaluating results.
The goal is not to read faster. The goal is to read more, retain more, and choose the correct reading speed for what the material deserves. Some books deserve to be read slowly. Some deserve to be read once at speed and never again. The skill is knowing which is which, and having the range to do both.— Von Lycaon
A man who reads widely thinks in more than one register. He has access to the thinking of people who lived in different centuries, different cultures, and different circumstances — and can apply their frameworks to his own problems without having to derive every principle from first-hand experience alone. The reading list is not a status display. It is a working tool.— Von Lycaon

How to Read — Before What to Read

Most people finish a book and retain perhaps 10% of it after six months. The problem is almost never the book — it is the absence of a reading method that produces retention. Reading for entertainment requires no method. Reading to genuinely acquire a framework, an idea, or a capability requires one.

  • Read with a pen. Underline what surprises you, what challenges your existing thinking, what seems directly applicable. Not what seems important in the abstract — what is specifically relevant to your current life. The act of underlining is the first encoding. The review of underlines later is the second.
  • Write the main idea of each chapter in one sentence in the margin before moving to the next. If you cannot produce one sentence, you did not understand the chapter — which is useful information to have before proceeding.
  • After finishing, write a one-page note in Obsidian: what the book argued, what you found most useful, what you disagreed with, and one thing you will do differently as a result. A book that produces no changed behaviour was entertainment. This is not a criticism — entertainment has value. But know which category the book belonged to.
  • Reread selectively. Some books are worth reading once. Some are worth reading every few years. The books on this list marked as foundational are the ones worth revisiting — not because you will discover new information, but because you will discover new relevance as your life changes.

Philosophy & The Examined Life

📖 Meditations — Marcus Aurelius

The private journal of a Roman emperor — never intended for publication. Stoic philosophy applied to daily life by a man who simultaneously held absolute power and tried to remain humble. Every page is a reminder that the internal life is the only one entirely within control. Read slowly, one section per day. The Penguin Classics translation by Martin Hammond is clean and readable. Directly complements the Philosophy tab of this Codex.

📖 Man's Search for Meaning — Viktor Frankl

A psychiatrist's account of surviving Auschwitz and the psychological framework he derived from the experience: that meaning, not pleasure or power, is the primary human motivator, and that meaning can be found in any circumstances. One of the most important books written in the 20th century. 200 pages. Read it in one sitting if possible — it is designed to be experienced as a whole.

📖 Letters from a Stoic — Seneca

Letters written to a friend over years, covering friendship, death, time, wealth, and how to live. More accessible than Marcus Aurelius and often more direct. The letter on time — "Ita fac, mi Lucili: vindica te tibi" (Claim yourself for yourself) — is worth reading before anything else in the book. Available free in the public domain.

📖 The Daily Stoic — Ryan Holiday

366 short Stoic readings with commentary — one per day. Designed exactly for the 5-minute morning reading slot in the Daily Upkeep tab. Not a replacement for primary Stoic texts but an excellent entry point and daily reinforcement mechanism. Practical rather than academic.

Psychology & Human Behaviour

📖 Thinking, Fast and Slow — Daniel Kahneman

Nobel Prize-winning psychologist on the two systems of human thought: fast (intuitive, automatic, frequently biased) and slow (deliberate, analytical, effortful). Understanding your own cognitive biases is the foundation of better decision-making. The chapters on anchoring, availability bias, and the planning fallacy are immediately applicable to financial, professional, and personal decisions.

📖 Influence — Robert Cialdini

The six principles of persuasion — reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity — with their mechanisms explained and their manipulation tactics identified. Essential reading for anyone in a professional context involving negotiation, client management, or leadership. Also the best defence against being manipulated by these same principles, which operate constantly in commercial and social environments.

📖 The Body Keeps the Score — Bessel van der Kolk

How trauma is stored in the body and nervous system rather than solely in conscious memory, and how this manifests in daily life and relationships. Not clinical reading — deeply human reading. Relevant for understanding stress responses, emotional regulation, and the physical basis of mental states covered in the Stress & Fatigue tab.

📖 Atomic Habits — James Clear

The most practical book on behaviour change available. The four-stage habit loop, the 1% improvement compound effect, and the identity-based approach to habit formation. Read alongside the consistency challenges described throughout this Codex — the journaling habit, the workout consistency, the nutrition protocol. The framework explains why they succeed or fail and what to adjust.

Finance & Economics

📖 The Psychology of Money — Morgan Housel

19 short essays on how people think about money — and how they think about it wrongly. More useful than any technical finance book for someone who already has the basics in place. The chapters on reasonable vs rational behaviour, on tail events, and on the highest dividend money can pay (the ability to control your time) are directly relevant to the Finance tab framework.

📖 The Millionaire Next Door — Thomas Stanley

Statistical research on how actual wealthy people live — not how they appear to live. The finding that most genuine long-term wealth is built through consistent frugality and investment rather than high income is useful corrective reading in a culture that conflates visible consumption with financial success. Applicable directly to the three-account system in the Finance tab.

📖 Rich Dad Poor Dad — Robert Kiyosaki

The most widely read personal finance book. Its framework — assets vs liabilities, financial education over job security — is genuinely useful despite the book's many critics. Read it for the framework, not the specific investment advice, which is dated and context-dependent. Best read early, before the Housel and Stanley books which provide more rigorous depth.

Health, Performance & the Body

📖 Why We Sleep — Matthew Walker

The most comprehensive and alarming book on sleep science available. Walker presents the evidence for sleep's role in every aspect of health — immune function, memory consolidation, emotional regulation, cardiovascular health, cancer risk — with the clarity of someone who has spent a career making the case. After reading this, the pre-sleep routine in the Codex feels less like discipline and more like the minimum required to not actively harm yourself. Read before arguing that five hours is sufficient.

📖 Outlive — Peter Attia

A physician's framework for longevity — not lifespan but healthspan. The distinction between Medicine 2.0 (treating disease reactively) and Medicine 3.0 (preventing it proactively through exercise, nutrition, sleep, and emotional health) is the book's central contribution. The sections on Zone 2 cardio, strength training's role in preventing frailty, and the metabolic basis of chronic disease are directly applicable to the workout and nutrition content in this Codex.

📖 The Comfort Crisis — Michael Easter

On the disappearance of voluntary discomfort from modern life and its consequences for physical and psychological resilience. Directly relevant to the Cold Exposure tab. Easter's argument — that seeking occasional difficulty produces capability unavailable to those who avoid it — is the intellectual foundation for every uncomfortable habit in this Codex.

Biography & History

📖 Leonardo da Vinci — Walter Isaacson

A biography of the most polymathic mind in recorded history. More useful than it first appears as a practical text: da Vinci's notebooks, his method of relentless curiosity across every domain, his willingness to leave things unfinished in pursuit of understanding — all of these are directly applicable to the self-education framework in the Learning tab. The lesson is not to be Leonardo. It is to adopt his quality of attention.

📖 Can't Hurt Me — David Goggins

Extreme in its content and approach but the underlying message — that the human capacity for discomfort is dramatically larger than most people ever test — is genuinely useful. The 40% rule (when the mind wants to quit, the body is typically at 40% of its actual capacity) is a framework worth having available during difficult training sessions and difficult professional situations alike.

📖 Sapiens — Yuval Noah Harari

A compressed history of humanity — from cognitive revolution to the present day. Produces a perspective on current civilisation that is difficult to obtain from any other single source. The chapters on the agricultural revolution's real cost, on the role of shared fictions in human cooperation, and on the nature of happiness are the most intellectually disruptive in the book. Read this before forming strong opinions about how society should be organised.

The Reading Rhythm

  • One book at a time. Finishing one book before beginning another produces deeper engagement and better retention than rotating between three simultaneously. Exception: one fiction and one non-fiction can run in parallel — they use different reading modes and do not compete.
  • 30 pages per day finishes most books in 10–14 days. At this rate, 25–30 books per year. Over ten years, 250–300 books — a genuine private education available to anyone who reads 30 pages before sleep.
  • Abandon books without guilt. A book that is not producing value by page 50 is unlikely to do so by page 200. Reading a bad or irrelevant book to completion out of obligation is time that belonged to a better book. The sunk cost fallacy applies to reading as much as to anything else.
  • Physical books for deep reading. Kindle for convenience. The tactile engagement of a physical book — the ability to flip back, to see progress, to write in margins — produces better retention for complex non-fiction. The Kindle is better than not reading. The physical book is better than the Kindle.
Every book worth reading is a conversation with someone who spent years thinking carefully about one thing. To read it attentively is to compress those years into hours. There is no more efficient form of intellectual companionship available at any price.— Von Lycaon
Writing is thinking made visible. The man who writes clearly thinks clearly — the process of constructing a sentence that is both accurate and readable forces a precision of thought that speaking allows to remain approximate. This tab covers creative writing and storytelling as a craft, distinct from the professional writing covered in Social Skills and the journaling in Mind & Rest. This is writing as an art form worth practising for its own sake.— Von Lycaon

Why Writing Matters Beyond the Page

Every skill developed in writing transfers: the narrative structure that makes a story compelling is the same structure that makes a presentation persuasive. The precision required to describe a scene accurately is the precision required to document a technical process clearly. The habit of finding the exact word rather than the approximate one sharpens every other form of communication.

Show, Don't Tell

The foundational principle: "He was angry" tells the reader an emotional state. "He set the glass down with a precision that suggested it was taking considerable restraint not to throw it" shows the state through observable behaviour. The reader experiences anger rather than being informed of it. The principle applies in professional writing equally — "the system performed poorly" tells. "The system processed 40% fewer transactions than the baseline" shows.

The Sentence

Most writing problems exist at the sentence level before they exist at the structural level. A paragraph of weak sentences cannot be saved by strong structure. The test for any sentence: can it be shorter without losing meaning? Can the passive voice be made active? Is every word present because it earns its place? "The meeting was attended by the team" versus "The team attended the meeting." The second is shorter, clearer, and more direct. Always.

Specificity Over Generality

"A car drove past" is forgettable. "A dark green Škoda Octavia, one headlight dimmer than the other, moved through the intersection at exactly the speed limit" is specific and therefore real. Specificity is the mechanism of credibility in fiction and the mechanism of clarity in non-fiction. Vague writing produces vague reading experiences. Specific writing produces specific ones.

The Edit is the Work

First drafts are for getting the ideas down. Editing is where writing becomes good. The ratio in professional writing: roughly one hour of editing for every two hours of drafting. In literary writing, the ratio inverts. The writer who submits first drafts is submitting unfinished work regardless of how fluent the initial generation was. Always read back before sending, submitting, or publishing anything.

Storytelling — The Universal Structure

Every compelling story — regardless of medium, culture, or era — shares a fundamental structure. Understanding it allows both the construction of original stories and the analysis of why existing stories work or fail.

The Story Spine

  • Once upon a time... — Establish the world and the protagonist's normal state.
  • Every day... — Show the established routine that is about to be disrupted.
  • Until one day... — The inciting incident. The event that breaks the routine and forces change.
  • Because of that... — Consequence chains. Each event causes the next. The protagonist is forced to act.
  • Until finally... — The climax. The point of maximum tension where the central conflict resolves.
  • Ever since then... — The new normal. How the protagonist (and the reader) are changed by what occurred.
  • This structure applies to a Pixar film, a Bosnian priča, a professional case study presentation, and a personal anecdote at a dinner party. The medium changes. The structure does not.

The Daily Writing Practice

  • The 200-word daily minimum. Not a journal entry — a constructed piece of writing, however small. A scene, a description of something observed, a short argument, a paragraph of fiction. 200 words is achievable in 10–15 minutes and produces, over a year, 73,000 words — a short novel's worth of practice regardless of whether any individual piece is worth keeping.
  • The reading-writing connection. Writers who read widely write better than those who do not — they have absorbed more sentence structures, more vocabulary, more narrative approaches than their reading-averse peers. The Reading List tab is as much a writing resource as an intellectual one.
  • Write about what you know specifically.
