The Sarajevo setting, the daily textures of your life, the precise quality of light on the Miljacka on a winter morning — these are available to you in a way they are available to almost no one writing in English. Specificity of place and experience is one of the most valuable assets a writer can possess. Do not waste it writing about generic places and generic people.

Resources

  • "On Writing" — Stephen King: Half memoir, half writing manual. The clearest articulation of the working writer's process available in a single volume. Read it regardless of whether genre fiction is of interest — the principles are universal.
  • "Bird by Bird" — Anne Lamott: On the fear and process of writing. The chapter on "shitty first drafts" is the most liberating thing a beginning writer can read.
  • "The Elements of Style" — Strunk & White: 85 pages. The most concentrated writing advice per page of any book in English. Every rule in it is arguable and most are correct. Read annually.
The page is patient. It accepts whatever is placed on it without judgment. The discipline is not in the writing — it is in returning to the page tomorrow and the day after, producing the accumulated body of work that no single session can constitute alone.— Von Lycaon

The Professional Writing Standard

Most professional communication fails not at the level of grammar or vocabulary but at the level of structure — the reader does not know what they are supposed to take away, or must read to the end to discover why the document exists. The Pyramid Principle, developed by Barbara Minto at McKinsey, addresses this directly: lead with the conclusion, then provide the supporting evidence. Every professional document — email, report, presentation — should be structured so that the reader can stop at any point and still have received the most important information.

  • BLUF (Bottom Line Up Front): Military writing standard. The most important information in the first sentence. Context and supporting detail follow. This structure works because readers are busy, attention is the scarcest resource in professional communication, and burying the conclusion at the end of a long email ensures that many recipients will not reach it.
  • The email standard: Subject line that contains the action required (not just the topic). First sentence: the conclusion or request. Following sentences: context required to act on it. No sentence that does not add information. If the email is longer than 150 words, it probably contains information that belongs in a separate document referenced by a shorter email.
  • The writing habit for professionals: One piece of deliberate writing daily — not a report, not a work email, but something written for its own sake. A journal entry, a description of something observed, a short argument about something you think is wrong. The daily practice builds the automaticity that makes professional writing faster and better. The person who writes every day produces better work emails than the person who writes only when required, because the underlying skill is higher.
A second language is the only cognitive tool that simultaneously opens a new culture, improves the brain's executive function, delays cognitive decline, and makes every trip to a country that speaks it a fundamentally different experience. The research on the cognitive benefits of bilingualism is substantial. The practical benefits of a second language for a Bosnian professional with European ambitions are more immediate still. This tab is the framework for acquiring one without a classroom.— Von Lycaon

Which Language — The Decision

The correct language to learn is determined by three factors: utility in your professional context, access to native speakers for practice, and personal motivation strong enough to sustain the effort. A language learned purely for external reasons and without personal pull will stall at intermediate level. A language with genuine personal resonance will be used even on the days when it feels difficult.

English — If Not Already Fluent

The language of international professional communication, technology, and the entire content ecosystem of this Codex. If English is not currently at C1 level (comfortable reading, writing, and speaking in professional contexts), it is the highest-return language investment available by a significant margin. Every other language suggestion below assumes English is already functional.

German

The most useful European language after English for a BiH resident: the largest European economy, the largest Bosnian diaspora destination, the language of Austria (the nearest Western European cultural and historical relationship to Bosnia), and a significant professional and academic infrastructure in the region. A2–B1 German opens visa options, professional opportunities, and the entire German-speaking cultural world.

Turkish

The historical and cultural language with the deepest roots in Sarajevo's identity. Significant business relationship between Turkey and BiH. Ottoman Turkish preserved in place names, food vocabulary, and architectural terminology throughout the city. Learning Turkish in Sarajevo specifically provides immediate daily context that accelerates acquisition.

Italian / Spanish

The most accessible European languages for BCS speakers due to shared Latin roots in vocabulary, similar phonology, and available resources. Italian for cultural access (architecture, art, food — directly relevant to the Photography and Art tabs). Spanish for the sheer scale of access it provides: 500+ million speakers, an entire hemisphere of culture and professional opportunity.

The Acquisition Method — What Actually Works

Language learning fails consistently when it relies on textbooks and grammar study alone. Grammar is the skeleton of a language — necessary but not sufficient for communication. Acquisition happens through comprehensible input: exposure to the language at a level slightly above current comprehension, repeatedly, over time. The method below is derived from Stephen Krashen's input hypothesis and its modern applications.

The Four-Component Daily Practice

  • Anki vocabulary (15 min): New vocabulary entered as cards with image or sentence context — not isolated words, but words in the sentence where they were encountered. 10–15 new cards per day, reviewed via spaced repetition. Vocabulary is the rate-limiting factor in reading comprehension — the faster it is built, the sooner input becomes comprehensible.
  • Comprehensible input (20–30 min): Reading or listening at the level where 95%+ of words are known. YouTube videos with subtitles in the target language (not your native language — in the target language). Graded readers. Podcasts designed for learners. The content should be engaging enough to forget you are studying — when attention is on the story rather than the language, acquisition occurs most naturally.
  • Speaking practice (15 min, 3× per week minimum): italki.com connects students with native-speaker tutors for conversation sessions. Typically 10–20 EUR per hour. More effective than any app. The discomfort of speaking imperfectly is the mechanism of acquisition — the brain resolves the gap between what it wants to express and what it can express by acquiring the missing structure.
  • Output (10 min, daily writing): One paragraph in the target language daily — about anything. Your day, what you cooked, what you read. Write in Obsidian in the target language. This forces active retrieval of vocabulary and grammar rather than passive recognition. Have a native speaker check it once a week via italki or a language exchange partner.

Resources — Specifically Effective

📱 Anki

Already in the Learning tab — the same tool for language vocabulary. Download the pre-made deck for your target language (Anki has decks for most major languages with thousands of cards). Start with the most frequent 1,000 words — these cover 85% of everyday language. Available free on Android.

🎧 Language Transfer

Free audio courses for multiple languages (German, Spanish, Italian, Turkish, Greek, and others). The most effective beginner resource available at any price. The course does not teach grammar rules — it builds grammatical intuition through guided thinking. Completely free at languagetransfer.org. Start here before any other resource.

📺 YouTube — Comprehensible Input

Channels specifically designed for language acquisition: Dreaming Spanish (Spanish, multiple levels), Easy German (German with subtitles in German and English), Italiano per Stranieri (Italian). These channels produce content specifically calibrated to learner levels — find your level and watch daily.

💬 italki

Online platform for finding native-speaker tutors for conversation practice. Available worldwide including from Bosnia. 1–3 sessions per week with a community tutor (less expensive than professional teachers) provides the speaking practice that no app can replicate. The feedback from a real human on real errors accelerates acquisition in a way solo study cannot.

📖 Graded Readers

Books written in the target language at controlled vocabulary levels. Available for all major European languages. Penguin Readers and Hueber Verlag (for German) produce quality graded readers. Begin at A1 level regardless of how basic it feels — reading fluency in a language is built from successful reading at manageable levels, not from struggling through native-level texts.

The Realistic Timeline

  • A1 (basic communication) — 3 months at one hour daily. Can navigate simple situations, introduce yourself, understand slow simple speech.
  • B1 (independent user) — 12–18 months at one hour daily. Can hold a conversation on familiar topics, understand the main points of clear standard speech, write simple connected text. This is the level where travel in the target language country becomes genuinely enjoyable rather than effortful.
  • B2 (upper intermediate) — 2–3 years at one hour daily. Can understand the main ideas of complex text, interact with a degree of fluency that makes normal interaction with native speakers possible. The professional utility threshold.
  • The critical period is month 3 to month 6. Initial progress is rapid and motivating. Then the beginner content becomes too easy, the intermediate content feels too hard, and the novelty has worn off. Most people quit here. The correct response is to push through to genuinely interesting content in the target language — find a YouTube channel, a podcast, or a topic you actually care about, and access it in the language. Personal interest sustains what discipline alone cannot.
Every language is a different way of being in the world — not merely a different code for the same thoughts, but a different set of concepts, distinctions, and expressions that the first language simply does not contain. The man who speaks two languages has two sets of tools for the same problems. The one he reaches for in any situation is the more interesting choice to observe.— Von Lycaon
Photography is the practice of seeing deliberately. Most people look — they process the visual world as navigation data. The photographer looks at — they examine light, composition, moment, and meaning. This shift in visual attention produces photographs worth keeping and, more usefully, an enhanced quality of attention to the world that transfers beyond the camera to everything else. The camera on the phone in your pocket is sufficient for everything in this tab.— Von Lycaon

The Fundamental Skill — Seeing Before Shooting

The most common mistake in photography is reaching for the camera first. Before raising the phone, spend five seconds observing: where is the light coming from, what is the subject, what should be excluded from the frame, is this the right moment or is the better moment ten seconds away? These five seconds produce better photographs than any technical knowledge applied without them.

Light — The Only Variable That Matters

Every photograph is a recording of light. The subject, the composition, the moment — all of these are secondary to the quality and direction of the light. The golden hour (one hour after sunrise, one hour before sunset) produces warm directional light that flatters almost every subject. Overcast days produce soft diffused light with no harsh shadows — excellent for portraits. Midday direct sunlight produces the least flattering light for any subject that includes people.

The Rule of Thirds

Divide the frame into a 3×3 grid (enable grid lines in the camera settings). Place the subject at one of the four intersection points rather than the centre. Place the horizon on the upper or lower third rather than the middle. This is not a rule to follow blindly — it is a default composition that works more often than centred composition does. Breaking it intentionally, once understood, produces stronger results than breaking it accidentally.

One Subject

The most common compositional failure is the absence of a clear subject. Before shooting, ask: what is this photograph of? Not of — what single element is the primary subject? Everything else in the frame should either support or recede relative to that subject. A photograph of everything is a photograph of nothing.

Get Closer

The advice given by the photographer Robert Capa — "if your photographs aren't good enough, you're not close enough." Most phone photographs are taken from too great a distance. Move closer to the subject. Fill the frame. The detail that makes a photograph interesting is almost always closer than the first instinctive position.

Phone Photography — Getting the Most from What You Have

Camera Settings to Know

  • Enable gridlines. Camera → Settings → Grid: On. Two minutes. Permanent improvement to composition awareness.
  • Shoot RAW if available. Most current Android flagships support RAW capture (DNG format). RAW files contain significantly more image data than JPEG, producing better results from editing. Adobe Lightroom Mobile (already in the Phone & Apps tab) opens and edits RAW files. The file size increase is worth it for any photograph you care about.
  • Lock exposure separately from focus. Tap to focus on the subject. Then hold the exposure slider (the sun icon) and slide to the correct brightness before shooting. The phone's automatic exposure is optimised for the overall frame — your subject may require a different exposure than the background.
  • Portrait mode — use it sparingly. Portrait mode's artificial background blur (bokeh) is convincing on faces at medium distance. It fails on complex edges (hair, glasses, anything with fine detail against a background). Use for clean portrait compositions, disable for anything where the artificial blur will produce visual artefacts.
  • Night mode — manual control. Night mode takes multiple exposures and composites them. Works well for static subjects. Produces motion blur on anything that moves (people, leaves, water). Disable for dynamic subjects at night and use a stable surface or tripod instead.

Sarajevo — A Photographer's City

Sarajevo is one of the most visually extraordinary cities in the Balkans and remains relatively underphotographed compared to its regional rivals. The following are the locations and conditions that produce the strongest images.

🕌 Baščaršija — Golden Hour

The Sebilj fountain and surrounding čaršija at golden hour before sunset. The warm light on the copper and stone architecture is extraordinary. Arrive 30 minutes before sunset. Shoot toward the minaret of Gazi Husrev-beg's mosque with the sun behind you for the most saturated light. The fountain with the pigeons — shoot at eye level, not standing height. The perspective matters.

🌁 Yellow Fortress (Žuta Tabija) — Dawn

The view of Sarajevo from the Yellow Fortress at dawn — the city emerging from the valley fog with the minarets and church spires visible simultaneously. Arrive before sunrise. The fog that fills the Sarajevo valley on autumn and winter mornings is nowhere more visually significant than from this vantage point.

🏔️ Trebević — Any Season

The cable car to Trebević provides both the ascent (glass cabin, the city falling away below) and the destination (the forested peak above the city, the abandoned bobsled track from the 1984 Olympics — one of the most photographically significant sites in the city for its combination of beauty and history). The bobsled track specifically: overcast days produce the most atmospheric light on the concrete and moss.

🌉 Latin Bridge — Blue Hour

The Latin Bridge at blue hour (20–30 minutes after sunset when the sky is deep blue and the artificial lights begin to balance with available light). The bridge's reflection in the Miljacka when the water is still. Shoot from either bank. Long exposures on a stable surface produce the water reflection that hand-held shooting cannot.

Editing — The Second Half of Photography

A good photograph made great through editing is photography. A bad photograph rescued through editing is graphic design. The editing principles below apply to the former.

  • Exposure first. Get the brightness correct before adjusting anything else. The target is a balanced histogram — not too much information in the shadows (underexposed) or highlights (overexposed). In Lightroom Mobile: the histogram is visible in the edit panel. Learn to read it.
  • Colour temperature second. Warming the temperature (moving the slider toward yellow) produces more inviting, natural-looking images for most subjects in natural light. Cooling (moving toward blue) produces a more cinematic, detached quality. Both are tools — neither is correct without context.
  • Contrast and clarity sparingly. Both of these are commonly over-applied. A slight increase in contrast adds definition. A large increase makes photographs look processed. Clarity (mid-tone contrast) used lightly adds texture to landscapes and architectural details; used heavily it looks like an Instagram filter from 2012.
  • Develop a consistent edit style and apply it across related photographs. In Lightroom, save the edit as a Preset and apply it as the starting point for all photographs from a particular session. Consistency in editing makes a collection of photographs feel cohesive rather than arbitrary.

Documenting Your Life — The Purpose Beyond Aesthetics

  • Photograph the ordinary. The extraordinary moments photograph themselves. The ordinary moments — coffee in the morning, the apartment in a particular light, your wife reading, the view from the window in different seasons — are the ones that will matter most in twenty years and the ones least often captured. The Codex and everything it describes is your daily life. Document it occasionally.
  • Print photographs. Digital photographs that exist only on a phone or cloud storage are not practically accessible as memories. Print 10–15 photographs per year. Frame two or three. The physical presence of a printed photograph produces a different quality of memory recall than scrolling through a camera roll.
  • One album per significant event or trip. Use Google Photos or the Lightroom album feature to curate — not keep every photograph taken, but select the 15–20 that represent the event or trip most fully. The curation is an act of memory formation, not organisation.
The camera does not make the photograph. The photographer's attention makes the photograph. The camera records it. This distinction explains why the same phone in two different hands produces images of categorically different quality — not because of the hardware, but because of the seeing that preceded the shutter.— Von Lycaon

Post-Processing — The Second Craft

Capture is the first decision; processing is the second. Every image you see from a professional photographer has been processed — even those described as "film simulation" or "straight out of camera" involve processing decisions, whether made in the camera's own software or in Lightroom. The question is not whether to process but whether to process intentionally or to accept the camera's default processing decisions, which are designed for the average image rather than for your specific image.

  • The three adjustments that matter most: Exposure (overall brightness — the histogram is the correct tool, not the preview, because preview brightness varies with screen settings), white balance (the colour temperature of the light — a warm adjustment on a cold grey day, a cool adjustment on an already-warm golden hour), and contrast (shadows and highlights separately rather than a single contrast slider). These three, applied with restraint, transform a technically correct image into one that feels deliberate.
  • Snapseed for mobile: Already in the Phone & Apps tab. The selective adjustment tool (brush a circle on any area of the image, then adjust only that area) is the single most powerful mobile editing tool available. A face 1 stop brighter than the background, a sky 1 stop darker — these are the decisions that make a photograph rather than a snapshot.
  • Lightroom Mobile (free tier): The desktop Lightroom workflow on the phone. Presets (saved combinations of adjustments) allow the application of a consistent visual style across multiple images — the beginning of developing a coherent photographic identity rather than a collection of individually processed images that share no visual logic.
  • The Sarajevo light: The valley geography produces specific light conditions that are worth understanding and exploiting. Winter inversions produce a diffused, flat light that makes colour photography difficult but black and white work exceptional — the low contrast of the light and the strong architectural geometry of the city work together in monochrome in ways they do not in colour. The summer golden hour light on the Ottoman architecture of Baščaršija lasts approximately 20 minutes and produces images available nowhere else in Europe. Know when it occurs (local sunset minus 45 minutes) and be there.
Drawing is not a talent — it is a skill, and like every skill in this Codex, it is acquired through the correct method applied consistently over time. The person who believes they "cannot draw" has usually not been taught to see rather than taught to draw. The two are almost the same thing, and both are teachable.— Von Lycaon

Learning to See

Drawing difficulty is almost always a seeing problem rather than a hand problem. The brain substitutes its symbol for a thing — the "eye symbol" for an eye, the "tree symbol" for a tree — in place of what is actually in front of it. Training drawing is training the brain to override these symbols and record what is actually seen: proportions, angles, relationships of light and shadow.

Contour Drawing

Blind contour drawing (drawing the subject without looking at the paper) forces the eyes to move at the same pace the pencil moves — training the hand-eye synchronisation that is the physical foundation of drawing. The result looks terrible. That is not the point. The habit of slow, observational movement is the point. Practise 5 minutes daily for one month before judging results.

Negative Space

Drawing the spaces around an object rather than the object itself bypasses the symbol-substitution problem. The brain has no symbol for "the space between a chair's legs" — it draws what it actually sees. Identify the negative spaces in any subject before drawing the subject itself. This produces more accurate proportions than attempting to draw the object directly.

Value Before Line

Professional drawing builds form through value (light and dark) rather than outline. An object does not have an outline in reality — it has surfaces that face toward and away from the light source. Beginning with the darkest shadows and building toward the light produces three-dimensional form. Beginning with an outline produces a flat cartoon.

Proportion and Relationships

Most proportion errors come from drawing things as they are "supposed to be" rather than as they appear from the specific viewing angle. The forehead is larger than most people draw it relative to the face. Hands are larger relative to faces than almost anyone draws them. Measure relationships by holding up the pencil and comparing sizes before drawing.

The Practical Setup

  • Equipment to start: A sketchbook (A5 is portable, A4 is better for practice), a set of graphite pencils (HB, 2B, 4B, 6B — the harder grades for light areas, softer for dark), a good eraser (kneaded eraser is more versatile than a standard rubber). Total cost: under 30 BAM at any papirnica in Sarajevo.
  • The 15-minute daily practice: Sit with the sketchbook and draw one thing from observation — an object on the desk, a hand, a view from the window. Not from imagination, not from a photograph initially — from direct observation. 15 minutes, one subject, complete attention. More than any other variable, this daily practice habit determines improvement speed.
  • Resources: "Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain" by Betty Edwards is the single most effective drawing instruction book available — it addresses the seeing problem directly. "Keys to Drawing" by Bert Dodson for the technical foundations. Both are available as PDFs or physical books.

Photography and Drawing — The Codex Connection

The observational skills developed in the Photography tab and the Drawing tab are the same skill in different media. The photographer asking "where is the light?" and the artist asking "where are the shadows?" are engaged in identical perceptual training. Practising both simultaneously accelerates both — each sharpens the visual attention that the other requires.

To draw something is to look at it more carefully than almost any other activity permits. The artist who draws a hand for an hour knows a hand more intimately than the person who has used their hands for a lifetime without examining them. The drawing is not the point. The quality of attention is.— Von Lycaon

The Foundation Skills in Depth

Contour Drawing — The Master Technique

Pure contour drawing: place the pencil on the paper and draw the outline of the subject without looking at the paper, moving the pencil only as fast as the eye moves along the edge of the subject. The result looks wrong but feels right — the disconnect between what you expect and what you produce is the gap between symbolic drawing (what you think things look like) and perceptual drawing (what you actually see). Practise this for 10 minutes daily for two weeks. By the end, your ability to see and record the actual edges of objects rather than your mental symbol for them is permanently altered.

Value Before Line

Most beginners draw outlines and then fill them in. This is the wrong sequence. The visible world does not consist of outlines — it consists of areas of light and dark. Practise drawing with no outlines at all: start from the darkest areas and build value (tonal range from light to dark), letting the forms emerge from the contrast rather than the boundary. This is how master draftsmen work and it produces drawings that appear to have light rather than merely depicting objects.

Negative Space

The space around and between objects is as visually definite as the objects themselves. Drawing the negative space — the shape of the air around the subject — forces the eye away from the symbolic representation of the subject and toward its actual proportional relationship to the surrounding space. To draw a chair accurately, draw the shapes between the chair legs. To draw a face accurately, draw the space between the features. The subject emerges correctly because the space around it is correct.

Proportion and Measurement

The pencil-as-measuring-tool technique: hold the pencil at arm's length, close one eye, align the top of the pencil with the top of the subject and the thumb with a significant horizontal or vertical measurement. This measurement becomes a unit — every other measurement in the drawing is expressed as a multiple or fraction of this unit. A head is one unit high; the torso is approximately three units; the legs approximately four. These relationships are objective and measurable, removing the guesswork that produces distortion.

Materials — What You Actually Need

  • A sketchbook: A4 or A5, cartridge paper (90-100gsm), hardback cover. Leuchtturm1917 makes an excellent sketchbook — the same manufacturer as the journal in the Mind and Rest tab. The sketchbook goes everywhere. Observations drawn from life are worth more than any studio practice session.
  • Pencils: 2H (light construction lines), HB (general drawing), 2B (mid tones), 4B (darks). These four cover the full value range. Beyond this is specialisation. Buy a pencil sharpener that produces a long taper rather than a blunt stub — the length of exposed graphite determines the sensitivity of the mark.
  • A kneaded eraser: This eraser is shaped by hand and can be formed into a point. It lifts graphite rather than abrading the paper surface, making it the correct tool for lightening areas without damaging the tooth of the paper. Available at papirnice in Sarajevo.
  • Cheap paper for practice: The sketchbook is for intentional work. A stack of cheap A4 printer paper is for practice exercises (contour drawing, value scales, perspective studies). Never withhold practice because the paper feels too precious.

Drawing Sarajevo — The City as Subject

Urban sketching is one of the richest drawing practices available — the city provides infinite variety of light, architecture, human activity, and historical layering. Sarajevo specifically offers: the Ottoman streetscape of Baščaršija (organic geometry, varied surface textures, the challenge of drawing irregular historic buildings), the Austro-Hungarian facades of Ferhadija (regular geometry, classical proportions — the correct subject for learning architectural perspective), the view of the city from Bistrik or Kovači (complex recession into depth, the challenge of drawing a city from above), and the faces in any kafana (the portrait challenge of drawing people who are briefly stationary).

  • The 10-minute sketch rule: Any subject can be drawn meaningfully in 10 minutes. The constraint forces selection — what are the essential marks that communicate this subject? This discipline produces better work than unlimited time because it prevents over-working and forces commitment to every mark.
  • Drawing from life vs photograph: Drawing from life is superior for developing observational skill — the three-dimensional subject forces the eye to make depth decisions that a photograph has already resolved. Photographs are useful for subjects that cannot be observed directly (historical architecture, animals in motion). For most drawing practice, draw from life.
Drawing is not the reproduction of what you see. It is the record of how you looked. The drawing that fails technically but was made with genuine attention is more valuable than the technically accomplished drawing made without it. Look more. Draw less. Then draw more.-- Von Lycaon
The guitar is the most accessible serious musical instrument available — relatively inexpensive, portable, capable of producing complete harmonic and rhythmic content alone, and with enough depth to sustain a lifetime of learning. This tab covers the path from first chord to genuine musical competence, including the theory that makes the instrument comprehensible rather than a collection of memorised shapes.— Von Lycaon

Getting Started — The Setup

The Instrument

For learning: a steel-string acoustic guitar. Classical guitars (nylon strings) are gentler on fingers but have a wider neck that makes chord formation harder for beginners. Electric guitars require amplification for meaningful practice. A steel-string acoustic in the 200–400 BAM range is sufficient for the first year of learning. Brands available in Sarajevo's music shops (Muzička oprema, Melodija): Yamaha, Fender, Ibanez entry-level. Avoid the cheapest instruments — poor intonation and high action make learning significantly harder than the 100 BAM saved justifies.

Setup Matters More Than Price

A mid-range guitar with proper setup (action adjusted by a luthier or music shop technician) plays better and easier than an expensive guitar with factory setup that was never optimised. The action (height of strings above the fretboard) should be low enough that chords don't require excessive force to produce clean notes. Ask any music shop to assess the action when purchasing.

Essential Accessories

Tuner (a clip-on tuner is the most convenient — under 15 BAM), picks (medium gauge to start), a capo (allows playing in any key without learning different chord shapes), a guitar strap, and a case or gig bag. All available at Sarajevo music shops or online.

The Learning Path — First Year

Month 1–2 — The Physical Foundation

  • Finger conditioning first. The fingertip calluses required for pain-free playing take 4–6 weeks to develop. There is no shortcut. 15–20 minutes daily is better than one-hour sessions three times per week — the calluses develop from frequency of contact, not duration of single sessions.
  • The four essential open chords: G major, C major, D major, Em. These four chords cover hundreds of songs. Learn them before any others. The criterion for "learned" is transitioning between any two of these chords cleanly within the space of one beat at a moderate tempo. Until this is achieved, learn no new chords.
  • Strumming patterns: Down-down-up-up-down-up is the foundational strumming pattern that covers most popular music. The wrist does the work, not the arm — loose wrist, relaxed grip on the pick.

Month 3–6 — Expansion

  • Add: Am, F (barre chord), A major, E major. The F barre chord is the first technically demanding chord — requiring the index finger to press across all strings simultaneously. It will take 2–4 weeks of daily practice to produce a clean sound. This is normal. Every guitarist has passed through this exact frustration.
  • Introduce fingerpicking: The alternating bass fingerpicking pattern — thumb alternates between bass strings while fingers pick melody strings. This pattern underlies folk, country, and much fingerstyle guitar. Learn it on easy chord changes before applying to songs.
  • First songs: "Knockin' on Heaven's Door," "Horse With No Name," "Wish You Were Here." These songs use the chords learned above, have slow tempos, and are recognisable — the recognisability provides the motivational feedback that abstract exercises cannot.

Music Theory — The Minimum Useful Amount

  • The major scale: The sequence of whole and half steps that produces the familiar do-re-mi pattern. On guitar: every scale pattern is moveable — learn the major scale shape in one position and it applies in every key by moving up or down the neck.
  • Chord construction: A major chord is built from the 1st, 3rd, and 5th notes of its major scale. A minor chord replaces the 3rd with a flattened 3rd. This explains why chord shapes work and allows derivation of any chord rather than memorisation of all of them.
  • The Nashville Number System: Chords in a key are numbered I through VII. In the key of G: I=G, II=Am, III=Bm, IV=C, V=D, VI=Em. The I-IV-V-I progression (G-C-D-G) is the harmonic foundation of most popular music in Western tradition. Recognising this pattern by ear is the single most useful musical skill for a guitarist.
The guitar is learned in the first five minutes and understood in the first five years. There is always another layer available — from the first chord to the last performance, the instrument never fully yields. That inexhaustibility is its particular quality as a companion to a life of deliberate growth.— Von Lycaon

Music Theory for the Self-Taught Guitarist

Most self-taught guitarists avoid music theory because it seems abstract. This avoidance limits their playing permanently. The following is the minimum theory that produces maximum practical benefit — not academic music theory, but the specific concepts that allow a guitarist to understand what they are playing and to construct things they have not learned by rote.

The Major Scale and Why It Matters

The major scale is the foundation of Western music. On the guitar, the C major scale (C D E F G A B C) uses no sharps or flats and is the clearest demonstration of the pattern. Every scale is built from the same interval pattern: Whole-Whole-Half-Whole-Whole-Whole-Half (W W H W W W H) steps. This pattern, starting from any note, produces the major scale of that note. Once this pattern is internalised, you can play any major scale from any note on the guitar without memorising twelve separate patterns.

Chord Construction

A major chord is built from the 1st, 3rd, and 5th degrees of the major scale. C major = C (1) + E (3) + G (5). A minor chord replaces the major 3rd with the minor 3rd (one semitone lower): C minor = C + Eb + G. This is why open chords on the guitar have the shapes they do — those shapes are the most practical fingerings for the notes that these intervals produce on the six strings. Understanding this allows you to construct any chord from its name rather than memorising shapes.

The CAGED System

The guitar's most important framework. Every chord on the guitar is a version of one of five open chord shapes (C, A, G, E, D) moved up the neck using a barre. The G chord open position, moved to the 5th fret with a barre, becomes C. The same shape at the 7th fret becomes D. This means five shapes cover every chord in every position on the neck. Once CAGED is understood, the guitar's entire fretboard becomes mapped rather than a series of isolated memorised positions.

The Pentatonic Scale

The minor pentatonic scale (five notes: root, minor 3rd, 4th, 5th, minor 7th) is the primary scale for improvisation in blues, rock, and most popular music. The first "box" position (starting at the 5th fret for A minor pentatonic) is the most played scale pattern in all of rock guitar. Once the first box is internalised, the other four boxes of the CAGED system complete the full fretboard coverage. Learn the first box. Play over backing tracks. Improvisation begins immediately.

Practice Structure — The Correct Approach

  • The 30-minute session structure: 5 minutes of technical warm-up (chromatic exercises, finger independence drills). 10 minutes of new material (the chord or scale or song currently being learned). 10 minutes of consolidation (material from the previous two weeks, keeping it fresh). 5 minutes of free play (no instruction, no exercise — just playing what feels good). This structure ensures that technical skill, new learning, retention, and enjoyment all receive dedicated time in every session.
  • Slow practice is fast learning: The most common practice error is playing at tempo. The brain installs whatever pattern is repeated — including the errors. Slow practice, at a tempo where every note is clean and every transition is deliberate, installs the correct pattern. Speed is then achieved by incrementally raising the tempo once the pattern is clean. A metronome is required for this. Available as a free app.
  • Record yourself: The gap between how you sound in your head and how you actually sound is initially large. Recording a 2-minute practice clip weekly and listening back reveals the specific technical weaknesses that playing without listening conceals. The recording does not lie. Your internal monitoring does.

The Sarajevo Guitar Scene

Sarajevo has an active music community. The kafana music tradition — sevdalinka (the indigenous Bosnian blues), traditional folk music, and the modern pop-folk hybrid that plays in most commercial spaces — provides a living musical context that most cities do not have. Learning one or two sevdalinka songs on the guitar is both a cultural engagement and a genuine musical achievement: the scales are modal, the ornaments are complex, and the emotional content is demanding. The National Theatre and the Bosanski Kulturni Centar both host live music programming.

The guitar rewards patience with a generosity that few other instruments match. The first year is difficult. The second year is fluent. By the third, music begins to flow without deliberate construction -- the theory has become reflex and the playing becomes conversation. Stay in the first year long enough to reach the second.-- Von Lycaon
Chess is the cleanest available model of strategic thinking under constraint. Every principle it develops — evaluating positions before acting, controlling the centre rather than the periphery, forcing the opponent to react rather than initiating, recognising when an advantage is sufficient to convert — transfers directly to professional and personal decision-making. It is also genuinely enjoyable once competence reaches the level where the game becomes interesting rather than merely confusing.— Von Lycaon

The Fundamentals — What Beginners Must Learn First

Piece Values

Pawn: 1. Knight: 3. Bishop: 3. Rook: 5. Queen: 9. King: infinite (cannot be traded). These values are approximations — piece value is position-dependent — but provide the basis for evaluating exchanges. Trading a bishop (3) for a knight (3) is roughly equal. Trading a rook (5) for a bishop (3) is a loss of material. Never trade a queen (9) for a minor piece unless the positional gain is overwhelming.

The Three Opening Principles

1. Control the centre (e4, d4, e5, d5 squares) with pawns or pieces. 2. Develop your pieces (get knights and bishops off the back rank) before attacking. 3. Castle early (king safety). These three principles correctly applied produce a sound position from which middle-game plans can be executed. Violating any of them without compensation is the most common cause of early positional deterioration.

Tactics Over Strategy

At beginner to intermediate level (below 1200 Elo), games are almost entirely decided by tactical errors — hanging pieces, missing forks and pins, overlooking checks. Studying strategy is premature when the primary losses are tactical. Invest in tactical training (puzzles) before opening theory or strategic concepts.

Calculate, Don't Guess

Before each move, ask: what is my opponent's best response to this? If the answer is not good for you, the move is not good. This single habit — checking the opponent's reply before moving — eliminates the majority of blunders that cost games at all levels below master. It takes 10 seconds per move.

The Learning Path

Month 1–3 — Foundation

  • Chess.com or Lichess.org (both free): Create an account, play daily games at 10-minute time control. Rapid chess is the correct format for learning — blitz (3 minutes) is too fast to think, classical is too slow to accumulate games.
  • Puzzle training daily: 10–20 puzzles on Chess.com or Lichess's puzzle section. Puzzles train pattern recognition — the ability to see tactical motifs (forks, pins, skewers, discovered attacks) in real games. At 10 puzzles per day for 3 months, approximately 900 tactical patterns are encountered and processed.
  • Learn the basic checkmates: Back-rank mate, Scholar's mate (and its defence), Fool's mate, two-rook checkmate, queen and king checkmate. These are the end-state patterns that tactical sequences resolve into.

Month 4–6 — Development

  • Learn one opening as White, one as Black: Not to memorise lines but to understand the plans. As White: the Italian Game (1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4) — clear plans, good pawn centre, easy to learn. As Black against e4: the Sicilian Defense or the French Defense. Against d4: the King's Indian or the Nimzo-Indian. One opening in each colour direction, understood conceptually rather than memorised mechanically.
  • Review your games: After each game on Chess.com or Lichess, use the built-in analysis to identify blunders and mistakes. The question after every error: what should I have calculated here? Understanding the error is more valuable than knowing the correct move.

The Mental Game — Chess as Thinking Training

  • Patience: Chess rewards the player who can wait for the correct moment rather than creating complications prematurely. A positional advantage converted through patient accumulation produces more reliable results than aggressive play that creates mutual complications.
  • Objectivity: The ability to assess a position accurately — not as you wish it to be, not as you fear it is, but as it actually is — is chess's primary cognitive demand. This same quality applied to professional and personal situations produces better decisions than either wishful thinking or excessive pessimism.
  • Playing in Sarajevo: The chess community in Sarajevo is active — clubs including the Šahovski savez Bosne i Hercegovine. Over-the-board play against a human opponent produces a qualitatively different experience from online play. The presence of the opponent, the physical pieces, and the clock create a focus that screens rarely replicate.
The chessboard is a closed system with complete information available to both players. Every loss is entirely explicable — there was a better move and you did not find it. This accountability, uncomfortable as it is, is precisely what makes chess effective as a thinking teacher. There are no excuses available. Only improvement.— Von Lycaon

Chess as a Thinking Laboratory

Chess is valuable not only as a game but as a compressed environment for practising thinking skills that transfer to every other domain. Pattern recognition, calculation under time pressure, managing uncertainty, the discipline of not making a move until you have checked it, the acceptance of loss and the extraction of learning from it — these are practised in every game. The chess board is a thinking laboratory that gives immediate, objective feedback in a way that most real-world decisions do not.

The Opening Principles — Deeper

  • Principle 1 — Control the centre: The four central squares (e4, e5, d4, d5) are the most valuable territory on the board. A piece in the centre controls more squares than a piece on the edge. Most opening strategies are fundamentally about controlling or fighting for these four squares, either by occupying them directly or by controlling them from a distance (the hypermodern openings — Nimzo-Indian, King's Indian — contest the centre with pieces rather than pawns).
  • Principle 2 — Develop with purpose: Each developing move should contribute to a clear plan. "Developing" a piece to a square where it does nothing useful wastes a tempo — time in chess is measured in moves, and wasting moves is wasting time. Every piece developed should have a clear role: this bishop is going to pressurise this square; this knight is going to the ideal square from which it controls the centre.
  • Principle 3 — King safety: Castle early. An uncastled king in the opening is a target. The specific danger: if both central files open (e-file and d-file) before you castle, an opponent's rooks and queen gain immediate access to your king. Castle before the centre opens, or have a concrete reason for delaying.

The Tactical Patterns — Master These First

The Fork

A single piece attacks two or more enemy pieces simultaneously, forcing the opponent to lose one. The knight fork is the most common: a knight moves to a square from which it attacks the king and the queen simultaneously. The king must move, and the queen is captured. Pattern recognition for forks: whenever your knight has a square available that attacks multiple enemy pieces — or whenever a fork square can be created by exchanging pieces first — this is a fork possibility.

The Pin

A piece is pinned when moving it would expose a more valuable piece behind it to capture. Absolute pin: the exposed piece is the king (the pinned piece literally cannot move legally). Relative pin: the exposed piece is a queen or rook (the pinned piece can move but the consequence is material loss). Pins are powerful because they restrict enemy mobility. Create them; exploit them; break your own pins before your opponent exploits them.

The Skewer

The reverse of a pin: a more valuable piece is attacked, forced to move, and a less valuable piece behind it is captured. A bishop skewer: attacks the queen, queen must move, rook behind it is captured. Skewers are most commonly found with bishops, rooks, and queens in open positions.

Discovered Attack

A piece moves, revealing an attack from the piece behind it. The power of discovered attacks: the moving piece can itself go to an attacking square, creating a double attack. The opponent cannot respond to both threats simultaneously. The most powerful form — the discovered check — forces the opponent to respond to the check, allowing the moving piece to capture material freely.

Endgame Foundations

Most beginner players neglect endgame study because it seems less exciting than opening theory or tactical combinations. This is an error. The player who cannot convert a won endgame loses points that their superior opening or middlegame performance earned. The three endgames to know before any others:

  • King and pawn vs King: The opposition concept — when kings face each other with one square between them, the side that just moved is in opposition and is at a disadvantage. The rule of the square — whether the defending king can catch a passed pawn by counting diagonals. These two concepts determine 80% of all king and pawn endings.
  • Rook and pawn vs Rook: The Lucena position (winning method for the stronger side) and the Philidor position (drawing method for the defending side). These are the two foundational rook ending positions. Knowing them saves games that are otherwise lost through uncertainty.
  • Basic mating patterns: King and queen vs king. King and two rooks vs king. King, bishop, and knight vs king (complex — requires specific technique). These should be deliverable without thinking — they arise regularly in games and time pressure is always present.
Chess teaches the most important lesson available in a game: that the correct move often feels wrong, and the wrong move often feels right. The discipline of checking your impulse before acting -- of asking what your opponent intends rather than only what you intend -- is the habit that chess builds and life rewards.-- Von Lycaon
Art history is not the memorisation of names and dates. It is the development of visual literacy — the ability to look at a painting, a sculpture, or a photograph and understand what it is communicating, why it was made the way it was, and what it reveals about the time and place that produced it. This literacy transfers immediately to photography, interior design, and the quality of attention to the visual world that this Codex consistently returns to.— Von Lycaon

Why Visual Literacy Matters

The ability to look carefully — at a painting, a city, a person's face — is the same skill applied in different domains. Photographers, architects, designers, and anyone who works with visual communication benefits from art historical training not because they need to know the names but because it teaches them to see the choices behind what they are looking at. Every visual decision — composition, colour, light, subject — was made by someone for a reason. Reading those reasons is visual literacy.

The Western Tradition — A Compressed Map

Ancient & Medieval (pre-1400)

Greek sculpture established the idealised human form that Western art returns to repeatedly. Roman art was largely propagandistic — portraits of emperors, commemorative reliefs. Medieval art was theological — images as instruction for the illiterate faithful, not representations of perceived reality. The gold backgrounds of Byzantine icons are not naive: they represent the divine realm that exists outside earthly space and time. Understanding that different periods made art for different purposes prevents the mistake of judging all art by modern representational standards.

Renaissance (1400–1600)

The recovery of classical Greek and Roman knowledge combined with new optical discoveries — most importantly linear perspective — produced art that for the first time systematically represented three-dimensional space on a two-dimensional surface. Key figures: Leonardo (the integration of art and science), Michelangelo (the human body as the primary expressive vehicle), Raphael (compositional harmony and grace). The Renaissance is the origin point of the Western assumption that art should represent the visible world accurately.

Baroque (1600–1750)

If the Renaissance sought harmony and clarity, the Baroque sought drama and emotional intensity. Caravaggio's tenebrism — extreme contrast between light and dark — is the most immediate visual shock in Western painting. Rembrandt's portraits are the most psychologically penetrating. Velázquez's Las Meninas is among the most conceptually complex paintings ever made. The Baroque period is when portrait painting became genuinely interested in the inner life of its subjects.

Impressionism (1860–1890)

The moment when Western art shifted its interest from what things look like to how they are perceived. Monet, Renoir, Degas: light, colour, and the fleeting moment rather than stable, finished form. Painted outdoors for the first time systematically. The Impressionists were the first movement to be rejected by the official establishment and then vindicated by history — a pattern that repeats in almost every subsequent movement. Understanding why they were controversial makes their achievement more rather than less impressive.

Modern & Contemporary (1900–present)

Cubism (multiple simultaneous viewpoints), Expressionism (emotional distortion over accurate representation), Abstract Expressionism (pure emotional gesture), Minimalism (the reduction of art to its bare formal elements), Conceptual art (the idea as the artwork). Each movement is a response to what preceded it and a rejection of what felt exhausted. Contemporary art is most legible when understood as conversation — each work speaking to, arguing with, or extending previous works.

How to Look at a Painting — A Method

  • Step 1 — Look without reading the label for two minutes. Note: where does your eye go first? Where does it travel next? What is the light source? What is the mood before you know anything about the work? First impressions are data.
  • Step 2 — Read the label. Title, artist, date, medium. Now look again with this context. Does knowing it is a self-portrait change what you see in the face? Does knowing the date place it in a movement you recognise?
  • Step 3 — Identify the compositional choices. Where are the strongest lights and darks? What lines lead the eye? Is the composition stable (symmetrical, triangle-based) or dynamic (diagonal, asymmetric)?
  • Step 4 — Ask what problem this work was solving. What was the artist trying to achieve that existing approaches had not? Every significant work is an answer to a question. Finding the question makes the answer comprehensible.

Sarajevo's Visual Culture

The Sarajevo region contains several significant art institutions worth knowing as a resident:

  • Galerija Collegium Artisticum — the primary contemporary art gallery in Sarajevo, hosting rotating exhibitions of Bosnian and regional artists. Free or low-cost entry. Located in the city centre.
  • Ars Aevi Museum of Contemporary Art — an extraordinary institution for its scale: a permanent collection of works donated by internationally significant contemporary artists (Kiefer, Clemente, Kosuth) specifically for Sarajevo, as an act of solidarity following the siege. The collection's existence in this city is itself a historically significant artwork.
  • The Vijećnica — beyond its historical significance, the restored building is architecturally one of the finest examples of Moorish Revival architecture in Europe. Visit it as an architectural object as much as a library.

Essential Books for Visual Literacy

  • "Ways of Seeing" — John Berger: The most accessible introduction to critical visual analysis ever written. 150 pages. Changes permanently how you look at images — including advertising, which Berger argues is the dominant visual language of the modern era.
  • "The Story of Art" — E.H. Gombrich: The standard introductory art history text, now in its 16th edition. Readable, authoritative, genuinely engaging. Start here before any specialist text.
  • "Seven Days in the Art World" — Sarah Thornton: How the contemporary art market actually functions — auctions, art fairs, studios, critics. Demystifies the contemporary art world without either celebrating or condemning it.
Every great painting is a window into a specific mind at a specific moment attempting to resolve a specific problem. The viewer who brings no knowledge stands outside the window. The viewer who brings even a little stands inside it.— Von Lycaon
Architecture is the art form you cannot avoid. Every day you move through buildings, streets, and spaces that someone designed — and those decisions about proportion, light, material, and sequence shape your mood and behaviour whether you are conscious of them or not. Developing architectural literacy means beginning to read those decisions, understanding why spaces feel the way they do, and seeing the history of human aspiration written in stone, concrete, and glass.— Von Lycaon

The Fundamental Vocabulary

Space, Light & Sequence

The three primary tools of architecture. Space: the volume between surfaces — the room as experience rather than floor plan. Light: the quality that changes space hourly, seasonally, and dramatically — from the oculus of the Pantheon to Le Corbusier's chapel at Ronchamp. Sequence: the experience of moving through a building — compression and release, darkness before light, low before high. The greatest buildings choreograph movement as deliberately as a film director cuts shots.

Proportion & Scale

The relationship between parts of a building and between the building and the human body. Classical architecture used mathematical ratios derived from the human body (the Vitruvian tradition). Modernism largely rejected these ratios and the result — buildings scaled to the car or the economic unit rather than the person — is measurably less comfortable to inhabit. The buildings that feel right almost always have proportions that reference the human scale, consciously or not.

Material & Construction

Buildings express their construction honestly or dishonestly. A stone arch expresses the logic of compression and the trajectory of force. A steel frame clad in fake stone expresses nothing about how it stands. The most admired buildings of every era tend to be honest about their materials and construction logic — from the Gothic cathedral (stone that wants to collapse outward, held by flying buttresses) to the Centre Pompidou (all structure and services exposed on the exterior).

The Major Movements

Classical & Renaissance

Greek temples, Roman civic buildings, Renaissance palaces: the orders (Doric, Ionic, Corinthian), the column as the primary expressive element, symmetry and proportion as the language of authority and reason. The grammar of Western architecture for 2,000 years. Palladio's villas in the Veneto are the most copied buildings in history — their influence reaches from Thomas Jefferson's Monticello to every neoclassical bank in every city.

Gothic

The structural achievement of directing all weight outward through flying buttresses, freeing the walls for glass. Gothic cathedrals are primarily light-delivery machines of extraordinary engineering ambition. The vertical emphasis — height as the architectural expression of aspiration toward the divine. Chartres Cathedral, the Sainte-Chapelle in Paris. The Gothic period invented the structural logic that skyscrapers later rediscovered.

Modernism

The rejection of historical ornament and the embrace of industrial materials — steel, concrete, glass. Le Corbusier's five points of architecture: pilotis (building raised on columns), free facade, open floor plan, horizontal windows, roof garden. Mies van der Rohe's reductionism: "less is more," the building as pure structural expression. The movement that produced both the most liberating spaces and the most dehumanising housing estates of the 20th century — often from the same theoretical basis.

Contemporary

Parametric design (form generated by algorithms), sustainable architecture (building as ecological system), contextual architecture (buildings that respond to their specific place and culture). The best contemporary architecture engages with all three while maintaining the fundamental requirement: spaces that are good to be in. The worst contemporary architecture sacrifices human experience for conceptual ambition or cost efficiency.

Sarajevo — An Architectural Biography

Sarajevo is one of the most architecturally layered cities in Europe. Reading its buildings is reading its history directly.

  • Ottoman layer (15th–19th century): The Baščaršija čaršija, the mosques and their minarets, the hans (merchant inns), the covered bazaar (bezistan). Low horizontal buildings, organic street patterns following topography rather than grid, the česmа (public fountain) as urban infrastructure. The Ottoman city is still largely intact east of the Miljacka.
  • Austro-Hungarian layer (1878–1918): The Vijećnica (City Hall) in Moorish Revival — an extraordinary choice, deploying an Islamic aesthetic for a European imperial building in a predominantly Muslim city. The National Museum (historicist), the post office (Secession style), the railway station, the elegant apartment buildings of Ferhadija and Titova. The formal European grid extending westward from Baščaršija.
  • Yugoslav Modernism (1945–1992): The skyscrapers of Marijin Dvor, the Skenderija complex, the Holiday Inn (built for the 1984 Olympics, an iconic if awkward yellow box). Utilitarian housing blocks in Dobrinja, Novo Sarajevo, and Alipašino Polje — the industrialised housing production that rapidly housed a growing city.
  • Post-war reconstruction and contemporary: The reconstruction of the Vijećnica (completed 2014), new commercial development in the city centre, and the ongoing patchwork of restored and unrestored siege damage that makes Sarajevo's architectural history physically readable to anyone paying attention.

Buildings Worth Visiting as Architecture

  • Gazi Husrev-beg Mosque (1531): The finest Ottoman religious building in the Balkans. The transition from the square base to the octagonal drum to the dome. The quality of light through the high windows. The proportional relationship between the interior volume and the human body. Visit during a non-prayer time for unhurried observation.
  • Vijećnica (1896, restored 2014): The most elaborate building in Sarajevo. The Moorish Revival interior — horseshoe arches, geometric tilework, the restored stained glass oculus — is extraordinary. The restoration after the siege destruction is itself a study in the politics of architectural heritage.
  • The National Museum (1913): A clean example of historicist architecture — the attempt to build in a historically derived style that communicates institutional permanence. Compare it to the Ottoman buildings a kilometre east and the modernist towers a kilometre west.
A city is a biography written in buildings. The man who can read it inhabits his city differently from the one who passes through it unseeing. Sarajevo is among the richest texts of this kind available in Europe. Read it.— Von Lycaon
Classical music is not difficult. It is unfamiliar — which is a different problem with a different solution. The barrier is not complexity but the absence of an entry point. This tab provides the entry points: the composers worth beginning with, what to listen for, and how to move from passive hearing to genuine understanding of one of the most sophisticated forms of human expression available.— Von Lycaon

What Makes It Different

Popular music operates primarily in the domain of texture and groove — the combination of timbres, rhythms, and vocal expression that produces an immediate physical and emotional response. Classical music adds a third dimension: extended formal development over time. A symphony develops its themes, transforms them, creates tension through harmonic movement, and resolves that tension in ways that unfold over 30–45 minutes. The pleasure is architectural — the satisfaction of a large structure that holds together and reveals itself gradually.

This is why classical music rewards repeated listening more than almost any other genre. The first hearing produces orientation. The second produces recognition. The third begins to reveal the structural decisions beneath the surface. The tenth hearing produces a relationship with the work rather than merely a response to it.

The Entry Points — Start Here

🎵 Bach — Brandenburg Concerto No. 3

The mathematical architecture of Baroque music at its most immediate. A conversation between string instruments operating at different scales simultaneously. No heavy emotional content — pure structural ingenuity that produces a physical pleasure in its own precision. If classical music feels cold and distant, Bach is often the composer that changes that because the pleasure is so clearly in the craft rather than in emotional manipulation.

🎵 Mozart — Piano Concerto No. 21 (K.467)

The slow movement (Andante) of this concerto is the most immediately beautiful thing Mozart wrote — which is saying something. The entry point for listeners who find Baroque music too dry and Romantic music too melodramatic. Mozart operates in a zone of perfect balance between emotional warmth and formal elegance. The second movement here is 7 minutes of absolute clarity.

🎵 Beethoven — Symphony No. 5

The most famous opening in Western music (da-da-da-DUM) is famous because it earns its fame: that four-note motif is developed, transformed, fragmented, and recombined across all four movements in a way that is genuinely unified. Not just dramatic — structurally coherent. Beethoven is the composer who expanded what music could carry emotionally and formally, and the Fifth is where to start understanding why.

🎵 Chopin — Nocturne in E-flat major (Op. 9 No. 2)

Already in the Music tab as a focus work recommendation — worth expanding here. Chopin wrote almost exclusively for solo piano, and the Nocturnes are his most intimate works. The Op. 9 No. 2 is 4 minutes of the most perfectly calibrated emotional expression in the piano repertoire. The right hand sings; the left hand provides a rocking accompaniment that suggests something between a lullaby and an elegy. Listen once with full attention and nothing else happening.

🎵 Debussy — Clair de Lune

The most-streamed classical piece annually. Deserves its popularity — it is genuinely extraordinary. Debussy rejected the German classical tradition and absorbed influences from Asian music, creating harmonies that float without the need to resolve in the way German harmony demands. The result sounds modern in a way that music from the same period (1905) often does not. The entry point for listeners who find older classical music too rigid or predictable.

🎵 Arvo Part — Spiegel im Spiegel

For the listener who finds historical classical music too busy: Part's tintinnabuli style reduces music to its near-minimum — a piano note, a violin line, silence. 10 minutes of extreme quiet that produces a meditative state unachievable through any busier music. Contemporary (written 1978), accessible to anyone regardless of classical music exposure, and genuinely one of the most beautiful pieces written in the last 50 years.

The Listening Method

  • First listening: no score, no analysis, no background reading. Simply hear it. Note what produces a physical or emotional response — a moment of tension, a surprising harmonic shift, a melody that catches the attention. These moments are the compass points for subsequent listening.
  • Read a brief programme note before the second listening. Not a musicological analysis — a paragraph about what the work is and what it was attempting. The Oxford Music Online (available free through library access) and AllMusic both provide this.
  • Third listening: follow one instrument. In an orchestra, follow only the cellos, or only the oboe. The ability to isolate one voice from a texture is a learnable skill that reveals the compositional decisions underlying what initially sounds like a single unified sound.
  • Use Spotify's Classical section or Apple Classical. Apple Classical is the most comprehensively curated classical streaming platform available — the metadata (performers, conductors, recording dates) is detailed enough to enable genuine comparative listening between different recordings of the same work.

Classical Music & Sarajevo

  • Sarajevo Philharmonic Orchestra (Sarajevska filharmonija): One of the oldest orchestras in the Balkans, founded 1923. Regular concert season from autumn through spring at the National Theatre (Narodno Pozorište). Ticket prices are accessible by European standards. Attending a live orchestra performance is a categorically different experience from recorded listening — the physical presence of the sound in a hall, the visible coordination of musicians, the irreversibility of the live moment.
  • Chamber music at Vijećnica and other venues: The Sarajevo Winter (Sarajevska zima) festival in February includes classical music programming across multiple venues including the Vijećnica. The acoustic of the restored main hall is excellent.
Classical music requires patience in the way that a long conversation with an intelligent person requires patience — you must stay present through the development to understand what was built. The man who gives it that patience discovers that it gives back more than any shorter form of music can. This is not an opinion about taste. It is a statement about what extended formal development can carry that a three-minute song structurally cannot.— Von Lycaon
Film literacy is the ability to watch a film as a made thing — to see the choices behind what appears on screen. Camera angle, editing rhythm, sound design, colour palette, performance direction — none of these are accidental in serious cinema. Developing this literacy does not reduce the pleasure of watching. It multiplies it. The film that worked on you emotionally becomes more rather than less interesting when you can identify how it did it.— Von Lycaon

The Grammar of Film

The Shot

The basic unit of film. Every shot decision communicates: Wide shot — context, environment, scale. Medium shot — social interaction, body language. Close-up — emotion, interiority, revelation. Extreme close-up — intensity, detail, discomfort. The camera angle adds another layer: high angle diminishes the subject; low angle empowers it; eye level is neutral and democratic. These are not rules — they are a vocabulary. A director who breaks them is making a choice against the expectation they have established.

Editing

The assembly of shots into sequence is where film becomes film rather than photography. The cut creates time, space, and meaning from the relationship between two images. The Kuleshov effect: the same shot of a man's neutral face was perceived as expressing hunger (next to food), grief (next to a coffin), or desire (next to a woman) solely based on what followed it. Editing is the tool that makes the audience construct meaning — the best editing is invisible because it feels inevitable.

Sound

Underestimated by most film viewers. Dialogue is the least interesting element of a film's sound design. The sound of a particular space, the choice of what sounds are emphasised or suppressed, the use of silence — these produce the felt experience of a film as powerfully as the image. Watch the opening of No Country for Old Men with the sound off, then with it on. The landscape becomes a different thing.

Colour & Light

The cinematographer's primary tools for mood and meaning. Warm tones (amber, orange) — nostalgia, comfort, danger. Cool tones (blue, green) — distance, unease, technology. Desaturated palettes — reality, documentary, grimness. High contrast light — drama, moral clarity or moral ambiguity depending on context. Low contrast, diffused light — dreamlike, uncertain, past. These associations are not absolute — their power comes from how they are used within a specific film's established visual logic.

The Essential Viewing List

Not the "greatest films of all time" — a curated list specifically designed to build film literacy across different approaches, styles, and traditions. Each one teaches something specific about what film can do.

🎬 Citizen Kane (1941) — Welles

Not the greatest film ever made, but the film that teaches the most about filmmaking. Welles and cinematographer Gregg Toland invented or consolidated more techniques in one film than most directors use in a career. Deep focus photography, non-linear narrative, low angles, ceilings in the frame (unprecedented — sets rarely had them). Watch with the specific intention of noticing what is strange about how it was shot.

🎬 2001: A Space Odyssey (1968) — Kubrick

The film that most radically demonstrates what cinema can do without dialogue. The first 20 minutes have no speech. The entire film operates primarily through image, sound, and music. Kubrick's use of classical music (Strauss, Ligeti) as the primary emotional vehicle rather than a composed score is the most influential sound decision in science fiction cinema.

🎬 Stalker (1979) — Tarkovsky

The slowest great film — deliberately, strategically slow. Tarkovsky believed that cinema's unique capacity was to sculpt time, and Stalker is the fullest realisation of that belief. Three men walking through a dangerous zone. The pace forces the viewer to attend to the image itself rather than narrative momentum. If 2001 is the gateway to Kubrick, Stalker is the gateway to a different conception of what cinema is for.

🎬 In the Mood for Love (2000) — Wong Kar-Wai

The most beautiful use of colour, slow motion, and music in modern cinema. A film about what is not said and not done — restraint as the primary emotional register. Tony Leung and Maggie Cheung in a 1960s Hong Kong corridor. The specific quality of longing that this film produces is available from almost no other source. Demonstrates that style is not separate from meaning — here they are identical.

🎬 No Country for Old Men (2007) — Coen Brothers

The most technically accomplished American film of the 2000s. Roger Deakins' cinematography of the West Texas landscape. The deliberate absence of a conventional film score — ambient sound only. Javier Bardem's performance as the most frightening film villain of the era. Study the editing: the Coens cut around violence rather than showing it, which makes it more rather than less disturbing. The film is a masterclass in what not to show.

🎬 Parasite (2019) — Bong Joon-ho

The most precisely constructed screenplay of recent cinema. Every detail introduced in the first act is used in the third. The architecture of the two houses — the upper house flooded with light, the basement apartment below the street — is the film's central visual argument about class. Bong controls information with exceptional precision. Watch twice: the second viewing reveals the architecture of the first.

Watching Actively

  • Watch with subtitles in the original language for any film not in your native language. Dubbing replaces the performance — the actor's voice is the performance, not merely its delivery mechanism.
  • Watch important films twice. First viewing for the story. Second viewing for the craft. The second viewing of a great film almost always produces a more interesting experience than the first because you can attend to how it works rather than what happens.
  • Read one piece of writing about a film after watching it. Roger Ebert's reviews (all available free at rogerebert.com) are the most consistently useful because Ebert wrote about film as a humanist rather than as a formalist — he was always asking what the film was about rather than only how it was made.
  • Sarajevo Film Festival (SFF): One of the most significant film festivals in Europe, held every August. Accessible to city residents at standard ticket prices. The competition programme introduces films months before wider distribution. Living in the city that hosts this festival and not attending it is an oversight worth correcting.
A film watched actively is a collaboration between the filmmaker and the viewer. The filmmaker provides the images and sounds; the viewer provides the meaning they construct from them. A passive viewer receives a story. An active viewer has an experience of cinema — which is different in kind, not only in degree.— Von Lycaon

Bosnian Cinema

The cinema of Bosnia and Herzegovina is one of the most internationally significant small national cinemas in the world, in proportion to the country's population. Danis Tanovic's No Man's Land (2001) won the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film and the Palme d'Or at Cannes — an exceptional double for a small country's first major international production. Understanding the context of Bosnian cinema provides both a window into Bosnian cultural identity and a set of films of genuine artistic merit available without subtitles (or with Bosnian subtitles that are readable).

  • No Man's Land (2001) — Danis Tanovic: Two soldiers, one Bosnian and one Bosnian Serb, trapped in a trench together during the 1992–95 war, with a third soldier lying on an active landmine. A dark comedy and a tragedy simultaneously. The first and best film about the absurdity and futility of the Bosnian war made by someone inside it.
  • Grbavica (2006) — Jasmila Zbanic: A mother and daughter in post-war Sarajevo, the mother concealing a secret from the war. Won the Golden Bear at the Berlin Film Festival. The most sensitive and specific examination of what the war did to civilian women that exists in film form.
  • An Episode in the Life of an Iron Picker (2013) — Danis Tanovic: A Roma family in contemporary Bosnia facing a medical emergency with no health insurance. Shot in 12 days with non-professional actors playing themselves. Won the Silver Bear at Berlin. One of the most immediate and honest films about contemporary Bosnian poverty available.
  • Quo Vadis, Aida? (2020) — Jasmila Zbanic: The Srebrenica massacre of July 1995, told through the perspective of a UN translator trying to save her family. Nominated for the Academy Award for Best International Feature Film. The most important film yet made about the worst single atrocity of the Bosnian war.
Marriage is the longest collaboration a person enters. Its quality is not determined at the wedding — it is determined by ten thousand small decisions made between that day and the last: whether to listen or defend, whether to repair or accumulate, whether to choose the person again on the ordinary Tuesday when choosing feels like nothing in particular.— Von Lycaon

What Actually Determines Marriage Quality

John Gottman's research — the most rigorous longitudinal study of marriage available, tracking couples over decades — identified predictors of success and failure with over 90% accuracy. The findings are counterintuitive in important ways.

The 5:1 Ratio

Stable couples maintain approximately five positive interactions for every one negative. Not the absence of conflict — the presence of sufficient positive connection that conflict does not define the relationship's texture.
