Couples who argue but maintain the ratio are more stable than couples who rarely argue but have low positive interaction overall.

The Four Horsemen

Four patterns that predict failure: Criticism (attacking the person not the behaviour), Contempt (superiority, mockery — the single strongest predictor of divorce), Defensiveness (meeting complaints with counter-complaints), Stonewalling (emotional withdrawal). Recognising these in yourself before your partner is the most practically useful relationship skill available.

Bids for Connection

Every day, partners make hundreds of small bids for connection — a comment about something seen outside, a question about the day, a touch on the shoulder. Turning toward (acknowledging), turning away (ignoring), or turning against (responding negatively) — this response pattern is the primary mechanism by which intimacy is built or eroded over years.

Repair Attempts

During conflict, one or both partners will gesture toward de-escalation. These attempts succeed in happy couples not because they are more skilled but because the relationship has enough positive history that the attempt is received rather than rejected. The repair is only as available as the history that preceded it.

The Daily Marriage

Non-Negotiable Daily Practices

  • Six-second kiss. Gottman's specific recommendation. A deliberate kiss that requires actual attention. Six seconds is long enough to mean something. Its cumulative effect over years is not proportionate to its apparent size.
  • The stress-reducing conversation. At least 20 minutes daily about something other than logistics — not scheduling, not bills, not what needs doing. What is actually happening in each other's inner life. The mechanism by which two people remain known to each other over time rather than merely cohabiting.
  • Specific appreciation. Not "you're great." "The way you handled that situation yesterday — I noticed how patient you were." Specific appreciation demonstrates that the other person is actually being seen.
  • Non-sexual physical affection. Touch that is not a prelude to anything — a hand on the back, sitting close. The accumulation maintains a baseline of physical intimacy that is distinct from and equally important to sexual intimacy.

Conflict — The Correct Framework

Solvable vs Perpetual Problems

  • Approximately 69% of relationship conflicts are perpetual problems — ongoing differences in personality, values, or needs that will never be resolved. Couples who are happy have learned to manage these dialogues rather than solve them. Couples who are unhappy continue trying to solve the unsolvable.
  • Identifying a perpetual problem: If you have had the same argument more than three times without resolution, it is likely perpetual. The correct response is not to argue harder — it is to understand each other's position more deeply and find accommodation.
  • Solvable problems: Soft startup ("I feel" not "you always"), take responsibility for your contribution first, agree on a specific behaviour change rather than a general intention.

When the Argument Has Started

  • Physiological flooding: When heart rate exceeds approximately 100 BPM during conflict, the prefrontal cortex's regulatory function degrades significantly. The argument cannot be productive. Call for a break explicitly — "I need 20 minutes" — and use those 20 minutes for genuine physiological calming, not rehearsing arguments.
  • Attack the problem, never the person. "The kitchen being left like this is a problem for me" versus "you never clean up." The first is addressable. The second activates defensiveness.
  • Repair early. A repair attempt made early — even imperfect — is worth more than a perfect repair after two hours of escalation.

Growing Together

  • Shared meaning. The rituals, traditions, and shared understanding of what matters constitute the culture of a marriage. Couples who build this deliberately have a reference framework for decisions that couples without it must negotiate from scratch each time.
  • Support individual growth. A partner who feels their development is supported by the marriage is more invested in it than one who feels their growth is in tension with it.
  • Monthly deliberate check-in. A conversation specifically about the relationship — what is working, what needs attention, what each person wants more or less of. Not a performance review. Marriages that never have this conversation drift; those that do have navigational data.
The choice to marry is made once. The choice to be married — to turn toward rather than away, to repair rather than accumulate, to see the person rather than the familiar outline of a person — is made every day. The daily choice is quieter. It is also more consequential.— Von Lycaon

The Long Game

The research on long-term marriage satisfaction shows a consistent pattern: couples who maintain high relationship quality across decades share several behaviours that distinguish them from couples whose satisfaction erodes. None of these behaviours are dramatic. All of them are consistent.

  • Bids for connection: John Gottman's observation that couples in successful long-term marriages respond to their partner's bids for attention — a comment about the weather, a shared observation, a request for acknowledgment — by turning toward them rather than turning away or against. The bids are small; the accumulated response pattern over years determines relationship quality more reliably than any major relationship event.
  • The gratitude practice: Explicitly noting and expressing gratitude for specific things your wife does — not the large occasional things but the routine ones that are invisible because they are routine. The dinner made, the household task maintained, the emotional support provided. The hedonic adaptation that causes people to stop appreciating what is consistently present is counteracted only by deliberate attention and deliberate expression.
  • Shared meaning: Couples with explicit shared rituals, shared values about what matters, and shared narratives about their relationship history show greater resilience under stress than couples without these. The ritual does not need to be elaborate — Sunday morning coffee before phones are checked, a particular way of saying goodbye, an annual trip. The ritual's function is to be a recurring reminder that the relationship has its own culture distinct from the rest of life.
  • The repair attempt: Gottman's finding that what distinguishes successful from unsuccessful couples is not the absence of conflict but the success rate of repair attempts during conflict. A repair attempt is any action — a joke, a touch, an acknowledgment, a pause request — that attempts to de-escalate the tension during an argument. Successful couples have a high rate of successful repair attempts. Developing the skill of making and receiving repair attempts is more valuable than learning to avoid conflict.
Romance, conducted well, is a form of the same precision and attention that governs everything else in this codex. The gesture that lands is the gesture that was designed for the specific person receiving it — not the gesture that is generically considered romantic. Attention is the raw material. Everything else is expression. What follows is both practical guidance and the deeper philosophy behind why each act works. — Von Lycaon

The Foundational Principle

Most romantic failures are not failures of intention — they are failures of specificity. Flowers are not romantic in themselves. Flowers purchased because you remembered she mentioned three weeks ago that she finds white peonies disproportionately beautiful, presented without announcement, are romantic. The difference is entirely in the attention that preceded the act, not in the act itself.

Learn what matters to the specific person. Not what is supposed to matter. Not what worked with someone else. What this person notices, remembers, returns to. That knowledge, applied with care, is the entire architecture of romantic conduct. Everything below is technique in service of that foundation.

The Five Love Languages — Applied

Gary Chapman's framework is worth knowing not as a personality test but as a diagnostic tool. People tend to express love in the language they prefer to receive it — and to miss love expressed in a different language. Understanding which language your partner speaks and which you default to prevents the genuine confusion of two people who care for each other but cannot feel it.

Words of Affirmation

Verbal and written expressions of appreciation, admiration, and specific observation. Not "you look nice" — "the way you explained that to the room tonight was remarkable — you made something complicated sound completely clear." Specific compliments on character, not appearance alone.

Acts of Service

Doing something that removes a burden or adds ease to the other person's day. Without being asked. Without mentioning it afterwards. The gesture that is done and then left unacknowledged by the giver — only noticed by the recipient — is the most powerful form of this language.

Quality Time

Undivided, present attention. Not sitting in the same room while both are on phones. Eye contact, active listening, genuine engagement. For this person, half-attention is worse than no time at all. When you are with them, be with them entirely.

Physical Touch

Not merely intimate touch — the small, ambient expressions of physical connection. A hand on the shoulder passing by. Sitting close rather than apart. The hand extended without prompting. For this person, the absence of touch in daily life reads as emotional distance regardless of other expressions.

Receiving Gifts

The gift is not about cost — it is about the evidence of thought. Something small, perfectly chosen, presented at an unexpected moment outperforms an expensive gift given on a predictable occasion. The receipt of a gift demonstrates that someone thought of them when they were not present.

The Small Daily Gestures — Consistently Impactful

  • Remember what they mention once. When they mention something in passing — a film they want to see, a place they want to visit, a food they have been thinking about — write it down. Return to it unexpectedly weeks later. Nothing communicates sustained attention more clearly than the demonstration that you were listening when they spoke casually.
  • The morning message with no ask. A message sent in the morning that contains nothing but warmth — no question, no logistical content, no request. "Good morning. I hope your meeting goes well today." sent before they wake costs nothing and begins their day knowing they were thought of.
  • Make their coffee the way they take it without asking. This is not a small gesture — it is the daily evidence of paying attention. Every time it is done correctly without prompting, it communicates fluency in who they are.
  • Notice when they are tired before they say it. And respond to it — not by asking repeatedly "are you okay?" which transfers the labour of the emotion back to them, but by quietly adjusting the evening to be easier. Offering, not asking.
  • Speak well of them when they are not present. This is the gesture they will hear about eventually from others and never forget. The partner who speaks with genuine admiration and warmth about their person in their absence is the partner whose loyalty is beyond question.

Planned Gestures — Specific to Sarajevo

The Early Morning Walk
  • Baščaršija before 8am, before the tourist footfall. The kafanas are quiet, the cobblestones are empty, the light is particular
  • Bosanska kafa at a small kafana together. The ritual of the slow coffee is itself the gesture — unhurried time, with no agenda
  • Šeher-ćehajina česma fountain, the Baščaršija fountain — these are the ambient details that make a morning walk specific to this city and unrepeatable elsewhere

The early morning is the gesture. The city belongs to the two of you in a way it does not at any other hour.

The Language of Daily Love

The most important romantic acts are not the grand gestures — those are impressive and memorable but they occur rarely. The daily acts, accumulated over years, are the actual substance of a relationship. The man who surprises his wife with an anniversary trip but does not look up from his phone during dinner has the equation inverted.

  • The morning acknowledgment: Before checking the phone, before beginning any task — a specific acknowledgment of your wife's presence. Not "good morning" as a reflex, but a moment of actual attention. Her name. Eye contact. A question about how she slept or what she is thinking about. Two minutes. The cumulative effect of two minutes of genuine daily attention exceeds any occasional grand gesture.
  • Physical affection without agenda: Touch that is not a precursor to anything. A hand on the back while passing through a room. Sitting close on the sofa when you could sit anywhere. The six-second kiss (Gottman's research — kisses under two seconds are perfunctory; six seconds or longer produce measurable oxytocin release and serve the same bonding function as physical intimacy). These acts communicate physical affection that is not transactional.
  • Knowing what she is carrying: What is she worried about this week? What is she working on? What is making her tired? The man who knows the current emotional and practical contents of his wife's life is present in a way that the man who does not know is not, regardless of how much physical time they spend together. Ask. Remember. Refer to it in subsequent conversations. This is what being seen feels like.

Sarajevo Dates — Curated

The Vijećnica Evening

A cultural event at the Vijećnica (the City Hall) — concert, exhibition, or simply attending the space in the evening when it is lit. Walk along Obala through Baščaršija afterward. Coffee at Dibek or one of the traditional kafanas on the čaršija. The evening moves through five centuries of the city's history in two hours.

The Trebević Morning

The cable car from the lower station near Bistrik to the Trebević plateau. A morning walk on the summit, coffee at the planinski dom. The view of Sarajevo from above, the city visible in its valley. Return for lunch. The combination of physical activity, nature, and the specific quality of morning light on the mountain is one of the finest experiences available within 30 minutes of the city centre.

Cooking Together

Not going out -- cooking a significant meal at home together. The Bosnian cuisine tab and the Sauces tab provide material. The act of cooking together is collaborative, tactile, and produces a shared creation. It also produces a conversation that unfolds naturally over the duration of the preparation rather than being manufactured at a restaurant table. Cook something neither of you has made before -- the shared learning is the point.

The Pijaca and Market Morning

Markale on a Saturday morning. The seasonal produce, the vendors, the coffee from the nearby kafana, the people-watching. Then cooking what was bought -- the meal is the continuation of the morning. This is the most Sarajevo-specific date available and one that most residents of the city never organise deliberately, though the elements are present every week.

Understanding Her Love Language in Practice

Gary Chapman's five love languages (Words of Affirmation, Acts of Service, Receiving Gifts, Quality Time, Physical Touch) are a simplified but practically useful model. The key insight: people tend to express love in their own primary language rather than their partner's. The result: genuine acts of love that are not received as such because they are not in the language the recipient primarily speaks. Identifying your wife's primary language -- through observation (what does she request most? what does she complain about the absence of most?) -- and expressing love deliberately in that language produces a qualitatively different relationship than expressing love in your own language and assuming it is received.

Romance is not an event. It is the texture of daily life -- the accumulated evidence that your wife is seen, considered, and chosen. The grand gesture is the exclamation point. The daily attention is the sentence it concludes.-- Von Lycaon
A gift given without thought is a transaction. A gift given with genuine attention to the recipient is a statement that you have been paying attention — that you know them well enough to select something that fits. The price is almost never the variable that matters. The consideration is.— Von Lycaon

The Philosophy of Gifting

The Observation Method

The best gift is usually mentioned months before it is given. "I've always wanted to try..." "I need to replace my..." "I saw this and..." — these statements, noted and remembered, produce gifts that feel almost telepathic. Keep a note in Obsidian labelled with the person's name. Add to it when these statements occur. Gift-giving becomes effortless when the research was done passively over months.

Experience Over Object

For most adults who have what they need, an experience — dinner at a restaurant they would not choose for themselves, a day trip, a class in something they mentioned wanting to try — produces more lasting positive memory than an equivalent-value object. Objects accumulate. Experiences compound as memories.

The Presentation

The wrapping is not decoration — it is the first communication of how much attention was paid. An unwrapped gift in a plastic bag communicates the same thing regardless of what is inside. Clean paper, a ribbon, a handwritten card. The card specifically: handwritten, specific, not a greeting card sentiment. Two sentences about why this gift, for this person, at this time.

Timing and Occasion

Birthdays, anniversaries, and holidays are the obligatory gifting occasions. The more memorable gift is the one with no occasion — the thing you saw that made you think of them, given on an ordinary Tuesday. The element of surprise amplifies the felt experience of being thought of disproportionately to the cost of the gift itself.

Gift Framework by Person

Wife — Gifts That Land

  • The rule: never appliances. A kitchen appliance, a cleaning tool, anything that implies her domestic labour is the gift — even if it is something she specifically wants — carries an unfortunate implication when given on a significant occasion. Save these for ordinary days when she has expressed specific need.
  • What consistently works: Something she mentioned wanting but would not buy for herself (the observation method above). An experience you will share together — dinner somewhere she would not choose for cost reasons, a weekend away. A piece of jewellery or accessory chosen specifically — not a gift voucher. Something connected to an interest she has developed recently.
  • Anniversary specifically: The gift should mark the year. Something that references where you were, what you experienced, or what the year meant. A framed photograph from the year. A piece from a place you visited. A handwritten letter describing the year from your perspective. These cost nothing and are irreplaceable.

Parents and Family

  • For parents: Time and attention are often more valuable than objects. A meal you cook for them. A day trip organised. A framed photograph of the family from a recent occasion. Older generations frequently do not need objects — they need presence.
  • For siblings or peers: The observation method applies here equally. A book by an author they mentioned. A voucher for an experience in their field of interest. A thoughtful consumable — quality coffee, good food from the Cooking or Baking tabs — for the person who is difficult to buy for.
  • Sarajevo-specific occasions: Bajram (Eid) and family celebration visits carry the expectation of sweets or pastries brought from a quality slastičarna. Čokolada or baklava from a recognised Sarajevo establishment is always appropriate. Arrival empty-handed to a significant celebration is always noticed.

Special Occasions — Sarajevo Calendar

💍 Anniversary

Traditional anniversary gifts by year exist but are not binding. The meaningful gift references the specific year of the marriage. If travel occurred — something from the destination. If a significant event happened — something that marks it. A handwritten letter remains the most intimate gift available at any year, at any cost. Do not underestimate it.

🎂 Birthday

The observation method applied. One gift chosen with thought is superior to multiple gifts chosen without it. The birthday dinner — regardless of whether you cook or take her out — should be the meal she would choose, not the meal you prefer. Her day. Her choices. Your execution.

🌹 Valentine's Day

Not a day requiring elaborate gesture — it is a day requiring deliberate attention. Flowers (a single stem of something specific and beautiful rather than a supermarket dozen), a handwritten note, a meal. The cliché gifts — chocolates from a petrol station, a mass-produced card — communicate that the obligation was discharged rather than the person was considered.

🏠 Housewarming / Host Gift

When visiting someone's home for the first time or for a significant dinner: quality olive oil, a good bottle of something non-alcoholic (premium juice, quality syrup for drinks), or quality sweets. Plants are increasingly popular and appropriate. The gift should be usable, not decorative — decorative gifts impose on the recipient's space.

The gift tells the recipient who you believe them to be and how closely you have been paying attention. Choose accordingly.— Von Lycaon

The Psychology of Giving

Research by Cassie Mogilner and colleagues on gift giving reveals a consistent finding: givers systematically overestimate how much recipients value material gifts and underestimate how much they value experiential gifts and the gesture of remembrance itself. The most remembered gifts are almost never the most expensive ones -- they are the ones that demonstrated that the giver was paying attention.

  • The observation journal: When someone mentions something they want, need, or would enjoy -- in passing, not as a direct request -- write it in your phone's notes immediately. "She mentioned wanting to try the new cafe near the BBI Centre." "He said he's been meaning to read Meditations." "She said she misses having fresh flowers in the house." These notes, accumulated over months, produce a gift list of things that will be received with genuine surprise and appreciation rather than polite acknowledgment.
  • The experience over object principle: Experiences are valued more highly than objects of equivalent cost both at the time of receiving and in retrospect. An evening at the Sarajevo Philharmonic, a cooking class, a wine tasting, a weekend trip to Mostar -- these create memories that a physical object cannot. The exception: an object that enables or accompanies an experience the person values (a book by an author they love, a tool for a hobby they are developing) combines both categories.

Wife -- The Gift Philosophy

Gifts for your wife operate on a different calculus from gifts for anyone else. The frequency is higher, the personal knowledge is deeper, and the cumulative effect of consistent thoughtfulness is more significant than any individual gift. The principles:

  • The non-occasion gift: A gift given on no occasion is more meaningful than one given on a required occasion, because it communicates that she was thought of specifically rather than that a date triggered an obligation. A book she mentioned. A flower from the pijaca. A coffee left ready for her in the morning. These non-occasion gifts cost little and communicate continuously.
  • Presentation always matters: The same gift presented carelessly and presented beautifully are received differently. Wrapping paper, a ribbon, a handwritten card with something specific about why this gift and why now -- these elements take ten minutes and multiply the received value of the gift.
  • Bosnian occasion calendar: Bajram (gift giving within the family -- including spouse), Saint Valentine's Day (broadly observed in Sarajevo's commercial culture), the anniversary (both the marriage anniversary and the anniversary of when you met -- knowing and marking both communicates that you hold the history of the relationship as important), her birthday, and the occasional "no reason" gift. These occasions should never be missed through distraction or forgetting. They are in the calendar with a two-day advance reminder.

The Thoughtful Gift Process

  • Listen for 90 days: The best gifts are assembled from 90 days of genuine attention to what a person mentions, enjoys, needs, or aspires to. Most gift failures come from searching for a gift in the week before an occasion rather than noticing the gift over months of presence.
  • When uncertain: An experience together is always correct when a specific gift is not identified. The choice communicates that you prioritise shared time over a purchased object, which is both philosophically correct and practically safe. A dinner at a restaurant you have not tried, a day trip somewhere neither of you has been, a cultural event she has mentioned.
The perfect gift is the evidence of attention accumulated over time and delivered as a surprise. It cannot be manufactured at the point of giving. It requires the same daily investment as every other relationship practice in this Codex: presence, observation, and memory.-- Von Lycaon
This tab is written for the version of you that does not yet exist but is thinking about it. Fatherhood is the most significant role a man will ever take on — not in the dramatic sense, but in the daily, compounding, decades-long sense. The preparation is not a checklist of purchases. It is a deliberate examination of what kind of father you intend to be before the reality of it makes deliberate examination difficult. Think about it now, while thinking is still easy.— Von Lycaon

Before — What To Consider Now

The Marriage Foundation First

The research on child outcomes is unambiguous: the quality of the parental relationship is the strongest predictor of child psychological development — more than income, more than education, more than parenting technique. A child raised by two parents who genuinely respect and care for each other has a developmental advantage that no parenting method can fully replicate. The work done in this Codex on the relationship — the romantic gestures, the presence, the partnership in the household — is already the most important parenting preparation available.

Financial Preparation

A child born in Sarajevo incurs immediate costs: hospital fees (partially covered by KCUS but with private clinic options), equipment (crib, pram, car seat if a car is obtained, clothing that is replaced every few months), and ongoing food, healthcare, and activity costs. The emergency fund from the Finance tab should be at its full 6-month threshold before a child arrives — not because emergencies become more likely but because the capacity to absorb them while managing new costs needs to be pre-established.

Physical Capability

The goal described throughout this Codex — carrying your wife, moving well — applies with greater urgency to fatherhood. A father carries children. He gets down on the floor and gets back up repeatedly. He lifts, he chases, he plays at a physical intensity that outlasts what most sedentary adults can sustain. The workout programme and the mobility work are not vanity projects in this context. They are functional preparation for a physically demanding role.

Emotional Preparation

The aspect most men do not prepare for: the emotional intensity of fatherhood. The vulnerability of loving something so completely is unlike any other relationship. It produces irrational fear, profound tenderness, and a capacity for patience that will be tested far beyond what daily life currently demands. Reading about this does not prepare you — but naming it does. The man who expects emotional difficulty handles it differently than the man who is surprised by it.

The Father's Role — What Actually Matters

Child development research has produced consistent findings over decades. The following are the parental behaviours with the strongest evidence base for child outcomes — not parenting philosophies, not techniques, but specific observable behaviours.

Presence — Physical and Psychological

  • Being physically present is necessary but not sufficient. A father who is in the same building but on his phone is not present in the developmental sense. The quality of attention during shared time matters more than its quantity — though quantity matters too. Twenty minutes of fully present engagement produces more developmental benefit than two hours of co-existing in the same room with divided attention.
  • Get on the floor. Literally. Child's play happens at floor level. A father who participates in play at the child's physical level communicates something non-verbal about the relationship that standing over and directing does not. This sounds small. The research on play engagement suggests it is not.
  • The phone is the primary presence competitor. Not work, not other relationships — the phone. A child who repeatedly seeks a parent's attention and finds the parent looking at a screen learns something specific about their own value relative to that screen. This is one of the most important sentences in this tab.

Emotional Availability

  • Name emotions — your own and theirs. Children whose parents name emotions accurately and openly — "I'm feeling frustrated right now" — develop superior emotional regulation, social competence, and resilience compared to children raised in emotionally unacknowledged environments. This is not softness. It is the foundation of psychological capability.
  • Repair after conflict. No parent manages every moment perfectly. The quality of parenting is measured not by the absence of difficult moments but by the quality of repair after them. An apology to a child — genuine, specific, not explained away — models the exact behaviour described in the Social Skills tab. Children who receive genuine apologies from parents learn to give them.
  • Physical affection consistently. Hugging, physical reassurance, the rough-and-tumble physical play that fathers specifically tend to provide — all produce measurable benefits in stress regulation, immune function, and social development in children. Do not underestimate its importance.

The Father's Specific Contribution

  • Research distinguishes what fathers specifically contribute to child development: physical play that tests boundaries and builds resilience, exposure to mild controlled risk, modelling of how a man manages difficulty with composure, and the experience of a relationship with male authority that is safe and trustworthy rather than threatening. These are not replaceable by other adults in the same way.
  • Read to them. From birth. Before they can understand the words. The rhythm of language, the sound of a calm voice, the association of the parent's presence with narrative — all of this begins building the neural architecture for language and reading years before school does. The Reading tab in this Codex begins with a child, not at school.
  • Let them see you learning. A child who watches a parent read, attempt new things, make mistakes and continue — inherits a relationship with learning that no instruction can install. The self-education practices in the Learning tab, practised visibly, are one of the most effective things a parent can do for a child's intellectual development.

The Practical Preparation Checklist

  • Discuss explicitly with your wife: parenting philosophy, division of night-time responsibilities, how family decisions will be made, what role extended family will play in childcare. These conversations are significantly harder after the child arrives when everyone is sleep-deprived and operating on instinct.
  • First aid certification before the baby arrives. The Crveni križ (Red Cross BiH) infant CPR course is different from adult CPR — infant airway size, compression depth, and rescue breath technique all differ. This is not the same course mentioned in the First Aid tab. Book it separately.
  • Maternity/paternity rights in BiH: Maternity leave (porodiljsko odsustvo) in FBiH is 12 months. Paternity rights are more limited — understand what your employer's policy is. The financial planning for a period of reduced household income requires advance preparation in Account 2 and 3.
  • The sleep preparation: Begin protecting sleep with greater urgency before the child arrives. Sleep deprivation with a newborn is a given — arriving at that period from a baseline of well-established sleep hygiene handles it significantly better than arriving from a baseline of already compromised sleep.

Books Worth Reading Before Fatherhood

  • The Whole-Brain Child — Daniel Siegel & Tina Payne Bryson: Neuroscience-based parenting strategies for different developmental stages. Written accessibly without condescension. The most practically useful parenting book available.
  • How to Talk So Kids Will Listen — Faber & Mazlish: Communication techniques that actually work with children at different developmental stages. The principles overlap significantly with the Social Skills tab — reflective listening, acknowledgment before solution, respecting autonomy.
  • The Book You Wish Your Parents Had Read — Philippa Perry: On the intergenerational transmission of psychological patterns. Understanding what you inherited from your own upbringing is relevant preparation for not transmitting the unhelpful parts to the next generation.
A child does not need a perfect father. Perfection in parenting is a myth that produces anxiety rather than capability. A child needs a present father — one who shows up, who repairs when he fails, who grows alongside the child rather than expecting to arrive at fatherhood already complete. The man this Codex is building is already more prepared than he knows.— Von Lycaon
Adult friendship requires deliberate effort in a way that childhood friendship does not. The shared context that produced childhood and school friendships — the same building, the same schedule, the same life stage — disappears after education ends. What remains are people who matter to you, separated by the increasing friction of adult life, gradually becoming strangers unless someone decides otherwise. That someone needs to be you as much as them.— Von Lycaon

Why Adult Friendship Fades

Three structural conditions produce friendship naturally: proximity (living or working near each other), repeated unplanned interaction (encountering each other regularly without scheduling it), and a setting that encourages vulnerability (shared challenge, shared experience). Adult life systematically removes all three. Understanding this removes self-blame from the equation — the friendships that faded were not failures of character. They were failures to replace the structural conditions that no longer existed automatically.

The Dunbar Layers

Robin Dunbar's research on social group size: an inner circle of approximately 5 (intimate relationships — intense contact, emotional support), a sympathy group of approximately 15 (people you would genuinely help in a crisis), an affinity group of approximately 50 (regular contact, meaningful relationship), and an active network of approximately 150. Most adults lose track of which layer each person occupies and therefore invest inconsistently. Knowing the layers allows deliberate investment at the right level.

The Investment Asymmetry

Most people assume that if a friendship matters, it will sustain itself. It will not. The research on friendship maintenance consistently shows that the person who initiates contact more often is perceived as the better friend — regardless of who actually cares more. The friction of initiation is what separates maintained friendships from lapsed ones. Initiate more than feels necessary.

Quality of Contact Over Frequency

A 90-minute dinner where both people are genuinely present and the conversation reaches depth is worth more to friendship maintenance than six brief check-in messages over the same period. Not because the messages are worthless — but because depth of contact builds the intimacy that sustains friendship through the gaps between contact. Prioritise depth when time is limited.

The Friendship Maintenance System

The Contact Calendar

  • Categorise your friendships by the contact frequency they require. Inner circle: weekly or bi-weekly contact minimum to maintain the relationship at that depth. Sympathy group: monthly. Affinity group: quarterly. Active network: annually at events or occasions.
  • In Obsidian, keep a simple contact log. Name, last contact, next intended contact. Not a corporate CRM — a personal record that prevents the six-month gap that neither party intended but neither prevented.
  • The low-friction touch: A message when something reminds you of them. An article relevant to their interests. A voice note rather than a text. These do not require scheduling and accumulate into a pattern of consistent presence that the recipient experiences as care.

Building New Friendships as an Adult

  • Shared activity over shared proximity. Without the structural conditions of youth, new friendships in adulthood form most reliably through repeated shared activity — a running group, a class, a team sport, a regular event. The Toastmasters Sarajevo chapter, a martial arts gym, a book club, a cooking class. The activity is the pretext; the repeated contact is the mechanism.
  • Be the one who proposes the next thing. After any positive social interaction — a good conversation at an event, a colleague you have enjoyed working with — propose a specific next contact. Not "we should get coffee sometime" (which means nothing) but "are you free Friday after work?" The specificity is the difference between intention and action.
  • Reciprocal vulnerability. Friendships deepen through appropriate self-disclosure — sharing something genuine, something that required some trust to say. This is not therapy-level disclosure on first meeting. It is the gradual raising of the honesty level in conversation that signals that the relationship is more than surface. If every conversation with someone remains completely safe and surface-level, the friendship will remain surface-level.

The Sarajevo Social Context

  • The kafana and café culture is the primary social infrastructure of Sarajevo male friendship. The standard of regular dođi na kafu already in the Social Skills tab applies here — maintaining the habit of regular coffee with friends is the Sarajevo-specific friendship maintenance mechanism. Let it be regular rather than exceptional.
  • Sports as social infrastructure. A regular weekly football game, a gym partner, a hiking group on Bjelašnica — these provide the repeated unplanned-feeling interaction (even when planned) that adult friendship formation requires. The activity is almost irrelevant. The consistency is everything.
  • Be a host. The Hosting tab describes the mechanics. The friendship benefit of hosting is significant — people remember where they were welcomed. Hosting once quarterly creates a rhythm of connection that individual one-on-one meetings cannot fully replicate.
A man without genuine friendship is a man whose interior life has no external witness. The work in this Codex — the growth, the discipline, the becoming — means something different when there are people present to observe it, challenge it, and share in it. Build those people into your life with the same deliberateness you build everything else.— Von Lycaon

Friendship and the City

Sarajevo is a small city with a high density of social connection — the kafana, the mahala culture, the specific Sarajevo practice of running into acquaintances on Ferhadija with a frequency that surprises visitors from larger cities. This density is both an asset and a pressure. The asset: spontaneous social connection is structurally easy. The pressure: the expectation of social visibility means that selective withdrawal is noticed and interpreted. Understanding both sides of this dynamic allows you to use the asset without being constrained by the pressure.

  • The Sarajevo social geography: Different parts of the city attract different social groups at different times. Baščaršija in the evening is the social mixing ground — all demographics, all ages. Ferhadija during the day is the professional mid-range. Specific kafanas have specific regulars — knowing which kafana belongs to which social circle is local intelligence that develops over time and is worth developing deliberately.
  • Maintaining connections across the diaspora: Sarajevo has one of the highest diaspora-to-resident ratios of any European capital — a significant proportion of the educated population has left for Germany, Austria, Sweden, and other destinations. Maintaining these connections across distance is not merely sentimental; it is practically valuable. The Sarajevo diaspora network is professionally active and personally loyal. A WhatsApp group is not a friendship — but a call once a month and a meeting when someone returns is.
Conflict avoided is not conflict resolved. It is conflict stored — accumulating interest until it surfaces at a moment, scale, and intensity disproportionate to whatever trivial event finally triggered it. The man who learns to navigate conflict directly, calmly, and with precision does not have fewer conflicts. He has shorter, cleaner ones that resolve rather than resurface.— Von Lycaon

The Anatomy of an Argument

Most arguments follow a predictable escalation pattern. Understanding the stages allows intervention at the earliest point — which is always easier and more effective than intervention at later stages.

Stage 1 — Trigger

Something happens that activates a negative emotional response. The trigger is rarely the actual issue — it is the surface event that touches something underneath. The person who argues about dishes is usually arguing about feeling unseen or undervalued. Understanding the layer beneath the trigger is more useful than debating the trigger itself.

Stage 2 — Escalation

Both parties begin defending positions rather than exploring the actual issue. Language shifts from specific to general ("you always," "you never"), from present to historical ("and remember when..."), from behaviour to identity ("you are the kind of person who..."). Each of these shifts makes resolution harder. Recognise them and refuse to use them.

Stage 3 — Flooding

Physiological overwhelm — heart rate, cortisol, adrenaline at levels that functionally impair rational processing. Both parties are now operating on instinct rather than reason. No productive resolution occurs at this stage. The correct intervention is a break — not a win.

Stage 4 — Recovery or Accumulation

Either the conflict is genuinely resolved and released, or it is suppressed and added to the ledger of unresolved grievances. Couples who accumulate unresolved conflicts develop a negative filter — new events are interpreted through the accumulated grievances rather than on their own merits. The ledger is the relationship's most dangerous document.

Conflict Resolution Tools

Before the Difficult Conversation

  • Choose the moment. A difficult conversation initiated immediately after someone returns from work, when either party is hungry, tired, or in a public space will produce a worse outcome than the same conversation initiated after dinner in a calm environment. Timing is not avoidance — it is calibration.
  • Know what you actually want. Before beginning, answer this question honestly: what is the outcome I am seeking? Behaviour change? Understanding? An apology? If the answer is "to express my frustration" — valid, but know that this is different from seeking resolution and manage expectations accordingly.
  • The XYZ formula: "When you do X in situation Y, I feel Z." Specific behaviour, specific context, specific emotional impact. Not "you always make me feel..." which is unfalsifiable and general. "When you looked at your phone while I was telling you about my day, I felt like what I was saying didn't matter to you." This is addressable.

During the Conversation

  • Listen to understand, not to respond. The most common conversation failure is listening for the pause that signals your turn to speak rather than listening for what is actually being communicated. The practice: after the other person finishes, summarise what you heard before responding. "What I'm hearing is that you felt..." This demonstrates listening and allows correction of misunderstanding before it escalates.
  • Take ownership before requesting change. "I have been [contribution to the problem] and I recognise that. What I need is [specific request]." The acknowledgment of your own contribution disarms defensiveness and models the behaviour you are requesting.
  • Distinguish impact from intent. "I know you didn't mean to make me feel dismissed — but the impact was that I felt dismissed." People defend their intentions. People can accept impact. The distinction allows the conversation to move forward rather than stalling on "but I didn't mean to."
  • The 24-hour rule for significant conflicts. For conflicts involving a significant decision, a major grievance, or elevated emotional states — agree to return to the conversation in 24 hours. Use the interval for genuine reflection rather than argument preparation. The second conversation is almost always more productive than the first.

Professional Conflict

  • Disagree privately first. Take disagreement with a colleague or manager to them directly before raising it elsewhere. Public disagreement creates defensiveness and damages professional relationships in ways that private conversation rarely does.
  • Separate the relationship from the issue. "I have significant respect for your work. I disagree with this specific decision." Conflating the relationship and the issue makes every disagreement feel like a personal attack. Keep them separate explicitly.
  • The interest behind the position. In professional conflict, both parties have a stated position and an underlying interest. The position is what they say they want. The interest is why they want it. Agreement is often impossible at the position level and straightforward at the interest level. Ask "what are you most trying to protect or achieve here?" to surface the interest.
  • Document significant workplace conflicts. A brief dated note after any significant professional disagreement — what was discussed, what was agreed, who was present. Not to build a case — to have an accurate record if the situation escalates and memory becomes contested.
The man who can navigate conflict without losing his composure or his integrity holds an advantage in every relationship he inhabits. Not because he wins arguments — but because people who disagree with him do not dread the disagreement. That quality is rarer than it should be and more valuable than most people recognise.— Von Lycaon

The Anatomy of a Productive Disagreement

Most conflicts are not about what they appear to be about on the surface. The surface issue (who forgot to do something, who said what to whom) is the trigger. The actual conflict is almost always about an unmet need, an unacknowledged feeling, or a perceived pattern that the surface issue represents. Resolving the surface issue without addressing the underlying dynamic produces a temporary settlement that the next surface issue immediately reopens.

The Gottman Four Horsemen -- Recognition and Response

Criticism: Attacking the person's character rather than the specific behaviour. "You never think of anyone but yourself" rather than "I felt dismissed when you didn't ask about my day." The antidote: a gentle startup that makes a complaint about a specific situation rather than a character judgment. Contempt: The most corrosive of the four -- eye-rolling, mockery, sarcasm that communicates superiority. The antidote: building a culture of appreciation that makes contempt contextually impossible. Defensiveness: Meeting a complaint with a counter-complaint rather than acknowledging any validity in the concern. The antidote: taking responsibility for even a portion of what is raised. Stonewalling: Emotional withdrawal, silence, leaving the room. Usually a flooding response (physiological overwhelm) rather than hostility. The antidote: taking a requested break with a clear return time.

Flooding -- The Physiological Reality

When heart rate exceeds approximately 100 BPM during a conflict, rational processing is significantly impaired. The prefrontal cortex's moderating influence on the amygdala's emotional response degrades, making thoughtful, measured communication genuinely difficult rather than merely inconvenient. The correct response to flooding is not to push through -- it is to request a break explicitly: "I need 20 minutes to calm down before we continue this conversation." The break is not avoidance. It is the precondition for a conversation that can actually produce resolution.

Perpetual vs Solvable Problems

Gottman's finding: 69% of relationship conflicts are perpetual -- they recur because they reflect fundamental differences in personality, values, or needs that will not change. The attempt to solve perpetual problems permanently produces chronic conflict. The goal for perpetual problems is not resolution but dialogue -- the development of a shared understanding that allows the difference to coexist without damaging the relationship. Solvable problems (the 31%) require specific solutions with clear commitments. Knowing which category a conflict belongs to determines the correct approach.

Professional Conflict

  • The documentation discipline: In any professional conflict involving a specific commitment, deliverable, or agreement -- send a follow-up email the same day confirming what was discussed and agreed. "Following our conversation today, I understand that X will be delivered by Y date and that I am responsible for Z." This is not aggressive or suspicious -- it is professional. It prevents the most common source of professional conflict: genuinely different recollections of what was agreed, both held sincerely.
  • The interest vs position distinction: Fisher and Ury's framework from Getting to Yes. In any negotiation or conflict, the stated position ("I need X") often conceals the underlying interest ("I need to feel that my contribution is recognised"). Addressing the interest rather than the position opens solutions that satisfying the stated position would not. Ask: why do they want X? What problem would X solve for them? Often the answer reveals an alternative solution that satisfies both parties' interests without conceding either party's stated position.
  • When to escalate: Not every professional conflict should be resolved through direct conversation. Conflicts involving policy violation, harassment, significant financial impact, or a clear power imbalance warrant formal escalation. The test: if this situation were described to an objective third party, would they recommend direct resolution or formal process? Apply the same standard you would recommend to a colleague in an identical situation.
Conflict avoided accumulates interest. The conversation that would take 10 minutes today will take 40 minutes in a month and a year of repair work in a year. The discipline of addressing conflict promptly, with the correct tools, and with the genuine intention of understanding as well as being understood is among the most practically consequential skills a man can develop.-- Von Lycaon
Etiquette is not about knowing which fork to use. It is about making others feel considered. The technical rules exist in service of that single goal. Understand the goal, and the rules become obvious. Etiquette without warmth is theatre. Warmth without etiquette is noise. The two together are what I call genuine presence.— Von Lycaon

The Language of Presence — Physical

The Careless HabitThe Lycaon Standard
Slumping at desk or during callsSit upright, feet flat. Stillness in the body signals presence in the mind. This is not rigidity — it is engagement made visible.
Checking your phone mid-conversationThe person before you has your complete attention. The phone waits. This single habit, practised consistently, will distinguish you from nearly everyone in any professional setting.
Filling every silence with wordsLet silences breathe. A well-placed pause before answering adds weight to what follows. The instinct to fill silence comes from discomfort with it — overcome this discomfort and you will appear more confident than any sentence could make you.
Forgetting names immediately after introductionUse the name once during the first exchange: "It's good to meet you, [Name]." The act of using it burns it into memory and communicates respect simultaneously.
Entering a room without acknowledging itAcknowledge the room on arrival — a nod, a "good morning," eye contact with whoever is present. An entrance that goes unacknowledged by the person making it creates an awkward ambient silence that benefits no one.
Interrupting before a point is completedWait. Genuinely wait until the sentence is complete before formulating your response. Most people begin composing their reply mid-sentence. This produces responses to what they expect the person to say, not what the person actually said.
Expressing frustration aloud in companyProcess privately. Communicate calmly. The room does not require the weather report of my inner state.
Crossing arms in conversationArms uncrossed, hands visible. Crossed arms signal defensiveness to observers regardless of your intent. Open posture invites honest exchange.
Breaking eye contact downwardWhen you must break eye contact, break it sideways — to the side as if thinking. Breaking it downward reads as submission or evasion. These are signals sent below the level of conscious awareness.

Refined Speech — The Complete Guide

Receiving a task

"Ok, I'll get to it."

→

"Understood. I'll have that addressed before end of day and will confirm once complete." — The addition of a specific timeline and a confirmation commitment transforms a vague assurance into a trackable promise.

The Art of Presence

Etiquette is not a set of arbitrary rules designed by an elite to exclude others. It is the accumulated wisdom of how to make other people feel comfortable, respected, and attended to. Every rule of etiquette, examined carefully, serves this function. The man who understands this does not follow etiquette as a performance of social status — he practises it as an expression of genuine attention to the people around him.

Conversation — The Core Skill

The Art of Asking

The most socially skilled people ask better questions than they give answers. A question that demonstrates genuine curiosity about the other person — their experience, their perspective, their work — produces a conversation that the other person finds valuable. The sequence that produces connection: a factual question (What do you do?) leads to a meaningful question (What drew you to that?) leads to a values question (What do you find most rewarding about it?). Each level moves from information exchange to genuine understanding. Most conversations never advance past the first level. The person who advances them to the second or third is remembered.

Listening as a Skill

Active listening is not the same as waiting politely while the other person finishes speaking. It is the genuine attempt to understand what the person means, not just what they say. The indicators: you can summarise what they said accurately. You ask follow-up questions based on specific things they said (not generic prompts). You do not redirect the conversation to yourself immediately after they finish. The test for genuine listening: when a conversation ends, can you describe three specific things the other person told you? Most people cannot, because they were processing their own response rather than the other person's content.

Managing Disagreement

The socially graceful handling of disagreement: acknowledge what is correct or understandable in the other position before presenting your own. "I understand why you'd see it that way — the evidence for X is real. My reading of it is different because..." This construction is not weakness or evasion. It is the demonstration that you have genuinely engaged with the other position rather than merely waiting to present your own. People accept disagreement much more readily from someone who has demonstrably understood them than from someone who appears to have dismissed them.

The Bosnian Context

Bosnian social interaction has specific conventions worth knowing. The greeting: a handshake with direct eye contact for acquaintances; cheek kisses (one or three, depending on region and relationship) for closer contacts. The offer of coffee is a social obligation — declining it in a home context should be done graciously with a specific reason ("I've just had one, thank you") rather than a flat refusal. The discussion of the 1990s war: present in every conversation eventually, approached with sensitivity and listening rather than opinion from an outsider position. The explicit acknowledgment of Sarajevo's suffering without political attribution is the correct posture for anyone not from a directly affected family.

Dining Etiquette — The Practical Guide

  • Arrival: On time means 5 minutes early for a dinner at someone's home. Fashionably late (15+ minutes) communicates disregard for the host's preparation. If delayed unavoidably, a message before the time of arrival.
  • At the table: Phone face down or in a pocket from the moment you sit until you leave the table. This is not a rule — it is a statement about whose company you value. Napkin unfolded and placed on the lap immediately. Do not begin eating until everyone has been served or the host signals to begin.
  • The bread rule: Tear bread, do not cut it. Hold it above the plate, not over the tablecloth, when tearing.
  • Cutlery navigation: Work from the outside in for multiple courses. The fork is held in the left hand, knife in the right, throughout continental European eating (not cut-and-switch). Utensils resting on the plate at four o'clock position signal "I am still eating." Parallel across the plate at six o'clock signals "I am finished."
  • The host: Compliment specific dishes, not generic praise. "This specific thing is excellent and here is why" communicates more than "everything is wonderful." Offer to help clear before dessert — accept graciously if declined.

Professional Etiquette

  • Email response time: Same business day for anything requiring a response. Within 4 hours for anything marked urgent. The person who responds promptly is trusted with more responsibility than the person who is unpredictable. Reliability in small things communicates reliability in large ones.
  • Meeting punctuality: Early. Five minutes early is on time. On time is late. Late is a statement about whose time you value more. In Bosnian professional culture, where interpersonal relationships carry more weight than in Northern European cultures, repeated lateness damages professional trust in ways that are difficult to repair.
  • The follow-up: After any professional meeting, a brief email the same day noting the key decisions, actions, and owners. This produces three benefits: it demonstrates professionalism, it confirms shared understanding (preventing later disputes about what was decided), and it creates a written record. The person who consistently does this becomes the most trusted person in any professional environment.
Etiquette is not about which fork to use. It is about making every person in your presence feel that their time and comfort matter to you. The man who has mastered this is welcome in any room and at any table.-- Von Lycaon
Social and professional competence is not charm — charm is an innate quality that cannot be taught. What can be taught, and what produces nearly identical outcomes, is a set of precise habits: knowing when to speak and when to be silent, how to enter a room, how to handle disagreement without damage, how to be remembered for the right reasons. The basics you already possess. What follows fills the gaps.— Von Lycaon

Conversation — The Gaps Worth Filling

The Art of Asking Better Questions

  • Open questions over closed ones. "What has that been like for you?" produces a conversation. "Was it good?" produces yes or no. The distinction is simple and the effect on the quality of interaction is significant. Most people ask closed questions out of habit, not intention.
  • Follow the thread, don't redirect. When someone answers a question, the next move is to follow what they said — not pivot to something you wanted to say next. "You mentioned your father was also an engineer — did that shape how you approached the work?" is a gift. Ignoring what they said and pivoting to your own story is the most common conversation failure in professional settings.
  • The most powerful conversation technique available: remembering something small from a previous conversation and referencing it. "How did the project you were worried about in March turn out?" This costs nothing and produces a felt experience of genuine care that most professional relationships never achieve.

Silence — Its Correct Use

  • Most people are uncomfortable with silence and fill it immediately. This produces rushed, unconsidered speech and signals anxiety. A person who is comfortable with three seconds of silence after a question was asked appears more thoughtful, more confident, and more worth listening to when they do speak.
  • After asking a question — wait. The first answer is often not the complete answer. People edit in real time. The second part of what they say, after the slight pause, is frequently the more honest and interesting part. Most people never hear it because they spoke first.
  • Do not finish other people's sentences. Even when you know exactly where the sentence is going. The act of finishing someone's sentence communicates that their delivery matters less than your speed. It is not perceived as helpfulness.

Disagreement — Professionally and Personally

  • Acknowledge before countering. "That makes sense given what you know about the situation — the part I see differently is..." is heard. "That's wrong because..." is defended against. The information in both is identical. The reception is not.
  • Attack the argument, never the person making it. "The data doesn't support that conclusion" is professional disagreement. "You always jump to conclusions" is personal attack. In professional settings this distinction is the line between someone who is respected when they disagree and someone who is avoided because disagreement with them is unpleasant.
  • Know when to let it go. Not every disagreement requires resolution. A colleague who holds an incorrect opinion about a minor matter does not need to be corrected every time it surfaces. Choose the battles that matter. Win them decisively. Leave the rest.

Professional Conduct — The Gaps

Email and Written Communication

  • The subject line is a commitment. "Meeting follow-up" tells the recipient nothing. "Action items from Monday IT review — 3 items, response needed by Friday" tells them everything they need before opening the email. Write the subject line last, after you know what the email actually contains.
  • One email, one purpose. An email asking for three different things will have two of them ignored. If multiple items require a response, number them explicitly: 1. 2. 3. The recipient can action each item separately and you can reference item 2 specifically in follow-up without ambiguity.
  • The 24-hour rule for emotional responses. If an email made you angry, frustrated, or defensive — write the response, then save it as a draft and send it tomorrow. You will edit it. The edit will be correct. An emotional email sent immediately is almost never the most effective version of the communication it was trying to make.
  • Reply to all with discipline. Before hitting Reply All, ask: does every person on this chain need this response? In most cases, no. The person who uses Reply All sparingly is noticed positively. The person who uses it reflexively produces a low-level frustration in everyone on large chains.

Meetings — How To Be the Person Worth Having In The Room

  • Arrive on time. Not five minutes late with a coffee. On time means seated and ready at the start time. Arriving late to a meeting communicates that your time is more valuable than everyone else's combined. It is noticed every time regardless of whether it is commented on.
  • Speak once on each topic, clearly. The person who makes one precise point in a meeting is heard. The person who speaks at length on every item becomes noise. Quality of contribution is remembered; quantity of words is not.
  • Take notes visibly. Writing during a meeting signals that you consider what is being said worth recording. It also provides the raw material for the follow-up email that distinguishes the person who was present from the person who attended.
  • The follow-up email. After any meeting with action items, send a brief summary within 24 hours: decisions made, actions assigned, deadlines confirmed. The person who does this consistently is the person the team begins to rely on for clarity. It takes ten minutes and produces a professional reputation that is difficult to build any other way.

Managing Up — Working With Your Manager

  • Bring solutions, not just problems. Arriving at a manager's desk with a problem and no proposed solution is delegation upward. Arriving with "I have a problem, here are two possible approaches, I lean toward option A because..." is professional consultation. The second version is welcomed. The first is eventually resented.
  • Keep your manager informed of progress without being asked. A brief weekly update — what was completed, what is in progress, any blockers — requires three minutes to write and eliminates the need for your manager to chase you for information. Being chased for updates is one of the most damaging things that can happen to a professional reputation and it is entirely preventable.
  • Disagree privately, support publicly. If you disagree with a decision, raise it privately with the decision-maker first. Once a decision is made and communicated, support it in public regardless of your private position. This is not weakness — it is the understanding that organisations require coherent execution, and that continued public disagreement with settled decisions is professionally damaging to everyone involved, including you.

Social Situations — Specific Scenarios

Entering a Room Where You Know No One

  • Pause briefly at the entrance — not long enough to appear uncertain, just long enough to read the room. Where are the groups? Who appears to be hosting? Is there a natural peripheral role (the bar, the food) from which conversation begins organically?
  • Approach someone standing alone before approaching a group. A person standing alone at a social event is mildly uncomfortable and will be actively grateful for an approach. A tight group of three is mid-conversation and the entry is more complex.
  • Open with a genuine observation about the shared context — not a line. "How do you know [the host]?" is the cleanest opening at any social event because it establishes connection, invites a story, and requires no wit.

Receiving a Compliment

  • Accept it directly. "Thank you — I'm glad it came together well." Not deflection ("oh it was nothing"), not false modesty ("it could have been much better"), not redirection to others unless genuinely warranted. Deflecting a compliment is uncomfortable for the person who gave it — they must now insist, which is an awkward position you have created for them.
  • A compliment accepted gracefully closes the loop. A compliment deflected opens a negotiation neither party wanted.

Apologising Effectively

  • A complete apology has three components: acknowledgment of what happened ("I missed the deadline"), ownership without qualification ("that was my responsibility"), and a concrete forward commitment ("it will be delivered by Thursday"). All three. Apologies missing any component are felt as incomplete by the recipient even when they cannot articulate why.
  • "I'm sorry you feel that way" is not an apology. It is a statement that the problem is the other person's emotional response, not your action. It consistently makes situations worse.
  • Apologise once, specifically, and move forward. Repeated apologies for the same incident become a social burden — the other person must now manage your guilt as well as their own response to the original event.

The Bosnian Social Context — Specific Notes

  • Coffee as social infrastructure. In Bosnia, "dođi na kafu" (come for coffee) is a social institution, not a literal statement about caffeine. Declining repeatedly signals social withdrawal. Accepting and being fully present — phone away, genuinely engaged — is the correct response. The coffee matters less than what it represents.
  • The gift when visiting. Arriving at a Bosnian home empty-handed for a dinner or significant visit is noticed. A box of quality čokolada, a bottle of quality non-alcoholic drink, flowers for the host — small and consistent. The value is in the habit, not the item.
  • Multi-generational respect. Bosnian social culture maintains visible respect for older generations in social settings. Standing when an elder enters the room, addressing them formally until invited otherwise, serving them before others — these are not performative. They are expected and their absence is noted.
  • The meza culture. Gatherings in Bosnia frequently involve extended meza — small dishes, shared, over long periods. The correct participation is unhurried engagement with the food and the conversation together. Rushing a meza is socially illiterate. The meal is not the point — the time together is the point and the meal is its vehicle.
The socially competent person is not the most entertaining person in the room. They are the person who makes everyone else feel most at ease in it. That quality is produced not by performance but by genuine attention to the people present — which costs nothing and is rarer than it should be.— Von Lycaon
A career managed passively produces outcomes determined by other people's assessments and priorities. A career managed deliberately produces outcomes aligned with your own. The difference is not luck, connection, or talent — it is the consistent application of attention to where you are, where you want to be, and what specifically closes the gap between the two.— Von Lycaon

The Career Map — Know Where You Stand

The Annual Career Review — 2 Hours, Once Per Year

  • Current position assessment: What skills do you have? What skills does your current role require? What is the gap? What skills does the next level require? This gap analysis is the foundation of all deliberate career development.
  • Market value check: Research current market rates for your role, experience level, and location. Glassdoor, LinkedIn Salary, conversations with peers. If your current compensation has fallen below market rate without a corresponding increase in non-monetary benefits, this is information to act on.
  • Trajectory assessment: Is this role developing skills that will be more valuable in three years than they are today? Is the organisation one where advancement is realistic? Are the people above you people you want to learn from? If the answers are no, the timeline for a transition should be in the plan rather than undefined.

The IT Analyst Career Path

📊 Technical Deepening

Data analysis skills (SQL, Python, Power BI) are the technical foundation with the highest compounding return in IT analyst roles. Each additional technical capability multiplies the usefulness of the others. The order of investment: SQL first (universal in data work), Python second (automation and analysis), Power BI or Tableau third (visualisation and stakeholder communication). Each is learnable to professional competence within 3–6 months of consistent daily practice using the Learning tab methodology.

🎯 Business Acumen

The analyst who understands the business as well as the technology is significantly more valuable than the one who understands only the technology. This means: understanding financial reporting, knowing what the organisation's KPIs actually measure, being able to frame technical findings in business impact terms. The Finance tab provides the foundation; the rest comes from deliberate attention to the business context of every technical task.

🗣️ Communication Skills

The limiting factor in most technical careers is communication, not technical ability. The Public Speaking tab covers presentation. The Social Skills tab covers professional communication. The specific addition for IT analysts: written communication of technical findings to non-technical audiences. The ability to write a clear, concise technical report that a senior manager without technical background can act on is a rare and highly valued skill.

🏆 Certifications Worth Pursuing

In BiH's market: Microsoft certifications (AZ-900 Azure Fundamentals as an entry point, then role-specific), CompTIA (A+, Network+, Security+), ITIL (IT service management — widely valued in the corporate sector). Certifications signal commitment and provide structured learning paths. Cost in BiH: exam fees typically 150–300 EUR. Online preparation courses: Udemy and Coursera at 15–30 EUR.

Promotion — The Mechanics

  • Make your work visible. The best work done invisibly produces no career advancement. The work must be seen by the people who make advancement decisions. Regular updates to management, presentations of findings, documentation that circulates — visibility is not self-promotion, it is ensuring your contribution is accurately assessed.
  • Solve problems your manager hasn't identified yet. Reactive competence — doing what is asked well — is the baseline. Proactive value — identifying and solving problems before being asked — is what distinguishes the person who advances from the person who remains.
  • Build internal relationships deliberately. Know the people in adjacent teams whose work affects yours and whose work you affect. These relationships produce referrals, collaboration opportunities, and advocates when advancement decisions are made — often by people who have not directly observed your work but have heard about it from these relationships.
  • Ask for what you want explicitly. "I am interested in the senior analyst role when it becomes available. What would I need to demonstrate to be a strong candidate?" This is not aggression — it is clarity. Managers generally prefer to develop people who have expressed a clear direction over those whose aspirations must be guessed.
The career that unfolds by accident produces whatever the environment offers. The career that unfolds by intention produces what you have built the capacity for. The gap between these two outcomes, over a working life, is not marginal.— Von Lycaon

The IT Analyst in BiH — The Specific Trajectory

The IT job market in Bosnia has specific characteristics that affect the correct career strategy. Salaries at Bosnian companies are significantly below Western European equivalents for identical work. Remote work for international companies, however, removes this disadvantage — an IT analyst working remotely for a German, Austrian, or UK company earns at the market rate for their skills regardless of where they live. The Sarajevo cost of living combined with a Western European salary is one of the most financially advantageous positions available in Europe.

  • The remote work pivot: The path from a Sarajevo-based IT role to a remote role for an international company typically involves: building the specific technical skills the international market values (SQL, Python, Power BI — already in the Career tab), obtaining internationally recognised certifications (Microsoft AZ-900, CompTIA Data+), building a LinkedIn profile that signals competence to international recruiters, and applying to remote-first companies that explicitly hire across Eastern Europe (Toptal, Remote.com listings, and direct applications to remote-first tech companies).
  • The consulting model: IT analysts with 3+ years of specialised experience can transition to consulting — working with multiple clients rather than one employer, typically earning 2–3x the equivalent salary at the cost of administrative complexity (invoicing, tax, client acquisition). The Freelancing tab covers the BiH legal structure. The consulting model is the highest-earning trajectory available from a Sarajevo base without emigrating.
  • Documentation as career infrastructure: Every significant project, process improvement, or system analysis you produce should be documented in a format that demonstrates your capability to someone who was not present for the work. This documentation becomes a portfolio — the asset that makes the next role or next rate negotiation possible. The person who does excellent work without documenting it is invisible to everyone outside their immediate team.
I am described as someone who can resolve any matter. This is not hyperbole — it is the result of preparation, communication, and the refusal to panic. You hold a role as an IT analyst. The principles that make a great attendant make an exceptional analyst. The work differs entirely. The discipline is identical.— Von Lycaon

The IT Analyst as Attendant

Consider what the role of IT analyst actually requires at its best: the ability to understand complex systems, diagnose problems under pressure, explain technical matters to non-technical people with patience and clarity, and be the calm presence in the room when everything is on fire. This is precisely what an attendant of Victoria Housekeeping does, substituting systems for households.

The primary error I observe in technical roles is the assumption that technical competence is sufficient. It is not. Technical competence earns the role. Communication earns the reputation. Every insight you produce exists, for practical purposes, only as well as you can articulate it.

Communication Architecture

📊

The Problem Brief

When reporting on any issue, in exactly this order: (1) What is the problem, in one sentence. (2) What is the cause, if known. (3) What is the current status. (4) What action is being taken. (5) Timeline for resolution. This structure works verbally and in writing. It prevents the common alternative: a narrative that begins with background, adds detail, and only reveals the actual problem in the fifth sentence, by which time the listener has begun solving the wrong problem.

🎯

One Ask Per Communication

An email or message with three questions receives an answer to one — often not the most important one. When multiple things are needed: three separate short messages with a single clear request each, or a structured numbered list in one message where each item is visually and conceptually separate.
