Clarity is a courtesy. Vague requests transfer the burden of interpretation to the recipient.

⏱️

Commitments with Timelines

"I'll handle it" offers zero information. "I'll have that resolved by 3pm today and will send confirmation when done" is a commitment that can be tracked, relied upon, and builds trust with every instance of delivery. Over time, the professional who consistently delivers precisely what they promised becomes the professional others go to when something genuinely must be done.

📝

Documentation as Legacy

Your incident notes, runbooks, and ticket records are your professional legacy. A well-documented system can be maintained by someone else when you are unavailable. A poorly documented one cannot. The professional who documents well is never fully absent — their knowledge persists. This is also the fastest way to demonstrate depth of contribution to those who evaluate it.

Technical Communication — Explained Clearly

A significant part of your role is translating technical reality into language that non-technical stakeholders can act on. This is a skill entirely separate from technical ability and one that most technically talented people underinvest in.

What Not to Say

"The DHCP server is failing to allocate IP addresses due to scope exhaustion, and the DNS resolution is cascading as a result."

The Lycaon Translation

"The network cannot assign addresses to new devices because we've run out of available slots — it's like a hotel with no vacant rooms. I'm expanding capacity now and will have this resolved within 30 minutes. In the meantime, devices already connected will not be affected."

What Not to Say

"We've had a SQL injection vulnerability exposed through the login endpoint — need to patch the WAF rule and sanitise inputs."

The Lycaon Translation

"There's a security gap in the login system that could allow an attacker to access data they shouldn't. I'm closing it now — estimated 45 minutes. No evidence it's been exploited yet, but I'll confirm this in my report within 2 hours."

The analogy is the bridge. The specific technical term is replaced with something familiar. The conclusion (what is being done and when) is given before the technical detail, not after it. The listener's first and primary need is: is this being handled? Answer that first, always.

The Email Standard

  • Subject line: Specific enough to be retrieved in twelve months. Not "Question" — "VPN Access Request — [Name] — [Date]." Not "Issue" — "Resolved: Email Gateway Outage 04/03." A subject line is an index entry, not a teaser.
  • Three-to-five sentence body rule: For most professional emails, the entire message should be three to five sentences. State the purpose, provide the necessary context, make the ask or deliver the information, and close with a clear next step. If an email exceeds ten sentences, ask whether a conversation would serve better.
  • The passive voice removes accountability. "Mistakes were made" is the famous example. In professional email: "The system was configured incorrectly" vs "I configured the system incorrectly and here is how I have corrected it." Own your work — including its failures. This is unusual enough to build significant trust when done consistently.
  • Never send angry email. Write it. Save it as a draft. Read it in the morning. Send the version you would be comfortable with your senior leadership reading. The version written in frustration and the version that serves your professional interests are never the same document.
  • The signature: Name, role, contact number, and nothing else. No motivational quotes. No large graphic logos. No lists of qualifications. Professional brevity in a signature signals confidence, not modesty.

Handling Difficult Situations

The Frustrated User+

The error is to begin solving immediately. The correct sequence: acknowledge before you analyse. Two sentences: "I understand this has disrupted your work — that's genuinely frustrating and I'm going to prioritise getting this resolved for you right now." Then go technical. The problem will take the same time to fix either way. The difference is how they experience the interaction and how they describe it afterwards. "The IT guy fixed it" versus "he was incredibly helpful and had it sorted within the hour" are produced by different approaches to the same fix.

When You Don't Know the Answer+

The incorrect response: a guess, delivered with false confidence, that later proves wrong. The correct response: "I don't have the answer to that immediately, but I can find out. Give me [specific time] and I'll have a definitive answer for you." Then do exactly that. The ability to say "I don't know yet but I will" and then deliver on that commitment is genuinely rare and immediately distinguishing. No one expects omniscience. Everyone notices honesty followed by reliability.

Being Asked to Do Something Outside Your Role+

Two approaches depending on the request. If it is adjacent to your skills and the relationship merits it: do it, without complaint, and quietly note the precedent you have set. Versatility builds capital. If it is genuinely outside what you should be doing: "I want to help with this — I'm not sure I'm the right person, but I can either point you to who is, or discuss what support I'm actually in a position to offer. Which would be more useful?" This is neither refusal nor capitulation. It is clarity offered generously.

Taking Criticism Professionally+

The natural reaction to criticism is defence. The professional reaction is inquiry. "Thank you for raising this — can you help me understand specifically what you'd like to see done differently?" This disarms defensive exchanges, produces actionable feedback instead of vague criticism, and positions you as someone interested in improvement rather than someone interested in winning an argument. These responses produce entirely different professional reputations over time.

The Critical Incident — Incident Mode+

In a major outage or security incident: composure is the first deployment. The room will take its emotional cue from whoever is most visibly calm. If that person is you, the room settles. If you are visibly panicking, others panic and compound the dysfunction. Practical incident mode: state clearly what is known, what is unknown, what is being done, and what the communication cadence will be. "Here is what we know. Here is what we don't yet know. Here are the three actions being taken right now. I will update you in 30 minutes regardless of whether there is news." Then execute. The contrast between this composure and the chaos around it will be noticed and remembered.

Career Development — The Long Game

  • Document your contributions specifically. Keep a private running document of projects completed, problems solved, and improvements made — with metrics where possible. "Reduced ticket resolution time by 20%" is evidence. "Did a lot of good work" is not. This document becomes the raw material for every performance review and every promotion conversation.
  • Cultivate one mentor. Not a formal arrangement necessarily — a senior person whose thinking you respect and whose feedback you occasionally seek. The perspective of someone who has already navigated what you are currently navigating is disproportionately valuable relative to the time it costs to cultivate.
  • Invest in certifications strategically. Not every certification that exists — the ones that open the specific next door you wish to open. CompTIA A+, Security+, Microsoft AZ-900, ITIL Foundation — these are credible stepping stones depending on your direction. One pursued seriously is worth more than three abandoned halfway.
  • Become the person who writes things down. In meetings, be the person who produces the summary afterwards. This habit puts you at the centre of information flow, ensures your interpretation of discussions is the recorded one, and creates the impression of diligence with a fifteen-minute investment per meeting.
Negotiation is not conflict. It is the process by which two parties with different interests find an arrangement that serves both. Most people avoid it because they confuse negotiation with confrontation, or because they believe that asking for something better is presumptuous. Both beliefs cost money, opportunities, and respect over a lifetime. The following is the framework for negotiating in any context — salary, purchases, contracts, and interpersonal situations — with composure and precision.— Von Lycaon

The Fundamental Principle

The single most important insight in negotiation: the goal is not to win. It is to reach an agreement that both parties will honour. An agreement extracted by pressure that the other party resents will be executed poorly, reversed at the first opportunity, or will damage the relationship in ways that cost more than the concession was worth. This principle eliminates the zero-sum mindset that makes most people either aggressive or avoidant in negotiation.

BATNA — Your Most Important Number

Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement. Before any negotiation, know yours: if this negotiation fails entirely, what is your best alternative? The strength of your BATNA determines your negotiating leverage. A job offer negotiation with no alternative job offer has weak BATNA. The same negotiation with a competing offer has strong BATNA. Improving your BATNA before entering a negotiation is more powerful than any technique applied during it.

The Anchor

The first number stated in a negotiation disproportionately influences the final outcome — this is the anchoring effect from the Kahneman reading in the Reading tab. Whoever states the first number sets the frame that all subsequent discussion references. When you have good information, anchor first and anchor ambitiously. When you have poor information, invite the other party to anchor first and use their number as the starting frame.

Silence as a Tool

After making an offer or stating a position — be silent. Do not fill the silence with justifications, reductions, or qualifications. The discomfort of silence in negotiation falls on both parties. Most people relieve it by conceding. The person who can sit with it longest usually holds the better position at the end of the silence.

Never Accept the First Offer

Not because the first offer is always insufficient — but because accepting immediately signals that you expected less, which retroactively damages the other party's confidence in the agreement. A brief pause and a single counteroffer — even a modest one — is appropriate in almost every negotiation context. The exception: genuinely excellent offers in competitive situations where delay loses the opportunity.

Salary Negotiation

The most financially consequential negotiation most people will ever conduct, and the one most people either skip entirely or handle least effectively. A 10% salary increase negotiated at the start of a job compounds over the entire tenure — it is the raise that all future raises are calculated from.

Before the Conversation

  • Research the market rate. Glassdoor, LinkedIn Salary, and direct conversations with peers in similar roles. Know the range for an IT analyst in Sarajevo and the BiH market specifically — international comparison sites will overestimate significantly. Your BATNA in a salary negotiation is the market rate: "the market for this role pays X" is not an opinion, it is data.
  • Quantify your contributions. Before the conversation, prepare specific examples of value delivered: problems solved, projects completed, efficiency improvements, costs saved. Salary negotiation based on tenure alone (I have been here two years) is weak. Salary negotiation based on documented contribution is strong.
  • Know your number — both target and floor. The target is what you actually want. The floor is the minimum you will accept. Never reveal the floor. Always anchor at the target or slightly above.

During the Conversation

  • "What does the role pay?" — Deflect this question. If asked for your salary expectation before an offer is made, say: "I'd like to understand the full scope of the role before discussing compensation. What is the budgeted range for this position?" If pressed: give a range where your target is the bottom number, not the middle.
  • After the offer — the three-second pause. When an offer is made, pause visibly before responding. Do not accept immediately. Do not reject immediately. "I appreciate the offer. I was expecting something closer to [your target number] based on my research into market rates and what I bring to the role. Is there flexibility?" This is the complete script. It is polite, specific, and leaves space for the other party to move.
  • If salary is fixed — negotiate everything else. Start date, remote work flexibility, additional leave, professional development budget, equipment. These have monetary value and are often more flexible than base salary, particularly in structured organisations.

Everyday Negotiation — Sarajevo Context

🏠 Large Purchases

Electronics, appliances, furniture — the displayed price at Bauhaus, Lesnina, or any major retailer is not necessarily the final price. For purchases above 500 BAM, it is always worth asking: "Is there anything you can do on the price?" The worst response is no. The best response saves 5–15%. In Bosnia specifically, direct negotiation on significant purchases is culturally normal — more so than in Western European retail environments.

🏗️ Services & Majstori

For home repairs and services, always get three quotes. The first quote tells you the price range exists. The second confirms it. The third gives you leverage with the preferred contractor: "I have a quote for X from another contractor — if you can match it I would prefer to work with you." This is not confrontational. It is how procurement works.

📱 Subscriptions & Contracts

Telecom contracts (BH Telecom, HT, Telemach) are negotiable at renewal. Before renewal, research the best current offer for new customers. Call to cancel — or say you are considering switching. Retention departments have authority to match new customer offers that are not available to existing customers who do not ask. The call takes ten minutes.

Persuasion — The Ethical Framework

Persuasion and manipulation share techniques but differ in intent. Manipulation serves the persuader at the expense of the persuaded. Ethical persuasion serves both by helping the other party recognise that an agreement genuinely serves their interests. The following are ethical persuasion tools.

  • Lead with their interest, not yours. "This will help you achieve X" is more persuasive than "I need you to do Y." This is not deceptive — it is accurate. If your request does not genuinely serve their interest, you should examine the request before the persuasion technique.
  • Reciprocity is real and powerful. Giving something first — a concession, information, assistance — creates a genuine felt obligation to reciprocate. This is not manipulation when the initial giving is genuine. Build relationships through genuine generosity and the reciprocity that follows is both earned and ethical.
  • Ask, don't tell. Questions are more persuasive than statements. "What would need to be true for this to work for you?" invites the other party to identify their own conditions rather than defend against yours. The answer tells you exactly what the agreement requires.
  • The label. Naming what the other person appears to be feeling — "It seems like you're concerned about the timeline" — produces immediate de-escalation and rapport. The person feels understood. An understood person is more open to alternative positions than a person who feels their concern has not been registered.
The man who cannot negotiate on his own behalf leaves the terms of his life to others. Every salary he accepts without question, every price he pays without inquiry, every contract he signs without reading — these are not acts of generosity. They are acts of abdication. Negotiation is not aggression. It is the competent management of one's own interests, conducted with composure and respect for the other party.— Von Lycaon

The Bosnian Negotiation Context

Negotiation in the Bosnian cultural context has specific characteristics that differ from the Western frameworks most negotiation books are written within. Understanding these differences prevents misreading the situation.

  • Relationship before transaction: In Bosnian business culture, significant transactions are typically preceded by relationship establishment. Attempting to negotiate on first contact — before coffee, before conversation about non-business topics, before the relationship has been acknowledged — is perceived as disrespectful and produces worse outcomes than the same negotiation conducted after a proper introduction period. The coffee is not a delay tactic. It is the foundation the negotiation requires.
  • The initial offer gap: In markets where bargaining is expected (the pijaca, car purchases, some service negotiations), the initial offer is typically significantly removed from the acceptable outcome for both parties. Reading the opening offer as a genuine proposal rather than an opening position produces naive acceptance. The correct response is a counter-offer, not a refusal or an acceptance. The pijaca vendor who quotes 15 KM for produce that will go for 10 KM is not lying — they are beginning a conversation.
  • Face preservation: Public disagreement or explicit rejection produces a different social cost in Bosnian culture than in Northern European contexts. Negotiations that reach an impasse can often be revived by framing the new position as a revision rather than a reversal — allowing the other party to change position without losing face. This is not manipulation; it is the application of social intelligence to the negotiation context.
The ability to speak clearly, precisely, and without visible fear in front of other people is among the highest-returning skills available to a professional. It compounds: the person who presents well is given more opportunities to present, which makes them better, which gives them more opportunities. The person who avoids it remains invisible regardless of the quality of their thinking. Thinking well and communicating it well are not the same skill — but both are learnable.— Von Lycaon

The Fear — Named and Demystified

Glossophobia — the fear of public speaking — is consistently the most commonly reported fear, ranking above the fear of death in many surveys. This tells us something important: it is an extremely common human experience, not a personal failing. Its physiological basis is identical to performance anxiety of any kind — cortisol and adrenaline release activating the sympathetic nervous system. The symptoms (increased heart rate, dry mouth, shaking hands, mind going blank) are not signs of incompetence. They are signs of normal human physiology.

The key reframe: the physiological state of anxiety and the physiological state of excitement are identical. The interpretation is the variable. "I am nervous" and "I am excited" produce the same heart rate, the same cortisol, the same adrenaline. Telling yourself "I am excited about this" rather than "I am nervous about this" measurably improves performance — this has been studied specifically in public speaking contexts. The physiology is fixed. The story about the physiology is not.

Structure — The Foundation of Every Good Presentation

The Three-Part Framework

  • Tell them what you are going to say. One sentence. "Today I am going to explain why the current backup process is a risk and what we should do about it." The audience now knows what to listen for. Their cognitive load drops immediately.
  • Say it. The body of the presentation. Maximum three main points for any presentation under 20 minutes. Human working memory holds approximately three to four chunks of new information. More than three main points and the last ones displace the first ones. Three points, supported by evidence, explained clearly.
  • Tell them what you said. A brief summary restates the main points and states the action or conclusion. "So in summary: the current backup fails silently, this has happened twice this year, and the recommended solution is X." The ending is not a trailing off — it is a landing.

Delivery — The Mechanics

Pace

Most people speak too fast when nervous — the adrenaline increases the rate of speech. The correct pace for clear comprehension is slower than feels natural under stress. Record yourself presenting something and play it back. You will speak faster than you perceived. Deliberate pauses between sentences are not awkward silences — they are comprehension windows. Give the audience time to process each point before introducing the next.

Eye Contact

Not a sustained stare at one person — that is uncomfortable for both. A deliberate sweep across the room, landing on different individuals for 2–3 seconds each, creates the felt experience of being spoken to for everyone present. In a small meeting or one-on-one presentation, maintain eye contact for complete sentences at a time — breaking eye contact to look at notes or a screen signals uncertainty.

Voice

Projection (speaking to the back of the room, not the person in front of you), variation (changes in pace, volume, and pitch prevent monotone delivery that loses the audience within minutes), and ending sentences with falling rather than rising inflection (rising inflection at the end of statements sounds like questions — it communicates uncertainty rather than conviction).

Physical Presence

Stand with weight evenly distributed — not shifting from foot to foot. Hands at the sides or gesturing naturally — not in pockets, not clasped, not fiddling with anything. A still, grounded physical posture communicates authority before a word is spoken. The Mizutsune composure principle from earlier in this Codex applies here directly.

Preparation — The Only Reliable Confidence Source

  • Know your material to three levels of depth. What you intend to say, what underlies what you intend to say, and what underlies that. If a question sends you one level deeper than your presentation, you should be able to answer confidently. If it sends you two levels deeper, it is honest to say you will find out. Knowing that you have depth available produces a different quality of confidence than knowing you have exactly the material prepared and nothing more.
  • Practise out loud, not in your head. The presentation that sounds clear in your head may not survive contact with your vocal apparatus. Practising silently does not prepare the mouth, the pace, or the pauses. Stand up. Speak at presentation volume. Practise the complete presentation at least twice before delivering it.
  • Practise the opening specifically. The first 30 seconds are the highest anxiety moment — the audience's first impression is forming, the cortisol is at peak. Having the opening so well-practised it is almost automatic means the worst moment is handled by muscle memory rather than by a brain operating under stress.
  • Anticipate the difficult questions. Before any professional presentation, list the three questions you would least like to be asked. Prepare clear answers. The questions you dread most are the ones the sharpest people in the room are most likely to ask.

Presentations in the IT/Analyst Context

  • The executive summary first. Technical presentations to non-technical audiences fail when they begin with methodology and end with conclusions. The audience wanted the conclusions first. Lead with the finding, then support it with the evidence. "The system has a critical vulnerability. Here is what it is, why it matters, and what I recommend."
  • Slides are speaker notes for the audience, not for you. If the slides contain everything you are going to say, why is the audience listening rather than reading? Slides should carry the key data, the charts, the framework. The speaker provides the interpretation, the context, and the meaning. One key point per slide. A slide with twelve bullet points is a document that has been incorrectly formatted as a presentation.
  • Handling "I don't know": The most credibility-preserving response to a question you cannot answer is "I don't know — I will find out and come back to you by [specific date]." Not a guess. Not a deflection. "I don't know, I'll find out" said confidently is more respected than an uncertain speculation that later proves wrong.

Building the Skill — Deliberate Practice

  • Toastmasters International has a chapter in Sarajevo — regular practice sessions in a structured supportive environment. The fastest skill development route available for public speaking at any level. Free to attend as a guest before joining. Look up Toastmasters Sarajevo for current meeting schedule.
  • Record yourself monthly. Review with specific questions: pace, eye contact, vocal variety, physical stillness. Track improvement over time. The gap between how you think you present and how you actually present closes through video review in a way that feedback from others often cannot achieve.
  • Take every small opportunity. Presenting a project update in a team meeting, explaining a process to a new colleague, summarising a report verbally — each of these is a low-stakes repetition of the same skill. The person who presents well in large formal settings built that capability through hundreds of small informal ones.
The voice is an instrument that most people leave untuned. A well-structured thought, delivered in a clear steady voice by a physically composed person, carries more weight in a professional context than the same thought delivered with uncertainty and apology. The thought is the content. The delivery is the credibility. Both are within control.— Von Lycaon
Networking done poorly is the most uncomfortable activity in professional life — a transactional exchange of business cards with people you have no genuine interest in. Networking done well is simply the deliberate development of genuine professional relationships. The distinction is in the intent: building a network to extract from it produces the former. Building relationships because professionals who know each other well do better work together produces the latter.— Von Lycaon

The Networking Mindset

Give Before You Take

The most effective networkers are the most useful ones. Before asking for anything from a professional relationship — a referral, an introduction, advice — ask what you can offer. Information, a connection to someone they would benefit from knowing, a relevant article, a solution to a problem they mentioned. The reciprocity principle from the Negotiation tab operates here: genuine giving creates genuine obligation. Transactional requesting creates transactional obligation — which is far weaker.

Quality Over Quantity

A professional network of 30 people who know you well, trust your work, and would speak positively on your behalf is more valuable than 300 LinkedIn connections who could not pick you from a list. The metric is depth of relationship, not breadth of connections. Prioritise deepening existing relationships over accumulating new ones.

The Long Game

Professional relationships that produce significant career value are typically 2–5 years in development. The connection made today that produces an opportunity is usually not a connection made specifically for that opportunity — it is a relationship maintained consistently over time that is activated when an opportunity arrives. Build the network before you need it.

The Sarajevo Professional Context

Sarajevo's professional community is smaller and more interconnected than international equivalents. In a city of 350,000, the IT and technology sector is a community where significant percentages of professionals know or know of each other. This has two implications: reputation travels faster (positive and negative), and relationship-building has a higher return per unit of effort than in larger, more anonymous markets.

  • Industry events in BiH: InfoBlok, PUNKT conference, and various IT sector meetups run regularly in Sarajevo and the broader BiH market. Attending these events — not passively but with the intent to have three genuine conversations per event — produces more relationship depth than online networking alone.
  • The alumni network: University connections in BiH carry significant weight. If you attended university in Sarajevo — the University of Sarajevo or UNSA faculties — the alumni network is an underutilised resource. Former classmates now spread across industries and organisations are warm network contacts that cold outreach cannot replicate.
  • Professional associations: The IT Chamber of BiH (IT Komora BiH) and related professional organisations provide structured networking context and industry credibility.

Maintaining the Network

  • The contact note system: In Obsidian, a simple note for each significant professional contact — their role, their interests, the last time you spoke, and one thing you remember from the conversation. This is not surveillance — it is memory. Using it when you reconnect ("last time we spoke you were working on the migration project — how did that turn out?") produces a felt experience of genuine attention.
  • Regular low-friction check-ins: A message when you read something they would find relevant. A congratulation when they change roles or receive a recognition. These do not require scheduling and maintain the relationship in the gaps between substantive contact.
  • The annual coffee. For significant professional relationships — former managers, senior colleagues, people whose work you respect — one coffee per year as a deliberate maintenance activity. Not to ask for anything. To maintain the relationship. The investment is one hour annually per person.
Your professional reputation is built in the conversations you are not present for — what colleagues say when someone asks about you. The network is the infrastructure through which those conversations travel. Build it with the same care as any infrastructure you rely on.— Von Lycaon

The Sarajevo Professional Network -- A Map

Sarajevo's professional community is small enough to be genuinely navigable and large enough to contain significant opportunities. The city's smallness is an asset: a well-regarded professional in Sarajevo is known by a significant proportion of their sector within a few years. The same reputation, built in Vienna or Berlin, might affect 200 people. Built in Sarajevo, it affects the entire professional community of the sector.

The IT and Technology Community

The Sarajevo technology sector has developed significantly since 2010. Key touchpoints: InfoBlok (the annual technology conference in Sarajevo), the IT Park Sarajevo initiative, and the various startup meetups that have emerged through the Hub387 and similar co-working spaces. The Facebook groups for IT professionals in BiH (several with 5,000+ members) serve as the primary informal network for job information, freelance opportunities, and knowledge sharing. Participation -- providing answers, sharing useful information -- builds reputation faster in these groups than in most professional networks.

The Alumni Network

The alumni network of the Elektrotehnicki fakultet (ETF) and the Ekonomski fakultet at the University of Sarajevo represent two of the most useful professional networks in the city. These alumni are spread across every organisation in Sarajevo's professional sector. An email to an ETF alumnus working in an organisation you are interested in -- "I studied at ETF, I am now working in X, I would value 20 minutes of your perspective on Y" -- has a high response rate that a cold contact to the same person does not.

The Expatriate Community

Sarajevo has a significant international NGO and diplomatic community -- the EU delegation, the OSCE mission, the international development organisations. This community often needs local IT and analytical support but struggles to find it through the formal job market. A relationship with one person in this community (through InfoBlok, through mutual professional contacts, or through the international social events that these organisations occasionally host) opens access to the project-based work that these organisations commission at Western European rates.

The Annual Contact Rhythm

A professional network decays without maintenance. The contacts that were warm after a meeting become cold after six months of silence and need to be rebuilt from scratch after a year. The maintenance system that prevents this:

  • Tier 1 contacts (your closest professional allies -- 5-10 people): Coffee or lunch once per quarter. These are the people you would call first for a reference, a referral, or advice on a major decision. The relationship requires the investment of regular contact.
  • Tier 2 contacts (valuable but less frequent connections -- 30-50 people): One genuine interaction per year. This can be as minimal as a personalised message when you see something relevant to their work, a congratulations on a visible professional achievement, or a share of something they would find valuable. The interaction must be genuinely tailored to them -- a generic "hope you're well" achieves nothing.
  • Tier 3 contacts (broad network -- everyone else): Passive maintenance through LinkedIn activity. They see your content; you see theirs. The relationship is warm enough to be revived when needed without requiring active maintenance.
A network is not a collection of contacts. It is a collection of relationships -- and relationships are built through genuine mutual benefit over time, not through the accumulation of business cards and LinkedIn connections. Give first and more than you take. Remember what people are working on. Show up when it is not convenient. This is the entire method.-- Von Lycaon
LinkedIn is either a professional asset or a dormant profile that accumulates connection requests from people who will never be relevant. The difference is approximately four hours of focused effort once, followed by 30 minutes monthly. This tab covers the four hours.— Von Lycaon

The Profile — The Foundation

The Elements That Matter

  • The headline: Not your job title — your professional value proposition. "IT Analyst at [Company]" tells a visitor your current employer. "IT Analyst | Data Analysis · Process Optimisation · IT Systems BiH" tells them what you do and what they get. The headline appears in search results and connection requests — it is the first impression before the profile is opened.
  • The photograph: Professional, clear, recent. Not a passport photo, not a social media photo, not a photo where you have been cropped out of a group. A clean background, facing the camera, appropriate clothing. The investment: ask someone with a decent phone and five minutes of time. It is the most-viewed element of the profile.
  • The About section: Three paragraphs. First: what you do and what you are known for. Second: what specifically you have achieved (quantified where possible — "improved reporting process reducing time from 3 days to 4 hours"). Third: what you are interested in professionally — the direction you are building toward. Written in first person, not third. Specific, not generic.
  • Experience: Each role with 3–5 bullet points covering what you were responsible for and what you specifically achieved. Past roles in past tense, current role in present. Quantify wherever possible — percentages, time savings, team sizes, project values. Numbers are more credible than descriptions.
  • Skills: Add the skills you actually have. Endorse connections for skills you have genuinely observed. The LinkedIn skills section affects search ranking — ensure the skills relevant to your target work are present.

The Content Strategy — 30 Minutes Monthly

  • Post once per month minimum. A brief post on something relevant to your field — a problem you solved, a tool you found useful, an observation about the IT sector in BiH, a summary of something learned from a course or book. It does not need to be long. 3–5 sentences is sufficient. Consistency over brilliance.
  • Comment more than you post. Thoughtful comments on posts by others in your field — adding a specific perspective, asking a genuine question, sharing a relevant experience — produce profile visibility and relationship building simultaneously. Three thoughtful comments produce more network effect than one post.
  • Share selectively. Sharing relevant articles with a one-sentence commentary ("This matches what we observed at work last year — the pattern is consistent") provides value to your network and positions you as someone who reads and thinks about the field.

Connection Strategy

  • Always add a personalised note to connection requests. The default "I'd like to connect" message produces low acceptance rates and no relationship. "I read your post about [topic] and found the point about [specific element] relevant to work I'm doing in [context] — I'd appreciate being connected" produces both higher acceptance and a conversation starting point.
  • Quality connections over quantity. A LinkedIn network of 500 people who are genuinely in your field, who you have met or interacted with meaningfully, is more valuable than 2,000 connections accumulated through indiscriminate connecting.
  • Recruiters will find you. A complete, keyword-rich LinkedIn profile with regular activity will produce unsolicited recruiter contact. This is useful not because you must change jobs but because it provides market data on your value and the options available. Engage briefly with relevant outreach even when not actively looking.
The LinkedIn profile is your professional face to everyone who looks you up before a meeting, before an interview, before a decision. It is seen most often when you are not present to supplement it. Make it speak accurately and well on your behalf.— Von Lycaon

The Algorithm -- What Actually Works

LinkedIn's content algorithm rewards specific behaviours that most users do not engage in, and penalises others that most users default to. Understanding it allows the building of professional visibility without the time investment that uninformed LinkedIn use requires.

  • What the algorithm rewards: Comments more than likes (comments require effort and are weighted more heavily). Native content more than external links (LinkedIn wants to keep users on the platform). Engagement within the first 60 minutes of a post going live (early engagement signals quality and triggers broader distribution). Text-only posts or single-image posts more than video or PDF documents (the algorithm currently deprioritises formats that take users off the feed).
  • The comment strategy: Commenting substantively on other people's posts produces visibility in a way that creating content does not initially achieve. A comment of 3-4 sentences that adds genuine insight to a conversation is seen by everyone who sees the original post and everyone in the commenter's network. This is the fastest way to build visibility on LinkedIn without the barrier of creating original content.
  • Post frequency: 2-3 times per week is optimal for most professionals. Daily posting produces diminishing returns and often dilutes content quality. Less than once per week means the algorithm essentially ignores you. Consistency over a 3-6 month period produces compounding visibility -- each post reaches a slightly larger audience than the previous one as the algorithm establishes your account's engagement credibility.

Content Strategy for an IT Analyst

  • The insight post: One specific thing you learned this week from your work -- a SQL technique, a data visualisation choice, a process improvement observation. Not "here is everything I know about data analysis." One specific thing, explained clearly, that a colleague in your field would find immediately useful. This type of content builds credibility faster than any other format because it demonstrates domain expertise through specificity.
  • The case study post: A problem you encountered, how you approached it, what you found, and what the result was. Anonymised where necessary for client confidentiality. This is the portfolio content that produces hiring manager and client interest. It shows thinking process, not just technical knowledge.
  • The opinion post: A specific position on something in your field -- a tool you think is overrated, a practice you think is undervalued, a trend you think is misunderstood. Opinion posts drive engagement because they invite agreement and disagreement. Keep them professional and constructive -- the goal is to be seen as a thoughtful voice, not a provocateur.
  • The recommendation: A book, article, tool, or resource that you found genuinely valuable, with a specific description of why and for whom. This positions you as someone who shares value rather than only self-promotes.

Profile Optimisation -- The Technical Details

  • The headline formula: [Specific Role] | [Specific Skill] | [Specific Outcome or Context]. "IT Analyst | SQL and Power BI | Turning complex data into decisions at [Company] or for international clients." The keyword density in the headline directly determines how often your profile appears in recruiter searches.
  • The profile photo: Professional does not mean formal. A clear, well-lit photo with a plain background and a natural expression. The photo should look like you at your most composed rather than a studio portrait of someone else. Updated every 2-3 years. Never a group photo cropped, never a casual social media photo.
  • The Featured section: The most underused real estate on LinkedIn. Use it to display your best piece of work -- a report, a case study, a project, a certification. This is the first thing a visitor sees after the summary and it converts profile views into connection requests more effectively than any other section.
LinkedIn is a professional tool that most professionals use unprofessionally -- either by ignoring it entirely or by treating it as a social media channel. The professional who uses it with the discipline of a communication strategy -- consistent, substantive, genuinely valuable -- builds a compound asset that generates opportunities without active solicitation. This is worth the 30 minutes per week it requires.-- Von Lycaon
A second income stream is not primarily about money — though money is real. It is about optionality. The man with one income source is entirely dependent on that source continuing. The man with two is not. The freelance or side income need not be large to produce this optionality. It needs only to exist and to be developed with the same consistency applied to the primary career.— Von Lycaon

The Framework for Side Income

Skill Arbitrage

The most sustainable side income derives from skills already developed professionally, offered to clients who need those skills but cannot or will not hire full-time for them. As an IT analyst, the skills with freelance market value: data analysis and reporting, process documentation and optimisation, IT support and consulting for small businesses, database work. These require no additional skill acquisition — only packaging and presenting existing capability for a different context.

The 10% Rule

Do not allocate more than 10% of your weekly available time to freelance work in the first six months. The purpose of this period is validation — does the market want what you offer, at the price you need, in the time you have? The answer cannot be known in theory. Limiting early time investment limits the cost of the learning period.

BiH Legal Considerations

Freelance income in Bosnia and Herzegovina requires registration. Options: register as a sole trader (obrtnik) in the relevant municipality, or operate through a registered d.o.o. (društvo s ograničenom odgovornošću — LLC equivalent). Both have tax and contribution implications. Consult a računovođa (accountant) before the first client invoice — the cost of incorrect registration is greater than the cost of advice on correct registration.

Platforms for Remote Work

Upwork, Freelancer, and Toptal all accept profiles from BiH. For IT-adjacent work, these platforms provide access to international clients who pay in EUR or USD — significantly above domestic BiH rates for equivalent work. English proficiency is the primary requirement. The hourly rates achievable for data analysis work from a Sarajevo base are competitive with regional living costs in a way domestic rates are not.

Starting — The First 90 Days

  • Define the offering specifically. Not "IT consulting" — "I help small businesses in Bosnia set up and automate their reporting processes in Excel and Power BI." Specific offerings are found; general ones are not. The narrower the offering, the more findable the person.
  • The first client is the hardest. Work for a reduced rate or pro bono for the first client in exchange for a detailed testimonial. A verified testimonial from a real business is worth significantly more than an empty profile when the second client is evaluating. The discounted first project is the cost of acquiring the testimonial.
  • Contracts for every engagement. A simple statement of work: what will be delivered, by when, for what payment, with what revision scope. This protects both parties and establishes professional seriousness that positions you differently from casual competitors. Template contracts are available on Sejfa and similar Bosnian business resource platforms.
  • Separate finances immediately. Open a dedicated bank account for freelance income from day one. This makes accounting simpler, taxes clearer, and the income tracking from the Finance tab accurate.
The first 100 BAM earned outside of employment produces a psychological shift out of proportion to its financial significance. The knowledge that the income was created — not received — changes the relationship with work and with financial security in a way that is difficult to appreciate before experiencing it.— Von Lycaon

The Economics of Freelancing from Sarajevo

The economic case for freelancing from Sarajevo is among the strongest available to any knowledge worker in Europe. The gap between the cost of living in Sarajevo and the rate that Western European clients pay for IT and analytical work creates an arbitrage that is genuinely significant. A Bosnian freelance IT analyst billing at the mid-range Western European rate (60-100 EUR/hour) while incurring Sarajevo living costs (estimated 1,200-1,800 EUR/month for a comfortable standard of living) achieves a financial surplus that would be impossible in the client's own city at the same billing rate.

Finding the First Client

The most common obstacle: the platform-dependent approach (Upwork, Freelancer) requires reviews to get reviews, creating a catch-22 for beginners. The bypass: your professional network. The first client is almost always someone you already know in a professional capacity, or a referral from someone you know. Begin by listing every professional contact you have who might know of organisations needing your specific skills. An email to ten relevant contacts describing what you are now offering often produces the first project without any platform involvement.

Rate Setting

The most common freelancing error is underpricing. The reasoning: "I am new, I should charge less to compensate." The reality: clients correlate price with quality. A very low rate does not attract more clients -- it attracts clients who want the lowest possible price and who are often the most demanding. Set your rate at 70-80% of the local market rate for your skill level and experience. This is competitive without signalling desperation. Increase it with each new client engagement rather than staying at the starting rate.

The Proposal

A winning freelance proposal contains three elements: a demonstration that you have understood the specific problem (paraphrase it back in your own words -- this differentiates you from every generic proposal), a clear description of your approach (not a guarantee of the outcome, but a methodology that demonstrates competence), and social proof (a specific example of similar work you have done, with a result). Length: one page maximum. Clients reading 50 proposals want brevity and clarity, not comprehensiveness.

BiH Legal Structures for Freelancers

  • Pausal obrtnik (fixed-rate sole trader): The simplest legal structure. A fixed monthly contribution to the government (typically 200-400 BAM/month depending on the canton, regardless of income). Ideal for freelancers earning up to approximately 50,000 BAM/year. The contributions cover health insurance and pension. Registration at the relevant cantonal ministry -- the process takes 1-2 weeks and requires minimal documentation.
  • Redovni obrtnik (standard sole trader): Tax is paid as a percentage of net income (income minus business expenses). More complex but more appropriate once income exceeds the pausal threshold. Requires a bookkeeper (računovoda) -- this cost (typically 100-200 BAM/month) is a business expense and is worth it for the compliance certainty it provides.
  • d.o.o. (limited liability company): Separate legal entity, limited personal liability, 10% flat income tax on distributed profits. The correct structure once annual revenue exceeds approximately 100,000 BAM or if you want to invoice EU clients with VAT (a BiH d.o.o. can register for VAT in the EU under certain conditions). The overhead is higher -- company registration, annual financial statements, a properly registered bank account.
  • Invoice and payment: For international clients, receive payment in EUR to a foreign currency account (available at all major Bosnian banks). Wise (formerly TransferWise) is the most cost-effective way to receive international payments into a EUR account accessible from Sarajevo.
Freelancing is not a backup plan. For a skilled IT professional in Sarajevo in 2025, it is the highest-return career path available -- higher rates than local employment, more autonomy than any employer offers, and the leverage of Sarajevo's cost structure against Western European billing rates. The barrier is the first client. Everything after that is iteration.-- Von Lycaon
Money managed poorly produces a specific kind of low-grade anxiety that sits beneath every other decision in a man's life. Money managed well produces something quieter and more valuable than wealth: options. The following is not investment advice in the legal sense. It is a practical framework for a reasonably organised person in Sarajevo who wants to stop leaving money on the table and start building something deliberate.— Von Lycaon

The Foundation — Where You Actually Stand

Most people who consider themselves reasonably organised have an approximate sense of their finances but have never produced an exact picture. The exact picture is the starting point for everything that follows. It takes one hour to produce and is only ever done once — after that, maintenance takes fifteen minutes monthly.

The Net Worth Snapshot
One time · 60 min · The complete financial picture
  • Assets: List everything you own with a current value. Apartment (current market value — check Nekretnine.ba for comparable properties in your area). Savings accounts (exact balance). Any investments. Any valuables of significant worth. Sum these.
  • Liabilities: List everything you owe. Any loans. Any credit card balances. Any informal debts. Sum these.
  • Net worth = Assets minus Liabilities. This number is your starting point. Write it down with today's date. Recalculate every six months. The direction of change matters more than the absolute number.
  • Monthly cash flow: Total income minus total fixed expenses (rent of owned apartment is zero, but: komunalije, struja, grijanje, internet, phone, food, transport, subscriptions). What remains is discretionary income — the number the rest of this tab is about deploying intelligently.

The Three-Account System

One bank account is a single container for all money — income arrives, expenses leave, and whatever remains is undefined and therefore unprotected. Three accounts costs nothing to set up and produces complete clarity about where money is, what it is for, and whether spending in any category is on track.

Account 1 — Operations

All income arrives here. All fixed bills paid from here. All daily spending comes from here. This account should contain: one month of fixed expenses as a buffer, plus your monthly spending money. Nothing more. When the month ends, whatever remains above the buffer transfers to Account 2. The discipline is not looking at this account's balance as a spending ceiling — the spending ceiling is the monthly budget.

Account 2 — Emergency Fund

Three to six months of total living expenses, held in a separate account, never touched except for genuine emergencies. Definition of genuine emergency: job loss, medical expense, major appliance failure. Not a holiday. Not a large purchase you wanted. The emergency fund is the financial equivalent of the spare tyre — ignored entirely until the moment it is the only thing that matters. For Sarajevo: target 6,000–10,000 BAM depending on monthly expenses.

Account 3 — Goals & Investment

Everything above the emergency fund threshold goes here. This account funds specific goals (apartment renovation, a car in future, travel) and long-term investment. It is not touched for daily life. Seeing it grow monthly is one of the most reliable behavioural reinforcements for continued financial discipline.

Budgeting — The Sarajevo Reality

Generic budgeting frameworks (50/30/20 etc.) were built around Western European or American income and cost structures. Bosnia Herzegovina has specific characteristics that alter the proportions: lower average income, lower but variable communal costs, a significant informal economy, and cultural expectations around family financial support that are real costs even when they do not appear on a bank statement.

Fixed Monthly Costs — Know These Exactly

  • Komunalije (communal charges): Building maintenance fund, cleaning, lift maintenance. Typically 30–80 BAM monthly depending on building and apartment size. Know your exact figure.
  • Struja (electricity): Variable by season. Sarajevo winters with electric heating can reach 150–200 BAM monthly. Summer typically 40–60 BAM. Budget the winter figure year-round and treat the summer reduction as a savings contribution.
  • Grijanje (district heating): If your building is on the district heating network (Toplane Sarajevo), this is a fixed seasonal charge billed separately from electricity. Know the annual total and divide by 12 for monthly budgeting purposes.
  • Internet + phone: BH Telecom, HT Eronet, or Telemach packages typically 50–80 BAM monthly combined. Review annually — providers regularly offer better packages to new customers that existing customers can negotiate onto.
  • Building insurance: Mandatory for owned apartments. If not currently held, obtain it — Sarajevo is in a seismic zone and flood risk exists in certain areas. Euroherc, Triglav, and Uniqa all operate here. Annual premium for a standard apartment: 200–400 BAM.

Investment — Starting Points for Bosnia

Disclaimer: This is general financial literacy, not personalised investment advice. Consult a certified financial advisor for decisions involving significant capital.

📈 Index Funds & ETFs

The most evidence-supported long-term wealth building tool available to a retail investor. A global index ETF (such as Vanguard FTSE All-World or iShares MSCI World) buys a small piece of thousands of companies simultaneously. Historical average return: 7–10% annually over 20+ year periods. Available through European brokers accessible from Bosnia: Trading 212, DEGIRO, or Interactive Brokers. All accept Bosnian residents. Start with 50–100 EUR monthly — the amount matters less than the consistency and the time horizon.

🏦 Savings Accounts — Bosnian Banks

Term deposits (oročena štednja) at Bosnian banks currently offer 2–4% annual interest on BAM deposits. Not inflation-beating but better than a current account. UniCredit, Raiffeisen, and Sparkasse Bosnia all offer competitive term deposit rates. Useful for the emergency fund portion that should not be invested — liquid enough to access, earning something while it sits.

🏠 Property

Sarajevo property values have appreciated consistently over the past decade. If the apartment is owned outright, it is already the largest asset on the net worth statement. Additional property investment requires significant capital and carries management complexity. Not the starting point for most people — but worth understanding as the capital base grows.

💶 EUR/USD Holdings

Given BAM's peg to the Euro and regional economic uncertainty, holding a portion of savings in EUR provides currency stability. Most Bosnian banks offer EUR savings accounts. Not an investment strategy — a hedging strategy. 20–30% of total savings in EUR is a reasonable position for a Sarajevo resident.

The Monthly Financial Review — 15 Minutes

  • First of every month: Open the bank app. Review last month's transactions by category. Identify any category that exceeded budget. Note it — not with judgment, but with data. Patterns visible over three months are actionable. Single months are noise.
  • Transfer surplus to Account 3. Whatever remains in Account 1 above the one-month buffer moves immediately. The transfer happens before discretionary spending decisions are made for the new month — not after.
  • Check Account 3 balance and growth. Watching the number increase is the most reliable motivational tool in personal finance. It costs nothing and takes ten seconds.
  • Review any subscriptions. Monthly. Cancel anything unused in the past 30 days. Subscriptions accumulate silently and the aggregated monthly cost of forgotten subscriptions is consistently surprising when enumerated.

Financial Protection — Non-Negotiable

  • Life insurance: If your wife depends on your income, life insurance is not optional — it is the responsible baseline. Term life insurance (ročno osiguranje života) through any Bosnian insurer. The premium at your age and health will be minimal. The protection in the event of the unthinkable is not.
  • Health insurance supplement: Bosnia's public health system covers basics. Supplemental private health insurance covers faster specialist access, dental, and procedures with long public waiting lists. Merkur, Triglav, and Generali all offer plans in Bosnia. Review annually.
  • Will and property documentation: Owned apartment documentation should be current, accessible, and your wife should know exactly where it is. This is not morbid planning — it is responsible ownership. A solicitor (notar) in Sarajevo charges minimal fees for basic document notarisation.
Financial discipline is not the restriction of pleasure. It is the deliberate allocation of resources toward the life you have chosen. A man who does not know where his money goes does not know where his priorities actually lie — regardless of what he believes about himself.— Von Lycaon

Investment Vehicles Available to a Sarajevo Resident

The investment options available from BiH are more limited than those available to EU residents but more accessible than most people assume. The primary barriers are awareness and the absence of the automatic pension contribution infrastructure that EU workers access by default.

  • Interactive Brokers: The most accessible international brokerage for Bosnian residents. Account opening is online, identification is via passport, and the account provides access to US and European ETFs (Exchange-Traded Funds), individual stocks, and bonds. The minimum deposit is low, the fees are competitive for small investors, and the tax reporting is clear enough to use with a BiH accountant. This is the correct vehicle for investing in index funds from BiH.
  • Global index ETFs: The Vanguard VWRA (All-World accumulating ETF) and the iShares MSCI World (IWDA) are the two most recommended global index funds for European investors. Both trade on the London Stock Exchange in USD or EUR, are accessible through Interactive Brokers, and provide exposure to thousands of companies across developed and emerging markets in a single instrument. The accumulating version reinvests dividends automatically, which is tax-efficient for residents whose country does not tax accumulation (BiH tax law on this is worth confirming with a local accountant — the rules are less clearly established than in EU countries).
  • The emergency fund before investment: No investment programme is sensible before a 3–6 month emergency fund exists in a savings account. The emergency fund's purpose is to prevent the need to liquidate investments during downturns to cover unexpected expenses — the moment that produces the worst investment returns. Emergency fund first, then the monthly ETF contribution, in that order without exception.
  • The KM savings account: Bosnian banks offer savings accounts in KM with interest rates that, in the current rate environment, are genuinely worth using for the emergency fund component. UniCredit Bank Banja Luka and Raiffeisen Bank BiH both offer competitive term deposit rates. Compare current rates before committing — the difference between 1% and 3% on a 6-month emergency fund is meaningful over time.
Legal literacy is not knowing the law — lawyers know the law. Legal literacy is knowing enough to identify when a situation has legal implications, what your rights are in the situations you commonly encounter, and when you need professional assistance rather than personal improvisation. In Bosnia and Herzegovina, this knowledge is particularly valuable in a legal system that combines civil law traditions with a complex constitutional structure unique in Europe.— Von Lycaon
Disclaimer: This tab provides general legal literacy for the BiH context. It is not legal advice. For any specific legal situation, consult a licensed lawyer (advokat) in Bosnia and Herzegovina. The Advokatska komora Federacije BiH maintains a register of licensed practitioners.

Property — Owning an Apartment in Sarajevo

The Documents You Must Have and Know

  • Vlasnički list (Land Registry Extract): The definitive proof of ownership. Available from the Zemljišno-knjižni ured (Land Registry Office) at the relevant Municipal Court (Općinski sud). Verify it is current — the land registry should match the actual ownership situation. Any discrepancy between the vlasnički list and actual possession is a legal problem that resolves on the vlasnički list's terms.
  • Posjedovni list (Cadastral Record): Describes the physical characteristics of the property. Should match the vlasnički list. Discrepancies between the two records are common in Sarajevo from the privatisation period of the 1990s and require legal resolution before any property transaction.
  • Etažna svojina (Condominium Title): The legal title specific to apartment ownership — distinguishes your individual unit from the common areas of the building. Ensure this is correctly registered.
  • Keep originals in the emergency kit document envelope. Keep digital scans in cloud storage. The cost of replacing lost property documents through the courts is months of time and significant legal fees.

When Selling or Buying Property

  • Always use a notar (notary public). Property transactions in BiH require notarial certification to be legally valid. Never complete a property transaction without it regardless of how well you know the other party.
  • Conduct a full land registry check before any purchase. Verify there are no tereti (encumbrances) — mortgages, liens, or third-party rights registered against the property. A property with unresolved encumbrances passes them to the new owner.
  • Porez na promet nekretnina (Property transfer tax): Currently 5% of the transaction value in FBiH. Factor this into any purchase or sale calculation.
  • Preemption rights (pravo preče kupovine): Co-owners of a property have the right of first refusal if another co-owner wishes to sell their share. If the property has multiple registered owners (common in inherited properties), this right must be properly waived in writing before the sale to a third party.

Employment Law — Your Rights as an Employee

The Employment Contract

  • Read every employment contract before signing. Every word. The probationary period (probni rad), notice period (otkazni rok), non-compete clauses (klauzula o zabrani konkurencije), and intellectual property assignment clauses all have significant implications. Signing without reading is not a defence against any provision.
  • Ugovor o radu (employment contract) vs Ugovor o djelu (service contract): The distinction matters significantly for rights. An ugovor o radu grants full employment rights — annual leave, sick leave, social contributions, unfair dismissal protection. An ugovor o djelu does not. Ensure you are correctly classified for the work you perform.
  • Annual leave: In FBiH, minimum 20 working days of paid annual leave (godišnji odmor) is guaranteed by law regardless of what an employment contract says. Any contract providing less is unenforceable on that point. Unused leave must generally be used within a prescribed period or compensated — check your specific contract terms.
  • Sick leave: Bolovanje rights are complex and employer/government split. First 42 days typically employer-funded, after which the zdravstveno osiguranje (health insurance) fund takes over. Know your employer's sick leave policy specifically.

Contracts — Reading and Signing

  • Read before signing. Always. "It's just a standard contract" is not a guarantee of its contents. Standard contracts are drafted by lawyers representing the other party's interests. They are standard for them.
  • Key clauses to identify in any contract: Duration and termination conditions. Payment terms and late payment consequences. Liability limitation clauses. Dispute resolution mechanism (which court, which law applies). Force majeure provisions.
  • Anything can be negotiated before signing. A signed contract is binding. A proposed contract is an offer. Strike through what you do not accept, initial the change, return it. If they refuse to negotiate, you have learned something important about the other party.
  • Verbal agreements are contracts. In BiH civil law, verbal contracts are generally valid but practically unenforceable without corroborating evidence. Any agreement of significance — money, services, property, obligations — should be in writing. A message confirming terms agreed verbally is better than nothing. A signed document is better than a message.

Consumer Rights in BiH

  • Zakon o zaštiti potrošača FBiH: The Federal Consumer Protection Law. Key rights: right to correct information about products, right to repair or replacement for defective goods, right to cancel distance contracts (online purchases) within 14 days without reason.
  • Warranty (garancija): For new goods, minimum 2 years legal warranty in BiH. Within the first 6 months, defects are presumed to have existed at purchase — the seller must prove otherwise. After 6 months, the burden shifts to the consumer. Keep receipts and proof of purchase for any significant item for 2 years minimum.
  • Online purchases from EU platforms: Subject to EU consumer law if the seller targets BiH consumers, but enforcement is practical only through the platform's dispute resolution (PayPal, credit card chargeback, platform dispute system). Know the platform's process before assuming legal recourse is the first option.

When To Call a Lawyer

  • Any property transaction — purchase, sale, inheritance, gifting.
  • Any employment dispute — wrongful termination, unpaid salary, discrimination.
  • Any contract above 2,000 BAM in value that is not a standard retail transaction.
  • Any situation where you have received a legal document (court summons, formal notice, demand letter) from another party.
  • Any business formation — registering a company, entering a partnership.
  • Do not attempt to navigate these alone. The cost of an advokat for a consultation is 100–300 BAM. The cost of a legal error in any of the above categories is measured in thousands of marks, months of time, or both.
The man who understands the legal framework of his own life is not paranoid — he is prepared. The papers are in order, the rights are known, the obligations are understood. When something goes wrong, and occasionally things go wrong, he acts with information rather than with confusion. That distinction, in a legal context, is frequently the difference between resolution and damage.— Von Lycaon
Digital security is not paranoia — it is the recognition that the same negligence that leaves a physical door unlocked leaves a digital one open. The consequences of a compromised email account, a stolen password, or an unencrypted device are not theoretical. They are financial, professional, and deeply personal. This tab closes the gaps that most people leave open indefinitely.— Von Lycaon

The Threat Model — What You Are Actually Protecting Against

Effective security is proportionate to the actual threat. The average person in Sarajevo is not a target of state-level surveillance or sophisticated targeted attacks. The realistic threats are: credential stuffing (automated use of leaked passwords), phishing (deceptive emails or messages that extract credentials), device theft, and account compromise through weak authentication. The measures below address these specifically.

The Password Problem

The average person reuses 5–10 passwords across dozens of accounts. A single data breach at any of those services exposes every account using the same password. This is not hypothetical — credential stuffing attacks use leaked password databases to automatically test credentials across banking, email, and social media services. The solution is a unique strong password for every account. The only way to implement this at scale is a password manager.

Bitwarden — Already in the Codex

The password manager recommended in both the Phone & Apps and Windows 11 tabs. If it is not yet in use: install it today, before anything else in this tab. Generate a unique 16-character random password for every account. Store them in Bitwarden. The master password for Bitwarden is the only password you need to remember — make it a passphrase (four random words) rather than a complex string. Longer is stronger; memorable beats complex.

Two-Factor Authentication

2FA means that even if a password is compromised, the account cannot be accessed without a second factor. Enable it on every account that offers it: email first (the master key to every other account), banking, social media, cloud storage. Use an authenticator app (Aegis, recommended in Phone & Apps) rather than SMS — SMS 2FA is vulnerable to SIM-swapping attacks. The 30 seconds it takes to enter a 2FA code is the most time-efficient security measure available.

Phishing Recognition

Phishing is the delivery of deceptive communications designed to extract credentials or install malware. Indicators: urgency ("your account will be closed in 24 hours"), requests to click a link rather than navigate directly, email addresses that look correct but contain substitutions ([email protected] vs amazon.com), attachments from unexpected senders. The rule: if in doubt, navigate to the service directly rather than clicking the link. One habit that eliminates most phishing risk.

Device Security

  • Full device encryption: Android: Settings → Security → Encryption. Modern Android devices are encrypted by default if a PIN or password is set. Verify this is enabled. An unencrypted stolen device exposes all data on it. An encrypted one is effectively inaccessible without the PIN.
  • Screen lock: PIN minimum 6 digits (not biometric alone — biometrics can be compelled). Set auto-lock to 30 seconds. An unlocked unattended phone in Sarajevo is the digital equivalent of a wallet left on a café table.
  • Windows BitLocker: Settings → Privacy & Security → Device encryption. Encrypts the entire drive. If the laptop is stolen, data is inaccessible without the Windows login. Enable this on any laptop that leaves the home.
  • Software updates: Security vulnerabilities are discovered and patched continuously. An unpatched operating system or application is an open door. Enable automatic security updates on all devices. The inconvenience of a restart is the cost of closing vulnerabilities that are actively exploited in the wild.

Privacy — Beyond Security

  • Email privacy: Proton Mail already recommended in Phone & Apps. For sensitive communications — medical, financial, legal — Proton Mail's end-to-end encryption ensures that even Proton cannot read the content. Gmail and standard email providers read content for advertising purposes. The distinction matters when the content is genuinely private.
  • Browser tracking: Firefox + uBlock Origin (already in Windows 11 tab) blocks the majority of tracking infrastructure. Additionally: consider using Firefox's Enhanced Tracking Protection set to Strict. Brave browser as an alternative — privacy-by-default with no configuration required.
  • Data broker removal: Companies compile personal data profiles from public records and sell them. Periodically search your name + Sarajevo or BiH on Google and review what is publicly indexed. Remove what can be removed from public records where the option exists.
  • Have I Been Pwned: Visit haveibeenpwned.com and enter your email addresses. This free service checks whether your credentials appear in known data breaches. If any email appears — change the password for the affected service immediately and any other service where that password was reused.
The man who secures his digital life maintains the same standards he applies to his physical one. A locked door, a sealed envelope, a private conversation — these are not paranoid acts. They are the baseline of a person who understands that what is private has value precisely because it is private.— Von Lycaon

The Advanced Layer — Beyond the Basics

The basics (Bitwarden, 2FA with Aegis, device encryption, phishing awareness) cover 95% of the realistic threat surface for a private individual in Sarajevo. The following addresses the remaining 5% for those who want comprehensive digital hygiene.

Network Security

Your home WiFi network is the foundation of your digital security at home. The basics: WPA3 encryption (available on all modern routers -- check Settings in your router admin panel). A strong, unique router admin password (not the factory default -- these are publicly documented). A guest network for smart home devices and visitors, separate from the network that carries your computers and phones. The guest network isolation means that a compromised smart home device cannot see traffic on your primary network.

VPN -- When and Why

A VPN encrypts your internet traffic and routes it through a server in another location. Legitimate uses: accessing your home network remotely, bypassing geographic content restrictions, and protection on genuinely untrusted networks (airport WiFi, hotel WiFi). Not a security panacea -- a VPN protects traffic between you and the VPN server, but the VPN provider sees all your traffic. Use only providers with independently audited no-logs policies (Mullvad, ProtonVPN). Free VPNs are either logging your data or funding themselves through advertising -- both defeat the purpose.

Email Security

Email is the most valuable account to protect because it is the recovery mechanism for every other account -- password reset links are sent to email. The email account must have: a strong unique password in Bitwarden, 2FA with Aegis (not SMS), and recovery codes stored offline (printed and stored physically, not in another digital account). If this account is compromised, every other account that uses it for recovery is accessible. Treat email security as more important than any other account security by the multiple of accounts it can unlock.

Physical Security

The most sophisticated digital security is bypassed by physical access. Screen lock with PIN (not just biometric -- biometrics can be compelled). Full disk encryption on all devices (already covered, worth reiterating). A clean desk policy -- papers with personal information not left visible. Consider a physical security key (YubiKey) for the most sensitive accounts -- a hardware device that must be physically present for authentication, making remote account compromise essentially impossible regardless of credential theft.

The Incident Response Protocol

If you believe an account has been compromised -- you receive a notification of login from an unknown location, you notice unfamiliar activity, or a service you use announces a data breach -- the response sequence:

  • Immediate: Change the password for the compromised account immediately from a known-clean device. Enable or verify 2FA. Review recent account activity for any actions taken by the attacker (emails sent, settings changed, purchases made).
  • Within 24 hours: Check if any other accounts used the same password (search in Bitwarden -- if you have been following the protocol, this should be zero). Check haveibeenpwned.com for whether your email address appears in breach databases. If the compromised account was your email, review all other accounts that use it for recovery and change those passwords.
  • Report: If the compromise involved financial accounts, contact the bank immediately. In BiH, card fraud can be reported directly to the bank's fraud line. Document the timeline of events for any insurance or legal claim.
Digital security is not a project to complete. It is a maintenance standard to sustain. The attack surface changes continuously as new services are adopted and new vulnerabilities are discovered. The man who reviews his security posture annually -- updating passwords, reviewing account permissions, checking breach databases -- maintains his defences without paranoia. The man who set it up once five years ago is already compromised in ways he has not yet discovered.-- Von Lycaon
A productivity system is not about doing more. It is about doing the right things and knowing, with confidence, that nothing important is being forgotten. The anxiety of the disorganised mind — the background hum of tasks that might be slipping — is a cognitive tax paid continuously. A reliable system eliminates that tax. What follows is the system that works for someone who needs to manage professional obligations, personal projects, and domestic life simultaneously.— Von Lycaon

The Second Brain — The Concept

Tiago Forte's "Building a Second Brain" framework: the human brain is for generating ideas and making decisions, not for storing information. Information storage in the brain produces the cognitive equivalent of RAM full — available processing power is consumed by the effort to hold things in memory that could be stored externally. The "second brain" is an external system that stores everything worth keeping, freeing the biological brain for its actual function.

The PARA System
