Projects: Things with a defined end state and a deadline. "Write Q3 report" is a project. "Write" is not. Areas: Ongoing responsibilities without end dates — Health, Finance, Work, Relationships. Resources: Topics and reference material you find interesting or useful — a growing library of notes, articles, and ideas. Archive: Completed projects and inactive material. Everything in your life fits into one of these four categories. Knowing which category something belongs to determines where it lives and how frequently it needs attention.

The GTD Capture Habit

David Allen's Getting Things Done core principle: every thought, task, obligation, or idea that enters the mind is immediately captured in a trusted inbox — a single place where everything lands before being processed. The capture happens without judgment — sorting is a separate step. The inbox is processed on a schedule (daily for time-sensitive items, weekly for everything else). The result: nothing lives in the head that can live in the system.

Obsidian as the Second Brain

Already in the Codex. The full application: Projects folder (active projects with their associated notes and tasks), Areas folder (ongoing responsibility areas with relevant reference material), Resources folder (notes from books, articles, courses), Archive folder (completed work). The Daily Note as the inbox — everything captured in today's note, processed into PARA in the weekly review.

Todoist as the Task Manager

Already in the Phone & Apps tab. The specific configuration for GTD: Projects in Todoist mirror Projects in Obsidian. The daily view shows today's tasks and overdue items. The weekly review processes the inbox into the correct project. The rule: if a task can be done in under two minutes, do it immediately rather than capturing it. For everything else, capture first, schedule second.

The Weekly Review — The System's Maintenance

The weekly review is the single practice that keeps the system functional. Without it, inboxes fill, projects stall, and the system becomes another abandoned productivity experiment. It takes 30–45 minutes and happens on Sunday.

  • Clear the capture inboxes: Process every item in the Obsidian Daily Notes from the week, every Todoist inbox item, every physical paper on the desk. Each item: delete (not actionable), do (under 2 minutes), delegate (assign to someone else with a follow-up), or defer (schedule for a specific date or put in a project).
  • Review active projects: For each project: is it moving? What is the next action? Is the deadline still realistic? Update project notes in Obsidian. Ensure every active project has at least one task scheduled in Todoist for the coming week.
  • Review calendar: Look at the coming week. What preparation is required for upcoming events and meetings? Schedule that preparation now, not the morning of.
  • Review Areas: A brief scan of each ongoing responsibility area — Health (any appointments due?), Finance (monthly review timing?), Relationships (anyone who needs a check-in this week?). This 2-minute scan prevents important ongoing responsibilities from being crowded out by urgent but less important project work.

The Daily Shutdown Ritual

  • Five minutes at the end of each work day: review tomorrow's calendar, ensure today's tasks are complete or rescheduled, update the Obsidian daily note with any captures from the day. End with a literal verbal statement: "Shutdown complete." Cal Newport's specific ritual — the spoken statement signals to the brain that the work is finished and resists the urge to continue reviewing work email through the evening.
A trusted system does not eliminate the work. It eliminates the anxiety about the work — which is often heavier than the work itself. The man with a reliable system does not lie awake at 2am reviewing the day's obligations. He wrote them down and trusted the system to surface them at the correct time.— Von Lycaon

The Second Brain — Full Implementation

Tiago Forte's concept in practice: the goal is to externalise all knowledge management so that the brain is freed from storage and retrieval and can focus entirely on synthesis and creation. The full Obsidian implementation:

The Zettelkasten Method

Developed by German sociologist Niklas Luhmann, who used it to produce 70 books and 400 articles over a 40-year career. The principle: every note contains a single idea, stated completely in your own words. Each note is connected to other notes through explicit links. The resulting network of ideas becomes a thinking tool -- combinations of notes that you did not intentionally create produce new ideas through their connections. In Obsidian, the backlinks panel and the graph view visualise this network. The key practice: when writing any note, explicitly link it to every other relevant note. The links are the system's value.

Progressive Summarisation

Forte's technique for processing captured content: Layer 1 -- save the original. Layer 2 -- bold the most important passages when you have time to read it. Layer 3 -- highlight within the bold (the most important of the most important). Layer 4 -- executive summary in your own words. This layered approach means that any captured note can be processed at different levels of depth depending on how important it becomes over time, without committing to full processing at the moment of capture.

The Projects Vault

Every active project has a note in Obsidian that functions as a hub: the project's goal, the current status, links to all relevant reference notes, a running log of decisions and progress, and the next action. Before any work session on a project, open its hub note and read the last three log entries -- this instantly restores context that the mind loses between sessions. Before ending a work session, update the log with what was done and what comes next. The morning of a deadline should never be the first time you look at the project hub.

Time Blocking — The Schedule as a Design

Cal Newport's time blocking: schedule every hour of the working day in advance, blocking time for specific categories of work rather than managing a task list reactively. The research on knowledge worker productivity consistently shows that reactive task management (checking email, responding to messages, addressing whatever appears) produces the feeling of productivity while consistently failing to advance the highest-value work. Time blocking forces the prioritisation decision to be made in the morning (when cognitive resources are highest) rather than continuously throughout the day as tasks compete for attention.

  • The morning block (9:00-11:00 or equivalent): Protected for the most cognitively demanding work. No email, no Slack, no meetings. The two morning hours of concentrated work frequently produce more output of genuine quality than the remaining six hours of reactive work combined.
  • The communication block (11:00-12:00, 15:00-16:00): Email, messages, and brief meetings consolidated into designated windows. Processing communication in batches takes less total time than processing it continuously because the context-switching cost between deep work and communication is eliminated.
  • The shallow work block (afternoon): Administrative tasks, routine requests, filling out forms, anything that requires presence but not deep concentration. Scheduling these in the afternoon when cognitive energy is lower means that deep work is never displaced by shallow work.

Energy Management — The Deeper Layer

Tony Schwartz's insight: time is finite and fixed; energy is renewable but requires management. The person who manages their energy manages their time automatically, because high-energy periods produce more output per hour than low-energy periods regardless of how many hours are worked. The energy sources and their management:

  • Physical energy (the foundation): Sleep, nutrition, and training from the Body category of this Codex. Without these in place, no productivity system produces consistent results because the underlying energy supply is depleted.
  • Emotional energy: Unresolved emotional tension (conflict, anxiety, resentment) consumes cognitive resources continuously. The conflict resolution and stress management practices in this Codex are not soft skills -- they are energy management tools that protect cognitive performance.
  • Focus energy: Depleted by context-switching, interruptions, and the continuous partial attention mode that smartphone use produces. Rebuilt by extended periods of single-task concentration, time in nature, and the deliberate absence of stimulation that the morning routine without a phone creates.
Productivity is not the management of tasks. It is the management of energy applied to the work that matters most. The system serves this purpose -- it does not constitute it. A perfect system in service of the wrong work produces impressive activity and trivial results.-- Von Lycaon
AI tools in 2025 represent the most significant productivity multiplier available to a knowledge worker since the spreadsheet. The person who integrates them intelligently into daily work produces more, learns faster, and operates with a lower cognitive overhead on routine tasks. The person who ignores them operates at an increasing disadvantage relative to those who do not. This tab is the practical guide to using them correctly.— Von Lycaon

The Correct Mental Model

AI tools are not oracles — they are capable, fast, sometimes wrong, and entirely dependent on the quality of the direction they are given. The person who achieves the most from AI tools is the one who brings precise thinking to their use: clear specification of what is needed, critical evaluation of what is produced, and the domain knowledge to identify errors that would not be apparent to a non-expert. The tool amplifies existing capability; it does not replace the capability itself.

Claude (Anthropic)

The AI you are using now. Strongest for: complex analysis and reasoning, long-form writing and editing, nuanced research synthesis, coding assistance, explaining complex topics at the depth you specify. The Codex itself was built in collaboration with Claude. Best accessed via claude.ai — the free tier is functional, Pro provides access to the most capable models for demanding tasks.

ChatGPT (OpenAI)

The most widely used AI assistant. Strong for: general knowledge questions, creative brainstorming, image generation (DALL-E integration), code generation. GPT-4o is the current capable model. Free tier available; Plus subscription provides priority access and advanced features. Complementary to Claude rather than competitive — different strengths, both worth having access to.

Perplexity

An AI-native search engine — combines web search with AI synthesis. More reliable than ChatGPT for current information (it searches the web in real time). Use for: current events and recent information, research that requires up-to-date sources, fact-checking claims. The free tier is fully functional for most use cases. Available at perplexity.ai.

GitHub Copilot

AI coding assistant that integrates directly into development environments. For an IT analyst who writes scripts, SQL queries, or automation code — Copilot significantly reduces the time required for boilerplate code and suggests implementations based on comments describing intent. Available via GitHub subscription. The most directly financially returnable AI tool for a technical professional.

Practical Applications — IT Analyst Context

📊 Data Analysis

Describe a dataset and the analysis needed → Claude or ChatGPT generates the SQL query, Python script, or Excel formula. This is not cheating — it is using the available tool. A formula that took 20 minutes to construct from documentation takes 30 seconds with AI assistance. The time saved is available for the analysis interpretation that AI cannot perform, which is where your actual value lies.

📝 Writing & Documentation

First draft generation for reports, process documentation, and technical specifications. The correct use: provide a detailed brief and the key points to be made, generate a first draft, edit substantially. The AI produces the structure and the prose skeleton; you provide the accuracy, the judgment, and the domain-specific nuance. Never submit AI-generated content without significant review and editing.

🔍 Research Synthesis

Provide a complex topic and ask for a synthesis of the key considerations, competing frameworks, or current state of the field. Use Perplexity for current information, Claude for analytical depth. Always verify specific claims with primary sources before using them in professional work — AI tools hallucinate facts with the same confident tone they use for accurate ones. The synthesis is the starting point, not the endpoint.

🎓 Learning Acceleration

The Feynman technique augmented by AI: explain a concept you are learning to Claude and ask it to identify gaps, errors, or oversimplifications in your explanation. This provides immediate Feynman-technique feedback without requiring a human teacher. For any technical topic being studied — SQL, a new programming concept, a regulatory framework — this use case alone justifies the tool.

Effective Prompting — The Skill That Multiplies Everything

  • Specify the role: "You are a senior IT analyst reviewing a process documentation draft" produces more targeted output than an unprefaced request.
  • Specify the output format: "Respond in bullet points" / "Write this as a formal email" / "Provide a numbered step-by-step guide" — the AI will produce whatever format is requested.
  • Provide context: The more relevant context provided, the more relevant the output. The AI knows nothing about your specific situation beyond what you tell it in the conversation.
  • Iterate: The first response is rarely the best one. "Make it more concise" / "Add a section on X" / "Rewrite the third paragraph with more specificity" — treating the AI as a collaborative editor rather than a one-shot machine produces significantly better results.
The AI tool is the sharpest instrument in the current professional toolkit. Like any sharp instrument, it requires skill to use well, produces excellent results in trained hands, and can cause significant damage when used carelessly. Learn to use it. Do not fear it. Do not trust it blindly.— Von Lycaon
An environment reflects its inhabitant. Your desktop is where your work lives — where you spend, perhaps, more hours than any physical room in your life. I find it entirely appropriate to maintain it with the same standards applied to everything else: ordered, purposeful, and quietly elegant. A cluttered desktop does not merely look disordered — it produces a subtle, constant cognitive tax on everyone who uses it.— Von Lycaon

Desktop Philosophy

  • Zero desktop icons — without exception. Every icon on the desktop is a decision deferred. All shortcuts belong in the Start Menu or Taskbar. The desktop is a viewing surface for your wallpaper, not a filing system.
  • Virtual desktops — use them. Win + Tab opens the virtual desktop manager. Create one for Work, one for Personal, one for a current project. Switch with Ctrl + Win + Arrow. The cognitive separation between contexts is genuine and worth the setup.
  • The taskbar is not a storage solution. Pin only what you use daily. Everything else opens from Start or a keyboard shortcut.

Taskbar & Start Menu

Taskbar Configuration

  • Alignment: Left. Settings → Personalisation → Taskbar → Alignment: Left. The brain reads left-to-right; a centred taskbar disrupts spatial consistency and muscle memory built over years of use.
  • Auto-hide: On. The taskbar appears on hover and disappears otherwise. Reclaims ~48px of screen — meaningful on a 1080p display when reading documents or code.
  • Remove clutter: Task View: Off. Search icon: Off. Widgets: Off. Chat: Off. These are decorations on a working toolbar.
  • System tray: Show only Clock, Network, and Volume. Everything else hidden. Right-click tray → Taskbar settings → Other system tray icons: Off for all except these three.
  • Taskbar icon spacing (via Registry): For those who want tighter icon spacing: regedit → HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Advanced → Create DWORD TaskbarSi with value 0 (small), 1 (medium/default), or 2 (large). Requires sign-out to take effect.

Start Menu Reclaim

  • Remove all pinned apps from Start. Right-click each → Unpin from Start. The default pinned apps are Microsoft's suggestions, not yours.
  • Pin only: File Explorer, Settings, and your three or four most-used applications. Everything else is found faster by typing than by scrolling through a grid of icons.
  • Start Menu search: Press Win, type the first 2–3 letters of any application. This is faster than clicking through any interface element. Train this habit within a week and clicking the Start icon will feel archaic.
  • OpenShell (optional): Free, open-source Start Menu replacement that restores a more efficient classic-style menu. openShell.net — for those who find the Windows 11 Start Menu genuinely limiting. Does not modify system files.

Visual Customisation

Colour, Theme & Transparency

  • Dark mode: On. Settings → Personalisation → Colours → Dark. Non-negotiable. Applies system-wide including File Explorer and Settings.
  • Accent colour — custom. Settings → Personalisation → Colours → Custom colour. Enter a hex value: deep burgundy #4d1520, charcoal #2a2a2a, or slate #1e3a5f. Show accent on Start and Taskbar: On. On title bars and window borders: On. The distinction between a default Windows install and a personalised one begins here.
  • Transparency effects: Off. Settings → Personalisation → Colours → Transparency effects: Off. Consumes GPU resources, reduces legibility of text against blurred backgrounds, and adds visual noise. Function over effect.
  • Animation effects: Off (optional). Settings → Accessibility → Visual effects → Animation effects: Off. Removes window open/close animations. The system feels markedly more immediate — everything appears and disappears without theatrical delay. A preference, not a necessity.

Display Scaling & Font Rendering

  • Scaling: 125%. Settings → Display → Scale: 125%. Interface text reads cleanly without feeling enlarged. 100% is comfortable at 27"+ displays. For 24" or below, 125% reduces eye strain over a full day significantly.
  • ClearType Text Tuning: Start → search "Adjust ClearType text" → follow the wizard. Calibrates font sub-pixel rendering to your specific monitor. The difference in text sharpness is visible and the calibration takes three minutes.
  • Night Light: Settings → Display → Night light. Schedule from 20:00 to 07:00. Reduces blue light emission during evening hours. A meaningful reduction in sleep-disrupting stimulus for anyone who works late.
  • HDR (if monitor supports it): Settings → Display → HDR. Enable only if your monitor is rated for it — enabling HDR on a non-HDR monitor washes out colours and reduces apparent contrast. Check your monitor specifications before enabling.

Wallpaper & Desktop Background

  • Aesthetic direction: Dark. Atmospheric. Not busy. Fog-covered mountain, night forest, architectural photograph at dusk, minimal dark geometric composition. Nothing that contains text, faces, or complex colour that competes with application windows placed over it.
  • Wallhaven.cc: Filter by Purity (SFW), Colour (near black or deep charcoal), Resolution matching your display. A library vast enough to provide months of rotation without repetition.
  • Unsplash.com: Search "dark forest", "foggy mountain", "brutalist architecture night", "minimal dark". Download at maximum resolution. Royalty-free.
  • Slideshow rotation: Settings → Personalisation → Background → Slideshow. Build a curated folder of 8–12 images. Rotation every hour. The variety is pleasant; the curation ensures it is always appropriate.
  • Dynamic Wallpaper (WinDynamicDesktop): Free app — changes wallpaper based on time of day, using macOS-style day-to-night transitions. Available at Microsoft Store. Adds a subtle temporal dimension to the desktop without any active management.

Advanced Customisation

ExplorerPatcher — Reclaim the Interface

  • What it is: A free, open-source tool that restores Windows 10-style interface elements and adds configuration options not available in Settings. Used by millions — not experimental software.
  • Key features: Restore the classic context menu (right-click) — the Windows 11 default requires an extra click to access most options. Restore the full taskbar appearance. Move the clock to the left side of the taskbar. Highly configurable. github.com/valinet/ExplorerPatcher
  • Context menu specifically: Windows 11's abbreviated right-click menu is the most widely criticised UI decision in the release. ExplorerPatcher restores the full menu immediately. Alternatively: regedit → HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Classes\CLSID → create key {86ca1aa0-34aa-4e8b-a509-50c905bae2a2}\InprocServer32 with empty default value → restart Explorer. This achieves the same without the tool.

Rainmeter — Desktop Widgets Done Right

  • What it is: A free, open-source desktop customisation platform. Adds system monitors, clocks, weather widgets, and visualisers directly to the desktop — not in a sidebar, not in a widget panel, but overlaid on the wallpaper itself.
  • Recommended skins: Mond (minimal, dark, clock and system stats — the one that photographs well), Elegance 2 (dark, sophisticated), and Illustro (the clean default that ships with Rainmeter — underrated). All available at rainmeter.net and DeviantArt.
  • Setup: Install Rainmeter, download a skin, extract to Documents\Rainmeter\Skins, right-click the Rainmeter tray icon → Manage → Load. Position by dragging while holding Alt.
  • Discipline: Rainmeter can consume hours. Set a rule — one clock widget, one system monitor, one weather widget, and nothing more. The desktop should be functional, not a project.

Font & Icon Customisation

  • System font replacement (advanced): Windows 11 uses Segoe UI Variable by default — a good font. For those who prefer something different: install any TrueType or OpenType font, then use the free "Change Font" tool or regedit to set it system-wide. HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\FontSubstitutes → set "Segoe UI" to your chosen font name. Test in a VM first if unfamiliar with registry editing.
  • Icon packs: Replace default Windows icons for a more cohesive aesthetic. 7tsp GUI (free) allows system-wide icon replacement without manual registry editing. Recommended packs: Fluent UI Icon Pack, macOS Big Sur Icon Pack (for a minimal rounded aesthetic), or Numix. Apply selectively — replace Folder, Recycle Bin, and This PC icons at minimum.
  • Cursor replacement: Settings → Bluetooth & devices → Mouse → Additional mouse settings → Pointers tab. Download cursor packs from DeviantArt or CursorCC. A minimal dark cursor set — "Posy's Cursor" or "Bibata Modern Dark" — is cleaner than the default Windows arrow on a dark desktop.

Window Management Beyond Snap

  • FancyZones (PowerToys): Define custom window layout zones. Win + Shift + drag a window to assign it to a zone. More flexible than the built-in Snap — create asymmetric layouts (60/40 split, three-column unequal, etc.). Essential for 27"+ monitors where equal halves waste screen real estate.
  • AltDrag (free): Hold Alt + click anywhere in a window to drag it — not just the title bar. Hold Alt + right-click anywhere to resize. Removes the precision required for dragging windows by their narrow title bars. Subtle habit, significant daily friction reduction.
  • Virtual Desktop shortcuts: Win + Ctrl + D: create new virtual desktop. Win + Ctrl + F4: close current virtual desktop. Win + Ctrl + Left/Right: switch. Rename each desktop by double-clicking its name in Task View. Work · Personal · Project is a complete functional setup.

Performance Configuration

Startup & Background Optimisation

  • Startup programs audit: Ctrl + Shift + Esc → Startup Apps. Disable every application not required at login: Spotify, Discord, OneDrive consumer (unless actively syncing), Teams (if not needed immediately), any third-party software that auto-starts. A machine starting in 12 seconds instead of 45 is a different daily experience.
  • Services cleanup (advanced): Win + R → services.msc. Set to Manual (not Disabled) the following services if unused: Fax, Print Spooler (if no printer), Remote Registry, Windows Search (if you use Everything instead). Manual means they start only when requested — not at every boot.
  • Power plan: High Performance. Settings → Power & Sleep → Additional power settings → High Performance. Prevents CPU throttling during active use. For sustained processing tasks — compiling, running VMs, analysis — the difference in responsiveness is tangible.
  • Storage Sense: On, Weekly. Settings → System → Storage → Storage Sense: On. Automatically clears recycle bin and temp files weekly. Not a substitute for intentional file management, but a reliable maintenance background process.

Privacy & Telemetry Reduction

  • Diagnostic data: Basic only. Settings → Privacy & Security → Diagnostics & Feedback → Diagnostic data: Required only. Stops sending detailed usage telemetry to Microsoft while keeping essential system stability reporting.
  • Ad ID: Off. Settings → Privacy & Security → General → Let apps use advertising ID: Off. Prevents cross-app tracking for targeted advertising within Windows applications.
  • O&O ShutUp10++ (optional, advanced): Free, portable application — no installation required. Provides a checklist of all Windows telemetry and privacy settings with recommended configurations clearly marked. One session with this tool configures what would otherwise take hours of manual Settings navigation. oo-software.com/en/shutup10
  • Location: Off for most apps. Settings → Privacy & Security → Location. Allow for Maps. Disable for everything else unless a specific application has a clear functional reason for it.

Windows Update Management

  • Active Hours: Set correctly. Settings → Windows Update → Advanced options → Active hours. Set to your actual working hours. Windows will not restart during this window. Without this configured, an automatic restart at 9am on a working day is a genuine professional incident.
  • Pause updates during critical periods. Settings → Windows Update → Pause updates. Pause for up to 5 weeks when a major project or deadline period demands system stability. Resume when the period has passed.
  • Driver updates: Manual control. Settings → Windows Update → Advanced options → Optional updates. Review driver updates before installing — graphics driver updates in particular occasionally introduce instability. Check the version release notes before accepting.

The Application Suite

📋

Obsidian

Local-first note-taking and knowledge management. Notes stored as plain Markdown files. The graph view visualises connections between notes over time. Free. obsidian.md — no regional restrictions.

⌨️

PowerToys

Maintained by Microsoft. FancyZones, PowerRename, Run launcher (Alt+Space), Awake, Text Extractor (OCR from any screen area), and more. Free — Microsoft Store or GitHub.

🔑

Bitwarden

Open-source password manager. Free tier fully functional. Browser extension autofills everywhere. The professional who reuses passwords is one breach away from a serious incident.

🌐

Firefox + uBlock Origin

Firefox respects privacy more than Chrome by default. Install uBlock Origin immediately — the web without it is a fundamentally different, noisier experience. No exceptions to this recommendation.

🖥️

Everything (voidtools)

Instant file search. Finds any file on the machine within milliseconds by filename. Windows Search is slow and unreliable. Everything is neither. Free — voidtools.com.

📦

7-Zip

The only archive manager required. Lightweight, handles every format. Does not ask to be your default browser. Free at 7-zip.org.

📸

ShareX

Screenshot and screen recording replacing the Snipping Tool entirely. Region capture, annotation, configurable save formats. Free and open-source — getsharex.com.

🔒

Proton VPN

Free tier genuinely functional. Swiss-based, no-logs policy. Essential on any network outside your home. Available globally — protonvpn.com.

🎵

Spotify

Dark theme available in Appearance settings. For work: "lofi jazz", "dark academia study", "instrumental focus". Lyrics-free music does not compete with reading and writing tasks.

📝

Notepad++

The standard lightweight text editor for developers and analysts. Syntax highlighting for every language, multi-tab editing, regex search, plugin ecosystem. Replaces the default Notepad entirely. Free — notepad-plus-plus.org.

🖼️

IrfanView

Lightweight image viewer — opens any format instantly, no loading screen, no subscription prompt. Batch conversion, basic editing. The default Photos app on Windows 11 is slow and feature-poor by comparison. Free — irfanview.com.

📊

HWiNFO64

Detailed hardware monitoring — CPU temperature, GPU load, RAM usage, fan speeds, drive health. Runs quietly in the system tray, logs data over time. For understanding what your machine is doing when it runs hot or slow. Free — hwinfo.com.

File Organisation — The Complete System

  • The root structure: Four top-level folders in your user directory: Work, Personal, Archive, and Inbox. Everything goes into one of these four. Inbox is for unsorted items, reviewed weekly and distributed to their correct home.
  • Date-prefixed naming: Files relating to a specific date or version use ISO format: YYYY-MM-DD. "2025-03-04 Project Brief v2" sorts chronologically in any file explorer view without configuration.
  • Downloads folder is not a storage location. It is a transit point. Review it weekly. Every file is either moved to its correct location or deleted. Nothing stays in Downloads beyond one week.
  • Version control for documents: Append v1, v2, v3 to files that go through revisions. Never overwrite a document you may need to revert to. Disc space is cheap; recovering a previous version is not always possible.
  • 3-2-1 backup rule: Three copies. On two different media types (internal drive + external drive). One off-site (OneDrive, Google Drive, or Backblaze). Data that exists in one place does not truly exist. In Sarajevo with occasional power instability, a local external drive backup in addition to cloud sync is prudent.

Keyboard Shortcuts Worth Mastering

  • Win + D: Show desktop instantly. Second press restores all windows.
  • Win + L: Lock the screen. Every time you leave your desk. In an office environment, an unlocked unattended computer is a security incident waiting to occur.
  • Win + V: Clipboard history — access the last 25 items copied. Enable first: Settings → System → Clipboard → Clipboard History: On.
  • Win + Shift + S: Screenshot selection tool. Drag to select any area, automatically copied to clipboard for immediate pasting.
  • Win + Z: Snap Layouts overlay — choose window arrangement without dragging. Master this before trying FancyZones.
  • Win + . (period): Emoji and symbol picker. Also contains kaomoji and special characters. Faster than any character map application.
  • Ctrl + Shift + V: Paste without formatting in most applications. Pastes plain text, removing source font and colour. Among the most frequently needed and least-known shortcuts.
  • Alt + Tab (held): Window switcher. With PowerToys Run (Alt+Space) for application launching, keyboard navigation eliminates most reasons to reach for the mouse during focused work.
  • F2: Rename a selected file. Select, F2, type, Enter. Faster than the right-click equivalent every time.
  • Ctrl + Shift + Esc: Task Manager directly — bypasses the Ctrl+Alt+Del screen. Faster access to performance monitoring and process management.
  • Win + Ctrl + D / F4 / Arrow: Create / close / switch virtual desktops. The foundation of context-separated working environments.
  • Win + X: Opens the Power User menu — quick access to Device Manager, Event Viewer, Disk Management, PowerShell, and other system tools without navigating Control Panel.
A gentleman understands his city. Not merely its streets, but its character — its history, its rhythms, its best offerings, and its hidden hours. Sarajevo is a city of unusual depth: a place where centuries of Ottoman, Austro-Hungarian, socialist, and post-war history exist in the same square kilometre. To live here without engaging with that is to live in a remarkably interesting city and experience only its surface.— Von Lycaon

Understanding Sarajevo

Sarajevo is one of the few cities in the world where you can stand at a single intersection and see a mosque, an Orthodox church, a Catholic cathedral, and a Sephardic synagogue within the same city block. This is not an accident — it is the legacy of the Ottoman millet system, which administered different religious communities under the same umbrella with considerable local autonomy. The city at its best still functions this way: multiple identities coexisting in a small, navigable space.

Understanding this history does not require formal study. Walk slowly through Baščaršija. Notice the architecture changing as you move west from the Ottoman old town into the Austro-Hungarian grid of the 19th century, then into the Socialist modernism of the mid-20th century, then into the contemporary construction at the edges. The city is a physical timeline. You are walking through it every day.

Where to Spend Time Deliberately

🌿

Vrelo Bosne

The source of the Bosna river, 12km southwest of the city centre. A park of unusual tranquillity: clear blue water, swan ponds, and forest walks. Accessible by tram to Ilidža and a short walk. The early morning visit — before 9am on weekdays — is the visit worth making. It is quiet enough that you can spend an hour without hearing another conversation.

🏔️

Trebević Mountain

Accessible by cable car from the city. The rebuilt gondola (reopened 2018) offers views of Sarajevo that make the scale of the city viscerally apparent. The forest at the top has walking trails ranging from 20 minutes to two hours. In winter: light snow conditions; in summer: forested and cool when the city below is warm.

☕

Kafana Culture

Sarajevo's coffee culture is among the most distinct in Europe. Bosanska kafa — served in a džezva with sugar cubes and a glass of water — is not consumed quickly. It is sat with. A kafana on a side street in Baščaršija in the morning, with a notebook, before the city becomes loud, is one of the best ways to begin a day in this city.

📚

Vijećnica

The Sarajevo City Hall — a Moorish Revival building burned in 1992 and restored and reopened in 2014. Houses rotating exhibitions and the National and University Library. Worth entering even if only to stand beneath the stained-glass ceiling in the central hall. Entry is generally free or nominal charge for exhibitions.

Seasonal Guide

Spring (March–May)
  • The Miljacka riverbanks are most pleasant in April and May — cool but walkable
  • Outdoor kafana terraces begin operating mid-April
  • Rain is frequent — the compact umbrella in your EDC bag earns its place
  • Farmers market at Markale begins its full season — fresh produce, local dairy, and the best honey in the region

The transitional wardrobe (light jacket over sweater) is most practical. The city is at its greenest.

Sarajevo's Neighbourhoods — The Resident's Map

Each Sarajevo neighbourhood has a distinct character developed over centuries that remains legible today despite the overlay of Yugoslav-era housing and post-war construction.

  • Baščaršija: The Ottoman heart. Copper workshops, the Gazi Husrev-beg mosque, the bezistan, the čaršija streetscape that survives largely intact from the 16th century. The most photographed and most visited, which creates a tension between the tourist experience and the neighbourhood's actual daily life. The early morning (before 09:00) and the winter (when tourism drops dramatically) reveal the neighbourhood's character most clearly — the bakers, the coppersmiths, the older residents who maintain the kafana culture without performing it for visitors.
  • Bistrik and Kovači: The hillside mahale above Baščaršija. Ottoman residential architecture (stamboli kuće — traditional wooden houses with enclosed gardens), narrow stepped lanes (sokaci), the Kovači martyrs cemetery with its view of the entire city. The most intact residential Ottoman streetscape in Sarajevo. Worth walking slowly and unhurriedly in any season.
  • Marijin Dvor: The Austro-Hungarian administrative centre and the Yugoslav modernist additions — the Holiday Inn, the UNIS towers, the BBI Centre and SCC malls. The city's business and commercial core. The contrast between the Secession-style early 20th-century buildings and the 1970s–80s brutalist towers is the most compressed architectural biography available in the city.
  • Grbavica: Quiet residential, predominantly apartment blocks. The neighbourhood that was the front line during the siege — Sniper Alley (Zmaja od Bosne) forms its northern boundary. The physical scars are still present if you know how to read buildings. A residential neighbourhood that functions normally while carrying an extraordinary recent history.

What Sarajevo Does Better Than Any Western European City

  • Coffee: Bosnian coffee culture is not an import or an affectation. It is an indigenous institution with its own implement (the džezva), its own sugar system (the rahat lokum eaten alongside, not dissolved in the coffee), and its own pace — a coffee here is never consumed in transit. The quality of a properly made Bosnian coffee at any of the kafanas in the Baščaršija is among the finest coffee experiences available anywhere at any price.
  • Ćevapi: The Sarajevo version of ćevapi is different from the Serbian, Macedonian, or Croatian versions in specific ways that matter: beef only, the small format (around 8–10cm), the somun bread, the raw onion and kajmak accompaniment. Želvić on Bravadžiluk is the reference point — not because it is the best in the city (debates are ongoing) but because it is the clearest expression of what the dish is supposed to be.
  • The Sarajevo Film Festival: A world-class film festival that happens to occur in a city small enough that you can run into the directors and actors on Ferhadija after a screening. For a film-literate resident (Film Literacy tab), August in Sarajevo during the SFF is one of the finest cultural experiences available in Europe at accessible prices.
To live in Sarajevo without knowing its history is to inhabit a stage set without understanding the play. Every neighbourhood, every building, every scar in the landscape carries a layer of meaning that daily life either illuminates or obscures. Sarajevo is one of the most historically dense cities in Europe — the place where the 20th century began, where East met West for five centuries, and where a city was besieged for the longest period of any capital city in modern warfare. Know where you live.— Von Lycaon

The Layers of the City

Illyrian & Roman Foundations

The Sarajevo valley was inhabited by Illyrian tribes — the indigenous people of the western Balkans — long before recorded history. Roman presence is evidenced by archaeological finds throughout the region, though the city as a recognisable urban entity did not emerge until the medieval Bosnian kingdom period. The name "Bosnia" itself derives from the Bosna river, whose source is at Vrelo Bosne near Ilidža — the same park the tram line terminates at today.

Medieval Bosnia

The medieval Bosnian kingdom (12th–15th century) developed a unique religious identity — the Bosnian Church, neither Catholic nor Orthodox, whose adherents were sometimes called Bogumils. The stećci (medieval tombstones) found throughout BiH — including sites accessible from Sarajevo — are UNESCO World Heritage sites and the most visible remnant of this period. The kingdom fell to the Ottoman Empire in 1463.

Ottoman Sarajevo (1462–1878)

Isa-beg Ishaković founded the city as an Ottoman administrative centre in 1461–62. The Baščaršija (bazaar district) was established, the first mosque built (the Careva džamija — Emperor's Mosque), and the city grew rapidly as a trading hub between the Adriatic coast and the Anatolian heartland. By the 16th century under Gazi Husrev-beg (who funded the mosque, the hammam, the bezistan, and the Gazi Husrev-beg Library — all still standing), Sarajevo was the largest city in the Balkans outside Istanbul. The coexistence of four religious communities — Muslim, Orthodox, Catholic, and Jewish — in distinct but adjacent mahallas (neighbourhoods) produced the urban pattern still visible today.

Austro-Hungarian Period (1878–1918)

The Congress of Berlin transferred Bosnia to Austro-Hungarian administration in 1878. The transformation was rapid and dramatic: European-style administrative buildings, the railway connection to Vienna (the station still stands near Marijin Dvor), the Latin Bridge, and the urban expansion westward from Baščaršija toward what is now the modern city centre. The architectural contrast between the Ottoman east and the Austro-Hungarian west of the city is one of Sarajevo's defining visual characteristics.

The Assassination and Its Consequences

On 28 June 1914, Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria and his wife Sophie were assassinated on the Latin Bridge by Gavrilo Princip, a Bosnian Serb member of the nationalist organisation Mlada Bosna. The assassination set in motion the chain of ultimatums, mobilisations, and declarations that produced the First World War — the conflict that ended four empires (Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, Russian, and German) and redrew the map of Europe. The Latin Bridge is five minutes from the centre of the city. The museum at the corner of the bridge documents the event in detail. This is not a footnote of history — this is the corner where the 20th century began.

Yugoslavia and the 20th Century

  • The Kingdom of Yugoslavia (1918–1941): Bosnia became part of the new South Slavic state. Sarajevo remained a significant regional city but lost its status as a provincial capital under the new administrative structure.
  • The Second World War (1941–1945): Yugoslavia was occupied and partitioned by the Axis powers. Bosnia became part of the Independent State of Croatia (NDH), a fascist puppet state that conducted systematic mass murder of Serbs, Jews, and Roma. The Jasenovac concentration camp was the largest atrocity site. Tito's Partisan resistance fought throughout the country, and Bosnia was a major theatre of the guerrilla war.
  • Socialist Yugoslavia (1945–1992): Under Josip Broz Tito, Yugoslavia developed as a non-aligned socialist federation. Sarajevo hosted the 1984 Winter Olympics — an event that produced significant infrastructure (the Zetra stadium, the Skenderija complex, the Trebević bobsled track) and placed the city on the world stage. The period is remembered by many in Sarajevo as one of genuine prosperity and multiethnic coexistence.

The Siege of Sarajevo (1992–1996)

The dissolution of Yugoslavia produced a war in Bosnia that included the longest siege of a capital city in modern warfare. From April 1992 to February 1996 — 1,425 days — Sarajevo was surrounded by Bosnian Serb forces who shelled and sniped the civilian population continuously. Approximately 13,952 people were killed during the siege, of whom over 5,000 were civilians. The city operated without reliable electricity, water, or food supply for extended periods. The Tunnel of Hope (Tunel spasa), dug beneath the airport runway, was the city's lifeline — 800 metres long, 1 metre wide, 1.6 metres high, through which food, weapons, and people moved. The tunnel entrance in Butmir is open to visitors.

The physical evidence of the siege is still present in the city: the rose-shaped concrete scars where mortar shells struck (Sarajevo Roses, some filled with red resin as memorials), bullet-marked buildings particularly in Sniper Alley (Zmaja od Bosne), and the Kovači cemetery on the hillside above Baščaršija where many siege victims are buried.

Sarajevo Today — The Post-War City

  • The Dayton Agreement (1995) ended the war and established the constitutional structure of Bosnia and Herzegovina that remains today — an extraordinarily complex system with two entities (Federacija BiH and Republika Srpska), a district (Brčko), three presidents rotating the state presidency, and a political structure widely recognised as dysfunctional but producing stability of a kind. Understanding this structure explains much of the frustration with governance that Sarajevo residents express.
  • The city's recovery has been significant but uneven. The Vijećnica (City Hall) was destroyed during the siege and restored in 2014. The Holiday Inn, where journalists sheltered during the siege, remains on Sniper Alley. New development has occurred alongside unrestored siege damage in a patchwork that makes the city's history physically legible to anyone paying attention.
  • The cultural vitality of contemporary Sarajevo — the Sarajevo Film Festival (one of the most significant in Europe), the active café culture, the music scene, the food culture, the coexistence of mosque, church, and synagogue in a few hundred metres — reflects the resilience of a city that rebuilt itself after being systematically destroyed.

Sites Worth Knowing as a Resident

🕌 Gazi Husrev-beg Mosque

The most significant Ottoman-era structure in Bosnia, built 1530–1531. The mosque, the bezistan (covered market), the hammam, and the medresa (Islamic school) surrounding it constitute the finest surviving example of 16th-century Ottoman urban planning outside Turkey. Free entry outside prayer times. The courtyard is one of the most peaceful spaces in the city.

🏛️ Vijećnica

The former Austro-Hungarian city hall, rebuilt after its destruction during the siege. The interior — a Moorish Revival confection of coloured stone and stained glass — is extraordinary. Now houses the National and University Library. Open to visitors. Entry fee is modest. The library's destruction during the siege, when incendiary shells burned 1.5 million volumes, was one of the greatest acts of cultural destruction in late 20th-century Europe.

🌹 The Tunnel Museum

The entrance to the Tunnel of Hope in Butmir, southwest of the city. A preserved section of the original tunnel, documentary material, and equipment used during the siege. One of the most affecting sites in the city — the physical scale of the tunnel (crouching height, narrow width) makes the 1,425 days of the siege comprehensible in a way that statistics alone cannot.

🪦 Staro Groblje & Kovači

The old cemetery at Alifakovac (multi-religious, Ottoman through modern) and the Kovači martyr cemetery from the 1990s war. Sarajevo's cemeteries are unusual in containing graves from multiple religious traditions in close proximity — a physical record of the city's history of coexistence and conflict. The view of the city from Kovači at dusk is one of the finest in Sarajevo.

A man who knows the history of the ground beneath him moves through the city differently. Every street name, every building, every scar carries information that most people pass without reading. Read the city. It has been trying to tell its story for five centuries.— Von Lycaon
Sarajevo's climate is not merely background — it actively shapes the daily experience of living here in ways that reward understanding. The valley geography, the altitude, the proximity of both Alpine and Mediterranean influences, and the specific microclimate effects of the surrounding hills produce a weather pattern that surprises even long-term residents when they examine it carefully. Living well in Sarajevo means adapting to it seasonally rather than being surprised by it annually.— Von Lycaon

Sarajevo's Climate — The Overview

Sarajevo sits at approximately 511 metres above sea level in a valley surrounded by mountains reaching 1,700–2,000 metres. This geography produces a modified continental climate with cold winters, warm summers, and significant temperature variation between the valley floor and the surrounding hills. The valley traps cold air in winter (temperature inversions producing fog and smog) and heat in summer, making Sarajevo hotter in July and colder in January than surrounding areas at similar latitudes but lower elevation.

Winter (November–March)

Average temperatures: -1°C to 4°C in January. Extremes reach -15°C to -20°C in severe winters, which occur approximately every 5–7 years. Snow is reliable from December through February — Sarajevo receives an average of 70cm of snow annually, though distribution is irregular. The valley fog (magla) is the defining winter atmospheric phenomenon: cold dense air settles in the valley while the hills above are clear. The air quality during temperature inversions is measurably poor from wood burning and vehicle emissions trapped below the inversion layer.

Spring (April–May)

The most variable season. April brings the first warm days interspersed with late snow and cold rain. May is one of the most pleasant months — temperatures 15–22°C, the hills green and lush, afternoon thunderstorms that refresh rather than oppress. The Miljacka floods occasionally in spring from snowmelt. Sarajevo in May has a quality of light and vegetation density that the summer heat subsequently burns away.

Summer (June–September)

July and August average 25–28°C with peaks reaching 35–38°C in heat waves. Afternoon thunderstorms are common and rapid — the city can go from full sun to violent storm in 20 minutes. The storms are preceded by a characteristic darkening of the sky above the western hills. The valley geography amplifies summer heat — the surrounding hills reflect and trap it. The upper city (Bistrik, Alifakovac) and the hills above are noticeably cooler than Marijin Dvor or Novo Sarajevo on hot days.

Autumn (October–November)

The finest season in Sarajevo by most measures. October particularly: temperatures 10–18°C, the chestnut and maple trees of the city turning, low-angle golden light that makes the Ottoman architecture of Baščaršija extraordinary, and the first frosts on the hills while the valley remains mild. The Sarajevo autumn has a distinctive quality that residents who have left consistently identify as what they miss most about the city.

Seasonal Living — Adapting Practically

Winter Protocol

  • Layering system: The Sarajevo winter requires genuine layering, not just a heavy coat. Base layer (thermal), mid layer (fleece or wool), outer layer (waterproof and windproof). The temperature between an indoor space (overheated by Balkan standards) and the outdoor walk between buildings can differ by 25°C. A single heavy coat is inflexible — the layering system adapts.
  • Air quality awareness: During temperature inversions, particularly January–February, particulate matter in Sarajevo's valley air reaches levels that are genuinely unhealthy for extended outdoor exposure. The IQAIR app or purpleair.com provides real-time Sarajevo air quality data. On high-pollution days, reduce outdoor exercise duration and consider a mask for the commute.
  • Vitamin D supplementation: Already in the Nutrition Science tab — but worth reiterating in the seasonal context. From November through March, Sarajevo's cloud cover and low sun angle make meaningful Vitamin D synthesis through skin exposure essentially impossible. Supplement consistently through winter.
  • Road conditions: Black ice on Sarajevo's hills is a genuine hazard from November through March. The city's road treatment capacity is often overwhelmed during heavy snowfall. Build extra time into any journey requiring uphill movement on icy mornings.

Summer Protocol

  • Heat management: Keep apartment windows closed and shuttered during the day to trap the cooler morning air. Open fully in the evening when outside temperature drops below inside temperature — this cross-ventilation cools the space in 30–45 minutes. This is the traditional Mediterranean technique and it works.
  • Hydration emphasis: The daily 2–2.5 litre target from the Beverages tab becomes a floor in summer, not a ceiling. Add electrolytes (the Ayran recipe from Beverages tab, or simple salt added to water) on days of significant sweating.
  • Storm awareness: Sarajevo summer thunderstorms can produce hail and flash flooding with very little warning. The Meteoalarm.eu alerts for BiH provide advance warning — download and enable notifications during summer months. The Miljacka rises rapidly in severe storms.

The Mountain Seasons

Sarajevo's immediate mountain surroundings — Bjelašnica, Jahorina, Trebević, Igman — operate on a different seasonal schedule than the city and extend the range of activities available year-round.

  • Skiing (January–March): Bjelašnica and Jahorina are 30–45 minutes from the city centre by car or organised transport. Both hosted the 1984 Winter Olympics and retain the infrastructure. Jahorina has the more developed resort experience; Bjelašnica is less crowded. Both offer runs suitable for all levels. The proximity of ski terrain to a capital city of Sarajevo's quality is genuinely unusual in European terms.
  • Hiking (May–October): The marked trail network around Trebević, Jahorina, and Bjelašnica is accessible and well-maintained. The view of Sarajevo from the Trebević summit is the best available of the city. The Skakavac waterfall above Nahorevo is a 45-minute hike from the trailhead and one of the most impressive natural sites within an hour of the city.
  • Vrelo Bosne (April–October): The spring source of the Bosna river in Ilidža — where the tram terminates. A natural park of extraordinary quality with the spring emerging cold and clear from the base of the mountains. Free entry. The walk along the river from the park entrance to the spring takes 20 minutes.
The seasons in Sarajevo are not merely temperature changes. They are distinct modes of the city — different light qualities, different sounds, different smells, different social patterns. The man who learns to live with them rather than against them inhabits the city more fully than the one who treats winter as an inconvenience and summer as a complaint.— Von Lycaon
Sarajevo is a walkable city constrained by geography — the valley forces density along the main east-west axis in a way that makes certain areas effortlessly accessible and others demanding. Understanding the transport system reduces both the time and the friction of daily movement, which is a quality-of-life variable consistently underestimated by those who have not optimised it.— Von Lycaon

Sarajevo Public Transport — The Complete Map

The Tram — The Spine

The tram line runs the full length of the city from Ilidža in the west to Baščaršija in the east — a single corridor that connects the major districts. Line 1, 2, and 3 cover this axis. The tram is the most reliable and fastest public transport option for any journey along this corridor. Learn the stops between your home and office and between your home and the centre — this is the routing 80% of your public transport needs will use.

Trolleybus & Bus

The trolleybus network extends north-south from the tram corridor — up to Pofalići, Ciglane, and the newer settlements above the valley. Bus routes cover the outlying areas: Dobrinja, Nedžarići, Vogošća, the airport area. The GRAS (Gradski saobraćaj Sarajevo) app or Google Maps transit mode gives current routes and schedules. Download and set home and work addresses — journey planning from any point becomes immediate.

Ticketing

Single tickets purchased from the driver: approximately 1.80 BAM. Monthly pass (pretplatna karta): considerably cheaper for daily commuters — around 45–55 BAM monthly depending on zone. Available at GRAS ticket offices. The monthly pass pays back within 2 weeks of daily use. If commuting by public transport daily, the monthly pass is the correct economic choice without calculation.

Timing Reality

Trams run approximately every 5–10 minutes during peak hours, less frequently evenings and weekends. The Sarajevo rush hour (07:30–09:00 and 15:30–17:30) produces significant crowding on the tram. Leave 10–15 minutes earlier than the journey time suggests during these windows. The tram is delayed primarily by road congestion at certain crossings — the Čengić Vila area and the Marijin Dvor intersection specifically.

Cycling in Sarajevo

Sarajevo's cycling infrastructure is improving but remains limited compared to Western European cities. The valley geography creates a straightforward flat cycling corridor along the main tram route but makes north-south movement significantly more demanding. Understanding this shapes what cycling is useful for.

Where Cycling Works

  • The Miljacka riverside path: A dedicated cycling path runs along much of the Miljacka river from the city centre toward Ilidža — flat, pleasant, safe. For weekend recreational cycling and any east-west commute adjacent to the river, this is the correct route.
  • Baščaršija to Ilidža axis: The flat main corridor is cyclable with reasonable confidence. The road surface quality varies — Velešići and certain older sections require attention. The tram tracks are the primary hazard: crossing tram rails at any angle less than 90 degrees risks catching a wheel. Always cross rails at a right angle.
  • Trebević and hills: Road cycling and mountain biking above the city are growing activities in Sarajevo. Trebević specifically has marked MTB trails. Not commuter territory — weekend sport territory.

The Practical Cycling Setup

  • Bike choice for Sarajevo: A hybrid or city bike — not a road bike (too sensitive to Sarajevo's road surface quality) and not a mountain bike (unnecessary resistance on flat urban cycling). 7–21 gears, flat bars, sturdy tyres. Second-hand market on Olx.ba frequently has solid options at 200–400 BAM.
  • Essential equipment: Helmet (non-negotiable — Sarajevo drivers are not cyclist-aware), front and rear lights (required by law after dark), a quality D-lock or chain lock (bike theft occurs — a cheap cable lock is decorative), mudguards (Sarajevo rain is frequent).
  • Maintenance basics: Check tyre pressure weekly (under-inflated tyres produce rolling resistance and puncture more easily). Oil the chain monthly — a dry chain wears rapidly. Keep brake pads adjusted — Sarajevo's hills make reliable brakes a safety requirement, not a comfort.

Walking — The Underrated Option

Sarajevo's old city and central areas are most efficiently explored on foot. Several key journeys that appear to require transport are faster and more pleasant on foot:

  • Baščaršija to Marijin Dvor: 15–20 minutes on foot, often slower by tram when waiting and crowding are factored in. The walk along Ferhadija and the pedestrian zone is genuinely one of the most pleasant daily routes in the city.
  • City centre to Bistrik / Kovači: 10–15 minutes uphill — not tram accessible. Walking is the only practical option and produces both the exercise from the Workout tab and genuine neighbourhood familiarity that driving bypasses.
  • 10,000 steps daily: Already in the Workout tab's NEAT context. The commute is the most efficient way to accumulate steps without additional time allocation — 15 minutes of walking each way produces approximately 3,000–4,000 steps daily without any dedicated walking time.
The man who knows his city on foot knows it differently than the man who passes through it by car. Sarajevo rewards the walker — its neighbourhoods have a texture, a smell, a quality of light at different hours that the tram window reduces to scenery and the car window eliminates entirely.— Von Lycaon
Travel done poorly is expensive discomfort. Travel done well is one of the most concentrated sources of perspective, education, and shared memory available. The difference between the two is almost entirely preparation — not elaborate planning, but knowing precisely what to do and what not to do before, during, and after a trip. This tab covers both the logistical and the experiential.— Von Lycaon

Travel Philosophy

The tourist and the traveller visit the same places and return with different experiences. The tourist moves through a checklist of sights. The traveller moves through a place — eating where locals eat, walking without destination occasionally, sitting with coffee long enough to observe how a city actually behaves. Sarajevo is itself a world-class city for exactly this kind of experience — and the habit of observing your own city with a traveller's attention is the best preparation for travelling elsewhere.

The One-Third Rule

Plan no more than one-third of any trip in advance. Have a base (accommodation), the first day's activities, and any reservations that must be made (trains, specific restaurants, popular sites). Leave the rest to daily decision based on what the place reveals. The most memorable travel moments are almost never the planned ones.

Accommodation Strategy

For a couple: a well-located apartment (Airbnb or Booking.com) over a hotel. A kitchen available changes the economics of a trip significantly — one meal per day prepared rather than purchased saves 30–50 BAM daily on a European trip. Location matters more than size: walking distance to the things you came to see beats a larger room requiring transport to reach anything.

The Single Bag Principle

Trips up to 10 days: one carry-on bag between two people is achievable and transformative. No checked luggage fees, no waiting at carousels, no delayed bag incidents. Forces curation of what is actually necessary. The packing list below applies the same principle as the EDC Bag tab: nothing present that does not earn its place.

Travel as Education

Before any destination: read one book set there, watch one documentary or film, learn five phrases in the local language. This preparation takes 2–3 hours and converts a trip from a visual experience into a contextual one. The Aya Sofia is more than a building to someone who has read about the Byzantine Empire. The context is the education.

Packing — The Complete List

Clothing (5–7 Day Trip, Two People in One Bag)

  • The capsule wardrobe principle: every item must pair with at least two others. Dark neutral colours (navy, grey, black, white) pair with everything and show dirt less than lighter alternatives. Three bottoms, five tops, one versatile outer layer, one pair of shoes that works for both walking and an evening meal. That is the complete clothing requirement for a week.
  • Merino wool base layers: A single merino wool t-shirt worn twice does not smell on the second wearing due to the fibre's natural antimicrobial properties. Three merino tops replace five cotton tops in a bag. More expensive per item — cheaper per trip. Available at dm's Sport section or ordered online.
  • One dress option each. Even if the trip is casual, one item that works for a dinner or a more formal experience prevents the limitation of not being able to enter certain restaurants or events. For you: a plain well-fitted button shirt. For your wife: one versatile dress or equivalent.

Essentials — Non-Negotiable

  • Passports + photocopies stored separately in the bag
  • European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) if applicable, or travel insurance documents
  • Power bank 20,000mAh (same as emergency kit)
  • Universal travel adapter (EU to UK, US)
  • Headphones — noise-cancelling if budget allows (Anker Soundcore Q45 — excellent at under 100 EUR)
  • Microfibre towel — compresses to nothing, dries in 30 minutes
  • Small first aid kit — plasters, ibuprofen, antihistamine, rehydration sachets
  • Bitwarden on phone — all accommodation confirmation codes, transport tickets, and reservation numbers accessible offline

Logistics — Before You Leave

  • Travel insurance: always. Euroherc and Uniqa both offer travel insurance products in Bosnia. For EU travel: the European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) provides some coverage but is not a substitute for travel insurance — it covers emergency medical treatment, not trip cancellation, lost luggage, or evacuation. The premium for a week's travel insurance is 20–40 BAM. The cost of an uninsured emergency abroad is unbounded.
  • Notify your bank before travel. Cards blocked abroad due to suspected fraud are a guaranteed inconvenience. One phone call or app notification to your bank before departure prevents it.
  • Download offline maps (Maps.me) before departure. Not for navigation only — for the moment the phone has no data and you need to find the apartment or the station. This costs 5 minutes and has saved many travellers a significant amount of stress.
  • Scan all documents to Proton Drive or Google Drive. Passport, insurance, accommodation confirmations. If the bag is lost, the phone contains everything needed to proceed.
  • Biometric data — passport requirement: Bosnia citizens travelling to the Schengen area can enter visa-free for 90 days in any 180-day period. Ensure the passport is valid for at least 6 months beyond the travel date — many destinations require this even if the stay is much shorter.

At the Destination

  • Walk the neighbourhood first. On arrival, before any planned activity, walk 30 minutes in each direction from the accommodation. Find the nearest supermarket, the coffee that looks right, the route to the main transport. This orientation walk converts a foreign place into a comprehensible one and reduces the low-level navigation anxiety that depletes energy on the first day.
  • Eat where there are no photographs of the food on the menu. The restaurant with photographs on the menu is designed for tourists who need visual confirmation of what they are ordering. The restaurant without them assumes a local clientele who already know. Find the second type.
  • Learn one thing per day. A museum, a historical site, a conversation with a local, reading about the city's history in the evening. Travel without learning is tourism. Travel with learning is education with better lighting.
  • Budget daily, not by trip total. Knowing the daily budget and tracking against it (a note in the journal each evening) prevents the end-of-trip shock of total expenditure that exceeded expectations without any single day feeling excessive.

Destinations Worth Considering From Sarajevo

🇭🇷 Dubrovnik — 4h drive

The most visually striking coastal city in the Adriatic. June or September — avoid July/August when the cruise ship crowds reduce the experience significantly. The city walls walk, Lokrum island, and the restaurants in the old town (avoid the main street — one street back produces better food at half the price). Accessible by bus from Sarajevo's Istočno Sarajevo terminal.

🇸🇮 Ljubljana & Lake Bled — 6h drive

Slovenia's capital is one of the most liveable and beautiful small European capitals. Combine with Lake Bled (40 minutes from Ljubljana) — the island church and surrounding Alps are genuinely as extraordinary as the photographs suggest. Best April–June or September–October. The train from Ljubljana to other European cities is excellent if extending the trip.

🇮🇹 Split & Hvar — 5h drive

Split's Diocletian's Palace — the old town is built inside a Roman emperor's retirement residence. Still inhabited. Extraordinary. Hvar island accessible by ferry from Split — 1 hour, reserve in advance in summer. The Dalmatian coast from Sarajevo is one of the most underused short-trip combinations available to BiH residents.

🇦🇹 Vienna — 7h bus / train

FlixBus operates direct Sarajevo–Vienna routes. Vienna's coffee house culture, the Kunsthistorisches Museum, Belvedere Palace, and the Naschmarkt. Best October–April when summer tourism has reduced. A weekend in Vienna from Sarajevo costs less than most assume when the FlixBus fare is used rather than flights.

🇬🇷 Thessaloniki — 6h drive

Greece's second city — more authentic than Athens, less touristed, exceptional food, Byzantine history at every corner. Drive through North Macedonia. The waterfront, the Ano Poli (upper city), and the Ladadika district for evening meals. Greek coffee culture is the closest analogue to Bosnian coffee culture outside the country.

The man who has seen other places returns to his own place knowing something he did not before. Not that elsewhere is better — but that here is specific, and specificity is where beauty lives. Sarajevo is extraordinary to anyone who has left it and returned with the attention that travel teaches. That attention should not require a departure to access.— Von Lycaon
A man who understands only his immediate surroundings is navigating by landmarks alone. The broader map — how nations interact, why conflicts arise, how economic and political forces shape the world that shapes his daily life — is not abstract knowledge. It is the context without which local events are incomprehensible. This tab is not exhaustive world history. It is the framework for understanding the present.— Von Lycaon

The Mental Model — How the World Actually Works

Nation-States and Interests

Every nation-state acts primarily in its perceived national interest — economic, security, and prestige. Moral rhetoric in international relations is almost always instrumental — it serves the interest rather than constituting it. Understanding this does not produce cynicism; it produces accurate analysis. When a powerful nation advocates for human rights in one country and ignores them in another, the relevant variable is almost always the national interest differential between the two cases.

Geography as Destiny

Robert Kaplan and Tim Marshall's framework: geography is the most stable determinant of geopolitical behaviour. Nations with access to warm-water ports, navigable rivers, and flat agricultural land have historically been more economically powerful than those without. Russia's entire foreign policy for three centuries is explicable as the pursuit of warm-water port access. China's Belt and Road Initiative is explicable as the construction of land and sea trade routes that geography previously denied it.

The Post-Cold War Order

The period 1991–2022 was characterised by US unipolarity — a single superpower with unchallenged global military reach and the ideological dominance of liberal democracy and market economics. This period is ending. The multipolar world emerging from the Russia-Ukraine war, Chinese economic rise, and fracturing of Western consensus represents a genuine structural shift in international relations — not merely a temporary disruption. Understanding this transition contextualises almost every major news event of the present decade.

Bosnia in the Geopolitical Context

Bosnia sits at a fault line of three major geopolitical influences: EU/NATO integration (the Western orientation of FBiH), Serbian/Russian influence (oriented through Republika Srpska), and Turkish neo-Ottoman soft power (historical and cultural ties to the Muslim Bosniak population). The Dayton constitutional structure was designed to end the war, not to produce a functional state — and these competing influences exploit its dysfunctions continuously. Understanding this context makes Bosnian domestic politics, which appear uniquely bizarre, immediately comprehensible.

The Key Frameworks Worth Knowing

Power Transition Theory

  • When a rising power approaches the capabilities of a dominant power, the probability of major conflict increases — not because either side necessarily wants war, but because the dominant power fears decline and the rising power fears being contained before it can consolidate its position. Thucydides identified this dynamic in ancient Greece; Graham Allison documented it across 16 historical cases in "Destined for War." The US-China relationship is the contemporary application. Of the 16 historical cases Allison examined, 12 ended in major war.

The Resource Curse

  • Nations with significant natural resource wealth — oil, gas, minerals — tend to have weaker democratic institutions, more authoritarian governance, and more internal conflict than resource-poor nations. The explanation: resource wealth allows governments to fund patronage networks and security services without taxing the population, eliminating the accountability mechanism that taxation-dependent governments require. Saudi Arabia, Russia, Venezuela, Nigeria — the pattern is consistent across regions and cultures.

The Democratic Peace Theory

  • Liberal democracies almost never go to war with each other. The empirical record since 1815 is striking — no two stable democracies have fought a conventional war. The explanation is contested (shared values, economic interdependence, institutional accountability) but the pattern is robust. This is why NATO expansion is existentially threatening to Russia — not because of military capability but because democracies at Russia's borders constitute an ideological challenge to the authoritarian model.

The Major Contemporary Tensions

🇷🇺🇺🇦 Russia-Ukraine

The 2022 full-scale invasion represents the largest conventional military operation in Europe since 1945. Its roots: NATO's post-Cold War expansion eastward, the 2014 Maidan revolution, and Russia's strategic interest in preventing a democratic, Western-oriented Ukraine on its border. The war's outcome will determine the European security architecture for a generation. For Bosnia specifically — a European country that experienced its own 1990s war of territorial aggression — the conflict has direct psychological and political resonance.

🇨🇳🇺🇸 US-China

The central geopolitical competition of the 21st century. China's economic rise has produced military modernisation, territorial assertiveness in the South China Sea and Taiwan Strait, and a direct challenge to the US-led international order. The competition spans technology (semiconductors, AI), finance (dollar hegemony vs yuan internationalisation), and military positioning (Pacific bases, carrier groups). The outcome will determine the international system your children inhabit.

🇪🇺 European Integration

The EU's relevance to Bosnia specifically: EU membership remains the stated strategic goal of BiH, but the country's constitutional dysfunction and corruption levels have prevented progress. Croatia and Slovenia (BiH's neighbours) are full EU members. Serbia is a candidate. The economic and political gap between BiH and its neighbours widens each year that accession stalls. Understanding EU integration dynamics explains much of BiH's domestic political paralysis.

Essential Reading for Context

  • "Prisoners of Geography" — Tim Marshall: The most accessible geopolitics book available. Ten maps that explain the constraints shaping every major power's behaviour. Read before forming opinions about international news.
  • "The End of History and the Last Man" — Francis Fukuyama: The 1992 argument that liberal democracy had permanently prevailed. Read alongside its refutation by subsequent events — the tension between the thesis and reality is the intellectual framework for understanding the past 30 years.
  • "Why Nations Fail" — Acemoglu & Robinson: Why some countries are rich and others poor — the answer is institutional rather than cultural or geographical. Directly applicable to understanding BiH's economic development trajectory.
The man who understands only his own country understands it poorly. The context that explains domestic events — why prices rise, why the currency is pegged, why politicians make certain choices — is almost always international. Read broadly. Think systemically. The world is not random.— Von Lycaon
The hills surrounding Sarajevo contain one of the most diverse foraging environments in Europe. The Dinaric Alps, the Sarajevo valley, and the mixed forests of Trebević, Bjelašnica, and Jahorina yield mushrooms, wild herbs, berries, and greens that form the basis of the Bosnian country kitchen. This is not survival knowledge — it is cultural knowledge that connects a Sarajevo resident to the land that immediately surrounds the city. Begin with the safe and certain. Never with the uncertain.— Von Lycaon
The Primary Rule of Foraging: Never consume any plant or mushroom unless identification is absolutely certain. Uncertainty is not a reason to try carefully. It is a reason not to consume. The consequences of misidentification with certain species — death cap mushroom, hemlock — are fatal and not reversible by prompt medical attention. This tab covers only species with no dangerous lookalikes or with clearly distinguishing features. Every other species requires expert field verification before consumption.

The Sarajevo Foraging Calendar

Spring (March–May)

Wild garlic (medvjeđi luk), nettles (kopriva), dandelion greens (maslačak), sorrel (kiseljak). The most productive season for wild greens. The hillsides above Bistrik and the forests approaching Trebević are accessible on foot from the city and productive from late March. Wild garlic is the most prized — its season is brief (3–4 weeks), its smell is unmistakeable, and it has no dangerous lookalike when identified by scent.

Summer (June–August)

Elderflower (zova — flower then berry), wild strawberries (šumska jagoda), blackberries (kupina), linden flowers (lipa), chamomile (kamilica). The elderflower window is narrow — 2–3 weeks in June — and the flower clusters must be harvested before they begin to decay. Already in the Beverages tab as a cordial and mocktail ingredient: the fresh source is the hillsides above the city.

Autumn (September–November)

The mushroom season. Ceps/porcini (vrganj — Boletus edulis), chanterelles (lisičarke), slippery jacks (maslenjak). Also: rosehips (šipak), elderberries (zova), walnuts (orah), chestnuts (kesten) in the forests around Sarajevo. Autumn is the season that draws the most foragers into the Sarajevo hills — it is a significant cultural event, not merely a food source.

Winter (December–February)

The least productive season. Dried and preserved autumn harvest is the primary use of winter foraging knowledge. Some evergreen herbs (rosemary if cultivated, dried thyme) remain accessible. The winter focus is preservation — drying mushrooms and rosehips, making elderberry syrup, jarring ajvar from the autumn pepper harvest.

The Sarajevo Species — Safe to Learn

Wild Garlic (Medvjeđi Luk — Allium ursinum)

  • When: March to May. Peak: early April.
  • Where: Shaded, moist forest floors. The forests above Bistrik, in the Miljacka valley above the city, and the lower slopes of Trebević. Carpets entire forest floors where conditions suit it.
  • Identification: Broad, smooth, bright green leaves emerging directly from the ground (not from a stem). The definitive test: crush a leaf — it smells powerfully of garlic. No other woodland plant smells of garlic. This smell test makes misidentification essentially impossible.
  • The dangerous lookalike to know: Lily of the valley (đurđevak) and autumn crocus (mrazovac) grow in similar habitats and have similar-looking leaves. They do not smell of garlic. The smell test eliminates the risk entirely — if it does not smell of garlic, it is not wild garlic.
  • Use: Pesto, added to scrambled eggs and pasta, stirred into butter, in soups. The leaves are the usable part — pick from plants not yet in flower for the strongest flavour. Do not uproot — cut the leaves and leave the bulb to regenerate.

Nettles (Kopriva — Urtica dioica)
