Clean, light, citrus or marine notes. Appropriate for warmer months and professional settings. Acqua di Giò by Giorgio Armani (Plaza Parfumerija) is the classic of this category.

Oriental / Spiced

Amber, vanilla, spice. Heavier, warmer, more intimate. For evening wear and cooler seasons. Not appropriate for hot summer workdays — the projection is too strong in heat.

Notes Structure — What You're Smelling Changes

  • Top notes: What you smell first, immediately upon application. These are the volatile molecules — they evaporate within 15–30 minutes. Do not judge a fragrance by its first impression on the wrist in a shop.
  • Heart notes: The core of the fragrance, emerging after 20–30 minutes. This is what the fragrance actually smells like on you.
  • Base notes: The foundation — cedarwood, musk, amber. These emerge after an hour and are what lingers on your skin and clothes hours later. The most intimate stage of a fragrance.
  • Testing correctly: Spray on the wrist. Wait thirty minutes. Then smell it. This is the fragrance. The immediate spray is just the opening. Most people buy the opening and are disappointed by the fragrance.

Application

  • One to two sprays maximum. On a pulse point: the inner wrist, the base of the neck, or the inner elbow. Pulse points emit heat which diffuses the fragrance gradually.
  • Do not rub the wrists together. This breaks the molecular structure of the top notes and creates an off-note in the fragrance. Spray and let it settle.
  • Fragrance on clothing lasts longer than on skin. A light spray on the collar or chest of a shirt is appropriate for longer wear. Avoid direct spray on delicate fabrics — it can stain.
  • Seasonal rotation: Lighter aquatic and citrus fragrances in spring and summer. Warmer woody and oriental fragrances in autumn and winter. The same fragrance in summer heat has entirely different projection and character than in December.
Available in Sarajevo: Boss Bottled, Hugo Boss — Boss Bottled Night, Dior Sauvage, Acqua di Giò, and Bleu de Chanel are all stocked at Plaza Parfumerija (BBI Centar and Aria Mall). Budget alternatives: Davidoff Cool Water and Adidas Team Force at dm, Müller, or larger Konzum locations.

The Weekly Audit — Sunday Evening

  • Nails: Trimmed and filed — fingernails and toenails both. Use a nail file to smooth edges. Clipped nails that are not filed can snag. This detail is often noticed at close range in handshakes.
  • Eyebrows: Not shaped — tidied. Remove the stray hairs between the brows and above the arch using tweezers. Tweezerman or equivalent available at dm. The difference between groomed and untouched brows is meaningful.
  • Ear and nose hair: A compact trimmer (Philips NT series, available at SCC electronics) handles this in under two minutes. Past a certain age this becomes increasingly necessary and is almost universally neglected. Do not be the person whose sophistication is undercut by this one oversight.
  • Feet: Pumice stone or foot file used weekly in the shower. Cracked heels are entirely preventable with sixty seconds of attention per week. Applying foot cream (Florena or Nivea, both at dm) twice weekly maintains softness.
  • Posture check: Stand against a wall. Heels, calves, lower back, shoulder blades, and the back of the head should all touch the wall simultaneously. If they do not, you have identified exactly what to work on. Repeat this check weekly — the deviation from ideal posture is gradual and invisible until suddenly it is not.

Body Hair — The Honest Guide

Body hair is among the most personal and least honestly discussed aspects of male grooming. Most advice defaults to either "remove everything" (a preference, not a standard) or complete silence. I will give you the actual framework: what matters, what is a matter of preference, what serves a function, and what the practical options are. There is no universal correct answer here — but there is a correct way to think about it.

The principle is the same as the rest of this codex: not the absence of nature, but the management of it. Untouched chaos is not confidence. Managed naturalness is. The goal is never to appear processed — it is to appear considered.— Von Lycaon

The Framework — Four Zones, Four Approaches

🚫 Always Address

These areas are non-negotiable regardless of personal preference on body hair. Back of the neck below the hairline. Ear and nose hair. Monobrow / between the brows. Excessive shoulder/upper back hair visible above a collar. These are not style decisions — they are maintenance standards. Neglecting them undercuts every other effort made to present well.

⚖️ Personal Standard

These zones are genuinely a matter of personal and partner preference with no objective correct answer: chest hair, arm hair, leg hair, stomach hair. Both maintained and natural are fully acceptable choices. What is not acceptable is a choice made by neglect rather than intention. Decide what standard you hold yourself to — then hold it consistently.

🌿 Natural Function

Body hair serves genuine biological purposes: temperature regulation, friction reduction, pheromone dispersal, tactile sensitivity. This is not a reason to avoid managing it — it is context for making an informed choice rather than one driven purely by aesthetic trends that reverse every decade.

🔄 Maintenance Consistency

Whatever your standard, consistency is what makes it grooming rather than neglect. A man who shaves his chest must commit to the stubble management that follows. A man who trims maintains a consistent length. A man who leaves natural ensures the boundaries (neck, shoulders, brows) are still managed. The inconsistency is the problem, not the choice.

Arms & Legs — Natural vs Trimmed vs Removed

The Case for Natural

Arm and leg hair on men is entirely socially normative and requires no justification. It does not affect how clothing fits, how clean you appear, or how well-maintained you look when the rest of the grooming standards in this tab are met. There is no professional or social context in which natural arm hair on a man reads as unkempt. It is simply hair.

The choice to leave arm and leg hair natural is not default or lazy — it is a legitimate preference that the majority of men make and that requires no maintenance beyond the surrounding standards already covered.

The Case for Managing

Some men find that trimmed body hair gives a cleaner silhouette under fitted clothing, reduces heat in summer, and is personally preferred by their partner. This is equally valid. Trimming (not removing) arm and leg hair to a uniform length — typically 8–12mm — gives a managed appearance without the high-maintenance commitment of full removal.

Full waxing or shaving of arms and legs is a stronger choice that requires genuine commitment: stubble regrowth on the arms and legs within 2–3 days is more visible and tactilely uncomfortable than the natural state. If you choose removal, you are choosing a permanent maintenance schedule.

Natural — What to Actually Maintain
  • The borders matter most. If wearing short sleeves, the transition from arm hair to shoulder should be clean — no isolated long stragglers on the upper arm or shoulder. One pass with scissors or a trimmer along the upper arm border takes 30 seconds.
  • Wrist area: Some men have markedly denser hair near the wrist that bunches under a watch strap. A light trim in this area prevents the uncomfortable trapped-hair feeling and keeps the watch sitting cleanly on the wrist.
  • Leg hair around the ankle: If wearing trousers that sit above the sock line, the ankle area is visible when seated. The same border principle applies — no isolated long hairs extending beyond the natural line onto the foot.

Total time investment: 2 minutes monthly. This is the minimum required to maintain the natural choice with actual intentionality rather than passivity.

The Fragrance Layering System

The Perfume tab covers fragrance selection and the four-bottle wardrobe. This section covers application technique at a deeper level — the difference between wearing a fragrance and wearing it well.

  • Skin preparation: Fragrance adheres to moisturised skin significantly longer than to dry skin. Applying an unscented moisturiser immediately after showering, before the fragrance, extends the wear time by 2–3 hours and reduces the amount of fragrance required to achieve presence. This is the most underutilised technique in fragrance application — simpler than layering and more effective than over-application.
  • The projection arc: Every fragrance moves through a projection arc — strongest in the first 30–60 minutes (the top notes and the initial heat activation), then settling into the heart notes for the middle hours, then the base notes closest to the skin in the final hours. Understanding this arc prevents the common mistake of applying more fragrance at hour 2 because it seems to have faded — at hour 2 you are in the heart phase, which is quieter by design. The fragrance is still there; your nose has habituated to it.
  • Seasonal calibration: The same fragrance applies differently in Sarajevo's four distinct seasons. In winter cold, projection drops — apply to warmer skin areas (neck, inner wrist) and consider applying to the chest under clothing where body heat will radiate the fragrance upward throughout the day. In summer heat, projection amplifies — use less than in winter, and consider lighter concentration (EdT rather than EdP) to avoid the fragrance becoming oppressive in heat.
My palette is deliberate: ivory, charcoal, deep black, and a single point of crimson. I do not recommend you replicate it literally. I recommend you find your own deliberate palette — and then build everything from it. A man who chooses his clothes projects an entirely different quality than one who merely wears them.— Von Lycaon

The Fundamentals — Fit Above All

Before discussing what to wear, we must establish why fit matters more than any other variable. A €30 shirt that fits perfectly is categorically better than a €200 shirt that does not. The difference between "I have nice clothes" and "he is well-dressed" is almost entirely fit. Understanding fit is therefore the highest-leverage knowledge in this tab.

Shirt Fit

The shoulder seam should sit exactly at the edge of your shoulder — not hanging over it, not pulling across it. The chest should have room to move without excess fabric. The torso should taper slightly at the waist. Sleeve length: shirt cuffs should show approximately 1cm past a jacket sleeve when worn with one.

Trouser Fit

The waist should sit where you intend to wear them without a belt needed to hold them. The seat should have no excess fabric bunching. The thigh should be comfortable for movement without excess. The break — where the trouser meets the shoe — should be minimal to none for modern fit: no pooling fabric.

Jacket Fit

The collar should lie flat against the shirt collar with no gap. The chest button should close without straining. The sleeve should show shirt cuff. The back should be smooth with no pulling across the shoulders. A jacket that does not fit through the shoulders cannot be tailored into one that does — this is the one dimension where buying correctly matters most.

The Foundation Wardrobe

Everyday Office
  • Well-fitted dark chinos: charcoal, navy, or black. Slim fit, no break or minimal break
  • Oxford shirts in white, light blue, and pale grey — pressed or at minimum wrinkle-free
  • A fine-knit merino sweater in charcoal or navy for layering
  • Dark leather Oxford shoes or Derby shoes — always polished
  • A minimal steel or leather watch — not a smartwatch for formal settings
  • A belt in black or dark brown leather that matches your shoes precisely

Reserve: Importanne H&M and C&A for affordable basics. SCC Reserved for mid-range trousers and shirts. A visit to a tailor on Baščaršija to have trousers hemmed is essential.

The Capsule Wardrobe — Built for a Sarajevo Life

A capsule wardrobe is not a minimalist aesthetic choice — it is a decision-making system. The person whose wardrobe contains 40 items that all work together makes better daily clothing decisions faster than the person whose wardrobe contains 100 items with no coherent logic. The following is a wardrobe architecture calibrated for a Sarajevo professional who needs to move between office, kafana, weekend outdoors, and occasional formal occasions.

The Foundation Layer — Buy These First

Three white dress shirts (cotton, slim fit — not skinny). Two Oxford button-down shirts in pale blue and pale grey. Two quality plain crew-neck t-shirts (white and navy — Uniqlo Supima cotton or equivalent). Two pairs of dark navy chinos (no creases, clean hem). One pair of dark charcoal wool trousers. One pair of well-fitted dark jeans (no distressing, dark indigo). These twelve items produce thirty distinct combinations before adding anything else.

The Outer Layer

One navy wool blazer — the single most versatile garment a man can own. Worn over a white shirt with dark chinos: business casual. Over a t-shirt with dark jeans: smart casual. Over a roll-neck: autumn evening. One quality overcoat in camel, charcoal, or dark navy (wool blend — for Sarajevo's winters this is not optional). One clean waterproof jacket for the wet months and mountain use. One light cotton or linen jacket for summer.

Footwear — The Correct Hierarchy

One pair of dark brown leather Derby shoes (more versatile than Oxford, wears better across formality levels). One pair of clean white leather or suede low-top sneakers (common but essential — the most worn shoe for most occasions). One pair of dark leather Chelsea boots (the most elegant casual shoe available). One pair of quality hiking/trail shoes for weekends and transport use. Four pairs of footwear cover every Sarajevo context without overlap or gap.

The Colour Architecture

A coherent wardrobe operates within a colour palette that allows every item to work with every other. The Lycaon palette: Navy and dark indigo as the primary. White, pale grey, pale blue as the secondary (shirts and light layers). Camel, tan, and brown as warm accents (outerwear, footwear, accessories). Black used sparingly — shoes occasionally, nothing else. Brown leather goods throughout. This palette never clashes. Every combination is intentional.

Fit — The Variable That Overrides Everything Else

The single most important factor in how clothing looks and communicates is fit. A well-fitted garment from an inexpensive brand communicates more quality than a poorly fitted garment from a luxury one. The three most common fit failures:

  • Shoulder seams: The shoulder seam of any shirt, jacket, or coat should sit exactly at the edge of the shoulder — not hanging onto the upper arm, not pulling toward the neck. This seam cannot be adjusted by a tailor without significant cost and difficulty. It must be correct at purchase. Everything else can be altered.
  • Trouser break: The hem of the trouser should touch the top of the shoe with a minimal break (a slight horizontal fold) or no break at all. Trousers pooling at the ankle communicate disorder. Trousers ending at the ankle bone are modern and clean. The tailor shortens them for 10 BAM.
  • Shirt chest: When buttoned, a dress shirt should have no pulling across the chest. One closed fist inserted at the button placket is approximately the correct amount of ease. Tighter reads as too small; significantly looser reads as untidy.

Shoe Care — The Investment Protection

  • Cedar shoe trees immediately after every wear. Cedar absorbs the moisture from a day's wear, maintains the shoe's shape, and prevents the cracking and deformation that moisture causes. A quality leather shoe with cedar trees lasts 10–15 years. The same shoe without them lasts 2–3.
  • Polish schedule: Clean with a damp cloth after each wear to remove surface dirt. Apply cream polish (matching the shoe colour) once weekly for regular-wear shoes. Hard wax polish for shine on formal occasions — applied in thin layers, buffed with a horsehair brush. The wax polish fills the grain of the leather and produces the mirror-like finish; the cream polish nourishes the leather between wax applications.
  • Rotation: Never wear the same leather shoe on consecutive days. The leather requires 24 hours to fully dry between wears. A two-pair rotation produces shoes that last three times longer than a single pair worn daily.
  • Sarajevo sourcing: A cobbler (postolar) near the Markale market or in the old city can resole quality shoes for 30–50 BAM, extending their life by years. Resoling a 200 BAM pair of quality shoes twice costs 100 BAM — the shoe then has 400 BAM of value delivered. Resoling a 40 BAM pair costs more than it is worth.

Grooming and Dress — The Combined Signal

Clothing and grooming are a single communication, not two separate ones. The best wardrobe paired with neglected grooming reads as inconsistent. The most modest wardrobe paired with impeccable grooming reads as composed. The man who attends to both produces a first impression that very few people in any room can match — not through expense but through intentionality. Every item covered in this tab costs less than one evening at a Sarajevo restaurant. The return, measured in how you are perceived and how you feel, operates daily for years.

Clothing is not vanity. It is the management of the first information other people receive about you before you speak a word. The man who manages it deliberately is not vain — he is precise.-- Von Lycaon
Approximately 55% of social communication is conveyed through body language and facial expression. The verbal content of what is said carries less weight than how it is said and how the speaker is positioned when saying it. Understanding and deliberately managing your own non-verbal communication — and accurately reading others' — produces a quality of social effectiveness that verbal skill alone cannot achieve.— Von Lycaon

Your Own Body Language — The Baseline

Posture as Signal

Upright posture with shoulders back signals confidence and competence before a word is spoken. Slouched posture signals disengagement or low status. This is not merely social convention — posture directly influences cortisol and testosterone levels (Amy Cuddy's research), and the effect runs bidirectionally: adopting confident posture produces the hormonal profile of confidence. The Mobility tab's postural correction work is relevant here beyond its physical health benefits.

Open vs Closed

Open body language (uncrossed arms, torso facing the person, feet pointing toward them) signals interest and accessibility. Closed body language (crossed arms, body angled away, feet pointing toward the exit) signals discomfort, disagreement, or disinterest. Most people adopt closed positions unconsciously in uncomfortable situations — making the discomfort visible to everyone except themselves. Notice your default position in uncomfortable situations.

Stillness

Excessive movement — fidgeting, shifting weight, touching the face, adjusting clothing — signals anxiety. Deliberate stillness signals composure. The Mizutsune principle: a still physical presence communicates authority before a word is spoken. This is not rigidity — it is the absence of the unconscious movements that leak anxiety.

Mirroring

Subtly matching the body language, speech rate, and posture of the person you are speaking with builds rapport unconsciously. When done naturally (not mechanically), mirroring signals alignment and increases the felt experience of being understood. Notice it occurring in conversations where you feel genuine connection — it is almost always present.

Reading Others

The Baseline First

  • Before interpreting anyone's body language as meaningful, establish their baseline. Some people cross their arms by default regardless of emotional state — it is a comfort habit, not a defensive signal. Some people avoid eye contact by nature, not from deception. Body language signals are meaningful relative to the individual's baseline, not relative to a universal dictionary of gestures.
  • Clusters, not single gestures. A single gesture is rarely significant. A cluster of gestures pointing in the same direction — crossed arms, averted gaze, body angled away, reduced speech rate — is a reliable signal. Read clusters, not individual elements.

High-Value Signals to Observe

  • Feet: The most honest part of the body — least consciously managed. Feet pointing toward a person indicate genuine interest. Feet pointing toward the door during a conversation indicate the person wants to leave, regardless of what their face or words communicate.
  • Microexpressions: Involuntary facial expressions lasting less than 0.2 seconds that reveal genuine emotional state before the conscious expression is assembled. Disgust, contempt, fear, surprise — these flash across the face before suppression. Training to notice them requires attention but not special ability. Paul Ekman's work in this field is the primary reference.
  • The handshake: Palm down = dominant. Palm up = submissive. Vertical = equal. Excessive grip pressure = insecurity or dominance attempt. Limp grip = disengagement. The neutral equal handshake is vertical grip at moderate pressure, one or two pumps. No more is required.

Professional Application

  • Presentations: Face the audience, not the screen. Plant feet shoulder-width, weight distributed equally — this eliminates weight-shifting which signals anxiety. Gesture with open palms rather than pointed fingers — open palms signal openness; pointing triggers a mild threat response in the audience.
  • Negotiations: Maintain eye contact at moderate level — too little signals avoidance, too much signals aggression. Lean slightly forward when making important points — it signals engagement and conviction. Lean back slightly when listening — it signals that you are processing rather than simply waiting to speak.
  • One-on-one conversations: Position at a 90-degree angle rather than directly face-to-face for difficult conversations. Directly facing someone across a table activates a mild adversarial frame. Sitting side by side or at an angle produces a collaborative frame. The physical arrangement influences the emotional tone of the conversation before it begins.
The body is always communicating. The question is only whether the communication is deliberate or accidental. A man who manages his physical presence with the same care he manages his words has access to a channel of communication that most people leave entirely to chance.— Von Lycaon

Reading the Room — Group Dynamics

Body language becomes most useful not when reading individuals in isolation but when reading group dynamics — who holds status in a room, who defers to whom, who is included in a conversation and who is subtly excluded. These dynamics are expressed through body language continuously and involuntarily, and reading them correctly allows you to navigate any social environment more intelligently.

  • Torso orientation: People orient their torsos toward the people they consider most important in a group. In a standing conversation, the person whose torso is most turned toward is the person with highest social status in that group at that moment. This changes as the status dynamic shifts. Monitor it to understand who the group actually defers to, rather than who holds the official title.
  • The inclusion signal: When a two-person conversation opens to include a third, both original parties turn to create a gap in the circle for the newcomer. When only one person turns (leaving one side closed), the message is "you may join but you are not being invited." When neither turns, the conversation is explicitly closed. Reading this signal prevents the social cost of joining a conversation that does not want to be joined.
  • Status and space: High-status individuals occupy more physical space -- spread arms, wide stances, objects placed in front of them, lean-back posture. Low-status individuals compress -- arms close to the body, feet together, objects held close. This is universal across cultures. The person who wants to project authority should practise controlled expansion: a wider stance, arms resting away from the body, objects placed deliberately on surfaces rather than held.

Voice as Body Language

The voice is as much a component of non-verbal communication as posture or gesture. Vocal qualities that communicate authority and trustworthiness:

  • Pace: Slower speech is perceived as more authoritative. The instinct under pressure is to speak faster (to fill silence, to get through the difficult part). The correct response is the opposite -- deliberate slowing. Pausing before answering a difficult question communicates that you are considering it rather than reacting to it. The pause is a statement of confidence.
  • Pitch: Upward inflection at the end of statements (rising intonation) converts them to questions and communicates uncertainty. Flat or slightly falling intonation at the end of statements is the prosodic marker of declarative speech -- the voice of someone who means what they say. The rising intonation habit is common and easily corrected once identified.
  • Volume and breath: Quiet speaking that requires others to lean in produces more attention than loud speaking. The voice that is well-supported by the breath (originating from the diaphragm rather than the throat) carries further with less effort and does not thin under stress. The Public Speaking tab covers this in detail.

The Professional Application

  • The interview and negotiation posture: Seated at a table, the correct posture for a high-stakes professional interaction: back straight, feet flat on the floor, hands visible on the table (concealed hands are unconsciously perceived as untrustworthy). Lean forward slightly when listening -- it signals engagement. Lean back slightly when making a statement -- it signals confidence in the position.
  • The presentation stance: Feet shoulder-width apart, weight evenly distributed, arms available for gesture. Do not grip the podium, cross your arms, or put hands in pockets during a presentation -- all signal defensiveness or discomfort. The stance that communicates authority is open, grounded, and still between gestures.
  • Calibrating to the Bosnian context: Physical proximity in Bosnian professional culture is closer than in Northern European contexts -- standing at Western "personal space" distance (arm's length) in a Bosnian professional interaction can read as cold or formal. Matching the proximity of the other person within reason is correct social calibration rather than a rule violation.
The body speaks before the mouth opens and continues speaking after it closes. The man who governs his body language governs the first and last impression he makes in every interaction. This is not performance -- it is the alignment of how he holds himself with who he intends to be.-- Von Lycaon
Handwriting is not obsolete — it is rare, which means it carries weight that typed communication has lost through ubiquity. A handwritten note in 2025 is noticed. A handwritten letter is kept. The investment required is minimal: a reliable pen, decent paper, and the habit of using both for the occasions that warrant them.— Von Lycaon

Why Handwriting Still Matters

The Cognitive Benefit

Research consistently shows that handwritten note-taking produces better retention than typed notes — the relative slowness of handwriting forces the writer to synthesise and paraphrase rather than transcribe verbatim. The motor involvement of writing appears to strengthen the encoding of information into long-term memory in ways that typing does not. For the Learning tab's deep work sessions, handwritten notes alongside digital ones produce better retention than digital alone.

The Social Impact

A handwritten thank-you note after a dinner. A handwritten card with a gift. A handwritten letter to someone who matters. In a world of instant digital communication, these acts require deliberate effort — and the recipient knows it. The effort is the message. The content carries the meaning and the form carries the weight of the intention behind it.

The Journal Application

The Leuchtturm 1917 notebook in the Mind & Rest tab is the primary journaling tool. Handwriting in the journal produces a different quality of reflection than typing — the pace forces deliberation, the physical act creates a sensory record, and the result is a document that is genuinely personal in a way that digital text is not. The journal entries written ten years from now will be read differently because they are handwritten.

Improving Handwriting — The Method

The Practical Approach

  • Grip first. The tripod grip (pen held between thumb, index finger, and middle finger, resting on the ring finger) is the most controlled and sustainable grip for extended writing. If your current grip produces hand fatigue or cramp after 10 minutes, the grip is incorrect. The tripod grip allows the fingers rather than the wrist to do the fine movement work, which is both more precise and less fatiguing.
  • Slow down. Most poor handwriting is produced at speed. Deliberately slow, deliberate letterforms produce better results than fast, practiced ones in poor habits. Slow practice installs good habits; fast practice reinforces existing ones — good or bad.
  • Consistency over beauty. Consistent letterforms that are slightly plain are more readable and more professional than beautiful forms that vary in size and angle. The goal is a consistent hand that reads clearly, not calligraphic performance.
  • Practice 5 minutes daily — copy a paragraph from a book, write tomorrow's journal entry in draft form on practice paper, or practice the letters you know are inconsistent. Five minutes daily over a month produces visible improvement that casual weekend practice over a year does not.

The Right Tools

🖊️ Fountain Pens

The instrument that makes handwriting genuinely pleasurable. The nib glides rather than drags — the resistance that makes ballpoint writing tiring is absent. Entry level: Lamy Safari (30–40 EUR, available online) or Pilot Metropolitan (similar price). Both are reliable, comfortable, and produce a quality of line unavailable from a ballpoint. Ink cartridges or converter (allows bottled ink) available with both. The investment is 35 EUR and the daily experience is categorically different from a disposable pen.

🖋️ The Daily Pen

For daily use where a fountain pen is impractical (forms, quick notes, outdoor use): the Uni-ball Jetstream ballpoint is the gold standard of ballpoint writing — smooth, consistent, fast-drying, available in black and blue. Available at papirnice in Sarajevo. Under 5 BAM. The difference between a quality ballpoint and a cheap one is felt immediately and costs very little.

📓 Paper

The Leuchtturm 1917 already in the Codex. For correspondence and notes: Rhodia notepads — French paper of exceptional smoothness that works with both fountain pens and quality ballpoints. The quality of paper affects the writing experience as much as the pen. Available online from stationery retailers.

The handwritten word is the most personal document a person produces. Long after the sender is gone, the specific pressure and angle of the letterforms remains — a physical record of a particular hand on a particular day that no digital text can replicate. Write things worth keeping, in a hand worth reading.— Von Lycaon

The Fountain Pen in Depth

Nib Sizes and Their Uses

Fine (F) nib: produces a narrow line, ideal for small handwriting and controlled writing. The correct choice if your natural writing is small or you write for extended periods. Medium (M) nib: the most versatile size. Produces a line width that reads well at normal sizes and allows some variation through pressure and angle variation. Broad (B) nib: expressive, flows beautifully for signatures and larger writing, more demanding of good paper (bleeds on cheap paper). For a first fountain pen, medium is the correct nib size.

Ink — A Genuine Pleasure

Fountain pen ink is available in hundreds of colours and formulations. The practical categories: standard dye-based inks (most affordable, smooth, wide colour range -- Diamine and Waterman both excellent). Pigment inks (more water-resistant, archival, require more frequent cleaning to prevent clogging). Iron gall inks (slightly acidic, produce extremely sharp lines, the historical ink formulation -- requires more maintenance but produces the finest line quality). For a beginner: a standard blue-black dye ink is the correct starting point. Blue-black is professional enough for any written context and more distinctive than standard blue.

Care and Maintenance

A fountain pen used daily requires minimal maintenance: fill it completely (partial fills allow air bubbles that interrupt flow), cap it when not in use (nibs dry within minutes uncapped), and flush it with cool water monthly to prevent dried ink from blocking the feed. A pen stored unused for more than two weeks should be flushed before storage and refilled before use. The investment in proper care extends the pen's functional life indefinitely -- a quality fountain pen is essentially a lifetime tool if properly maintained.

Developing a Distinctive Hand

Beyond correctness and legibility, there is the question of character -- the handwriting that is identifiably yours, that carries a quality of personality that typed text structurally cannot. Developing this is not a rapid process. It emerges from consistent practice, the gradual elimination of inconsistency, and deliberate choices about the stylistic elements you want to develop.

  • The letter to study: The lowercase g, j, and y (the descending letters) and the uppercase Q, G, and J (the most variable across historical scripts) are where personal style most visibly emerges. Look at the historical chancery and humanist scripts of the Renaissance (Arrighi, Palatino -- available as free PDFs) for letter form inspiration that is both beautiful and historically grounded.
  • The copywork method: Daily copying of a text written in a hand you admire is the fastest route to improvement. It installs the rhythms and proportions of a good hand through muscle memory. 10 lines of copying from the Letters of Marcus Aurelius (or any text you are reading) before the original writing of the day.
  • The signature as a project: The signature is the most frequently produced piece of writing and the one that most directly represents you. Most people's signatures are a degraded abbreviation that evolved without intention. Design your signature deliberately: start from your name written fully and legibly, then simplify it into a form that is both distinctive and producible consistently. Practice 50 times in one session. The new signature installs in one deliberate session that the casual daily approach never achieves.

Occasions That Warrant Handwriting

  • A thank-you note after being hosted for a meal (not a text message). The note mentions something specific about the evening.
  • A card with any significant gift -- the card is the communication; the gift is the punctuation.
  • A letter to anyone elderly or in a context where handwritten correspondence carries weight that digital does not.
  • Journal entries -- already in the Codex as a practice. The handwriting in a journal is also a record of your state of mind at the time of writing in a way that typed text cannot be.
  • The occasional handwritten note to your wife left where she will find it unexpectedly. Not as a grand gesture -- as the smallest possible expression of attention. The handwriting is the evidence that it took deliberate effort.
The handwritten word is the most personal document a person produces -- a physical record of a specific hand on a specific day that no digital text can replicate or forge. Write things worth keeping in a hand worth reading. Both conditions require cultivation.-- Von Lycaon
A watch is the only piece of jewellery that most men will wear every day of their adult life. Chosen well, maintained correctly, it is the single accessory capable of communicating refinement, history, and personal taste simultaneously. This tab covers what to know, what to consider, and how to care for the investment once made.— Von Lycaon

The Categories Worth Knowing

Mechanical vs Quartz

Mechanical watches are driven by a wound spring. Automatic mechanicals wind from wrist movement. Manual mechanicals require daily winding. Quartz watches are battery-driven and significantly more accurate. The choice is not about timekeeping accuracy — quartz wins that argument entirely. It is about craft, history, and the relationship with an object that is alive in a small way. Both are legitimate choices. Know what you are choosing and why.

The Dress Watch

Thin case (under 10mm), simple dial, leather strap, no complications beyond date at most. Appropriate under a shirt cuff, appropriate at a formal dinner, appropriate in any professional or social context. The classic references: Tissot Le Locle (accessible), Longines Master Collection (mid-range), Omega De Ville (aspirational). All available new or pre-owned.

The Sports/Field Watch

Slightly thicker case, stainless or rubber strap, water resistance of 100m+. The daily wear category for most men — can transition from the office to outdoor activity. Classic references: Seiko SKX007 (discontinued but heavily pre-owned market), Orient Mako, Tissot PRX. The PRX specifically is a remarkably versatile piece at under 500 EUR that works dressed up or down.

Pre-Owned — The Correct Strategy

A pre-owned watch of genuine quality costs 30–50% less than the same watch new, while delivering an identical wearing experience. The pre-owned market for watches is liquid, well-documented, and increasingly accessible via Chrono24, WatchBox, and Bob's Watches. Authentication concerns are real but manageable: buy from reputable dealers with return policies, verify serial numbers with the manufacturer.

Care & Maintenance

  • Service intervals: Mechanical watches require servicing every 5–7 years — the oils that lubricate the movement degrade and require replacement. Service cost: 150–400 EUR depending on complexity and manufacturer. Neglected services lead to worn components that cost significantly more to replace. Factor service cost into the total cost of ownership when purchasing.
  • Water resistance reality: "Water resistant 30m" means splash resistant — not submersion. "100m" means swimming is acceptable. "200m+" means diving is acceptable. These ratings degrade over time as gaskets age — have water resistance retested annually if the watch is regularly exposed to water.
  • Strap care: Leather straps absorb sweat and degrade — not suitable for daily wear in warm weather or during activity. Rotate to a metal bracelet or rubber strap for summer and sport use. The watch itself lasts decades; the strap is a consumable. A quality leather strap costs 30–80 EUR and should last 1–2 years with proper rotation.
  • Storage: Away from strong magnetic fields (speakers, phone cases with magnets) — magnetism disrupts the regulation of mechanical movements and produces significant timekeeping errors. A watch box or roll with individual compartments prevents scratching and provides magnetic shielding.

First Serious Watch — The Recommendation

For a first watch that is worth keeping for decades: the Seiko 5 Sports series (200–250 EUR new) or the Orient Mako (150–200 EUR). Both are automatic mechanicals with established reliability records, available service infrastructure, and an aesthetic that works across casual and semi-formal contexts. Neither will embarrass you in any company. Both will last, correctly maintained, the rest of your life.

The aspirational path from here: as the budget allows over years — Tissot PRX (650 EUR), then Longines (1,200–2,000 EUR), then the Omega Seamaster or Speedy (4,000–6,000 EUR pre-owned). This is a years-long journey, not a single purchase. The watches at each level are genuinely different objects, not merely more expensive versions of the same thing.

A watch purchased for a lifetime changes the relationship with time itself — the mechanical movement visible through a caseback, counting the seconds with no battery, no signal, only physics, is among the most quietly extraordinary objects a person can carry. Own one that earns that attention.— Von Lycaon

The Mechanics — Understanding What You Own

A mechanical watch is one of the most extraordinary objects a person can carry. A hundred or more individual components, many smaller than a grain of rice, cooperate to divide time into equal segments through purely mechanical means — no battery, no circuit, no digital processing. Understanding the mechanism increases appreciation and enables better care.

The Escapement

The heart of any mechanical watch. The escapement regulates the release of the mainspring's energy by catching and releasing the gear train at precisely timed intervals. The tick you hear is the escapement wheel catching against the pallet fork — typically 6, 8, or 10 times per second (3, 4, or 5 Hz). Higher frequency watches are more accurate but more expensive to produce and service. The Swiss lever escapement used in virtually all modern mechanical watches was essentially perfected by the 19th century.

The Mainspring and Power Reserve

The mainspring stores the energy that powers the watch. In a manual-wind watch, the wearer winds it daily by turning the crown. In an automatic (self-winding) watch, a rotor — a half-disc that spins freely with wrist movement — winds the spring automatically during wear. Power reserve is how long the watch runs fully wound without wearing it: typically 40–72 hours. A watch worn daily never approaches its power reserve limit. A watch stored for a weekend will need winding on Monday morning.

Movement Grades

Swiss movement grades from most to least precise: Chronometer (COSC-certified, tested to -4/+6 seconds per day), Standard Swiss (approximately -10/+30 seconds per day), and below. Japanese movements (Seiko, Orient, Citizen) often perform to chronometer specification without the certification cost. The practical implication: a certified chronometer gains or loses less than half a minute per week. A standard mechanical might gain or lose two minutes per week. For daily wear this is irrelevant — you set it Sunday, it is accurate all week.

Water Resistance Reality

The water resistance ratings on watches are tested statically, not dynamically. The pressure of diving into a pool produces forces far exceeding the static pressure the watch was rated at. The practical guide: 30m/3ATM -- keep dry, rain only. 50m/5ATM -- handwashing fine, no swimming. 100m/10ATM -- swimming and snorkelling fine. 200m/20ATM and above -- diving. The ratings on dress watches (often 30m) mean less than most wearers assume. Never shower with a watch not rated above 100m.

The Pre-Owned Market — The Correct Entry Point

Buying pre-owned watches is the most rational entry point for anyone who wants quality without paying the new retail premium. Mechanical watches do not depreciate as consumer electronics do — a well-maintained watch from a quality manufacturer holds most of its value and in some cases appreciates. The pre-owned market offers two advantages: lower price for the same watch, and access to discontinued references that are no longer available new.

  • Chrono24: The largest pre-owned watch marketplace. Filter by movement type, condition, and price. Buyer protection is available. For a first pre-owned purchase in the 200-600 EUR range, this is the correct platform.
  • The inspection protocol: Request full photos of the dial (any spots or discolouration), case (scratches and dings), crown (wear here indicates heavy use), caseback (check the reference number against the listing), and any papers or box. A watch sold "no papers" should be priced significantly below the same reference with original documentation.
  • Service history: A watch that has been recently serviced (within the last 3 years) is worth more than one with unknown service history. Ask the seller when it was last serviced. A watch needing service should be priced to reflect the cost of that service (typically 100-300 EUR depending on complexity).

The Collection Path

  • First watch: Seiko 5 Sports (automatic, 100m, reliable, under 150 EUR new). Orient Mako II (automatic, 200m, genuinely excellent value, under 150 EUR). These are not compromise watches — they are the correct first watches, providing the experience of mechanical watchmaking without the financial exposure of a learning period with an expensive piece.
  • Second watch: Once you understand what you actually want in a watch from daily wearing experience, the second purchase is significantly more informed. The pre-owned market opens at this point. A pre-owned Tissot PR516 or Hamilton Khaki Field in the 200-400 EUR range represents a significant upgrade in build quality and refinement.
  • The aspiration: Grand Seiko (Japanese finishing at Swiss prices), Longines Heritage line (undervalued Swiss manufacture), Nomos Glashutte (German precision and design, entry around 1,200 EUR new). These are watches that reward years of wearing rather than months.
A watch is the only jewellery a man traditionally wears, and the only object he carries that does something. The mechanism on the wrist -- older in its principles than the computer, the telephone, or the automobile -- is an argument for craft over convenience that never needs to be made aloud.-- Von Lycaon
Fragrance is the most personal accessory and the least understood. Most men own one bottle and wear it regardless of context, season, or occasion — which is the olfactory equivalent of wearing the same clothes every day. A considered fragrance wardrobe of three to four bottles covers every context and every season, elevates every interaction, and costs less than a single quality piece of clothing. This tab builds that wardrobe.— Von Lycaon

Understanding Fragrance

The Structure — Notes

Top notes: What you smell in the first 5–15 minutes. Volatile, fast-evaporating. Usually citrus, light herbs, fresh elements. The first impression — not the fragrance's true character. Heart notes: What emerges after 15 minutes and lasts 2–4 hours. The core character of the fragrance — florals, spices, woods. Base notes: What remains after 4+ hours. The longest-lasting elements — musks, amber, woods, resins. The base note is the final impression and often the most seductive.

Concentration

Eau de Cologne (EdC): 2–4% aromatic compounds. Lasts 2 hours. Eau de Toilette (EdT): 5–15%. Lasts 3–4 hours. The standard male fragrance format. Eau de Parfum (EdP): 15–20%. Lasts 5–8 hours. More expensive, more projection. Parfum: 20–30%. Lasts all day. The most concentrated and most expensive format. For daily wear in the workplace — EdT is correct. For evenings and special occasions — EdP or Parfum.

Application

Pulse points (wrists, neck, behind ears) produce the most consistent diffusion. Do not rub the wrists together after application — this crushes the top notes and produces an altered scent character. Spray at 10–15cm distance. Less is more: 2–3 sprays is sufficient. The correct sillage (projection) means people near you can smell the fragrance, not people across the room.

Seasonal Logic

Heat amplifies fragrance — heavy oriental and woody fragrances worn in summer Sarajevo (35°C) become overpowering. The seasonal framework: Spring/Summer: Aquatic, citrus, light florals, green notes. Autumn/Winter: Woody, amber, spice, oud, leather. This is not a rigid rule — it is a guideline based on how heat interacts with molecular diffusion.

The Fragrance Wardrobe — Four Bottles

💧 Daily / Office (Already Owned)

Acqua di Giò by Giorgio Armani (EdT): Already in the Grooming tab. Aquatic, marine, clean. The correct daytime office fragrance — present without demanding, clean without being sterile. The most consistently well-received masculine fragrance of the last 30 years for a reason. Use this as the default.

🌙 Evening / Date

Dior Sauvage (EdP): Also already in the Grooming tab. Bergamot opening, lavender heart, ambroxan base. More complex and projecting than Acqua di Giò — appropriate for evenings, dinner, occasions where making an impression is the intention. The EdP version over the EdT for evening use — the increased concentration extends the dry-down and the base note is more distinctive.

❄️ Winter Signature

Bleu de Chanel (EdP) or Tom Ford Oud Wood: For the Sarajevo winter — cold weather needs a warmer, denser fragrance. Bleu de Chanel: clean woody with citrus and cedar, versatile across all winter contexts. Tom Ford Oud Wood: warm, smoky, oud and spice — more distinctive, more expensive, appropriate for evenings and occasions where something more unusual is appropriate. Both widely available in BiH perfumeries.

🌿 Summer Alternative

Boss Bottled (already owned) or Terre d'Hermès EdT: Boss Bottled is in the Grooming tab — a fresh woody fragrance that works well in summer heat. Terre d'Hermès is the alternative for those who want something with more complexity — grapefruit, flint, vetiver. Unusual, distinctive, highly regarded. If the budget allows one addition, this is it.

Sampling Before Buying

  • Never buy a full bottle unsampled. Fragrance interacts with individual skin chemistry — the same perfume smells different on different people. What smells excellent on paper or in an advertisement may not work on your skin.
  • Sample decants: Fragrance split communities online (Reddit's r/fragrance, Basenotes.net) sell small decants (2–5ml) of almost any fragrance for a few euros. Wear the decant for 3–5 days before committing to a full bottle. The investment is 5 EUR; it prevents wasting 80–200 EUR on a fragrance that does not work for you.
  • Sarajevo testing: Perfumeries in SCC, BBI Centre, and the city centre allow testing on skin, not just paper strips. Paper strips are useful for initial elimination; skin testing is required for genuine evaluation.
A man's fragrance is the most persistent impression he leaves in a room — it arrives before him and lingers after he has gone. Own it deliberately.— Von Lycaon

The Fragrance Families in Depth

Woody and Aromatic

The dominant family for masculine fragrance. Cedarwood, sandalwood, vetiver, patchouli as base materials. The quality of wood notes ranges from the synthetic, linear cedarwood of budget fragrances to the complex, living sandalwood of the finest Indian attars. Key distinction: synthetic musks (found in most mainstream fragrances) produce a clean, diffuse sillage; natural musks (found in niche fragrances and vintage bottles) produce a skin-like warmth that is intimate rather than projecting. Vetiver is the finest and most versatile of the wood-adjacent materials -- earthy, smoky, clean, and identifiably itself.

Fougere (Fern-like)

The archetypal masculine fragrance family. Built on the accord of lavender (fresh top), oakmoss or its synthetic replacements (earthy, green middle), and coumarin (warm, hay-like base). Fougere is not a smell found in nature -- it is a construction, invented in the 19th century, that established what "men's fragrance" meant for most of the 20th century. Most barbershop fragrances are fougeres. Understanding this family is understanding the historical vocabulary of masculine scent.

Oriental and Resinous

Amber, benzoin, labdanum, oud, frankincense. These are the oldest fragrance materials -- the resins and balsams traded across the ancient world. Oriental fragrances are heavy, warm, and project powerfully, making them appropriate for evening and cold weather. Oud (agarwood) is the most expensive fragrance material by weight -- genuine oud is found in niche and Middle Eastern fragrances rather than mainstream releases, where "oud" notes are almost exclusively synthetic approximations. The genuine material is extraordinary.

Fresh and Aquatic

The post-1988 fragrance revolution. Cool Water (1988) introduced the dihydromyrcenol aquatic note that defined the next decade. Fresh fragrances project a clean, airy character that is appropriate for daytime, warm weather, and professional environments where projection needs to be minimal. The weakness: longevity is lower than wood or oriental fragrances; the synthetic materials that produce freshness do not age as gracefully. Appropriate in their context; boring as a sole focus.

The Sampling Protocol

No fragrance should be purchased without wearing it for a full day first. The reasons: a fragrance smells different after two hours than it did in the first five minutes (the top notes burn off, revealing the heart and base); fragrance chemistry is individual -- the same fragrance smells genuinely different on different people's skin chemistry; and the experience of wearing a fragrance through a full day and its activities is the only reliable test of whether it is actually enjoyable to wear.

  • Obtaining samples: Fragrantica.com has a sample/decant marketplace. Decant sellers (Split-fragrance on Etsy and similar platforms) offer 2-5ml samples of virtually any fragrance for 5-15 EUR. A comprehensive sampling programme of ten fragrances costs less than one bottle of a fragrance purchased without sampling. The Sarajevo approach: the perfumeries in the city (the dedicated counters at BBI Centre and SCC carry most major houses) provide blotter and wrist samples on request.
  • Evaluating a sample: Apply to the inner wrist in the morning. Evaluate at 30 minutes (the top notes are burning off -- is this pleasant?), at 2 hours (the heart -- is this something you want to smell like for the next six hours?), and at 6 hours (the base -- is this what lingers on clothes and skin?). The six-hour evaluation is the one that matters most for purchase decisions.

The Codex Wardrobe Recommendations -- Expanded

  • Daily/professional (clean, restrained): Bleu de Chanel EdP (richer and longer-lasting than the EdT), or Dior Homme (the original iris-forward formula -- woody, powdery, genuinely distinctive). Both project appropriately for office wear without dominating a room.
  • Summer/casual: Acqua di Gio Profumo (the flanker to the original -- much more complex and interesting, with incense base). Or L'Eau d'Issey Pour Homme for a cleaner aquatic with unusual longevity for the category.
  • Evening/formal: Tom Ford Tobacco Vanille (tobacco, vanilla, spice -- the most approachable Tom Ford private blend). Or Guerlain L'Homme Ideal (almond, leather, wood -- genuinely romantic without being sweet).
  • The niche investment: When budget allows -- Terre d'Hermes by Hermes (the architecture of the fragrance is unlike anything mainstream; woody, earthy, mineral), or anything from Maison Margiela's Replica line for situational wearing.
A man's fragrance is the last detail of his appearance and the most persistent. It is remembered long after the conversation has ended and the face has blurred in memory. Choose it with the same care you apply to everything else that communicates who you are.-- Von Lycaon
First aid knowledge has a specific value that no other knowledge in this Codex shares: it is useless until the moment it is the only thing that matters. The gap between a person who knows what to do and a person who does not, in the first four minutes of a medical emergency, is measured in lives. Learn this. Practise the muscle memory. Hope never to use it.— Von Lycaon
Critical note: This guide provides general first aid knowledge for reference. It does not replace certified first aid training. Enrol in a Red Cross (Crveni križ BiH) first aid course in Sarajevo — they run regular sessions and the hands-on practice produces competence that reading alone cannot. Emergency number in Bosnia and Herzegovina: 124 (ambulance). 123 (police). 125 (fire). Save these in your phone now if they are not already there.

CPR — Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation

Cardiac arrest — the heart stops pumping blood effectively — is survivable if CPR is begun within four minutes. After ten minutes without CPR, survival is unlikely. You are statistically more likely to need to perform CPR on a family member than a stranger. This is not abstract knowledge.

Adult CPR — The Sequence
When a person is unresponsive and not breathing normally
  • Check safety. Ensure the environment is safe for you to approach.
  • Check response. Tap shoulders firmly, shout "Are you okay?" If no response —
  • Call for help. Shout for someone to call 124. If alone, call 124 yourself on speaker and place the phone nearby — the dispatcher will guide you.
  • Check breathing. Tilt the head back, lift the chin. Look, listen, feel for normal breathing for no more than 10 seconds. Occasional gasps are not normal breathing.
  • Begin chest compressions. Place the heel of one hand on the centre of the chest (lower half of the breastbone). Place the other hand on top, fingers interlaced. Keep arms straight. Compress the chest 5–6cm deep. Release fully between compressions. Rate: 100–120 compressions per minute — the beat of "Stayin' Alive" by the Bee Gees is the correct tempo. This is not a joke — it is a genuine memory aid used in medical training.
  • 30 compressions, then 2 rescue breaths (if trained and willing). If not trained in rescue breaths, continuous chest compressions alone are effective — do not stop to attempt breaths you are uncertain about.
  • Continue until: the person shows signs of life, professional help arrives and takes over, or you are physically unable to continue.
  • AED (defibrillator): If an AED is available, use it immediately. AEDs are present in Sarajevo's major shopping centres (SCC, BBI, Alta), the airport, and larger hotels. They are designed for untrained use — turn on, follow the voice instructions exactly.

Choking Response

Adult Choking — Conscious Person
Person cannot cough, speak, or breathe effectively
  • Encourage coughing first. If the person can cough forcefully, encourage them to continue — an effective cough produces more force than any external intervention.
  • Five back blows. Stand to the side and slightly behind. Support the chest with one hand. Lean the person forward. Deliver five firm blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your other hand. Check after each blow whether the obstruction has cleared.
  • Five abdominal thrusts (Heimlich manoeuvre). Stand behind the person. Make a fist with one hand, place it thumb-side against the abdomen just above the navel and well below the breastbone. Grasp with the other hand. Pull sharply inward and upward. Five thrusts. Check after each.
  • Alternate five back blows and five abdominal thrusts until the obstruction clears or the person loses consciousness.
  • If the person loses consciousness: lower them carefully to the ground and begin CPR. The chest compressions may dislodge the obstruction.
  • Modified for pregnant or obese individuals: chest thrusts instead of abdominal thrusts — same position but hands on the centre of the chest rather than the abdomen.

Wound Care & Bleeding Control

Significant Bleeding
The goal is to control bleeding until professional help arrives
  • Apply direct pressure immediately. Use the cleanest material available — ideally a sterile dressing, practically a clean cloth, a folded T-shirt, or your bare hand if nothing else is available. Press firmly and continuously. Do not remove the dressing to check — if blood soaks through, apply more material on top and press harder.
  • Elevate if possible. Raise the injured limb above the level of the heart. Reduces blood pressure at the wound site.
  • Maintain pressure for minimum 10 minutes without releasing. Most bleeding from lacerations is controlled by sustained direct pressure alone.
  • Tourniquet — for life-threatening limb bleeding only. Apply 5–7cm above the wound. Tighten until bleeding stops. Note the time of application. Do not remove once applied. A tourniquet causes tissue damage — it is the correct choice when the alternative is the person bleeding to death. Available in pre-made form or improvised from a belt and a rigid object (pen, spoon) to tighten the twist.
  • Call 124 for any wound with significant bleeding that does not slow within 10 minutes of direct pressure.
Minor Wounds — Clean and Dress
Cuts, grazes, and small lacerations
  • Rinse thoroughly under clean running water for 5–10 minutes. This is the most important step — most wound infections begin with inadequate cleaning, not inadequate dressing.
  • Remove any visible debris with clean tweezers. Do not probe deeply — that is hospital territory.
  • Apply antiseptic — Betadine (jod) available at any Sarajevo apoteka. Allow to dry.
  • Cover with an appropriate plaster or sterile dressing. Change daily and when wet.
  • Signs requiring medical attention: wound edges that will not stay together (may need stitches), wounds on the face or over joints, any wound that shows signs of infection after 48 hours (increasing redness, warmth, swelling, pus, fever).

Burns & Scalds

Burn Treatment Protocol
The first 20 minutes determine the outcome
  • Cool the burn immediately with cool running water for 20 minutes. Not ice — ice causes additional tissue damage. Not butter, oil, or toothpaste — these are damaging myths that persist in Balkan household tradition. Cool running water only. Start the 20 minutes from when the water begins — if you cooled it for 5 minutes and then stopped, the clock resets.
  • Remove jewellery and clothing near the burn before swelling begins, unless stuck to the wound — do not remove stuck material.
  • Cover loosely with a clean non-fluffy material — cling film (folija za hranu) is the ideal burn covering, available in any kitchen. Lay it loosely over the burn, do not wrap. It protects without adhering.
  • Do not burst blisters. They are a sterile protective barrier. A burst blister is an open wound with infection risk.
  • Seek medical attention for: burns larger than the size of the person's hand, burns on the face, hands, feet, genitals, or over joints, all burns in children, any burn that is white, brown, or black (deep burns — may be less painful due to nerve damage, which makes them more serious, not less).

Fractures & Sprains

RICE Protocol — Sprains and Soft Tissue Injuries

  • R — Rest. Stop using the injured area immediately. Continuing to use a sprained ankle converts a minor injury into a significant one.
  • I — Ice. Apply ice or a cold pack wrapped in a cloth (never directly on skin) for 20 minutes every 2 hours for the first 48 hours. Reduces swelling and pain.
  • C — Compression. Apply a bandage firmly but not tightly — you should be able to slip two fingers under it. Reduces swelling.
  • E — Elevation. Raise the injured limb above heart level where possible. Reduces swelling through gravity.

Suspected Fracture

  • Immobilise, do not straighten. Support the injury in the position found. Attempting to straighten a fracture causes additional damage and extreme pain.
  • Splint if moving the person is necessary. Improvise a splint by padding and securing a rigid object (folded magazine, wooden board) either side of the fracture with bandages or torn clothing. The splint should extend beyond the joints above and below the fracture.
  • For suspected spinal injury: Do not move the person unless they are in immediate danger. Support the head in the position found. Call 124. Spinal injuries are irreversibly worsened by incorrect movement.
  • All suspected fractures require X-ray confirmation. Urgentni centar, Koševo Hospital, or any privatna klinika in Sarajevo with imaging facilities.

Medication & Pharmacy Basics

The Home Medicine Cabinet

Maintain: paracetamol (Lupocet or Panadol — for pain and fever), ibuprofen (Brufen — for pain, inflammation, and fever), antihistamine (Claritine or Zyrtec — for allergic reactions), oral rehydration salts (Rehidrat — for illness with vomiting or diarrhoea), Betadine antiseptic, sterile dressings and plasters in assorted sizes, bandages, medical tape, and a digital thermometer. All available at any Sarajevo apoteka without prescription.

Paracetamol vs Ibuprofen

Paracetamol: pain and fever, gentle on the stomach, safe for most people including those with stomach issues. Maximum 4g (8 standard tablets) per 24 hours — exceeding this causes liver damage. Ibuprofen: pain, fever, and inflammation (better for muscle pain, headaches with tension component, dental pain). Take with food — it irritates the stomach lining on an empty stomach. Avoid with kidney problems or stomach ulcers.

Pharmacy in Sarajevo

Apoteke are distributed throughout the city. 24-hour pharmacies: Apoteka "Sarajevo" locations operate extended hours — confirm the nearest with a quick search. Dežurna apoteka (on-duty pharmacy) rotates — in an emergency, any pharmacy can direct you to the current duty pharmacy. Prescription medications require a valid prescription from a doktor — bring documentation for anything requiring repeat dispensing.

When To Go To The Doctor

Fever above 39°C lasting more than 48 hours. Any infection showing spreading redness. Chest pain of any kind — do not self-manage, do not wait. Difficulty breathing. Any neurological symptom (sudden severe headache, vision changes, facial drooping, arm weakness, speech difficulty) — call 124 immediately, these are stroke symptoms. The Sarajevo Clinical Centre (KCUS) at Koševo handles genuine emergencies 24 hours.

Mental Health First Response

The following is not clinical intervention — it is how to be present and useful for someone experiencing a mental health crisis, which is a skill as practically important as physical first aid and considerably less taught.

When Someone Is In Distress

  • Stay present and calm. Your composure is the most useful thing you can offer. A calm, unhurried presence reduces the physiological stress response in the person experiencing distress. Do not rush to fix or advise. Be there first.
  • Ask directly. "Are you okay?" "Are you having thoughts of hurting yourself?" Asking directly about suicidal thoughts does not plant the idea — this is a persistent myth with no evidence behind it. Direct questions give the person permission to be honest and reduce the isolation of the experience.
  • Listen without problem-solving. The impulse to offer solutions is understandable but often unhelpful in acute distress. "That sounds incredibly hard" is more useful than "have you tried..." in the first minutes of a difficult conversation. Validation before advice, always.
  • Do not leave someone alone who has expressed thoughts of self-harm. Stay with them or ensure someone else does while professional help is contacted.
  • Professional resources in Sarajevo: Psychiatrijska klinika at KCUS Koševo — acute psychiatric emergencies. SOS telefon: 1264 (Bosnia crisis line). For ongoing support: private psychologist practices throughout the city, increasingly available and increasingly de-stigmatised. Mental health treatment is medical treatment.

Panic Attack — Response Protocol

  • A panic attack is a genuine physiological event — not a choice or an overreaction. The person is experiencing real physical symptoms including heart palpitations, shortness of breath, dizziness, and a profound sense of danger.
  • Do not tell them to calm down. This is physiologically impossible in the moment and communicates that you do not understand what is happening.
  • Get to a quiet space if possible. Reduce sensory input.
  • Ground them with the 5-4-3-2-1 technique: Name 5 things you can see. 4 things you can physically feel. 3 things you can hear. 2 things you can smell. 1 thing you can taste. This technique redirects the nervous system's attention from the internal threat response to external sensory reality and is clinically validated.
  • Breathe together. Demonstrate the breathing pattern: 4 counts in, hold 4, out 6. Do it visibly and slowly. The person will often unconsciously mirror your breathing pattern.
  • A panic attack is not dangerous in itself and typically passes within 10–20 minutes. Recurring panic attacks warrant professional assessment.
Competence in a crisis is a form of love. The person who knows what to do when something goes wrong offers something no amount of good intention without knowledge can replace. Learn this. Not because emergencies are likely — but because the one time they are not unlikely, you will already know.— Von Lycaon
Preparedness is not paranoia. It is the rational acknowledgment that certain disruptions — power outages, earthquakes, severe weather, medical emergencies — are statistically inevitable over a lifetime in Sarajevo. The prepared person experiences these as inconveniences. The unprepared person experiences them as crises. The difference is almost entirely a function of decisions made before the event, not during it.— Von Lycaon

Sarajevo-Specific Context

Seismic Risk

Bosnia and Herzegovina sits on active fault lines. Sarajevo has experienced significant earthquakes historically. The 1969 Banja Luka earthquake caused widespread damage across the region. Preparedness for seismic events is not theoretical — it is geographically appropriate for this city.

Power Infrastructure

Sarajevo experiences occasional power outages, more frequently during winter peak demand and severe storms. Extended outages are uncommon but occur. An apartment in winter without heating during an extended outage is a genuine emergency at -10°C outside.

Flood Risk

The Miljacka river and its tributaries have caused localised flooding. Dobrinja and low-lying areas near the river carry higher flood risk. The 2014 floods across Bosnia were the worst in 120 years of recorded data. Know whether your apartment's location carries any flood exposure.

Winter Severity

Sarajevo winters can produce heavy snowfall, ice, and temperatures below -15°C. The city's infrastructure handles this reasonably well but disruptions to transport and utilities during severe winter events are normal rather than exceptional.

The Emergency Kit — Build This

The emergency kit lives in one accessible location — a bag or box that both you and your wife know the location of and can access in the dark. Not scattered across the apartment. One location. Review and rotate contents annually.

💧 Water

Minimum 4 litres per person per day. Store at least 8 litres per person for 72 hours — 16 litres minimum for two people. Commercial 1.5L or 2L bottles, rotated every 12 months. Sarajevo tap water is safe and excellent — but municipal supply can be disrupted by earthquake, pipe damage, or contamination events. The stored water covers the first 72 hours until supply is restored or alternatives arranged.

🍫 Non-Perishable Food

3-day supply minimum. Items requiring no cooking and no refrigeration: tinned tuna, tinned beans, crackers, peanut butter, nuts, dried fruit, chocolate, UHT milk. Rotate stock every 12 months by consuming and replacing. The food should be things you would actually eat — an emergency is not the moment to discover that you dislike tinned sardines.

🔦 Light Sources

Two torches with fresh batteries — one in the kit, one in a consistent bedside location for immediate access in a dark emergency. One headlamp (already in the tool kit from the Home tab — the crossover is intentional). Candles and matches as backup. During the 2014 floods and associated power outages, light sources were the first shortage reported by Sarajevo residents.

📻 Battery Radio

During a major emergency, mobile networks become congested or fail. A battery-powered or hand-crank radio receives RTV Federacije and other Bosnian state broadcasts — the official information source during declared emergencies. A smartphone with no network is less useful than a radio with batteries.

🏥 First Aid Kit

A complete kit beyond the home medicine cabinet: sterile dressings in multiple sizes, bandages (rolled and triangular sling), medical tape, scissors, tweezers, disposable gloves, emergency foil blanket, CPR face shield, tourniquet. Pre-assembled kits available at Sarajevo pharmacies and Bauhaus. Review contents against the First Aid tab.

📱 Power Bank

A high-capacity power bank (20,000mAh minimum) fully charged and stored in the emergency kit. Charges a smartphone 4–5 times. During a power outage lasting more than a few hours, the phone becomes the primary tool for information, communication, and emergency contact. A dead phone during a crisis is a serious liability.

📄 Documents

Copies of: lična karta (ID card), passport, apartment ownership documents (vlasnički list), insurance policies, bank account information, emergency contacts written on paper (not only stored digitally). Store in a waterproof envelope. If evacuation is necessary, these documents leave with you. Replacing them after an emergency is a months-long process.

💊 Medications

A 7-day supply of any regular prescription medications. If either you or your wife takes daily medication, a week's supply in the emergency kit is non-negotiable. Pharmacies close during major emergencies. The medication you need most will not be available when you need it most unless you already have it.

Earthquake — During and After

During an Earthquake

  • Drop, Cover, Hold On. Drop to hands and knees — this prevents being knocked down. Take cover under a sturdy table or desk, or against an interior wall away from windows. Hold on and protect your head and neck with your arms if no shelter is available.
  • Do not run outside during shaking. The most dangerous moment is crossing the threshold while the building is moving — falling debris is the primary cause of earthquake injury. Stay inside until the shaking stops.
  • Away from windows, exterior walls, and anything that can fall. Bookcases, mirrors, light fixtures. The interior doorframe is not the safe position it was once considered — Drop, Cover, Hold On under a table is the current evidence-based guidance.
  • In bed: Stay there. Pull the pillow over your head. The mattress provides significant protection from falling debris.

After an Earthquake

  • Check for injuries before moving. Adrenaline masks pain. Assess yourself and anyone with you before standing.
  • Check for gas smell. If detected — do not operate any switches or create any spark. Open windows, leave the building, and call JP Sarajevogas: 145 or 033 276 100. Do not re-enter until cleared.
  • Check for structural damage before staying in the apartment. Diagonal cracks in walls (not vertical or horizontal which are common settling cracks), shifted door frames, separated floor-to-ceiling cracks are warning signs. If uncertain, leave and seek assessment.
  • Expect aftershocks. They follow all significant earthquakes. Apply the same Drop, Cover, Hold On response.
  • Information: Federal Civil Protection (Federalna uprava civilne zaštite) is the official source during declared emergencies. Monitor RTV Federacije on the battery radio.

Power Outage Protocol

  • First five minutes: Check whether the outage is your apartment only (check the fuse box) or the building/neighbourhood. Look out the window — are neighbours' lights on? If building-wide, it is infrastructure. Report to JP Elektroprivreda BiH: 0800 20 200 (free call).
  • Preserve phone battery immediately. Reduce screen brightness to minimum. Disable WiFi and mobile data if not needed — these drain battery searching for signal. Enable power saving mode. The power bank from the emergency kit charges from now.
  • Food safety: A closed refrigerator maintains safe temperature for 4 hours. A full freezer maintains safe temperature for 48 hours. Do not open either unless necessary. If the outage extends beyond 4 hours, consume refrigerated items first — cooked foods, dairy, meat. Frozen items second.
  • Heating in winter: Close all interior doors to retain room temperature in the smallest occupied space. Body heat in a small room is significant. Layer clothing. The emergency foil blankets in the kit reflect body heat with remarkable efficiency.
  • Candles: Never leave lit unattended. Never place near curtains, paper, or anything flammable. Extinguish before sleep.

Emergency Contacts — Sarajevo

  • 124 — Hitna pomoć (ambulance)
  • 123 — Policija
  • 125 — Vatrogasci (fire service)
  • 145 — Sarajevogas (gas emergency)
  • 0800 20 200 — JP Elektroprivreda BiH (power outage)
  • 1264 — SOS telefon (crisis line)
  • 033 441 210 — KCUS Koševo (Clinical Centre)
  • 033 276 180 — Urgentni centar (emergency centre)

Print this list. Store one copy in the emergency kit document envelope. Store one copy visible in the kitchen — not because you will forget the numbers, but because a guest or your wife may need them when you are not present.

The prepared person does not fear emergencies — they have already made the decisions that emergencies demand. Every item in the kit, every number in the list, every drill in the protocol represents a decision made calmly in advance. When the moment arrives, calm decisions made in advance are the only kind that work.— Von Lycaon
